topics.title: Camera Calibration: Pinhole Camera Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Camera Calibration: Pinhole Camera Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Pinhole Camera Model** is the fundamental geometric abstraction used in computer vision to describe the mathematical relationship between a 3D point in the real world and its 2D projection on the image plane of an ideal camera. It assumes light rays from a scene pass through an infinitesimally small aperture (the **pinhole**) and project an inverted image onto a focal plane located at a distance equal to the **focal length** $f$ behind the aperture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Camera Calibration** is the offline process of estimating the complete set of parameters — the **intrinsic parameters** (focal length, principal point, skew, and distortion coefficients) and the **extrinsic parameters** (3D rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ describing the camera's pose in the world frame) — that map world coordinates to pixel coordinates through the pinhole model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT743 / Module 1):**
> Camera Calibration is the FIRST topic of Module 1 and forms the bedrock for all subsequent topics such as Stereo Vision, Depth Estimation, and 3D Reconstruction. Expect direct 3-mark questions on definitions and 14-mark derivations on the projection geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dark Room" Camera

Imagine you are standing in a **completely dark room** with only one tiny hole in one wall (the *pinhole*). Outside the room, there is a brightly lit tree. Light rays from the tree pass through the tiny hole and fall on the opposite wall inside the room, forming a perfectly **inverted** (upside-down and left-right flipped) image of the tree.

- The **distance between the pinhole and the back wall** is your *focal length* $f$.
- If you **increase $f$**, the image gets **bigger** but **dimmer** (more spread out).
- If you **decrease $f$**, the image gets **smaller** but **brighter**.
- The **position where the optical axis meets the back wall** is your *principal point* $(c_x, c_y)$.

This dark-room experiment is exactly the model used in every modern digital camera, smartphone, and even the Mars Rover's navigation cameras — the only difference is that a *lens* is added to gather more light (at the cost of introducing distortion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Lock-In (must memorize):**
> - **Pinhole / Center of Projection:** The point where all light rays converge, denoted $\mathbf{C}$ or $\mathbf{O}$.
> - **Image Plane (Focal Plane):** The 2D plane where the image is formed, at distance $f$ from the pinhole.
> - **Optical Axis:** The line passing through $\mathbf{C}$ perpendicular to the image plane.
> - **Principal Point:** The intersection of the optical axis with the image plane, ideally at the image center.
> - **Focal Length:** The perpendicular distance from $\mathbf{C}$ to the image plane, measured in millimeters (physical) or pixels (digital).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Field of View (FOV):** Typical values range from **$40°$ to $75°$** for consumer cameras, with wide-angle lenses (GoPro) exceeding **$120°$**.
- **Focal Length (35mm equivalent):** Smartphones: **$24$–$28$ mm**, DSLRs: **$18$–$200$ mm**, Telephoto: **$300$–$800$ mm**.
- **Sensor Resolution:** Modern CV cameras range from **VGA (640×480)** to **$4$K (3840×2160)** and beyond.
- **Aperture (Physical Pinhole Equivalent):** Physical pinhole diameter is typically **$0.1$ mm** to **$1.0$ mm** for a true pinhole camera; conventional cameras use lens diameters of **$5$ mm to $50$ mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of the Pinhole Projection

The pinhole projection pipeline transforms a 3D world point into a 2D pixel coordinate through a **strictly ordered sequence of four rigid transformations**. Each step uses a different coordinate frame.

1. **World Frame $\to$ Camera Frame (Extrinsic Transformation):**
   A 3D point $\mathbf{X}_W = (X_W, Y_W, Z_W)$ in the world coordinate system is first rotated and translated into the camera's own coordinate system $\mathbf{X}_C = (X_C, Y_C, Z_C)$ using the extrinsic parameters.

   $$\mathbf{X}_C = \mathbf{R} \, \mathbf{X}_W + \mathbf{t}$$

2. **Camera Frame $\to$ Normalized Image Plane (Perspective Projection):**
   Using similar triangles, the camera-frame point is projected onto the *virtual* image plane (located at unit distance in front of the camera, with the Z-axis flipped to remove inversion).

   $$x_n = \frac{X_C}{Z_C}, \qquad y_n = \frac{Y_C}{Z_C}$$

3. **Normalized Plane $\to$ Pixel Coordinates (Intrinsic Transformation):**
   The normalized coordinates are scaled by the focal length in pixels and shifted by the principal point offset.

   $$u = f_x \, x_n + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \, y_n + c_y$$

4. **Pixel Coordinates $\to$ Distorted Pixel Coordinates (Lens Distortion):**
   For real lenses, radial and tangential distortion corrections are applied:

   $$x_d = x_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + 2p_1 x_n y_n + p_2(r^2 + 2x_n^2)$$
   $$y_d = y_n (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6) + p_1(r^2 + 2y_n^2) + 2p_2 x_n y_n$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why extrinsic first?** Because the world is absolute and the camera moves. Establishing pose first lets us reason purely inside the camera frame.
- **Why the normalized plane at Z=1?** It removes the focal length from the projection math, giving us a *projection-invariant* 2D representation that is purely a function of direction.
- **Why principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$?** The optical center of a real CMOS/CCD sensor is rarely exactly at the geometric center of the pixel array — manufacturing tolerances introduce an offset.
- **Why separate $f_x$ and $f_y$?** Square pixels are an idealization. Real sensors may have slightly non-square pixels, giving different focal lengths in pixels along each axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Unified Pinhole Equation (The Most Important Equation of Module 1):**
> $$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic Matrix } \mathbf{K}} \cdot \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Extrinsic Matrix } (3 \times 4)} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
> where $\lambda$ is the homogeneous scaling factor, $s$ is the skew (zero for most modern sensors), and $c_x, c_y$ are principal point coordinates.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Name** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **DOF Count** |
|------------|----------|--------------------------|-----------|---------------|
| $f$ | Physical focal length | Distance from $\mathbf{C}$ to image plane | mm | 1 |
| $f_x$ | Focal length in X (pixels) | $f \cdot m_x$ | pixels | 1 |
| $f_y$ | Focal length in Y (pixels) | $f \cdot m_y$ | pixels | 1 |
| $c_x$ | Principal point X | Pixel column of optical center | pixels | 1 |
| $c_y$ | Principal point Y | Pixel row of optical center | pixels | 1 |
| $s$ | Skew parameter | $\tan(\theta) \cdot f_x$, $\theta$ = axis skew angle | pixels | 1 |
| $\mathbf{K}$ | Intrinsic matrix (3×3) | $\begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | unitless | 5 |
| $\mathbf{R}$ | Rotation matrix (3×3) | $\mathbf{R}^T \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, $\det(\mathbf{R}) = 1$ | unitless | 3 |
| $\mathbf{t}$ | Translation vector (3×1) | Position of camera origin in world frame | m / mm | 3 |
| $\mathbf{P}$ | Camera projection matrix (3×4) | $\mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ | unitless | 12 |
| $\mathbf{X}_C$ | Camera-frame point (3×1) | $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_W + \mathbf{t}$ | m / mm | 3 |
| $x_n, y_n$ | Normalized image coords | $X_C / Z_C, \, Y_C / Z_C$ | unitless | 2 |
| $u, v$ | Pixel image coords | $f_x x_n + c_x, \, f_y y_n + c_y$ | pixels | 2 |
| $k_1, k_2, k_3$ | Radial distortion coefficients | $x_n(1 + k_1 r^2 + \ldots)$ | unitless | 3 |
| $p_1, p_2$ | Tangential distortion coefficients | As shown in §2.1 | unitless | 2 |
| Total DOF | Full calibration | $\mathbf{K}$ (5) + $\mathbf{R}$ (3) + $\mathbf{t}$ (3) + distortion (5) | — | **16** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Answers:** When asked for the *total number of parameters* to be estimated, the answer is **$11$ (without distortion)** or **$15$ (with 5-parameter distortion model)**. Examiners specifically look for the breakdown: "5 intrinsic + 6 extrinsic = 11."

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics (SLAM):** Autonomous vehicles and drones use pinhole calibration to convert 2D image features into 3D ray directions for obstacle avoidance.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopes and microscopes are calibrated so surgeons can relate pixel measurements to physical tissue dimensions.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** ARCore, ARKit, and HoloLens perform online pinhole calibration to overlay virtual objects with correct perspective.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Sub-pixel calibration enables measurement accuracies of **$< 0.01$ mm** at working distances of **$1$ m**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Perspective Projection from Similar Triangles

**Statement:** Show that a 3D point $\mathbf{X}_C = (X_C, Y_C, Z_C)$ in the camera frame projects to normalized image coordinates $(x_n, y_n) = \left( \frac{X_C}{Z_C}, \frac{Y_C}{Z_C} \right)$ using the pinhole geometry.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** Place the camera center $\mathbf{C}$ at the origin of the camera frame. The image plane is placed at $Z = f$ in front of $\mathbf{C}$ (using the *virtual* image plane convention to avoid inverted images).

**Step 2 — Draw the two similar triangles.** A 3D point $\mathbf{X}_C$ and its projection $(x, y, f)$ are connected by a straight line passing through $\mathbf{C}$ (the origin).

**Step 3 — Apply similar triangle ratios along the X-axis.** The triangle formed by $(0, 0, 0)$, $(X_C, 0, 0)$, and $(X_C, 0, Z_C)$ is similar to the triangle formed by $(0, 0, 0)$, $(x, 0, 0)$, and $(x, 0, f)$. Therefore:

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X_C}{Z_C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{f \cdot X_C}{Z_C}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the same logic along the Y-axis.**

$$\frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y_C}{Z_C} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y = \frac{f \cdot Y_C}{Z_C}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to normalized coordinates** by dividing through by $f$ to get the projection at unit depth $Z = 1$:

$$x_n = \frac{x}{f} = \frac{X_C}{Z_C}, \qquad y_n = \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y_C}{Z_C}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of perspective projection**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion to Pixel Coordinates (Intrinsic Matrix)

**Statement:** Derive the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ that maps a 3D direction to pixel coordinates.

**Step 1 — Pixel scaling.** The image plane is sampled into discrete pixels. If there are $m_x$ pixels per millimeter along the X-axis and $m_y$ along the Y-axis, then the focal length in pixels is:

$$f_x = f \cdot m_x, \qquad f_y = f \cdot m_y$$

**Step 2 — Account for principal point offset.** The origin of the pixel coordinate system is typically the *top-left corner* of the image (in computer vision convention), while the projection geometry places the origin at the principal point. Therefore, the pixel coordinates are:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y$$

**Step 3 — Handle pixel skew.** In the rare case where the pixel rows and columns are not perpendicular, introduce a shear parameter $s$:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + s \cdot y_n + c_x$$

**Step 4 — Express in homogeneous form.** By introducing a third coordinate $w = 1$ and packaging the equations into a single matrix multiplication, we obtain the canonical form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_C / Z_C \\ Y_C / Z_C \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} x_n \\ y_n \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The symbol "$\sim$" denotes equality up to a non-zero scalar (homogeneous equivalence). This matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is the **camera intrinsic matrix**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Full World-to-Pixel Projection Equation

**Statement:** Derive the complete pinhole projection from world coordinates to pixel coordinates.

**Step 1 — World-to-camera transformation.** The 3D point in world coordinates $\mathbf{X}_W$ must first be transformed into the camera frame using the rigid-body transformation:

$$\mathbf{X}_C = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_W + \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 2 — Express in homogeneous form** by appending a fourth coordinate $w = 1$ to $\mathbf{X}_W$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_C \\ Y_C \\ Z_C \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{3 \times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3 \times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{4 \times 4 \, \text{rigid transform}} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Combine with intrinsic matrix.** The complete pinhole equation in homogeneous coordinates is:

$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 4} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Define the projection matrix** $\mathbf{P}_{3 \times 4} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$. The calibration problem is to find $\mathbf{P}$ (or equivalently, $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ separately) given a set of known 3D-2D correspondences.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Calibration Equations Solved

| **Stage** | **Mathematical Operation** | **Inputs Required** | **Output** | **DOF Recovered** |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1. Data collection | Capture checkerboard from $\geq 3$ views | N 3D-2D point pairs | $\{(X_i, Y_i, Z_i, u_i, v_i)\}$ | 0 |
| 2. Homography estimation | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{t}]$ (planar target) | One view at a time | $\mathbf{H}$ per view | 8 per view |
| 3. Intrinsic init | $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ from multiple $\mathbf{H}$'s | $\geq 3$ views | $\mathbf{K}$ | 5 |
| 4. Extrinsic recovery | $\mathbf{R} = [\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{r}_3], \, \mathbf{t} = \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_3$ | $\mathbf{K}$ and one $\mathbf{H}$ | Pose per view | 6 per view |
| 5. Non-linear refinement | Minimize $\sum_i \sum_j \lVert \mathbf{m}_{ij} - \hat{\mathbf{m}}(\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i, \mathbf{X}_j) \rVert^2$ via Levenberg-Marquardt | Initial estimates + distortion params | Refined $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}, \mathbf{dist}$ | 11 + 5 = 16 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Zhang's Method (2000) trick:** A flat checkerboard provides a 2D target (Z=0 for all points), reducing the projection to a homography $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{t}]$. This eliminates the need for a 3D calibration object and is the method used by **OpenCV's `calibrateCamera()`** function. The KTU syllabus emphasizes this method — be ready to explain it.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A camera has focal length $f = 50$ mm, image plane resolution $640 \times 480$ pixels, and pixel pitch $\rho = 0.01$ mm/pixel. The principal point is at the geometric center. Find the intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute $f_x = f / \rho_x = 50 / 0.01 = 5000$ pixels.

**Step 2** — Compute $f_y = f / \rho_y = 50 / 0.01 = 5000$ pixels (square pixels).

**Step 3** — Principal point: $c_x = 640 / 2 = 320$ pixels, $c_y = 480 / 2 = 240$ pixels.

**Step 4** — Skew $s = 0$ (square pixels).

**Step 5** — Therefore:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 5000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 5000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6** — A world point at $(1 \text{ m}, 2 \text{ m}, 10 \text{ m})$ in camera frame (with $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}, \mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$) projects to:

$$u = 5000 \cdot \frac{1}{10} + 320 = 820 \text{ pixels}, \quad v = 5000 \cdot \frac{2}{10} + 240 = 1240 \text{ pixels}$$

Since $v = 1240 > 480$, the point is **outside the image** — physically, the camera is too close or the FOV is too narrow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pinhole Projection Geometry (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["World Point XW = XW,YW,ZW"] --> B["Rigid Transform RXW + t"]
    B --> C["Camera Frame Point XC = XC,YC,ZC"]
    C --> D["Perspective Division XC/ZC , YC/ZC"]
    D --> E["Normalized Image Plane xn,yn"]
    E --> F["Apply Intrinsic Matrix K 3x3"]
    F --> G["Apply Distortion Model k1 k2 p1 p2"]
    G --> H["Final Pixel Coordinate u,v"]
    I["Calibration Inputs: Checkerboard Images"] --> J["Zhang Algorithm"]
    J --> K["Estimate K , R , t , dist"]
    K --> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#e8f5e9
    style H fill:#fce4ec
    style K fill:#f3e5f5
```

## 4.2 Camera Calibration Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Stage 1 Capture N views of planar checkerboard"] --> B["Stage 2 Detect 2D corners using findChessboardCorners"]
    B --> C["Stage 3 Initialize K using homography constraints"]
    C --> D["Stage 4 Recover R and t per view"]
    D --> E["Stage 5 Levenberg Marquardt nonlinear refinement"]
    E --> F["Stage 6 Output K , dist , R vec , t vec per view"]
    G["Validation: Reprojection error in pixels"] --> F
    F --> H["Quality check: mean error less than 0.5 px"]
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style B fill:#c8e6c9
    style C fill:#fff9c4
    style D fill:#ffccbc
    style E fill:#f8bbd0
    style F fill:#d1c4e9
    style H fill:#b2dfdb
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["World Frame W: Origin at scene reference point"] -->|"Rotation R 3x3"| B["Camera Frame C: Origin at pinhole C"]
    B -->|"Intrinsic K 3x3"| C["Image Frame I: Origin at top left pixel"]
    C -->|"Distortion model"| D["Distorted Frame D: Real lens output"]
    A -.->|"Inverse rotation R transpose"| A
    style A fill:#ffecb3
    style B fill:#c8e6c9
    style C fill:#bbdefb
    style D fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.4 Distortion Model Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Normalized undistorted xn,yn"] --> B["Compute r squared = xn squared + yn squared"]
    B --> C["Apply radial distortion 1 + k1 r2 + k2 r4 + k3 r6"]
    C --> D["Apply tangential correction 2 p1 xn yn and p2 r2 + 2 xn squared"]
    D --> E["Distorted normalized xd,yd"]
    E --> F["Convert to pixels using K matrix"]
    F --> G["Final pixel u,v on sensor"]
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#fff3e0
    style C fill:#f3e5f5
    style D fill:#e8f5e9
    style G fill:#ffebee
```
