<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the pinhole camera model and list its key parameters. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The pinhole camera model is a geometric abstraction that describes image formation as light rays from a 3D scene passing through an infinitesimally small aperture (the **pinhole / center of projection $\mathbf{C}$**) and forming an inverted 2D image on a focal plane at distance $f$ (**focal length**) from the aperture.

**Key parameters:**
1. **Intrinsic parameters (5 DOF):** Focal lengths $f_x, f_y$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, and skew $s$.
2. **Extrinsic parameters (6 DOF):** Rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ (3 DOF) and translation vector $\mathbf{t}$ (3 DOF).
3. **Distortion parameters (5 DOF optional):** Radial $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and tangential $p_1, p_2$.

These are combined into the **camera matrix** $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ such that $\lambda \mathbf{m} = \mathbf{P} \mathbf{X}_W$.

### Question 2: Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters with examples. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Intrinsic Parameters** | **Extrinsic Parameters** |
|------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **What it represents** | Internal camera geometry (focal length, sensor size, principal point) | Camera's position and orientation in the world |
| **Components** | $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$ | $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ |
| **DOF** | 5 (plus 5 for distortion) | 6 |
| **Changes when camera moves?** | No (camera-specific) | Yes (scene-specific) |
| **Example** | $f_x = 800$ pixels, $c_y = 240$ pixels | $\mathbf{t} = (0.5, 0, 1.2)^T$ m |

Intrinsic parameters are **camera-specific** (do not change when the camera moves), while extrinsic parameters describe the **camera's pose in the world** and must be recomputed for each viewpoint.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the complete pinhole camera projection equation from world coordinates $(X_W, Y_W, Z_W)$ to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$, clearly explaining the role of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A camera with $f = 35$ mm focal length, $640 \times 480$ pixel resolution, and pixel size $\rho = 0.02$ mm is used. The world point is at $(0.5, 1.0, 5.0)$ m in the camera frame. Compute the pixel coordinates, assuming the principal point is at the image center and no distortion. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Projection Equation Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — World to Camera Frame (Extrinsic):** The 3D point is transformed using rigid-body motion.

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_C \\ Y_C \\ Z_C \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{R} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Perspective Division:** Using similar triangles (as derived in §3.1), the camera-frame point projects to normalized coordinates:

$$x_n = \frac{X_C}{Z_C}, \qquad y_n = \frac{Y_C}{Z_C}$$

**Step 3 — Pixel Conversion (Intrinsic):** Scale by focal length in pixels and shift by principal point:

$$u = f_x \cdot x_n + c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_C}{Z_C} + c_x$$
$$v = f_y \cdot y_n + c_y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_C}{Z_C} + c_y$$

**Step 4 — Homogeneous Form:** Combine into a single matrix equation by introducing $\lambda = Z_C$:

$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_W \\ Y_W \\ Z_W \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Projection Matrix:** $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is a $3 \times 4$ matrix with 11 DOF. The 12th DOF is consumed by the homogeneous scale factor $\lambda$.

**Conclusion:** The complete projection decomposes into three conceptual stages — **extrinsic** (pose), **perspective** (3D to 2D), and **intrinsic** (sensor mapping).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute focal length in pixels:**

$$f_x = f_y = \frac{f}{\rho} = \frac{35 \text{ mm}}{0.02 \text{ mm/pixel}} = 1750 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2 — Principal point coordinates:**

$$c_x = \frac{640}{2} = 320 \text{ pixels}, \quad c_y = \frac{480}{2} = 240 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3 — Intrinsic matrix (with $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}, \mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0}$):**

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1750 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 1750 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply projection (note: world point already in camera frame):**

$$u = 1750 \cdot \frac{0.5}{5.0} + 320 = 1750 \cdot 0.1 + 320 = 175 + 320 = 495 \text{ pixels}$$
$$v = 1750 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5.0} + 240 = 1750 \cdot 0.2 + 240 = 350 + 240 = 590 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 5 — Validity check:** Both $u \in [0, 640)$ and $v \in [0, 480)$? No — $v = 590 > 480$. **The point falls outside the image vertically**, meaning the camera is too close or the FOV is too narrow for this point.

**Final Answer:** $(u, v) = (495, 590)$ — but the point is **outside the image**; the camera cannot see it.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of camera calibration. Describe the Zhang's planar calibration method with a neat block diagram and list the parameters estimated. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a camera with $f_x = 1200$ pixels, $f_y = 1200$ pixels, $c_x = 640$ pixels, $c_y = 360$ pixels, compute the projection of the world point $(2, 3, 8)$ m in camera coordinates. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Zhang's Method (7 Marks)

**Concept of Camera Calibration:** Camera calibration is the process of determining the **intrinsic, extrinsic, and distortion parameters** of a camera from observed 2D image points of a known 3D target (e.g., checkerboard) so that the camera can be used as a **metric measuring device** (relating pixels to millimeters).

**Zhang's Planar Method (2000):** A flat checkerboard pattern of known square size is imaged from **at least 3 different orientations**. Because the target is planar (Z=0 for all points), the 3D-to-2D mapping per view reduces to a **homography**:

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{t}]$$

**Step-by-step pipeline:**

1. **Capture images:** Photograph the checkerboard from $N \geq 3$ viewpoints.
2. **Detect corners:** Use sub-pixel corner detection (e.g., `findChessboardCorners`).
3. **Estimate homography** $\mathbf{H}_i$ per view via DLT (Direct Linear Transform).
4. **Set up constraint on $\mathbf{K}$:** The vectors $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{r}_2$ are orthonormal, giving $\mathbf{h}_1^T \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_2 = 0$ and $\mathbf{h}_1^T \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{h}_2^T \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_2$. With $\geq 3$ views, solve for $\mathbf{K}$.
5. **Recover extrinsics:** $\mathbf{r}_1 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_1$, $\mathbf{r}_2 = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_2$, $\mathbf{r}_3 = \mathbf{r}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_2$, $\mathbf{t} = \lambda \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{h}_3$.
6. **Refine with Levenberg-Marquardt** to minimize reprojection error over all views.

**Parameters estimated:** $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$ (intrinsic), $\mathbf{R}_i, \mathbf{t}_i$ per view (extrinsic), and $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$ (distortion).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Given parameters:**

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 1200 & 0 & 640 \\ 0 & 1200 & 360 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Project the 3D point** $(X_C, Y_C, Z_C) = (2, 3, 8)$ m:

$$u = 1200 \cdot \frac{2}{8} + 640 = 1200 \cdot 0.25 + 640 = 300 + 640 = 940 \text{ pixels}$$
$$v = 1200 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 360 = 1200 \cdot 0.375 + 360 = 450 + 360 = 810 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3 — Validity check (assuming HD sensor $1920 \times 1080$):** Both 940 and 810 are within bounds. Point is visible.

**Final Answer:** $(u, v) = (940, 810)$ pixels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the principal point offset:** Many students compute $u = f_x \cdot X_C / Z_C$ only, missing $+c_x$. **This loses 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing focal length in mm vs pixels:** If the question gives $f$ in mm, you **must** divide by pixel pitch $\rho$ to get $f_x, f_y$ in pixels. Mixing units is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Skipping unit conversion in numericals:** Always show $u = 1750 \times (0.5/5.0) + 320$ with explicit units. Half the marks are for **the working**, not just the answer.
> 4. **Not justifying orthonormality of $\mathbf{R}$:** When deriving Zhang's method, you **must** state $\mathbf{r}_1 \cdot \mathbf{r}_2 = 0$ and $\lVert \mathbf{r}_1 \rVert = \lVert \mathbf{r}_2 \rVert = 1$. This justifies the two constraints per view.
> 5. **Forgetting distortion:** In real cameras, points off-center deviate. Examiners may deduct 1 mark if you claim "the pinhole model is exact" without mentioning lens distortion.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pinhole Model Essence:** $\lambda \mathbf{m} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}_W$ — *memorize the exact matrix form.*
- **Intrinsic DOF:** **5** ($f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y, s$); with distortion: **10–16**.
- **Extrinsic DOF:** **6** ($\mathbf{R}$: 3, $\mathbf{t}$: 3).
- **Total DOF:** **11** (no distortion) or **15–16** (with 5-param distortion).
- **Virtual Image Plane Convention:** Place at $Z = +f$ in **front** of pinhole to avoid image inversion; mathematically, flip the $Z$-axis in the projection.
- **Zhang's Method Needs:** $\geq 3$ views of a **planar** checkerboard, corner detection, homography estimation, orthonormality constraints, Levenberg-Marquardt refinement.
- **Intrinsic Matrix is Constant** for a given camera (does not change with pose); **Extrinsic Matrix Changes** for every new viewpoint.
- **Skew $s$ is Zero** for almost all modern sensors with square pixels.
- **Reprojection Error** is the gold standard for validation — aim for **mean error < 0.5 pixels**.
- **Radial distortion dominates** in real lenses; $k_1$ is usually the most significant single distortion coefficient.
- **Camera Matrix $\mathbf{P}$ is $3 \times 4$**, has rank 3, and 11 DOF (one DOF is lost to the homogeneous scale).
- **Homogeneous Coordinates** eliminate the need for case-by-case handling of $Z_C = 0$ singularities and allow linear least-squares solutions.
- **Calibration Target Geometry:** A 2D planar target (checkerboard) is preferred over 3D targets for convenience; 3D targets are more accurate but harder to manufacture precisely.
- **The principal point is NOT always at the image center** — manufacturing tolerances can offset it by 5–20 pixels in consumer cameras.
- **Distortion model order:** $k_1, k_2$ are usually sufficient; $k_3, p_1, p_2$ are needed only for high-distortion wide-angle lenses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometric Image Features: Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Geometric Image Features: Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Geometric Image Features** are localized, structurally meaningful primitives extracted from a digital image that possess well-defined geometric properties such as position, orientation, scale, and shape. They serve as compact, stable descriptors of the underlying scene geometry and are foundational for tasks like object recognition, image registration, 3D reconstruction, and motion tracking.

**Curves** are a specific class of 1-dimensional geometric image features that represent smoothly varying intensity discontinuities (edges) or physically meaningful object boundaries in an image. Mathematically, a curve in an image is a locus of points $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ that satisfies a parametric or implicit equation of the form $\mathbf{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t))$ or $f(x, y) = 0$.

The most commonly studied curve types in computer vision include:
- **Straight lines** (linear primitives)
- **Conic sections** (circles, ellipses, parabolas, hyperbolas)
- **Parametric and free-form curves** (splines, Bézier curves, B-splines)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The course explicitly mandates the study of *lines, conics, algebraic curves, curve fitting, and the Hough Transform* as the principal methods for detecting and representing geometric curves. Students are expected to understand both the parametric and implicit representations, as well as the fitting (least-squares) and voting (Hough) paradigms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a hand-drawn sketch of a mountain landscape. The entire picture is made of thousands of tiny black dots (pixels), but to your brain, the picture "means something" only because it extracts **strokes** — the line of the mountain ridge, the arc of a rainbow, the elliptical shape of a stone. A computer, however, sees only a giant grid of intensity numbers.

**Geometric curve features are the computer's way of "seeing strokes."** Just as a child traces the outline of a face with a crayon to capture its essence, computer vision algorithms trace mathematically well-defined curves through the cloud of pixels to capture the **structural skeleton** of the image.

A straight line is to an image what a straight ruler-edge is to a drawing — a fundamental, easily recognizable primitive. A conic (circle, ellipse, parabola) is like recognizing a wheel, a ball, or a satellite dish in the scene. Detecting these primitives allows higher-level algorithms to "understand" the image: detect lanes on a road, recognize coins on a table, or identify the iris in an eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are curves studied first in computer vision?** Because they represent the simplest geometric structure that is *invariant to small amounts of noise* (due to redundancy of edge points along the curve) and yet *highly discriminative* for matching across views. Curves form the bridge between raw pixel data and symbolic scene understanding.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Standard)

The following **fundamental parameters** recur across all curve-detection algorithms in this module:

- **Hough Accumulator Resolution:** typically $\Delta\rho = 1$ pixel, $\Delta\theta = 1^\circ$
- **Edge Detection Threshold (Canny):** low = 20, high = 60 (grayscale intensity units)
- **Least-Squares Residual Tolerance:** $\varepsilon < 10^{-3}$ for polynomial fits
- **Curve Continuity Order:** $C^k$ continuity where $k = 0, 1, 2$ (positional, tangential, curvature continuity)
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a Line:** **2** (slope + intercept, or $\rho + \theta$)
- **DOF of a Circle:** **3** (center $x_c, y_c$ and radius $r$)
- **DOF of a General Conic:** **5** (six coefficients up to a scale factor)
- **DOF of a Cubic Polynomial Curve:** **4** (coefficients $a_3, a_2, a_1, a_0$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Curve Representations

Curves in computer vision can be represented at multiple levels of abstraction. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU examination answers.

**Level 1 — Pixel-Level Edge Pixels:**
- Individual pixels where the image gradient magnitude exceeds a threshold.
- Output of an edge detector such as **Canny**, **Sobel**, or **Prewitt**.
- Raw, unorganized set of points: $\mathcal{E} = \{(x_i, y_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, N\}$.

**Level 2 — Linked Edge Chains (Edge Following):**
- Edge pixels are connected into ordered sequences by following local gradient directions.
- Heuristic linking rules: similarity in gradient magnitude and orientation.
- Result: a list of $(x, y)$ points traced along the contour.

**Level 3 — Parametric Curve Models:**
- Edge chains are *fitted* to a parametric model such as $y = ax + b$ or $x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$.
- This converts noisy pixel chains into compact, semantically meaningful parameters.

**Level 4 — Higher-Order Geometric Structures:**
- Curves are combined into higher primitives: corners, junctions, T-structures, closed contours.
- These are the actual inputs to object-recognition and shape-matching algorithms.

### 2.2 Why and How of Each Detection Paradigm

#### A. The Hough Transform (Voting Paradigm)
- **Why:** Pixel-level edge detection produces broken, noisy contours. Grouping pixels into lines robustly requires a method tolerant to occlusions and noise. The Hough Transform achieves this by **transforming the detection problem into a peak-finding problem** in a parameter space.
- **How:** Each edge pixel "votes" for every possible curve passing through it in parameter space. Peaks in the accumulator correspond to curves supported by many edge pixels.

#### B. Least-Squares Curve Fitting (Fitting Paradigm)
- **Why:** Given a clean set of edge points assumed to lie on a known curve model, we wish to recover the model's parameters with minimum error. Closed-form solutions exist that minimize the algebraic or geometric residual.
- **How:** Minimize the sum of squared residuals $S = \sum_i (y_i - f(x_i; \mathbf{a}))^2$ by setting partial derivatives $\partial S / \partial a_j = 0$ and solving the resulting normal equations.

#### C. Iterative Refinement (Robust Paradigm)
- **Why:** Real images contain **outliers** (edge pixels from other structures, spurious noise). Least-squares alone is corrupted by outliers.
- **How:** Use **RANSAC** (Random Sample Consensus) — hypothesize a curve from a minimal random subset, count inliers within a tolerance, repeat, and keep the hypothesis with the maximum inlier count.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Key Variables | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line (slope-intercept) | $y = mx + c$ | $m$ (slope), $c$ (intercept) | $m \in \mathbb{R}$, $c \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Line (normal form) | $x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$ | $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, $\rho \in \mathbb{R}$ | $\rho$ in pixels |
| Circle | $(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = r^2$ | $(x_c, y_c)$ center, $r$ radius | $r > 0$ in pixels |
| General Conic | $Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$ | Five DOF (one free scale) | dimensionless |
| Parabola (vertex form) | $y = a(x - h)^2 + k$ | vertex $(h, k)$, curvature $a$ | $a \neq 0$ |
| Cubic Polynomial | $y = a_3 x^3 + a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0$ | Four DOF | dimensionless |
| Image Gradient | $\nabla I = (I_x, I_y)$ | $I_x = \partial I / \partial x$, $I_y = \partial I / \partial y$ | intensity/pixel |
| Gradient Magnitude | $\vert\nabla I\vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | — | intensity/pixel |
| Gradient Direction | $\phi = \arctan(I_y / I_x)$ | — | radians |
| Hough Accumulator | $A(\rho, \theta) = \sum_{(x,y) \in \mathcal{E}} \mathbb{1}[\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta]$ | bins of $(\rho, \theta)$ | counts |
| LS Normal Equation | $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{a}$ = parameter vector | matrix equation |
| Sum of Squared Residuals | $S(\mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - f(x_i; \mathbf{a}))^2$ | minimized w.r.t. $\mathbf{a}$ | pixels$^2$ |
| Hough Space Size | $\text{Size} = N_\rho \times N_\theta$ | $N_\rho = 2D/\Delta\rho$, $N_\theta = 180/\Delta\theta$ | integer bins |
| RANSAC Iterations | $k = \log(1 - p) / \log(1 - w^n)$ | $p$ confidence, $w$ inlier ratio, $n$ sample size | iterations |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Geometric curve features underpin several mission-critical applications:

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Lane detection uses line (Hough) and parabolic/circular-arc fitting. Tesla, Mobileye, and Waymo pipelines rely on these primitives.
- **Medical Imaging:** Ellipse fitting localizes the optic disc, cell boundaries, and the left ventricle in echocardiograms.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Circle fitting locates bolt holes, ball bearings, and printed circuit board pads.
- **Document Analysis:** Line and conic detection underwrites OCR pre-processing and form recognition.
- **Aerospace & Surveillance:** Runway detection, horizon detection, and tank/vehicle silhouette recognition all begin with curve primitives.
- **Augmented Reality:** Planar and curved surface tracking (e.g., the curved pages of a book) depends on robust conic and B-spline fitting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Worthy Insight:** Examiners frequently award additional credit to answers that explicitly mention the *real-world application* of the algorithm discussed. Always cite one concrete application in 14-mark descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive derivations** for three core curve-related algorithms. No step is skipped. All algebraic transitions are shown.

### 3.1 Derivation: Hough Transform for Straight Line Detection

**Goal:** Given a set of edge pixels $\mathcal{E} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, find the line $y = mx + c$ that is supported by the maximum number of pixels.

**Step 1 — The Slope-Intercept Problem.**
Each edge pixel lies on infinitely many lines: $y_i = m x_i + c$ for all $(m, c) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. Direct voting in $(m, c)$-space fails because:
- Vertical lines require $m = \infty$.
- The parameter space is unbounded.

**Step 2 — Reformulation in Normal Form.**
A unique representation of any line is the *normal form*:

$$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$

where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line, and $\theta$ is the angle the perpendicular makes with the $x$-axis. For any point $(x_i, y_i)$ on a particular line, the relationship

$$\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$

is a **sinusoid** in the $(\rho, \theta)$ parameter space.

**Step 3 — The Voting Mechanism.**
For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
- Iterate $\theta$ over the discretized range $\{0, \Delta\theta, 2\Delta\theta, \dots, \pi - \Delta\theta\}$.
- Compute the corresponding $\rho$ via the equation $\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$.
- Quantize $\rho$ to the nearest accumulator bin and increment $A(\rho, \theta) \leftarrow A(\rho, \theta) + 1$.

**Step 4 — Peak Detection.**
After processing all $N$ edge pixels, the accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ contains a peak at $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ corresponding to the dominant line in the image. The line equation is recovered as

$$x \cos\theta^* + y \sin\theta^* = \rho^*$$

**Step 5 — Complexity Analysis.**
For $N$ edge pixels and $K = N_\theta$ angular samples, the time complexity is $O(NK)$. The space complexity is $O(N_\rho \times N_\theta)$. For a 640×480 image with $\Delta\rho = 1, \Delta\theta = 1^\circ$, the accumulator size is $960 \times 180 = 172{,}800$ cells.

**Step 6 — Multi-Line Extension.**
To detect $L$ lines, the algorithm finds the top-$L$ peaks, but a *non-maximum suppression* step is applied in a $5 \times 5$ window around each peak to avoid detecting the same line repeatedly.

### 3.2 Derivation: Least-Squares Line Fit (Ordinary Linear Regression)

**Goal:** Given points $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, \dots, N$, find the line $y = a_1 x + a_0$ that minimizes the sum of squared vertical residuals.

**Step 1 — Define the Objective Function.**

$$S(a_0, a_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (a_0 + a_1 x_i) \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Take Partial Derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i \right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i \right) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand Into Two Normal Equations.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = N a_0 + a_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i = a_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + a_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve by Determinant Method.**
Writing the system in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} N & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i & \sum x_i^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum y_i \\ \sum x_i y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

The determinant is $D = N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2$. Provided $D \neq 0$ (i.e., $x_i$ values are not all equal), the solution is

$$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

$$a_0 = \frac{\sum y_i - a_1 \sum x_i}{N} = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ are the sample means.

**Step 5 — Quality of Fit.**
The coefficient of determination is

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

Values of $R^2$ close to $1$ indicate an excellent linear fit.

### 3.3 Derivation: Parabola Fitting (Second-Order Polynomial)

**Goal:** Fit $y = a x^2 + b x + c$ to $N$ data points by least squares.

**Step 1 — Define the Objective Function.**

$$S(a, b, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - a x_i^2 - b x_i - c \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Set Partial Derivatives to Zero.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2 \sum x_i^2 (y_i - a x_i^2 - b x_i - c) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - a x_i^2 - b x_i - c) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial c} = -2 \sum (y_i - a x_i^2 - b x_i - c) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Three Normal Equations.**

$$a \sum x_i^4 + b \sum x_i^3 + c \sum x_i^2 = \sum x_i^2 y_i$$

$$a \sum x_i^3 + b \sum x_i^2 + c \sum x_i = \sum x_i y_i$$

$$a \sum x_i^2 + b \sum x_i + c N = \sum y_i$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sum x_i^4 & \sum x_i^3 & \sum x_i^2 \\ \sum x_i^3 & \sum x_i^2 & \sum x_i \\ \sum x_i^2 & \sum x_i & N \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \\ c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum x_i^2 y_i \\ \sum x_i y_i \\ \sum y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

This $3 \times 3$ symmetric positive-definite system is solved by Cholesky decomposition or Gaussian elimination. The condition $N \geq 3$ ensures solvability (we need at least 3 data points).

**Step 5 — Special Case: Vertex Form.**
If the vertex $(h, k)$ is desired, complete the square:

$$y = a(x - h)^2 + k = a x^2 - 2 a h x + (a h^2 + k)$$

Therefore $a = a$, $b = -2ah \Rightarrow h = -b / (2a)$, and $c = ah^2 + k \Rightarrow k = c - ah^2$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Hough Transform for Circle Detection

**Goal:** Detect circles $(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = r^2$ from edge pixels.

**Step 1 — Parameter Space.**
A circle has three parameters: $x_c, y_c, r$. The parameter space is 3-D, requiring an accumulator $A(x_c, y_c, r)$ with size $O(N_x N_y N_r)$.

**Step 2 — Voting.**
For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ and for each candidate radius $r \in \{r_{\min}, r_{\min} + \Delta r, \dots, r_{\max}\}$:
- Iterate $\theta$ from $0$ to $2\pi$.
- Compute candidate centers:

$$x_c = x_i - r \cos\theta, \quad y_c = y_i - r \sin\theta$$

- Increment $A(x_c, y_c, r)$.

**Step 3 — Peak Detection.**
The 3-D accumulator peaks correspond to detected circles. The complexity $O(N \cdot N_r \cdot N_\theta)$ makes this expensive; the *Hough Gradient Method* (OpenCV implementation) computes the gradient direction at each edge pixel to reduce the angular search to a single $\theta$ value, cutting cost to $O(N \cdot N_r)$.

**Step 4 — Coordinate Quantization.**
$x_c$ and $y_c$ are quantized to the nearest integer bin. Peak detection uses a minimum distance between detected circles to avoid duplicate detections.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection Guide

| Scenario | Best Algorithm | Reason | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Many lines, noisy, broken | Hough Transform | Robust voting, handles occlusions | $O(NK)$ |
| Clean points, one line | Least Squares | Closed-form, optimal under Gaussian noise | $O(N)$ |
| Many outliers | RANSAC + LS | Outlier rejection via consensus | $O(N \cdot k)$ |
| Closed contours, ellipses | Hough Gradient / Direct Ellipse Fitting | Inherent symmetry exploitation | $O(N \cdot N_r)$ |
| Free-form smooth curves | B-spline / Cubic Spline | Local control, $C^2$ continuity | $O(N \cdot n_{ctrl})$ |
| Real-time (video) | RANSAC with constant iterations | Bounded compute per frame | $O(k)$ per frame |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Curve Detection Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete processing chain from raw image to geometric curve parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Image I x y] --> B[Noise Reduction Gaussian Filter]
    B --> C[Gradient Computation Sobel or Canny]
    C --> D[Non Maximum Suppression]
    D --> E[Double Thresholding]
    E --> F[Edge Map Binary Image]
    F --> G{Detection Strategy}
    G -->|Voting Paradigm| H[Initialize Hough Accumulator]
    H --> I[For Each Edge Pixel Vote in Parameter Space]
    I --> J[Peak Detection in Accumulator]
    J --> K[Recover Curve Parameters]
    G -->|Fitting Paradigm| L[Select Minimal Sample Subsets]
    L --> M[RANSAC Hypothesis Generation]
    M --> N[Count Inliers within Threshold]
    N --> O[Refit with All Inliers Least Squares]
    O --> K
    K --> P[Curve Parameters Output]
    P --> Q[Application Layer Recognition Tracking Reconstruction]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: Hough Transform Sinusoid Intersection Principle

The Mermaid graph below illustrates how multiple collinear edge points generate sinusoids in Hough space that all intersect at a single common point, which is the line's $(\rho, \theta)$ coordinate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XY["Image Space xy"]
        P1[Point x1 y1]
        P2[Point x2 y2]
        P3[Point x3 y3]
        P4[Point x4 y4]
    end
    subgraph RT["Hough Parameter Space r theta"]
        S1[Sinusoid 1]
        S2[Sinusoid 2]
        S3[Sinusoid 3]
        S4[Sinusoid 4]
    end
    C[Consensus Peak rho theta]
    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    P4 --> S4
    S1 --> C
    S2 --> C
    S3 --> C
    S4 --> C
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Hough Accumulator Voting Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Edge Pixel x_i y_i Entered] --> B0[Set theta to 0]
    B0 --> C0[theta less than pi]
    C0 -->|Yes| D0[Compute rho = x_i cos theta + y_i sin theta]
    D0 --> E0[Quantize rho to Bin Index]
    E0 --> F0[Increment A rho theta]
    F0 --> G0[theta = theta + delta theta]
    G0 --> C0
    C0 -->|No| H0[Process Next Edge Pixel]
    H0 --> I0{All Pixels Done}
    I0 -->|No| A0
    I0 -->|Yes| J0[Search Accumulator for Maxima]
    J0 --> K0[Apply Non Maximum Suppression]
    K0 --> L0[Output Detected Lines]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Algorithm Selection by Problem Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is being detected] -->|Line| Q2{Noise and Outlier Level}
    Q1 -->|Circle or Ellipse| Q3[Hough Gradient Method]
    Q1 -->|Arbitrary Curve| Q4[Spline Fitting]
    Q1 -->|Polynomial| Q5[Polynomial Least Squares]
    Q2 -->|Low Noise| Q6[Ordinary Least Squares]
    Q2 -->|High Noise| Q7[RANSAC then Refit]
    Q2 -->|Very Many Outliers| Q8[Probabilistic Hough]
    Q3 --> Q9[3D Accumulator with Gradient Constraint]
    Q4 --> Q10[Parametric Spline B or Cubic]
    Q5 --> Q11[Solve Normal Equations]
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Curve Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Geometric Image Features] --> B[Point Features]
    A --> C[Curve Features]
    A --> D[Region Features]
    C --> E[Lines 2 DOF]
    C --> F[Conics 5 DOF]
    C --> G[Splines Variable DOF]
    E --> H[Detected by Hough]
    E --> I[Fitted by Least Squares]
    F --> J[Circles 3 DOF]
    F --> K[Ellipses 5 DOF]
    F --> L[Parabolas 3 DOF]
    G --> M[B Splines]
    G --> N[Cardinal Splines]
    G --> O[Bezier Curves]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define geometric image features. Explain why curves are considered fundamental primitives in computer vision.

**Model Answer:**

Geometric image features are localized, structured descriptors extracted from an image that encode meaningful geometric information such as position, orientation, and shape. Examples include points (corners), curves (lines, conics), and regions (blobs).

Curves are considered fundamental because:

- They represent the *structural skeleton* of objects — most natural and man-made objects have well-defined boundaries that are smooth curves.
- They are *more robust to noise* than individual pixels, because a curve aggregates many edge points, providing redundancy.
- They are *invariant to small viewpoint changes* — a line remains a line under weak perspective.
- They provide a *compact representation*: a curve with 5 parameters (a conic) replaces hundreds of pixel coordinates.
- They are the *building blocks* of higher-level recognition: object detection, scene understanding, and 3D reconstruction all begin with curve primitives.

**[3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of importance]**

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List and briefly explain three different representations of a line in image coordinates.

**Model Answer:**

The three standard representations of a line in image coordinates are:

1. **Slope-Intercept Form:** $y = mx + c$, where $m$ is the slope and $c$ is the $y$-intercept. Simple but fails for vertical lines ($m = \infty$). Used in basic regression contexts.

2. **Two-Point Form:** A line through $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is written as $(y - y_1) / (x - x_1) = (y_2 - y_1) / (x_2 - x_1)$. Useful for line detection by linking edge points, but unstable when $x_1 \approx x_2$.

3. **Normal (Hessian) Form:** $x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$, where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal. This is the only form that handles vertical lines gracefully and is the basis of the Hough Transform. It uses a bounded parameter space: $\rho \in [-D, D]$, $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.

**[3 marks: 1 mark per representation with formula]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical formulation, explain the Hough Transform algorithm for straight-line detection in an image. Derive the expression for the Hough accumulator array and discuss the role of parameter space quantization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a set of five data points $(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 2.5), (3, 3.5), (4, 5)$:
  - (i) Determine the least-squares best-fit line $y = a_1 x + a_0$. Compute the coefficients $a_0$ and $a_1$ step by step. **(4 marks)**
  - (ii) Compute the sum of squared residuals $S$ and the coefficient of determination $R^2$. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hough Transform for Line Detection:**

The Hough Transform converts the problem of detecting collinear edge pixels into a peak-finding problem in a parameter space.

**Mathematical Formulation:**
A line in the image plane can be expressed in the normal form as

$$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$

where $\rho \geq 0$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line, and $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ is the angle the perpendicular makes with the positive $x$-axis.

For a fixed image point $(x_i, y_i)$, the set of all lines passing through it corresponds to a sinusoidal curve in the $(\rho, \theta)$ space:

$$\rho(\theta) = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Edge Detection:** Apply a Canny or Sobel edge detector to the input image to obtain a binary edge map $\mathcal{E} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

2. **Accumulator Initialization:** Create a 2-D accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ of size $N_\rho \times N_\theta$, where $N_\rho = \lfloor 2D / \Delta\rho \rfloor + 1$ ($D$ is the image diagonal) and $N_\theta = \lfloor \pi / \Delta\theta \rfloor$. Initialize all cells to zero.

3. **Voting Loop:** For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ and for each discretized $\theta_k = k \Delta\theta$ (for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N_\theta - 1$):
   - Compute $\rho_k = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_i \sin\theta_k$.
   - Quantize $\rho_k$ to the nearest bin index $\rho_j = \text{round}(\rho_k / \Delta\rho) \cdot \Delta\rho$.
   - Increment the accumulator: $A(\rho_j, \theta_k) \leftarrow A(\rho_j, \theta_k) + 1$.

4. **Peak Detection:** Search the accumulator for cells with values exceeding a threshold $T$. Apply non-maximum suppression in a small neighborhood (e.g., $5 \times 5$) around each candidate peak to avoid duplicate detections.

5. **Parameter Recovery:** For each detected peak $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$, recover the line equation $x \cos\theta^* + y \sin\theta^* = \rho^*$.

**Role of Quantization:**
The discretization $\Delta\rho, \Delta\theta$ controls the trade-off between:
- **Resolution:** Smaller $\Delta\rho, \Delta\theta$ yield finer peak localization but a larger accumulator and increased noise sensitivity.
- **Robustness:** Coarser bins accumulate more votes per bin, increasing robustness but reducing parameter precision.
- **Computational cost:** Time complexity is $O(N \cdot N_\theta)$ and space is $O(N_\rho \cdot N_\theta)$.

Typical values are $\Delta\rho = 1$ pixel and $\Delta\theta = 1^\circ$, which balance precision and efficiency.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Computing $a_0$ and $a_1$:**

First, compute the necessary summations from the data points.

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | 2.5 | 4 | 5 |
| 4 | 3 | 3.5 | 9 | 10.5 |
| 5 | 4 | 5 | 16 | 20 |
| **Sum** | **10** | **14** | **30** | **37.5** |

Thus $N = 5$, $\sum x_i = 10$, $\sum y_i = 14$, $\sum x_i^2 = 30$, $\sum x_i y_i = 37.5$.

Apply the formula

$$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{5 \cdot 37.5 - 10 \cdot 14}{5 \cdot 30 - 10^2} = \frac{187.5 - 140}{150 - 100} = \frac{47.5}{50} = 0.95$$

$$a_0 = \frac{\sum y_i}{N} - a_1 \frac{\sum x_i}{N} = \frac{14}{5} - 0.95 \cdot \frac{10}{5} = 2.8 - 1.9 = 0.9$$

Therefore the best-fit line is $y = 0.95 x + 0.9$.

**(ii) Computing $S$ and $R^2$:**

Compute predicted $\hat{y}_i = 0.95 x_i + 0.9$ and residuals $r_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $r_i$ | $r_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 0.9 | 0.1 | 0.01 |
| 1 | 2 | 1.85 | 0.15 | 0.0225 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 2.8 | -0.3 | 0.09 |
| 3 | 3.5 | 3.75 | -0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 4 | 5 | 4.7 | 0.3 | 0.09 |

$$S = \sum r_i^2 = 0.01 + 0.0225 + 0.09 + 0.0625 + 0.09 = 0.275$$

$$\bar{y} = 14 / 5 = 2.8, \quad \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (1-2.8)^2 + (2-2.8)^2 + (2.5-2.8)^2 + (3.5-2.8)^2 + (5-2.8)^2$$

$$= 3.24 + 0.64 + 0.09 + 0.49 + 4.84 = 9.3$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{S}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2} = 1 - \frac{0.275}{9.3} \approx 0.9704$$

The fit is excellent ($R^2 \approx 0.97$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the mathematical basis of circle detection using the Hough Transform. Discuss the parameter space dimensionality, accumulator design, and the role of edge gradients in reducing computational cost. Compare this with the alternative approach of algebraic circle fitting via least squares. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a set of data points $(1, 2.1), (2, 3.9), (3, 6.2), (4, 7.8), (5, 10.1)$, perform a least-squares fit of the parabola $y = a x^2 + b x + c$ by setting up and solving the normal equations. Report the final values of $a, b, c$ and the vertex of the parabola. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mathematical Basis of Circle Detection via Hough Transform:**

**Parameter Space Dimensionality:**
A circle is described by three parameters: the center coordinates $(x_c, y_c)$ and the radius $r$. The implicit equation is

$$(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = r^2$$

The Hough parameter space is therefore 3-D: $A(x_c, y_c, r)$. For an image of size $W \times H$ with radius range $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$ discretized into $N_r$ values, the accumulator has $W \cdot H \cdot N_r$ cells.

**Accumulator Voting Mechanism:**
For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
- For each candidate radius $r_k \in \{r_{\min}, r_{\min} + \Delta r, \dots, r_{\max}\}$:
  - For each angle $\theta_j \in \{0, \Delta\theta, \dots, 2\pi - \Delta\theta\}$:
    - Compute the candidate center: $x_c = x_i - r_k \cos\theta_j$, $y_c = y_i - r_k \sin\theta_j$.
    - Increment $A(x_c, y_c, r_k)$.

The total complexity is $O(N \cdot N_r \cdot N_\theta)$, which can be expensive.

**Role of Edge Gradients (Hough Gradient Method):**
The Canny edge detector provides both the location and the gradient direction $\phi_i$ at each edge pixel. For a true circle, the gradient at any edge point is **perpendicular to the radius vector**, so the center lies along the line from $(x_i, y_i)$ in direction $\phi_i$. This reduces the angular search to a single value, reducing complexity to $O(N \cdot N_r)$.

**Comparison with Algebraic Circle Fitting:**

| Aspect | Hough Transform | Algebraic Least-Squares |
|---|---|---|
| Robustness to outliers | High (voting suppresses noise) | Low (single outlier corrupts the fit) |
| Computational cost | $O(N \cdot N_r \cdot N_\theta)$ | $O(N)$ closed-form |
| Number of circles | Multiple detected via peak search | Single best-fit circle |
| Geometric vs. algebraic error | Approximates geometric distance | Minimizes algebraic distance (biased) |
| Parameter space size | $O(W H N_r)$ | None required |

Algebraic fitting expands the circle equation to $x^2 + y^2 + D x + E y + F = 0$ and solves the linear system in $(D, E, F)$ via least squares. The center and radius are then recovered as $x_c = -D/2$, $y_c = -E/2$, $r = \sqrt{D^2/4 + E^2/4 - F}$.

**(b) Parabola Fit by Least Squares:**

The model is $y = a x^2 + b x + c$, and we minimize

$$S(a, b, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (y_i - a x_i^2 - b x_i - c)^2$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Required Sums:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i^3$ | $x_i^4$ | $x_i y_i$ | $x_i^2 y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2.1 | 2.1 |
| 2 | 3.9 | 4 | 8 | 16 | 7.8 | 15.6 |
| 3 | 6.2 | 9 | 27 | 81 | 18.6 | 55.8 |
| 4 | 7.8 | 16 | 64 | 256 | 31.2 | 124.8 |
| 5 | 10.1 | 25 | 125 | 625 | 50.5 | 252.5 |
| **Sum** | **30.1** | **55** | **225** | **979** | **110.2** | **450.8** |

So $N = 5$, $\sum x_i^2 = 55$, $\sum x_i^3 = 225$, $\sum x_i^4 = 979$, $\sum x_i = 15$, $\sum y_i = 30.1$, $\sum x_i y_i = 110.2$, $\sum x_i^2 y_i = 450.8$.

**Step 2 — Write the Normal Equations:**

$$979 a + 225 b + 55 c = 450.8$$
$$225 a + 55 b + 15 c = 110.2$$
$$55 a + 15 b + 5 c = 30.1$$

**Step 3 — Solve by Gaussian Elimination.**

From the third equation: $c = (30.1 - 55 a - 15 b) / 5 = 6.02 - 11 a - 3 b$.

Substitute into the second equation:
$$225 a + 55 b + 15(6.02 - 11 a - 3 b) = 110.2$$
$$225 a + 55 b + 90.3 - 165 a - 45 b = 110.2$$
$$60 a + 10 b = 19.9 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

Substitute into the first equation:
$$979 a + 225 b + 55(6.02 - 11 a - 3 b) = 450.8$$
$$979 a + 225 b + 331.1 - 605 a - 165 b = 450.8$$
$$374 a + 60 b = 119.7 \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2 × 2 System.**

From Eq. A: $b = (19.9 - 60 a) / 10 = 1.99 - 6 a$.

Substitute into Eq. B:
$$374 a + 60 (1.99 - 6 a) = 119.7$$
$$374 a + 119.4 - 360 a = 119.7$$
$$14 a = 0.3$$
$$a = 0.3 / 14 \approx 0.02143$$

Then $b = 1.99 - 6(0.02143) = 1.99 - 0.12857 \approx 1.8614$.

And $c = 6.02 - 11(0.02143) - 3(1.8614) = 6.02 - 0.2357 - 5.5843 \approx 0.2000$.

**Step 5 — Final Equation and Vertex:**

The fitted parabola is $y \approx 0.0214 x^2 + 1.8614 x + 0.2000$.

The vertex is at

$$x_v = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{1.8614}{2 \cdot 0.0214} = -\frac{1.8614}{0.04286} \approx -43.43$$

$$y_v = c - \frac{b^2}{4a} = 0.2000 - \frac{(1.8614)^2}{4 \cdot 0.0214} = 0.2000 - \frac{3.4648}{0.0857} \approx 0.2000 - 40.42 \approx -40.22$$

The vertex is at approximately $(-43.43, -40.22)$. (The large negative vertex location outside the data range $[1, 5]$ is consistent with the data being approximately linear, which a parabola with small leading coefficient naturally extends.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Curve-Detection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the Normal Form for Hough:** Students often derive the Hough Transform in the slope-intercept form $y = mx + c$ and lose 2-3 marks because it cannot represent vertical lines. The KTU model answer key specifically demands the normal form $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$.
>
> 2. **Confusing image-space and parameter-space coordinates:** It is a common error to claim that "two points determine a line" in the Hough space. In fact, each point in the image becomes a *sinusoid* in the Hough space, and **two or more collinear image points produce sinusoids that intersect at the same Hough point** $(\rho, \theta)$.
>
> 3. **Skipping the partial derivative steps in least squares:** The KTU board expects to see the explicit formation of the normal equations from the first-order optimality conditions. Do not write "by least-squares, $a_1 = \dots$" without derivation.
>
> 4. **Not stating the matrix form of the normal equations:** For a 2-mark part-question, just writing the formula for $a_1$ is fine. For a 7-mark part-question, the matrix equation $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ is essentially mandatory.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the role of edge gradients in Hough circle detection:** Listing the algorithm without mentioning that gradient direction restricts the angular search to a single $\theta$ costs you the second half of the 7 marks.
>
> 6. **Not reporting fit quality metrics:** In numerical questions, always report $R^2$ or the residual sum. Examiners explicitly look for quality-of-fit comments.
>
> 7. **Off-by-one errors in summations:** When $N$ data points are given, students often sum $N-1$ terms. Double-check your table.
>
> 8. **Mistaking the vertex formula for a parabola:** The vertex is at $x_v = -b / (2a)$, $y_v = c - b^2 / (4a) = a h^2 + k$. Writing $y_v = c$ alone will lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Geometric Image Features: Curves):**

- **Geometric features** are structured, localized image descriptors — points, curves, and regions — that compactly encode scene geometry.
- **Curves** are 1-D image primitives representing smooth intensity discontinuities or object boundaries.
- **Two main paradigms** for curve detection: **Voting (Hough)** and **Fitting (Least Squares / RANSAC)**.
- **Line representations:** slope-intercept $y = mx + c$ (vertical-line failure), two-point form, and **normal form $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$** (Hough basis, $\rho \geq 0$, $\theta \in [0, \pi)$).
- **Hough Transform for lines:** edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ generates a sinusoid $\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$ in $(\rho, \theta)$ space; accumulator peak = line.
- **Complexity of line Hough:** $O(N \cdot N_\theta)$ time, $O(N_\rho \cdot N_\theta)$ space.
- **Hough for circles:** 3-D accumulator $A(x_c, y_c, r)$; complexity $O(N \cdot N_r \cdot N_\theta)$, reduced to $O(N \cdot N_r)$ with gradient direction constraint.
- **Least-squares line fit:** minimize $S = \sum (y_i - a_0 - a_1 x_i)^2$; solutions
  $$a_1 = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \quad a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x}$$
- **Matrix normal equations:** $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$.
- **Parabola fit:** solve $3 \times 3$ symmetric system in $(a, b, c)$ using $\sum x_i^4, \sum x_i^3, \sum x_i^2, \sum x_i, N, \sum x_i^2 y_i, \sum x_i y_i, \sum y_i$.
- **Vertex of parabola:** $x_v = -b / (2a)$, $y_v = c - b^2 / (4a)$.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Line = 2, Circle = 3, Conic = 5, Cubic = 4.
- **Quality of fit:** $R^2 = 1 - SS_{\text{res}} / SS_{\text{tot}}$; values near 1 indicate excellent fit.
- **RANSAC:** hypothesis-and-verify algorithm for outlier-robust fitting; iteration count $k = \log(1-p) / \log(1 - w^n)$.
- **Edge detection precedes curve fitting:** Canny / Sobel outputs form the input to Hough and LS.
- **Continuity orders:** $C^0$ (position), $C^1$ (tangent), $C^2$ (curvature).
- **Splines:** B-splines offer local control and $C^2$ continuity; used for free-form curves.
- **Real-world applications:** lane detection (lines, parabolas), iris localization (circles/ellipses), PCB inspection (circles), document analysis (lines), horizon detection (lines), and road sign recognition (conics).
- **Quantization trade-off in Hough:** finer bins = better precision but more noise and higher cost.
- **Multi-line extension:** Non-maximum suppression in a window around each peak.
- **Algebraic vs. geometric fitting:** algebraic is faster but biased; geometric (minimizing true perpendicular distance) is more accurate.
- **Hough Gradient Method:** OpenCV's efficient circle detector using edge gradient to restrict the angular search to one $\theta$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Day Strategy:** When the question asks "with neat mathematical formulation," always begin by writing the relevant equation in the **normal form** or in the **matrix normal-equation form**. This single step routinely lifts your answer from a 5/7 to a 7/7. Always draw the Hough-space sketch showing intersecting sinusoids — it is a free 1-mark "neat diagram" bonus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Surface" in Computer Vision?

In the language of **Foundations of Computer Vision (PECDT743)**, a **surface** is the mathematical abstraction used to describe the visible boundary of a 3D object as it is observed through a camera. Every pixel in an image is, in essence, the projection of a small patch of some physical surface into the image plane. A surface in 3D space is therefore a **2-dimensional manifold embedded in 3-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$**, parameterized either explicitly, implicitly, or parametrically.

Formally, a surface $S$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ can be described as a continuous mapping:

$$f: \mathbb{R}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^3, \quad (u, v) \mapsto (x(u, v), y(u, v), z(u, v))$$

where $(u, v)$ are the surface parameters and $(x, y, z)$ are the 3D world coordinates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A surface in computer vision is the geometric structure of an object that determines how light is reflected, how it appears under different viewpoints, and how shading is distributed across its image projection. Recovering surfaces from images is the *central inverse problem* of the module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the edge of the **Western Ghats** looking at a vast hilly landscape. The terrain is not flat — it has hills, valleys, ridges, and slopes. Now imagine that you close one eye and look through just a flat 2D photograph of that landscape. All the 3D structure — the heights, the slopes, the curvature — is **encoded** in the way light and shadow falls across the image. The terrain *is* a surface. The photograph *is* the 2D evidence of that surface. **Computer vision's job is to recover the terrain (surface) from the photograph (image).**

Think of three familiar real-world parallels:

1. **A crumpled sheet of paper** — even though the paper is physically flat, when crumpled, every tiny piece of it has its own orientation (its own *normal*). Surfaces in CV are the same — local orientation matters more than global shape.
2. **Topographical maps** — a 2D map encodes a 3D mountain via *contour lines* (lines of constant height). This is exactly the idea of an **implicit surface representation** $F(x, y, z) = 0$.
3. **A relief sculpture** — light falling from one side reveals the depth; move the light and you reveal more. This is the principle of **photometric stereo**, which uses multiple images under different lights to recover surface orientation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

In surface analysis, the key standard quantities are:

- **Surface Normal $\mathbf{n}$** — a unit vector $(n_x, n_y, n_z)$ with $\lVert \mathbf{n} \rVert = 1$.
- **Depth $Z$** — the perpendicular distance from the camera to the surface point, in **metres (m)**.
- **Gradient $(p, q)$** — the partial derivatives $p = \partial Z / \partial x$ and $q = \partial Z / \partial y$ (dimensionless slopes).
- **Albedo $\rho$** — the fraction of incident light reflected, with $0 \le \rho \le 1$ (dimensionless).
- **Radiance $L$** — light intensity per unit solid angle, in **watts per steradian per square metre ($\mathrm{W \cdot sr^{-1} \cdot m^{-2}}$)**.
- **Irradiance $E$** — incident light power per unit area, in **$\mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2}}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examinations, you are **expected** to know that the surface normal, surface gradients $(p, q)$, and the depth $Z(x, y)$ form a *triple* of equivalent surface descriptors — given any one, the other two can be derived (up to a constant of integration).

## 1.4 Why Surfaces Matter in Computer Vision

Every low-to-mid level vision task ultimately depends on surfaces:

- **Object recognition** requires invariant surface descriptors.
- **3D reconstruction** from stereo, multi-view, or shading directly produces surfaces.
- **Tracking** uses surface motion fields.
- **Scene understanding** partitions a scene into distinct surfaces.
- **Robotics / autonomous driving** relies on surfaces for obstacle geometry.

Hence, Module 1's treatment of *Surfaces* lays the geometric foundation for **every other module** in PECDT743.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Surface Representations

A surface can be described mathematically in three equivalent ways. KTU examiners love asking students to convert between them.

### 2.1.1 Explicit Form

$$Z = f(x, y)$$

The depth $Z$ is written as an explicit function of the image coordinates $(x, y)$. This is the most common representation in **Shape-from-Shading** because it directly matches the *depth map* that a depth camera would return.

- **Why useful?** Easy to visualize. Each pixel has exactly one depth value.
- **Limitation:** Cannot represent vertical walls, overhangs, or surfaces that fold over themselves.

### 2.1.2 Implicit Form

$$F(x, y, z) = 0$$

The surface is the *zero level-set* of a scalar field $F$. Example: a sphere of radius $r$ is $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - r^2 = 0$.

- **Why useful?** The surface normal is *trivially* obtained as the gradient: $\mathbf{n} = \nabla F / \lVert \nabla F \rVert$.
- **Used in:** Level-set methods, signed distance functions, neural implicit representations (NeRF, DeepSDF).

### 2.1.3 Parametric Form

$$\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (x(u, v),\ y(u, v),\ z(u, v))$$

The surface is a mapping from the 2D parameter domain $(u, v) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ into 3D space. This is the natural representation in **differential geometry** of surfaces.

- **Why useful?** Tangent vectors, curvature, and the first/second fundamental forms are easily expressed.

## 2.2 Surface Normal — The Most Important Geometric Quantity

The **surface normal** $\mathbf{n}$ at a point $P$ on a surface is the unit vector perpendicular to the tangent plane at $P$. It governs:

- How light reflects (governed by $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l}$, where $\mathbf{l}$ is the light direction).
- How the surface appears under different viewpoints.
- The shading intensity in the rendered image.

### 2.2.1 Normal from Explicit Form $Z = f(x, y)$

The tangent vectors are $\mathbf{t}_x = (1, 0, \partial f / \partial x)$ and $\mathbf{t}_y = (0, 1, \partial f / \partial y)$. Their cross product (with proper orientation) gives:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}} \begin{pmatrix} -p \\ -q \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad p = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x},\ q = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pair $(p, q)$ are called the **surface gradients** or **surface slopes**. They are dimensionless and fully describe the local orientation of the surface.

### 2.2.2 Normal from Implicit Form $F(x, y, z) = 0$

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla F}{\lVert \nabla F \rVert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}} \begin{pmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \end{pmatrix}$$

### 2.2.3 Normal from Parametric Form

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v}{\lVert \mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v \rVert}, \quad \mathbf{S}_u = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial u},\ \mathbf{S}_v = \frac{\partial \mathbf{S}}{\partial v}$$

## 2.3 Surface Curvature

Curvature measures *how fast the surface bends*. Two principal curvatures $\kappa_1$ and $\kappa_2$ at every point combine to give:

- **Gaussian curvature** $K = \kappa_1 \kappa_2$ — intrinsic, independent of embedding.
- **Mean curvature** $H = (\kappa_1 + \kappa_2)/2$ — governs soap-film minimal surfaces.

For an explicit surface $Z = f(x, y)$:

$$H = \frac{(1 + q^2) f_{xx} - 2pq\, f_{xy} + (1 + p^2) f_{yy}}{2(1 + p^2 + q^2)^{3/2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU commonly asks: *"What is the sign of Gaussian curvature for a sphere, a plane, a cylinder, and a saddle?"* Memorize: **sphere $K > 0$**, **plane $K = 0$**, **cylinder $K = 0$**, **saddle $K < 0$**.

## 2.4 Shape from Shading (SFS)

SFS recovers $Z(x, y)$ (and hence the surface) from a **single grayscale image** under known illumination.

**Image Irradiance Equation (Lambertian, single point light source):**

$$E(x, y) = \rho(x, y)\ \mathbf{n}(x, y) \cdot \mathbf{l}$$

where $\mathbf{l} = (l_x, l_y, l_z)$ is the unit light direction, $\rho$ is the (assumed known or constant) albedo, and $E(x, y)$ is the measured image brightness at pixel $(x, y)$.

Substituting the explicit-form normal:

$$E(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{-p\, l_x - q\, l_y + l_z}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

Rearranging yields the **Eikonal-type reflectance equation** that the SFS algorithm must solve for $(p, q, Z)$ at every pixel.

> [!NOTE]
> The SFS problem is **ill-posed**: a single image does not uniquely determine a surface. Additional constraints (smoothness, integrability) are required to regularize it.

## 2.5 Photometric Stereo

Photometric stereo uses **two or more images of the same surface** taken from the **same viewpoint** but with **different, known light directions** $\mathbf{l}^{(1)}, \mathbf{l}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{l}^{(m)}$.

For Lambertian surfaces with $m \ge 3$ lights, the system becomes **over-determined** and can be solved linearly for the unknown surface normal (or equivalently, for the "reflectance map" vector $\mathbf{g} = \rho \mathbf{n}$).

The **image formation equation** for the $i$-th image is:

$$E^{(i)} = \mathbf{l}^{(i)} \cdot \mathbf{g} = \mathbf{l}^{(i)} \cdot (\rho \mathbf{n}), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{pmatrix} E^{(1)} \\ E^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ E^{(m)} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} l_x^{(1)} & l_y^{(1)} & l_z^{(1)} \\ l_x^{(2)} & l_y^{(2)} & l_z^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ l_x^{(m)} & l_y^{(m)} & l_z^{(m)} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} g_x \\ g_y \\ g_z \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{g}$$

The least-squares solution is:

$$\mathbf{g} = (\mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{L})^{-1} \mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{E} \quad \text{(pseudo-inverse solution)}$$

Once $\mathbf{g}$ is found, **albedo** and **normal** are separated by:

$$\rho = \lVert \mathbf{g} \rVert, \qquad \mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{g}}{\lVert \mathbf{g} \rVert}$$

## 2.6 Recovering Depth from Normal Field (Surface Integration)

Given the recovered normals (or gradients $(p, q)$) over the image, the surface $Z(x, y)$ is recovered by integration:

$$Z(x, y) = Z(x_0, y_0) + \int_{x_0}^{x} p(x', y_0)\, dx' + \int_{y_0}^{y} q(x, y')\, dy'$$

The naive line-integral result is **path-dependent** unless the recovered gradients are *integrable* (i.e., $\partial p / \partial y = \partial q / \partial x$). For noisy normals, a least-squares integration (Frankot-Chellappa algorithm) enforces integrability.

## 2.7 Range Images and Point Clouds

Modern depth sensors (LiDAR, Kinect, ToF cameras) directly produce **range images** $Z(x, y)$ — essentially an *explicit* surface representation sampled on a regular grid. From a range image, the surface normal at each pixel is computed as:

$$\mathbf{n}(x, y) = \frac{(Z(x+1, y) - Z(x-1, y),\ Z(x, y+1) - Z(x, y-1),\ -2\Delta)}{\lVert \cdot \rVert}$$

where $\Delta$ is the spacing between pixels in world units.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every** high-yield formula you must memorize for the KTU board exam on the topic *Surfaces*.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Explicit surface | $Z = f(x, y)$ | depth in **m** |
| 2 | Implicit surface | $F(x, y, z) = 0$ | level-set of scalar field |
| 3 | Parametric surface | $\mathbf{S}(u, v) = (x, y, z)$ | tangent vectors $\mathbf{S}_u, \mathbf{S}_v$ |
| 4 | Surface gradient | $p = \partial Z / \partial x$, $q = \partial Z / \partial y$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Normal from explicit form | $\mathbf{n} = (-p, -q, 1) / \sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}$ | unit vector |
| 6 | Normal from implicit form | $\mathbf{n} = \nabla F / \lVert \nabla F \rVert$ | unit vector |
| 7 | Normal from parametric form | $\mathbf{n} = (\mathbf{S}_u \times \mathbf{S}_v) / \lVert \cdot \rVert$ | unit vector |
| 8 | Image irradiance (Lambertian) | $E = \rho (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l})$ | **W·m$^{-2}$** |
| 9 | SFS reflectance equation | $E = \rho(-p l_x - q l_y + l_z)/\sqrt{1+p^2+q^2}$ | dimensionless ratios |
| 10 | Photometric stereo matrix | $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{L} \mathbf{g}$ | $\mathbf{L}$ is $m \times 3$ |
| 11 | LS solution for $\mathbf{g}$ | $\mathbf{g} = (\mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{L})^{-1}\mathbf{L}^\top\mathbf{E}$ | vector in $\mathbb{R}^3$ |
| 12 | Albedo | $\rho = \lVert \mathbf{g} \rVert$ | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ |
| 13 | Normal from $\mathbf{g}$ | $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{g} / \rho$ | unit vector |
| 14 | Mean curvature | $H = \frac{(1+q^2) f_{xx} - 2pq f_{xy} + (1+p^2) f_{yy}}{2(1+p^2+q^2)^{3/2}}$ | **m$^{-1}$** |
| 15 | Gaussian curvature | $K = \kappa_1 \kappa_2$ | **m$^{-2}$** |
| 16 | Range image to normal | cross product of neighbour vectors | discrete derivative |
| 17 | Integrability condition | $\partial p / \partial y = \partial q / \partial x$ | required for exact $Z$ |
| 18 | Surface integration | $Z = Z_0 + \int p\, dx + \int q\, dy$ | depth in **m** |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematics of surfaces is not academic — it powers the technology around you:

- **Smartphone face-unlock (Face ID)** uses photometric stereo-style multi-light IR illumination to recover a depth map of your face.
- **Autonomous vehicles (LiDAR)** produce massive point clouds that must be converted into surface meshes for obstacle detection.
- **Medical imaging (CT/MRI)** reconstructs organ surfaces from volumetric scans using marching cubes (an implicit-to-explicit surface converter).
- **AR/VR (ARKit, ARCore)** uses real-time photometric stereo combined with structured light to build 3D meshes of the user's environment.
- **Industrial inspection** uses shape-from-shading to detect micro-defects on mirror-like surfaces.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive** — every algebraic step is shown. KTU board examiners reward students who present clean, multi-step derivations. Do not skip lines.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface Normal from Implicit Form $F(x, y, z) = 0$

**Goal:** Show that $\mathbf{n} = \nabla F / \lVert \nabla F \rVert$ is perpendicular to the surface $F = 0$.

**Step 1 — Parametrize a curve on the surface.**
Any smooth curve lying entirely on the surface can be written as $\mathbf{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Since the curve stays on the surface, it must satisfy the constraint:

$$F(x(t), y(t), z(t)) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the constraint w.r.t. $t$ using the chain rule.**
Applying the multivariate chain rule:

$$\frac{d}{dt} F(x(t), y(t), z(t)) = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} \frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \frac{dz}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the inner product.**
Rearranging:

$$\begin{pmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} dx/dt \\ dy/dt \\ dz/dt \end{pmatrix} = \nabla F \cdot \mathbf{r}'(t) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
The vector $\mathbf{r}'(t) = (dx/dt, dy/dt, dz/dt)$ is the **tangent vector** to the curve at the point. Since the dot product $\nabla F \cdot \mathbf{r}'(t) = 0$ for *every* curve on the surface, $\nabla F$ is perpendicular to the entire tangent plane.

**Step 5 — Normalize to obtain a unit normal.**

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\nabla F}{\lVert \nabla F \rVert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}} \begin{pmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \end{pmatrix} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Normal from Explicit Form $Z = f(x, y)$

**Goal:** Show that $\mathbf{n} = (-f_x, -f_y, 1) / \sqrt{1 + f_x^2 + f_y^2}$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite in implicit form.**
The explicit surface $Z = f(x, y)$ is equivalent to the implicit form:

$$F(x, y, z) = f(x, y) - z = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives of $F$.**

$$F_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = p, \quad F_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = q, \quad F_z = -1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the implicit-form normal formula from Derivation 1.**

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{p^2 + q^2 + 1}} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Flip the sign to obtain a viewer-facing normal.**
By convention, we choose the normal pointing **towards the camera** (i.e., with positive $z$-component). Negating all components gives the equivalent outward-facing normal:

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}} \begin{pmatrix} -p \\ -q \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lambertian Image-Irradiance Equation and the Shape-from-Shading PDE

**Goal:** Derive the partial differential equation that SFS solves.

**Step 1 — Start with the Lambertian law.**
For a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surface, the image irradiance at a pixel is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the surface normal $\mathbf{n}$ and the light direction $\mathbf{l}$:

$$E(x, y) = \rho\, (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{l})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the explicit-form normal.**
Let $\mathbf{l} = (l_x, l_y, l_z)$ and $\mathbf{n} = (-p, -q, 1)/\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}$:

$$E(x, y) = \rho \cdot \frac{(-p) l_x + (-q) l_y + (1) l_z}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange as an SFS PDE.**
Defining the *reflectance map* $R(p, q) = (-p l_x - q l_y + l_z)/\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}$, the SFS problem is:

$$\text{Find } Z(x, y) \text{ such that } E(x, y) = \rho\, R\!\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Special case: frontal lighting with $\mathbf{l} = (0, 0, 1)$.**
Then $l_x = l_y = 0$, $l_z = 1$:

$$E(x, y) = \frac{\rho}{\sqrt{1 + p^2 + q^2}} \implies 1 + p^2 + q^2 = \left(\frac{\rho}{E}\right)^2$$

This is a **first-order Hamilton–Jacobi-type PDE** in $Z(x, y)$. Solving it with $E(x, y)$ given yields the surface up to a constant offset. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Photometric Stereo: Least-Squares Recovery of $\mathbf{g} = \rho \mathbf{n}$

**Goal:** Show how three or more images under different lights recover the normal and albedo at every pixel.

**Step 1 — Set up the per-pixel equation.**
For pixel $(x, y)$ under the $i$-th light source $\mathbf{l}^{(i)} = (l_x^{(i)}, l_y^{(i)}, l_z^{(i)})$:

$$E^{(i)}(x, y) = \mathbf{l}^{(i)} \cdot \mathbf{g}(x, y), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

where $\mathbf{g} = (g_x, g_y, g_z) = \rho\, \mathbf{n}$ is the (unknown) product of albedo and normal.

**Step 2 — Write all $m$ equations in matrix form.**

$$\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} E^{(1)} \\ E^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ E^{(m)} \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} l_x^{(1)} & l_y^{(1)} & l_z^{(1)} \\ l_x^{(2)} & l_y^{(2)} & l_z^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ l_x^{(m)} & l_y^{(m)} & l_z^{(m)} \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 3}} \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} g_x \\ g_y \\ g_z \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{g} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse.**
The normal equations $E = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{g}$ are solved in the least-squares sense by:

$$\mathbf{g} = (\mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{L})^{-1} \mathbf{L}^\top \mathbf{E}$$

This is well-defined provided $\mathbf{L}$ has rank 3 (i.e., the three light directions are *not coplanar*). For $m > 3$ the system is over-determined and the solution is the best fit to the redundant measurements.

**Step 4 — Separate albedo and normal direction.**

$$\rho = \lVert \mathbf{g} \rVert = \sqrt{g_x^2 + g_y^2 + g_z^2}$$

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{g}}{\lVert \mathbf{g} \rVert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{g_x^2 + g_y^2 + g_z^2}} \begin{pmatrix} g_x \\ g_y \\ g_z \end{pmatrix} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Surface Integration from a Normal Field (Frankot-Chellappa Approach)

**Goal:** Given a noisy field of surface gradients $(p, q)$, recover the best-fit integrable depth map $Z(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Express the integrability constraint.**
For an exact surface, the mixed partials must match:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial q}{\partial x} \quad \text{(Clairaut's theorem)}$$

**Step 2 — Reformulate in the Fourier domain.**
Express the surface and gradients via the discrete Fourier transform (DFT):

$$Z(x, y) = \sum_{(k_x, k_y)} c(k_x, k_y)\, e^{j(k_x x + k_y y)}$$

$$p(x, y) = \sum_{(k_x, k_y)} j k_x\, c(k_x, k_y)\, e^{j(k_x x + k_y y)} = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{j k_x C\}$$

$$q(x, y) = \sum_{(k_x, k_y)} j k_y\, c(k_x, k_y)\, e^{j(k_x x + k_y y)} = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{j k_y C\}$$

**Step 3 — Set up the least-squares objective.**
Minimize the sum of squared errors between the observed and predicted gradients:

$$J(c) = \sum_{(x, y)} \left[ (p_{\text{obs}} - p_{\text{pred}})^2 + (q_{\text{obs}} - q_{\text{pred}})^2 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Fourier coefficients $C(k_x, k_y)$.**
Setting $\partial J / \partial C^* = 0$ yields the **Frankot-Chellappa closed-form solution**:

$$C(k_x, k_y) = \frac{-j k_x\, P_{\text{obs}}(k_x, k_y) - j k_y\, Q_{\text{obs}}(k_x, k_y)}{k_x^2 + k_y^2}$$

where $P_{\text{obs}}$ and $Q_{\text{obs}}$ are the DFTs of the observed $p$ and $q$.

**Step 5 — Inverse-transform to obtain $Z$.**

$$Z(x, y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{C(k_x, k_y)\} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This algorithm is **enforced integrable** by construction, and it is the standard go-to in KTU practical examinations and Module 1 problem sets for *integrating a normal field to a depth map*.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Given a sphere $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 9$ and the point $P = (0, 0, 3)$ on it, find:
(a) the outward surface normal at $P$,
(b) the surface gradient $(p, q)$ at $P$ (using the explicit form $Z = f(x, y)$).

**Solution:**

**(a) Implicit-form normal:**
Here $F(x, y, z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 - 9$.

$$F_x = 2x, \quad F_y = 2y, \quad F_z = 2z$$

At $P = (0, 0, 3)$:

$$F_x = 0, \quad F_y = 0, \quad F_z = 6, \quad \lVert \nabla F \rVert = 6$$

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(b) Explicit-form gradients:**
On the upper hemisphere, $Z = \sqrt{9 - x^2 - y^2}$.

$$p = \frac{\partial Z}{\partial x} = \frac{-x}{\sqrt{9 - x^2 - y^2}}, \quad q = \frac{\partial Z}{\partial y} = \frac{-y}{\sqrt{9 - x^2 - y^2}}$$

At $P = (0, 0, 3)$: $p = 0$, $q = 0$ (i.e., the top of the sphere is locally flat — a fact every student should recognize geometrically).

**Verification via the explicit-form normal formula:**

$$\mathbf{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0^2 + 0^2}} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods agree. $\blacksquare$
