# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Disparity–Depth Relationship (Parallel Rig)

This is the foundational equation of stereopsis. We derive it from pure similar triangles.

**Setup (Coordinate System):**
- Let the **left camera** be the origin, with optical axis along $Z$.
- The right camera is translated by $+b$ along the $X$-axis.
- Both cameras have the same focal length $f$.
- Consider a 3D point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)$ in the left camera's frame.

**Step 1 — Project $\mathbf{X}$ onto the left image plane:**

Using the pinhole projection model (similar triangles between $(X, Y, Z)$ and $(x_L, y_L, f)$):

$$x_L = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Project $\mathbf{X}$ onto the right image plane:**

Because the right camera is shifted by $b$ along $X$, the X-coordinate of the point *relative to the right camera* is $X - b$. The y-coordinate is unchanged (parallel axes).

$$x_R = f \cdot \dfrac{X - b}{Z}$$

$$y_R = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z} = y_L$$

**Step 3 — Define disparity as the horizontal shift:**

$$d \equiv x_L - x_R = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \dfrac{(X - b)}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify algebraically:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z} + f \cdot \dfrac{b}{Z} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{f \cdot b}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain depth:**

$$\boxed{\,Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Depth $Z$ is **inversely proportional to disparity $d$. A point at infinity has zero disparity; a point on the baseline plane has infinite disparity.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 3D Reconstruction via Triangulation

Given a matched pair $(x_L, y_L)$ and $(x_R, y_R)$ in pixel coordinates, recover $(X, Y, Z)$.

**Step 1 — From the projection equations:**

$$X = \dfrac{x_L \cdot Z}{f}, \qquad X = b + \dfrac{x_R \cdot Z}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for $X$ and solve for $Z$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} &= b + \dfrac{x_R \cdot Z}{f} \\[4pt]
Z \cdot \dfrac{x_L - x_R}{f} &= b \\[4pt]
Z &= \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x_L - x_R} = \dfrac{fb}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Back-substitute to find $X$ and $Y$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} = x_L \cdot \dfrac{b}{d} \\[4pt]
Y &= \dfrac{y_L \cdot Z}{f} = y_L \cdot \dfrac{b}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The triangulation result is **identical** to the disparity–depth equation. This consistency check confirms our derivation is correct.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth Uncertainty (Error Propagation)

Suppose the pixel-matching algorithm has an uncertainty $\delta d$ (typically $\pm 1$ pixel for sub-pixel methods, $\pm 0.2$ pixel). How does this propagate to depth?

**Step 1 — Start from $Z = fb / d$.**

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $d$, treating $f$ and $b$ as exact constants:**

$$\dfrac{dZ}{dd} = -\dfrac{fb}{d^{2}} = -\dfrac{Z^{2}}{fb}$$

**Step 3 — Take the absolute value for the magnitude of the error:**

$$\vert \delta Z \vert = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot b} \cdot \vert \delta d \vert$$

**Step 4 — Discuss the implications:**

- Depth error grows **quadratically** with $Z$ — a four-fold increase in distance causes a 16-fold increase in depth error.
- Doubling the baseline $b$ halves the error — this is why long-baseline stereo rigs (e.g., aerial photogrammetry with $b \approx 1$ m) outperform short-baseline ones at long range.
- Doubling the focal length $f$ also halves the error — telephoto lenses improve depth resolution.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Design Rule of Thumb:** For $\delta d = 1$ pixel and a target depth error of $\delta Z = 1$ cm at $Z = 10$ m, the minimum required $fb$ product is $Z^2 / \delta Z = 10^4 / 0.01 = 10^{6}$ pixel·metres. With $f = 1000$ pixels, you need $b \geq 1$ m.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Essential Matrix (Geometric Form)

The **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ encodes the epipolar geometry for calibrated cameras.

**Step 1 — Express the 3D point in the left camera's frame:**

$$\mathbf{X}_L = (X, Y, Z)^{\top}$$

**Step 2 — Transform it to the right camera's frame using the rigid-body transformation $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$:**

$$\mathbf{X}_R = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_L + \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 3 — Observe that the camera centers, $\mathbf{X}_L$, and $\mathbf{X}_R$ are coplanar** (they lie in the epipolar plane). For coplanarity, the scalar triple product vanishes:

$$(\mathbf{X}_L - \mathbf{0})^{\top} \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{X}_R) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange using the skew-symmetric cross-product matrix $[\mathbf{t}]_{\times}$:**

$$[\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_L = -[\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{t} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(used implicitly)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Essential Matrix:**

$$\boxed{\,\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Project to image coordinates ($\mathbf{x}_L = \mathbf{X}_L / Z_L$, $\mathbf{x}_R = \mathbf{X}_R / Z_R$) to get the constraint:**

$$\mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Essential Matrix has five degrees of freedom (3 for $\mathbf{R}$, 3 for $\mathbf{t}$, minus 1 for overall scale). It is a $3 \times 3$ matrix of **rank 2** with **two equal non-zero singular values**. This is the basis of the famous 5-point algorithm for $\mathbf{E}$ estimation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Result | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Disparity–Depth | $Z = fb/d$ | Parallel cameras, identical $f$ |
| Triangulation | $X = x_L b / d$, $Y = y_L b / d$ | Same as above |
| Depth Uncertainty | $\vert \delta Z \vert = (Z^2 / fb) \cdot \vert \delta d \vert$ | Small $\delta d$, exact $f, b$ |
| Essential Matrix | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$ | Calibrated cameras |
| Fundamental Matrix | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Known intrinsics $\mathbf{K}$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stereo Vision Pipeline

This block diagram maps the full data flow of a binocular camera system from raw pixel acquisition through to the final depth map. (Used in place of a physical drawing because of the multi-component pipeline.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camL["LEFT CAMERA L"]
    camR["RIGHT CAMERA R"]
    rectL["RECTIFICATION L"]
    rectR["RECTIFICATION R"]
    match["STEREO MATCHING"]
    disp["DISPARITY MAP"]
    depth["DEPTH MAP Z fb OVER d"]
    pcloud["3D POINT CLOUD"]
    app["APPLICATION LAYER"]

    camL --> rectL
    camR --> rectR
    rectL --> match
    rectR --> match
    match --> disp
    disp --> depth
    depth --> pcloud
    pcloud --> app
```

**Block descriptions (for the student to memorize):**

1. **LEFT CAMERA / RIGHT CAMERA** — synchronized image acquisition; both should have identical intrinsics ideally.
2. **RECTIFICATION L / R** — warp both images so epipolar lines are horizontal and aligned; reduces correspondence search to 1-D along scanlines.
3. **STEREO MATCHING** — find pixel-to-pixel correspondences (block matching, semi-global matching, or deep-learning based).
4. **DISPARITY MAP** — per-pixel value of $d = x_L - x_R$.
5. **DEPTH MAP** — apply $Z = fb/d$ per pixel.
6. **3D POINT CLOUD** — back-project to world coordinates via triangulation.
7. **APPLICATION LAYER** — object detection, obstacle avoidance, AR anchoring, etc.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Epipolar Geometry Relationships

This graph shows the conceptual dependency between the major entities of epipolar geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    XL["3D POINT X"]
    CL["LEFT CENTER CL"]
    CR["RIGHT CENTER CR"]
    EPIP["EPIPOLAR PLANE"]
    EL["EPIPOLAR LINE LEFT"]
    ER["EPIPOLAR LINE RIGHT"]
    ELP["LEFT PROJECTION XL"]
    ERP["RIGHT PROJECTION XR"]
    CON["EPIPOLAR CONSTRAINT XR ON EL"]

    XL --- EPIP
    CL --- EPIP
    CR --- EPIP
    EPIP --- EL
    EPIP --- ER
    XL --- ELP
    XL --- ERP
    ELP --- CON
    ER --- CON
```

**Reading guide:**
- The **epipolar plane** is the unique plane containing $\mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{C}_L$, and $\mathbf{C}_R$.
- The plane intersects each image plane in a line: these are the **epipolar lines**.
- All corresponding points must lie on the corresponding epipolar line — the **epipolar constraint** reduces the 2-D correspondence problem to a 1-D search.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Disparity vs. Depth (Inverse-Linear Trade-off)

This flowchart shows how disparity and depth are related at different ranges, useful for visualizing the quadratic error growth.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CLOSE OBJECT Z SMALL"] --> B["LARGE DISPARITY D LARGE"]
    B --> C["HIGH DEPTH RESOLUTION DELTA Z SMALL"]
    D["FAR OBJECT Z LARGE"] --> E["SMALL DISPARITY D NEAR ZERO"]
    E --> F["LOW DEPTH RESOLUTION DELTA Z LARGE QUADRATIC"]
    C --> G["TRIANGULATION ACCURATE"]
    F --> H["TRIANGULATION NOISY OR IMPOSSIBLE BEYOND Z MAX"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the conceptual reason for the **maximum range** of a stereo rig: when $d$ falls below 1 pixel, the scene point is effectively at infinity for that rig.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define stereopsis. What is meant by binocular camera geometry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Stereopsis** is the perception of depth that arises when a scene is viewed by two spatially separated imaging systems (eyes or cameras), each producing a slightly different 2-D projection of the same 3-D world. The human visual system fuses these two projections to recover depth.

> **Binocular camera geometry** is the mathematical framework describing the geometric relationship between two pinhole cameras with a known relative pose — i.e., known baseline $b$ and known relative rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$. It formalizes how a 3-D point projects into both image planes and how the resulting 2-D projections are constrained by the **epipolar geometry**.

**(Mark split: 1 mark for the definition of stereopsis, 1 mark for the depth-perception aspect, 1 mark for binocular camera geometry as the mathematical framework.)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the disparity–depth relationship for a parallel stereo rig and explain the role of the baseline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The disparity–depth equation for a parallel-axis stereo rig is:

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

where $Z$ is the depth of the 3-D point, $f$ is the focal length, $b$ is the baseline, and $d$ is the horizontal pixel disparity.

**Role of baseline:** The baseline $b$ is the Euclidean separation between the two camera optical centers. It appears in the numerator, so **a larger baseline yields a larger disparity for the same depth**, which in turn yields **smaller depth error** $\delta Z = (Z^2 / fb) \cdot \delta d$. Hence, a longer baseline improves depth resolution at the cost of a reduced common field of view and increased occlusions.

**(Mark split: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for explaining each variable, 1 mark for the role of baseline.)**

---

## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a clear diagram, derive the relationship between depth $Z$ and disparity $d$ for a parallel stereo camera rig. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel stereo rig has a baseline $b = 25$ cm and focal length $f = 800$ pixels. A scene point produces a horizontal disparity of $d = 40$ pixels in the rectified image pair.
    (i) Compute the depth $Z$ of the point in metres.
    (ii) If the pixel-matching uncertainty is $\delta d = 0.5$ pixels, estimate the depth uncertainty $\delta Z$.
    (iii) If the same scene point is now observed from a rig with twice the baseline, what is the new depth uncertainty? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions to state (1 mark):*
- Two pinhole cameras with identical focal length $f$.
- Optical axes are parallel; image planes are coplanar.
- The two cameras are separated by baseline $b$ along the common X-axis.
- Lens distortion has been removed by rectification.

*Derivation steps (5 marks):* Follow the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes, with the following key equations:

$$x_L = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \qquad x_R = f \cdot \dfrac{(X - b)}{Z}$$

$$d = x_L - x_R = \dfrac{fb}{Z} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; Z = \dfrac{fb}{d}$$

Include a labelled diagram (similar to the one in Section 4) of the two cameras, the baseline, the focal lengths, and the projections of the 3-D point on both image planes — **with the two similar triangles clearly marked** (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Depth (2 marks):**

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d} = \dfrac{800 \times 0.25}{40} = \dfrac{200}{40} = 5 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Depth uncertainty (3 marks):**

$$\delta Z = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot b} \cdot \delta d = \dfrac{5^{2}}{800 \times 0.25} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{25}{200} \times 0.5 = 0.125 \times 0.5 = 0.0625 \text{ m} = 6.25 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Doubled baseline (2 marks):** With $b' = 2b = 0.5$ m, all else equal:

$$\delta Z' = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot b'} \cdot \delta d = \dfrac{25}{800 \times 0.5} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{25}{400} \times 0.5 = 0.03125 \text{ m} = 3.125 \text{ cm}$$

**Comment (1 bonus point in valuation):** Doubling the baseline halves the depth uncertainty, confirming the trade-off discussed in class.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **epipolar geometry** in stereo vision. Define the terms *epipole*, *epipolar plane*, and *epipolar line*, and state the **epipolar constraint**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ and the **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$. How are they related? Why is $\mathbf{E}$ a $3 \times 3$ matrix of rank 2, and what is the **eight-point algorithm**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Epipolar geometry (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Epipolar geometry is the intrinsic projective geometry between two views (cameras) of the same 3-D scene. It describes *where* a point in one image can possibly appear in the other image, independent of the scene structure.

**Terms (3 marks, 1 each):**
- **Epipole** ($\mathbf{e}_L$, $\mathbf{e}_R$): the projection of one camera's optical centre into the other camera's image plane. For a parallel rig, both epipoles lie at infinity along the X-direction.
- **Epipolar plane**: the plane containing the 3-D point $\mathbf{X}$ and the two optical centres $\mathbf{C}_L$ and $\mathbf{C}_R$.
- **Epipolar line**: the intersection of the epipolar plane with an image plane. It is the locus of all possible correspondences for the given 3-D point.

**Epipolar constraint (2 marks):** Given a point $\mathbf{x}_L$ in the left image, its correspondence $\mathbf{x}_R$ in the right image is constrained to lie on the epipolar line $\ell_R$ that passes through the right epipole. Mathematically:

$$\mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_L = 0 \;\; \text{(calibrated case)}$$

This reduces correspondence search from 2-D to 1-D.

**(b) Essential and Fundamental matrices (7 marks):**

**Essential Matrix $\mathbf{E}$ (2 marks):** For *calibrated* cameras, the Essential Matrix encodes the relative rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ between the two cameras:

$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$$

where $[\mathbf{t}]_{\times}$ is the $3 \times 3$ skew-symmetric cross-product matrix of the translation vector. The constraint is $\mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ in *normalized* image coordinates.

**Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$ (2 marks):** For *uncalibrated* cameras, the Fundamental Matrix generalizes $\mathbf{E}$ to incorporate the camera intrinsics $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-\top} \, \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{K}^{-1}$$

The constraint is $\mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ in raw (pixel) homogeneous coordinates.

**Rank-2 property (1 mark):** Both $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{F}$ are singular, with rank exactly 2. This follows from the fact that $[\mathbf{t}]_{\times}$ is rank-deficient (cross-product matrix of a non-zero vector has rank 2), and translation, being non-zero in any meaningful stereo configuration, ensures the epipoles are finite and distinct.

**Eight-point algorithm (2 marks):** $\mathbf{F}$ has 9 entries but only 7 degrees of freedom (8 up to scale, minus 1 for the rank-2 constraint). The classical **8-point algorithm** uses **8 point correspondences** to set up a linear homogeneous system of 8 equations in 8 unknowns, then enforces the rank-2 constraint via SVD post-processing. From $\mathbf{F}$, one recovers the camera matrices and hence the 3-D structure by triangulation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of disparity:** Always use $d = x_L - x_R$ for the standard left-positive convention; reversing the order flips the sign of $Z$ in some derivations — students lose 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Units confusion:** $f$ and $b$ must be in *consistent units* (both in metres, or both in pixels). Mixing pixel focal length with metre baseline is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Parallel rig assumption:** The formula $Z = fb/d$ is invalid for the toed-in / converged configuration. If the question does not specify parallel, you must invoke general epipolar geometry — **don't blindly apply the formula**.
> 4. **Confusing $\mathbf{E}$ with $\mathbf{F}$:** $\mathbf{E}$ is for *calibrated* cameras (normalized coordinates), $\mathbf{F}$ for *uncalibrated* (pixel coordinates). Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 5. **Forgetting the rank-2 constraint:** When estimating $\mathbf{F}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ from data, the raw solution is full rank due to noise. You must enforce rank 2 via SVD (set the smallest singular value to zero). Many students omit this step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stereopsis** = depth from two displaced views; requires two cameras (or two eyes) and a *correspondence* between them.
- **Binocular camera geometry** = the mathematical framework (epipolar geometry + triangulation) for stereo vision.
- **Two configurations:** *parallel-axis* (ideal, simple $Z = fb/d$) and *converged / toed-in* (general epipolar geometry).
- **Three core quantities:** baseline $b$, focal length $f$, disparity $d$.
- **Master formula (parallel rig):** $Z = \dfrac{fb}{d}$ — inverse linear; **the most important equation in this module**.
- **Triangulation:** Given $(x_L, y_L, x_R, y_R)$, recover $X = x_L \cdot b / d$, $Y = y_L \cdot b / d$, $Z = f b / d$.
- **Depth uncertainty:** $\delta Z = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f b} \cdot \delta d$ — grows **quadratically** with $Z$.
- **Epipolar constraint:** $\mathbf{x}_R^{\top} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ — correspondence must lie on the epipolar line; reduces search to 1-D.
- **Epipole** = projection of one camera centre into the other image plane; **epipolar plane** = plane through 3-D point and both camera centres; **epipolar line** = intersection of epipolar plane with image plane.
- **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$ — calibrated, rank 2, 5 DoF.
- **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ — uncalibrated, rank 2, 7 DoF.
- **Eight-point algorithm** = linear method to estimate $\mathbf{F}$ from 8 correspondences.
- **Design trade-off:** larger $b$ → better depth resolution, but smaller common FOV and more occlusions.
- **Range limits:** $d_{\min} \geq 1$ pixel $\Rightarrow$ $Z_{\max} = fb$ (the **hyperfocal-style limit** of a stereo rig).
- **Typical baselines:** 5–10 cm (compact), 25–35 cm (automotive), 0.5–1 m (aerial / surveying).
- **Rectification** rewarp both images to make epipolar lines horizontal — essential pre-processing before matching.
- **Common stereo matching algorithms** (mention at least one in viva): Block Matching, Semi-Global Matching (SGM), AD-Census, deep-learning methods (e.g., PSMNet, RAFT-Stereo).
- **Applications to memorize for viva:** autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery, AR/VR, aerial DEM, industrial inspection, 3-D cinema.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Epipolar Constraint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Epipolar Constraint # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Epipolar Constraint (KTU 2024 — Formal Definition):** > The **Epipolar Constraint** is a fundamental geometric relationship in stereo vision (multi-view geometry) that restricts the possible locations of a projected 3D point in two different camera views. It states that *given a 2D point in one image, its corresponding point in the second image must lie on a specific line called the **epipolar line***. Mathematically, for corresponding homogeneous image points $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$, the constraint is: > $$\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$$ > where $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is the **Fundamental Matrix** of rank **2**, encoding the intrinsic and extrinsic geometry between the two cameras. The epipolar constraint is the **mathematical embodiment of stereo geometry**, allowing the correspondence search to be reduced from a 2D search in the entire second image to a 1D search along a single line. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shadow of a Torch" Imagine you are holding a **flashlight (torch)** in a dark room, and you point it at a small ball suspended in mid-air. - Your **left eye** and **right eye** are two cameras. The ball is a 3D world point $P$. - The ray from each eye to the ball is the **back-projected ray** (also called the *line of sight*). - Both rays, together with the baseline (the line connecting the two eyes/cameras), lie in a single flat plane — the **Epipolar Plane**. - The **torchlight metaphor**: The point $P$ casts a "geometric shadow" on each retina. The shadow on the right eye is the image point $\mathbf{x}'$. The epipolar constraint says: *"if you know the shadow on the left eye, then the shadow on the right eye cannot be anywhere in space — it must lie on the line where the epipolar plane slices through the right eye's image plane."* This line is the **epipolar line**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** > - The epipolar constraint reduces the search for a matching point from the *entire 2D image* to a *single 1D line*. > - It arises purely from the **rigid geometric setup** of two cameras observing a common scene. > - It requires **no knowledge of the 3D structure** of the scene — only camera calibration. ## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Mandate) | Term | Plain Meaning | Geometric Locus | |------|---------------|-----------------| | **Baseline** | Line joining the two camera centers $C, C'$ | The "third side" of the epipolar triangle | | **Epipole** $e, e'$ | Intersection of the baseline with each image plane | The image of the other camera's center | | **Epipolar Plane** | Plane containing $C, C'$ and the 3D point $P$ | 2D slice of 3D space | | **Epipolar Line** $l, l'$ | Intersection of the epipolar plane with each image plane | The search line for the corresponding point | | **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix encoding the epipolar geometry | Maps $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{l}'$ | | **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ | Specialization of $\mathbf{F}$ for calibrated cameras | Maps normalized $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{l}'$ | > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** > Epipolar geometry is the foundation of **stereo matching, 3D reconstruction, Structure-from-Motion (SfM), and SLAM**. Almost every KTU question on multi-view geometry will test your understanding of this constraint. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Geometric Construction — Step by Step Given two cameras with optical centers $C$ (first view) and $C'$ (second view), and a 3D scene point $P$: **Step 1 — Back-projection.** Projecting $\mathbf{x}$ back through camera $C$ yields a 3D ray. The 3D point $P$ must lie somewhere on this ray. **Step 2 — Plane Formation.** The 3D point $P$ together with the two camera centers $C$ and $C'$ uniquely defines a plane in $\mathbb{R}^3$ — the **Epipolar Plane**, $\pi$. **Step 3 — Image Intersection.** The plane $\pi$ intersects the second image plane $\Pi'$ in a line, called the **epipolar line** $\mathbf{l}'$. By construction, $\mathbf{x}'$ must lie on $\mathbf{l}'$. **Step 4 — The Algebraic Form.** Because $\mathbf{x}'$ is on $\mathbf{l}'$ and $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}$, the condition of incidence is $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{l}' = 0$, yielding the canonical epipolar constraint. ## 2.2 The "Why" — Where the Constraint Originates The epipolar constraint exists because **two cameras observing a 3D point form a rigid plane**. This plane intersects each image in a line. Since the matching point must lie on this line in each image, the search is restricted. Without this geometric truth, finding correspondences would be a hopelessly under-constrained 2D-to-2D matching problem. ## 2.3 Two Critical Algebraic Forms > [!IMPORTANT] > **Form 1 — Image Coordinates (Uncalibrated):** $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ > Here $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'$ are in raw pixel coordinates, and $\mathbf{F}$ is the **Fundamental Matrix** (rank 2, 7 degrees of freedom). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Form 2 — Normalized Coordinates (Calibrated):** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ > Here $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ are normalized image coordinates, and $\mathbf{E}$ is the **Essential Matrix** (rank 2, 5 degrees of freedom). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Constraints / Units | |---|---------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (fundamental) | $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}' \in \mathbb{P}^{2}$ (homogeneous) | | 2 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (essential) | Normalized coords, $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ | | 3 | $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}$ | Epipolar line in image 2 | Vector form of $\mathbf{F}$ | | 4 | $\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{F}^{T} \mathbf{x}'$ | Epipolar line in image 1 | Transposed form | | 5 | $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{K}'^{T} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$? *(corrected below)* | — | See corrected formula | | 5 (corrected) | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$ | Essential matrix from extrinsics | $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$, $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | | 6 | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Fundamental from essential | $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{K}'$ = intrinsics | | 7 | $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{e} = 0,\ \mathbf{F}^{T} \mathbf{e}' = 0$ | Epipole lies on every epipolar line | $\mathbf{e}, \mathbf{e}'$ are right/left null-vectors of $\mathbf{F}$ | | 8 | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{F}) = 2$ | Singularity of $\mathbf{F}$ | $\det(\mathbf{F}) = 0$ | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid (KTU 2024 Hot Trick):** The epipole $\mathbf{e}$ is the **right null-vector** of $\mathbf{F}$: $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$. This is because the epipole is the image of the other camera's center, and the line from any image point to the epipole is itself an epipolar line. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Stereo depth cameras** (e.g., Intel RealSense, stereo vision in autonomous vehicles) use the epipolar constraint to find matches between left and right cameras in **real-time** ($< 20$ ms per frame). - **Structure-from-Motion (SfM)** pipelines (e.g., COLMAP, OpenMVG) use the **eight-point algorithm** to estimate $\mathbf{F}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ from putative matches, then triangulate 3D points. - **Visual SLAM** (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono) uses the epipolar constraint in the initialization and loop-closure modules to validate feature correspondences. - **3D reconstruction** of cultural heritage, medical imaging, and aerial photogrammetry all rely on epipolar geometry. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Epipolar Constraint Equation We derive the central equation $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ from the geometry of two cameras observing a 3D point $P$. ### Setup — Coordinate Frames Let the world point $P$ have homogeneous coordinates $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^{T}$ in the world frame. Let the two camera projection matrices be: $$ \mathbf{P}_1 = \mathbf{K}_1 [\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}], \qquad \mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{K}_2 [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] $$ where $\mathbf{K}_1, \mathbf{K}_2$ are the $3 \times 3$ intrinsic matrices of cameras 1 and 2, $\mathbf{R}$ is the rotation, and $\mathbf{t}$ is the translation that brings a point in camera-1's frame to camera-2's frame. The image points are: $$ \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{P}_1 \mathbf{P}, \qquad \mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{P}_2 \mathbf{P} $$ ### Step 1 — Express 3D Point in Camera-1 Frame A point in the first camera's frame (with origin at $C$) can be written as: $$ \mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{K}_1^{-1} \mathbf{x} \quad \text{(a 3D vector in the camera-1 frame)} $$ The 3D ray from $C$ to $P$ consists of all points $\lambda \mathbf{P}_c$ for $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. ### Step 2 — Transform the Ray into Camera-2's Frame The same ray, expressed in the second camera's frame (origin at $C'$), is: $$ \mathbf{Q}(\lambda) = \mathbf{R} (\lambda \mathbf{P}_c) + \mathbf{t} = \lambda \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_c + \mathbf{t} $$ This is a parametric line in $\mathbb{R}^3$ — the ray in the second camera's coordinate frame. ### Step 3 — Project the Ray into the Second Image The image of the ray $\mathbf{Q}(\lambda)$ in the second image is given by $\mathbf{K}_2 \mathbf{Q}(\lambda)$, which is a 2D line (a 1-parameter family of 2D points). This is the **epipolar line** $\mathbf{l}'$. ### Step 4 — Algebraic Form of the Epipolar Line Since $\mathbf{l}'$ is a line in projective 2-space, we can express it as $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}$. To find $\mathbf{F}$, we need a homogeneous representation of the line. A classical result (Longuet-Higgins, 1981) gives the essential matrix for the **calibrated** case as: $$ \mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R} $$ where $[\mathbf{t}]_{\times}$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix**: $$ [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ The epipolar line in the second image (in normalized coordinates) is: $$ \mathbf{l}'_n = \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply the Incidence Condition Since the observed point $\hat{\mathbf{x}}'$ must lie on the line $\mathbf{l}'_n$: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{l}'_n = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0 $$ This is the **calibrated epipolar constraint**. ### Step 6 — Lift to Uncalibrated (Pixel) Coordinates Substitute $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}' = \mathbf{K}'^{-1} \mathbf{x}'$: $$ (\mathbf{K}'^{-1} \mathbf{x}')^{T} \mathbf{E} (\mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{x}) = 0 $$ $$ \mathbf{x}'^{T} (\mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}) \mathbf{x} = 0 $$ Define the **Fundamental Matrix**: $$ \mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1} $$ This yields the canonical epipolar constraint: $$ \boxed{\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0} $$ ### Step 7 — Algebraic Properties of $\mathbf{F}$ We can show two critical properties: **Property A — Singularity.** $\mathbf{F}$ has rank 2 because $\mathbf{E}$ has rank 2 and $\mathbf{K}, \mathbf{K}'$ are full-rank. **Property B — Epipoles as null-vectors.** The epipole in image 1, $\mathbf{e}$, satisfies $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$, and the epipole in image 2, $\mathbf{e}'$, satisfies $\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{e}' = \mathbf{0}$. This follows because the epipole is the image of the other camera's center, and any epipolar line must pass through the epipole. ## 3.2 The Eight-Point Algorithm (Estimation of $\mathbf{F}$) > [!NOTE] > **Why "Eight"?** $\mathbf{F}$ has 9 entries but is defined up to scale (8 unknowns) and is rank-constrained (1 constraint) → **7 DOF**. In practice, the **8-point algorithm** is used (ignoring rank), then the result is projected to rank-2 via SVD. For $N \geq 8$ point correspondences $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}'_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, each gives one linear equation in the 9 unknowns $f_{ij}$ of $\mathbf{F}$. Stack the equations into $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{0}$ and solve via SVD: $\mathbf{f}$ is the right singular vector of $\mathbf{A}$ corresponding to its smallest singular value. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Two cameras are related by a pure translation $\mathbf{t} = (1, 0, 0)^{T}$ and identity rotation $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$. Both cameras have $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}' = \mathbf{I}$ (calibrated, identity intrinsics). A 3D point $P = (2, 1, 3)^{T}$ is observed. Verify the epipolar constraint. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute the essential matrix: $$ [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ Step 2 — Project $P$ into both images (with $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{I}$): $$ \mathbf{x} = (2, 1, 3)^{T}, \quad \mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{R} P + \mathbf{t} = (3, 1, 3)^{T} $$ Step 3 — Verify the constraint: $$ \mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x} = (3, 1, 3) \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} $$ $$ = (3, 1, 3) \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = 0 - 3 + 3 = 0 \quad \checkmark $$ The epipolar constraint is satisfied, confirming our derivation. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Epipolar Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional pipeline** of epipolar geometry: how a 3D world point $P$ generates two image points $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'$ that are bound by the epipolar constraint. This avoids attempting a literal 3D perspective drawing inside Mermaid, which is unreliable. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["World Point P in 3D Scene"] --> B["Camera 1 Center C"] A --> C["Camera 2 Center C Prime"] B --> D["Image Point x in Image Plane Pi"] C --> E["Image Point x prime in Image Plane Pi Prime"] F["Baseline C to C Prime"] --> G["Epipole e in Image 1"] F --> H["Epipole e Prime in Image 2"] A --> I["Epipolar Plane Pi E containing C C Prime and P"] I --> J["Epipolar Line l in Image 1"] I --> K["Epipolar Line l Prime in Image 2"] D --> J E --> K G --> J H --> K L["Fundamental Matrix F 3x3 Rank 2"] --> M["Constraint x prime T F x equals 0"] D --> M E --> M L --> K L --> J N["Camera Intrinsics K K Prime and Extrinsics R t"] --> L N --> O["Essential Matrix E equals t cross R"] O --> L ``` ## 4.2 Computational Pipeline for Estimating $\mathbf{F}$ (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Step 1: Detect Feature Points
SIFT ORB SURF"] P2["Step 2: Match Features
Across Two Images"] P3["Step 3: Build Matrix A
From Correspondences"] P4["Step 4: SVD of A
f equals right singular vector"] P5["Step 5: Reshape f to F
3 by 3 Matrix"] P6["Step 6: Enforce Rank 2
SVD Thresholding"] P7["Step 7: Output F
Fundamental Matrix"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 ``` ## 4.3 Epipolar Line Generation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Input Point x in Image 1"] --> R["Apply F to x
l prime equals F x"] R --> S["Epipolar Line l prime in Image 2"] T["Search for x prime on l prime
1D Search along line"] --> U["Output Matched Correspondence"] S --> T ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a *computational or geometric stage*. The arrows indicate data or geometric dependency. The "Epipolar Plane" block is the central geometric structure that produces both the left and right epipolar lines simultaneously. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define the epipolar constraint. State the equation $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ and identify the role of the fundamental matrix.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The epipolar constraint is the geometric restriction that given a point $\mathbf{x}$ in one image, its corresponding point $\mathbf{x}'$ in another image of the same scene must lie on a specific line — the *epipolar line*. The algebraic form is $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$, where $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'$ are homogeneous image coordinates and $\mathbf{F}$ is the **fundamental matrix**, a $3 \times 3$ rank-2 matrix that encapsulates the intrinsic and extrinsic geometry of the two-camera setup. $\mathbf{F}$ maps a point in image 1 to its corresponding epipolar line in image 2 via $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}$. *(Mark distribution: 1M definition, 1M equation, 1M role of $\mathbf{F}$.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **What is an epipole? How is it related to the fundamental matrix?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **epipole** is the point in one image where the baseline (line joining the two camera centers) intersects the image plane. It is the projection of the other camera's center. In the first image it is denoted $\mathbf{e}$, and in the second image $\mathbf{e}'$. The relationship with the fundamental matrix is: $$ \mathbf{F} \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \mathbf{F}^{T} \mathbf{e}' = \mathbf{0} $$ i.e., the epipole is the **right null vector** of $\mathbf{F}$ in the first image and the **left null vector** of $\mathbf{F}^{T}$ in the second image. Equivalently, every epipolar line in image 2 passes through the epipole $\mathbf{e}'$, and every epipolar line in image 1 passes through $\mathbf{e}$. *(Mark distribution: 1M epipole definition, 1M null-vector relationship, 1M epipolar-line passing property.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting homogeneous coordinates** — Image points must be in *homogeneous* form $(u, v, 1)^{T}$ when using $\mathbf{F}$; mixing pixel and homogeneous will give 0 marks. > 2. **Wrong rank of $\mathbf{F}$** — Many students write $\mathbf{F}$ is rank 3; it is rank 2 (or rank 0 if degenerate). Examiners specifically check this. > 3. **Confusing $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{F}$** — Essential matrix is for *normalized* coordinates; Fundamental is for raw pixel coordinates. The relationship $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ is asked frequently. > 4. **Omitting the singular value $\sigma$ in the eight-point algorithm** — A common 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Stating degrees of freedom incorrectly** — $\mathbf{F}$ has **7 DOF** (9 entries − 1 scale − 1 rank = 7). Saying "8 DOF" loses a mark. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block-level architecture, explain the geometric construction of the **epipolar plane**, the **epipoles**, and the **epipolar lines**. Relate each to the fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$. (7 Marks) **(b)** Derive the **epipolar constraint** $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ from the two-camera projection model. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Geometric Construction (7 Marks):** 1. **Setup:** Two cameras with optical centers $C$ and $C'$ observe a 3D point $P$. Let $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$ be the projections of $P$ in the two images. 2. **Baseline:** The line $\overline{CC'}$ is called the **baseline**. 3. **Epipoles:** The baseline intersects the two image planes at the **epipoles** $\mathbf{e}$ and $\mathbf{e}'$. Equivalently, $\mathbf{e}$ is the projection of $C'$ in image 1, and $\mathbf{e}'$ is the projection of $C$ in image 2. 4. **Epipolar Plane:** The plane containing $C, C', P$ is the **epipolar plane** $\pi$. It is uniquely defined because three non-collinear points determine a plane. 5. **Epipolar Lines:** The intersection of $\pi$ with image plane $\Pi_1$ is a line $\mathbf{l}$ (passing through $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{e}$). The intersection of $\pi$ with $\Pi_2$ is a line $\mathbf{l}'$ (passing through $\mathbf{x}'$ and $\mathbf{e}'$). These are the **epipolar lines**. 6. **Relation to $\mathbf{F}$:** $\mathbf{F}$ maps $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{l}'$ via $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}$, and $\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{x}'$. The epipoles are the null vectors: $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{e}' = \mathbf{0}$. $\mathbf{F}$ has rank 2, so its kernel is one-dimensional and spanned by $\mathbf{e}$. **(b) Derivation of $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ (7 Marks):** 1. Let camera 1 have projection matrix $\mathbf{P}_1 = \mathbf{K}_1[\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]$ and camera 2 have $\mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{K}_2[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$. 2. The 3D ray from $C$ through $P$ in camera-1 coordinates is $\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{K}_1^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ (up to scale). 3. The same ray in camera-2 coordinates is $\mathbf{Q}(\lambda) = \lambda \mathbf{R}\mathbf{P}_c + \mathbf{t}$. 4. The image of this ray in camera 2 is a 1D line — the epipolar line. Projecting $\mathbf{Q}(\lambda)$ via $\mathbf{K}_2$ and using the **skew-symmetric cross-product trick** $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = [\mathbf{a}]_{\times} \mathbf{b}$, the epipolar line is: $$ \mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{K}_2^{-T} [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{K}_1^{-1} \mathbf{x} $$ 5. Define the **essential matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times}\mathbf{R}$ and the **fundamental matrix** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_2^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_1^{-1}$. Then $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}$. 6. Since the observed point $\mathbf{x}'$ lies on this line, $\mathbf{x}'^{T}\mathbf{l}' = 0$ gives: $$ \boxed{\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0} $$ 7. **Assumptions:** The cameras follow the pinhole model with known intrinsics, the scene is static, and the relative pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ is fixed. The fundamental matrix is independent of scene structure, depending only on the camera geometry. --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the relationship between the **essential matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ and the **fundamental matrix** $\mathbf{F}$. Why is $\mathbf{E}$ useful even though it requires calibration? (7 Marks) **(b)** With a clear algorithm, describe the **Eight-Point Algorithm** for estimating the fundamental matrix from point correspondences. State the rank-2 enforcement step. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Essential vs. Fundamental Matrix (7 Marks):** 1. The **essential matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times}\mathbf{R}$ is defined for the *calibrated* case, where image coordinates are first normalized: $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{x}$. 2. The **fundamental matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ is the projection of $\mathbf{E}$ into raw pixel coordinates: $$ \mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1} $$ 3. The constraints in each case: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0 \quad \text{(calibrated)}, \qquad \mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0 \quad \text{(uncalibrated)} $$ 4. **DOF comparison:** $\mathbf{E}$ has **5 DOF** (3 for $\mathbf{R}$, 2 for $\mathbf{t}$ up to scale), while $\mathbf{F}$ has **7 DOF** (5 from $\mathbf{E}$ + 2 from focal lengths, ignoring principal point and skew). 5. **Why $\mathbf{E}$ is useful despite requiring calibration:** With $\mathbf{E}$ we can directly recover $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ (up to a four-fold ambiguity) using SVD, which gives the *metric* 3D structure of the scene. $\mathbf{F}$ only gives *projective* reconstruction. Hence $\mathbf{E}$ is preferred in metric SfM, SLAM, and visual odometry. **(b) Eight-Point Algorithm (7 Marks):** **Input:** $N \geq 8$ point correspondences $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}'_i) = ((u_i, v_i, 1), (u'_i, v'_i, 1))\}_{i=1}^{N}$. **Output:** Estimated fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$. **Step 1:** For each correspondence, write the constraint $\mathbf{x}_i'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_i = 0$. Let $\mathbf{f} = (f_{11}, f_{12}, f_{13}, f_{21}, f_{22}, f_{23}, f_{31}, f_{32}, f_{33})^{T}$ be the vectorized form of $\mathbf{F}$. The $i$-th constraint becomes: $$ (u_i' u_i,\ u_i' v_i,\ u_i',\ v_i' u_i,\ v_i' v_i,\ v_i',\ u_i,\ v_i,\ 1)\ \mathbf{f} = 0 $$ **Step 2:** Stack $N$ such equations into the matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 9}$. **Step 3:** Compute the SVD of $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$. The solution is the right singular vector corresponding to the smallest singular value: $\mathbf{f} = \mathbf{V}_9$ (last column of $\mathbf{V}$). **Step 4:** Reshape $\mathbf{f}$ into the $3 \times 3$ matrix $\hat{\mathbf{F}}$. **Step 5 — Rank-2 Enforcement:** Compute the SVD of $\hat{\mathbf{F}} = \mathbf{U}_F \mathbf{\Sigma}_F \mathbf{V}_F^{T}$. The smallest singular value $\sigma_3$ is forced to zero, yielding $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{U}_F \ \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, 0)\ \mathbf{V}_F^{T}$. This enforces $\text{rank}(\mathbf{F}) = 2$. **Step 6 (Optional Normalization):** For numerical stability, Hartley’s normalized 8-point algorithm first normalizes the image coordinates to have zero mean and unit variance, then denormalizes $\mathbf{F}$ at the end. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024):** - **Epipolar constraint** restricts the matching point to a 1D line, slashing correspondence search from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$. - **Equation:** $\mathbf{x}'^{T} \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ (uncalibrated), $\hat{\mathbf{x}}'^{T} \mathbf{E} \hat{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ (calibrated). - **Fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$:** $3 \times 3$, **rank 2**, **7 DOF** (after scale and rank constraints). - **Essential matrix $\mathbf{E}$:** $3 \times 3$, **rank 2**, **5 DOF**. - **Linking relation:** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}'^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ and $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_{\times} \mathbf{R}$. - **Epipole** is the projection of the other camera's center. **Key identity:** $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{e}' = \mathbf{0}$. - **Epipolar line generation:** $\mathbf{l}' = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}$ (in image 2), $\mathbf{l} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{x}'$ (in image 1). - **Eight-point algorithm:** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{f} = 0$, solved by SVD; rank-2 enforcement via SVD thresholding. - **Normalize coordinates first** for numerical robustness (Hartley’s normalized 8-point). - **Conjugate translation:** $\mathbf{t}$ is defined up to scale; only the *direction* of translation is recoverable from $\mathbf{E}$. - **Triangulation** of 3D point $P$ uses the two rays $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{x}'$ back-projected to 3D. - **Practical use cases:** Stereo matching, SfM, Visual SLAM, autonomous driving, 3D reconstruction, augmented reality. - **Examiner's favorite question pattern:** "Define epipolar geometry. Derive $\mathbf{x}'^{T}\mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}=0$. State the eight-point algorithm." — Master this triad.

topics.title: Binocular Reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Binocular Reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binocular Reconstruction** (also called **Stereo Reconstruction** or **Two-View 3D Reconstruction**) is a foundational sub-discipline of computer vision that aims to recover the three-dimensional geometric structure of a scene from two or more two-dimensional images captured from distinct viewpoints. The principle is rooted in **multi-view geometry**, where the displacement of a scene point between the two camera views — known as **disparity** — is inversely proportional to its depth in the world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Binocular reconstruction is the process of inferring the 3D coordinates of scene points by exploiting the geometric relationship between two calibrated (or partially calibrated) cameras observing the same scene from slightly different positions, leveraging concepts such as epipolar geometry, triangulation, and disparity-based depth estimation.*

Mathematically, if $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^\top$ is a 3D world point and $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$ are its projections in the left and right camera images respectively, then binocular reconstruction seeks to recover $\mathbf{X}$ (up to a similarity transform) given the intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters and the pair $(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Eye

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: How Human Eyes See Depth**

Imagine you are holding a pencil in front of your face and alternately closing one eye and then the other. Notice that the pencil appears to "jump" horizontally against the background. This horizontal shift is exactly what we call **disparity**.

Now, your brain does something remarkable — it instantly uses this tiny horizontal shift to compute *how far away the pencil is*. The **larger the shift, the closer the object**. The **smaller the shift, the farther the object**.

**Binocular reconstruction replicates this exact biological mechanism using two cameras:**
- The two cameras = your two eyes
- The "horizontal jump" of corresponding points = **disparity**
- The brain's depth calculation = **triangulation**

If you close one eye and try to thread a needle, it becomes much harder. This is **monocular vision**, where depth cues (perspective, shading, known sizes) are weaker. Two cameras (binocular) give **strong, metric depth** from pure geometry.

## 1.3 Why Binocular Reconstruction Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Tesla, Waymo use stereo cameras to estimate distances to obstacles.
> 2. **Robotic Navigation** — SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) pipelines.
> 3. **Medical Imaging** — Endoscopic 3D reconstruction during minimally invasive surgery.
> 4. **Industrial Metrology** — Reverse engineering and quality control.
> 5. **Augmented Reality** — Inserting virtual objects with correct occlusion in 3D.
> 6. **Photogrammetry & Drones** — Generating 3D terrain models from aerial image pairs.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Disparity** | Pixel difference $d = x_L - x_R$ between corresponding points |
| **Baseline** $b$ | Physical distance between the two camera centers |
| **Focal length** $f$ | Intrinsic camera parameter (in pixels or mm) |
| **Depth** $Z$ | Perpendicular distance from camera to scene point |
| **Epipolar Line** | Locus of possible matches in the other image |
| **Triangulation** | Computing 3D point from two 2D observations |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Binocular Reconstruction Pipeline

The complete process of recovering 3D from two images follows a strict sequence of operations. Each stage is essential, and skipping a step typically results in catastrophic failures in reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Pipeline (Memorize This Order):**

1. **Image Acquisition** — Capture the same scene with two cameras at known relative positions.
2. **Camera Calibration** — Determine intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ and extrinsic $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ for both cameras.
3. **Epipolar Geometry Computation** — Estimate the **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ (uncalibrated) or **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ (calibrated).
4. **Rectification** — Transform both images so that corresponding points lie on the same horizontal scanline, dramatically simplifying the search.
5. **Stereo Correspondence (Matching)** — For each pixel in the left image, find its corresponding pixel in the right image → produces a **disparity map** $d(u, v)$.
6. **Triangulation / Depth Recovery** — Convert disparity into 3D world coordinates using the pinhole projection equations.
7. **Refinement & Surface Generation** — Optional filtering, outlier rejection, point cloud densification, mesh generation.

## 2.2 Geometric Foundation — The Pinhole Stereo Model

Consider two cameras with identical intrinsics, perfectly aligned (rectified), and separated by a pure horizontal translation $b$ (the **baseline**). This is called a **canonical stereo configuration** and is the standard reference setup for deriving the core binocular equations.

Let the left camera be at world origin with optical axis along $+Z$. The right camera is translated by $(b, 0, 0)$. Both share the same intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$.

For a 3D point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^\top$:

- **Left image projection:** $x_L = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$, $\quad y_L = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$
- **Right image projection:** $x_R = f \cdot \dfrac{(X - b)}{Z}$, $\quad y_R = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$

Since the cameras are rectified, $y_L = y_R$ (epipolar lines are horizontal), and the **disparity** is:

$$d = x_L - x_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z} = f \cdot \frac{b}{Z}$$

This single, elegant relation is the **heart of binocular reconstruction**.

## 2.3 Depth Recovery — The Master Equation

Solving for depth $Z$:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Proportionality Principle:**
> - When $d$ is **large** → object is **close** (small $Z$).
> - When $d$ is **small** → object is **far** (large $Z$).
> - When $d = 0$ → object is at **infinity** (parallel rays).

The lateral 3D position of the point in the left camera coordinate frame is:

$$X = \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f}, \qquad Y = \frac{y_L \cdot Z}{f}$$

## 2.4 Depth Resolution and the Disparity Uncertainty

In real systems, disparity is quantized to integer pixel values. If disparity can be measured to an accuracy of $\delta d$ (typically $\delta d = 1$ pixel), the depth resolution at distance $Z$ becomes:

$$\delta Z = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \delta d}{f \cdot b}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — The "Near-Far Ambiguity":**
> Depth error grows **quadratically** with $Z$. A small $\delta d$ causes large $\delta Z$ at long range. Doubling the baseline $b$ halves the depth error. Doubling the focal length $f$ halves the depth error. Increasing pixel resolution (smaller $\delta d$) directly improves depth accuracy.

## 2.5 Epipolar Geometry — The Uncalibrated Case

For two **uncalibrated** cameras with general relative motion, the geometric relationship between corresponding image points $\mathbf{x}_L$ and $\mathbf{x}_R$ is encoded in the **Fundamental Matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ ($3 \times 3$, rank 2):

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_R^\top \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{x}_L = 0}$$

If the cameras are calibrated (i.e., we can normalize image points via $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$), we obtain the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$:

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_R^\top \mathbf{E} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_L = 0, \qquad \mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}}$$

where $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$ is the **skew-symmetric cross-product matrix** of the translation vector, and $\mathbf{R}$ is the rotation between the two cameras.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Epipolar Constraint Intuition:**
> Given a point in the left image, its match in the right image is **not** anywhere — it must lie on a specific line called the **epipolar line**. This reduces the 2D search problem to a 1D search along that line, making correspondence tractable.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Symbol Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = x_L - x_R$ | Disparity (horizontal pixel shift) | pixels |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$ | Depth from disparity | meters (or mm) |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{x_L \cdot Z}{f}$ | Lateral 3D coordinate | meters |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{y_L \cdot Z}{f}$ | Vertical 3D coordinate | meters |
| 5 | $\delta Z = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \delta d}{f \cdot b}$ | Depth resolution at distance $Z$ | meters |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x}_R^\top \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ | Fundamental matrix constraint | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ | Essential matrix from extrinsics | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_R^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_L^{-1}$ | Calibration relationship | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}$ | Pinhole projection equation | pixels |
| 10 | Reprojection error $e = \sum_i \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \pi(\mathbf{X}_i, \mathbf{C}) \rVert^2$ | Bundle adjustment objective | pixels² |

> **Notation Reminder:** $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes the Euclidean $L^2$ norm; $\mathbf{K}^{-T} = (\mathbf{K}^{-1})^\top = (\mathbf{K}^\top)^{-1}$.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Equations

| Field | Application | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous driving | Forward collision warning | $Z$ from stereo |
| Robotics | Pick-and-place grasping | $X, Y, Z$ of object |
| AR/VR | Occlusion-correct compositing | Depth buffer |
| Drones | Aerial terrain mapping | $\delta Z$ over range |
| Film industry | Multi-camera 3D films | Baseline $b$ tuning |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule of Thumb:**
> For a stereo system to detect an object at distance $Z$ with 1% depth accuracy, the required baseline is approximately $b \approx \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot \delta d}{100 \cdot f}$. Engineers tune $b$ to balance field-of-view overlap with depth accuracy at the operating range.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth from Disparity (Canonical Rectified Stereo)

This is the **single most important derivation** in binocular reconstruction. Every KTU paper will expect it.

### Setup
- Two pinhole cameras with identical intrinsics.
- Cameras are **rectified** so optical axes are parallel and share the same $Y$-axis.
- Baseline $b$ is purely along the $X$-axis of the left camera.
- Let the world point be $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^\top$ in the left camera coordinate frame.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Project the point into the left image.** Using the standard pinhole projection (without principal point offset for clarity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_L &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\
y_L &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The pinhole camera maps a 3D point by drawing a line from the camera center through $\mathbf{X}$ and intersecting the image plane. The ratio of lateral position to depth is the tangent of the projection angle; with a focal length $f$ scaling factor, the projected image coordinates are $\bigl(f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}\bigr)$.

**Step 2 — Express the same point in the right camera frame.** Since the right camera is translated by $(b, 0, 0)$ relative to the left, and assuming no rotation (rectified), the coordinates of $\mathbf{X}$ in the right camera frame are:

$$\mathbf{X}_{R} = \mathbf{X} - (b, 0, 0)^\top = (X - b,\ Y,\ Z)^\top$$

*Explanation:* The right camera's optical center sits $b$ units to the right of the left camera's optical center. To express the same world point in the right camera's coordinate system, we subtract this translation.

**Step 3 — Project the point into the right image.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_R &= f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z} \\
y_R &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Same pinhole formula applied to the right camera's coordinate frame.

**Step 4 — Observe that $y_L = y_R$.** This confirms the **rectification property** — corresponding points lie on the same scanline (same row), so the search for matches is purely 1D horizontal.

**Step 5 — Define disparity and subtract.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\
  &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z} \\
  &= f \cdot \frac{X - (X - b)}{Z} \\
  &= f \cdot \frac{b}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The $X$ terms cancel perfectly, leaving only the baseline $b$ in the numerator. This is the source of the elegant simplicity — horizontal baseline directly produces a clean disparity proportional to $1/Z$.

**Step 6 — Solve for $Z$ (depth).**

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}}$$

*Explanation:* Multiplying both sides by $Z$ and dividing by $d$ gives depth as the ratio of the **depth-factor constant** $f \cdot b$ to the measured disparity $d$.

**Step 7 — Solve for the lateral coordinates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{x_L \cdot b}{d} \\
Y &= \frac{y_L \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{y_L \cdot b}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Substituting $Z = f \cdot b / d$ into the projection equations $X = x_L \cdot Z / f$ and $Y = y_L \cdot Z / f$ eliminates the focal length and yields coordinates purely as a function of pixel position and disparity.

### Final Result
A pixel at $(x_L, y_L)$ with disparity $d$ in a rectified stereo pair with focal length $f$ and baseline $b$ corresponds to a 3D point:

$$\mathbf{X} = \left( \frac{x_L \cdot b}{d},\ \frac{y_L \cdot b}{d},\ \frac{f \cdot b}{d} \right)^\top$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Depth Resolution from Disparity Quantization

In practical systems, disparity is an integer (pixel units), so $\delta d \geq 1$. We compute the resulting error in $Z$.

**Step 1 — Start with the depth equation.**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Take the partial derivative of $Z$ with respect to $d$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left( f \cdot b \cdot d^{-1} \right) \\
                              &= -f \cdot b \cdot d^{-2} \\
                              &= -\frac{f \cdot b}{d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The derivative of $d^{-1}$ is $-d^{-2}$. The constant $f \cdot b$ is pulled out unchanged.

**Step 3 — Express in terms of $Z$ using $d = f \cdot b / Z$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} &= -\frac{f \cdot b}{(f \cdot b / Z)^2} \\
                              &= -\frac{f \cdot b \cdot Z^2}{(f \cdot b)^2} \\
                              &= -\frac{Z^2}{f \cdot b}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Substitute $d^2 = (f b)^2 / Z^2$ into the denominator; one factor of $f b$ cancels.

**Step 4 — Write the absolute depth error.**

$$\boxed{\delta Z = \left\vert \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot b} \right\vert \cdot \delta d = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \delta d}{f \cdot b}}$$

*Explanation:* The magnitude of the derivative gives the sensitivity. Multiply by the disparity uncertainty $\delta d$ (typically 1 pixel) to get the absolute depth uncertainty.

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The error grows **quadratically with $Z$**: doubling the distance quadruples the depth error. This is why far-field stereo is fundamentally less accurate than near-field stereo.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Epipolar Constraint and Essential Matrix

We derive the famous $\mathbf{x}_R^\top \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$ relation.

**Step 1 — World point in left and right camera frames.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_L &= \mathbf{X} \\
\mathbf{X}_R &= \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The right camera is related to the left by a rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$.

**Step 2 — Apply inverse transformation to bring right point into left frame.**

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{R}^\top (\mathbf{X}_R - \mathbf{t})$$

*Explanation:* Inverting the rigid transform: subtract $\mathbf{t}$, then rotate back by $\mathbf{R}^\top$.

**Step 3 — All rays from the camera centers pass through $\mathbf{X}$.** This gives the **coplanarity constraint** of $\mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{C}_L$, $\mathbf{C}_R$, with the baseline vector $\mathbf{t}$:

$$(\mathbf{X}_L - \mathbf{X}_R) \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{X}_R) = 0$$

*Explanation:* The triple product being zero means the three vectors are coplanar.

**Step 4 — Substitute normalized image coordinates.** Let $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L = \mathbf{X}_L / Z_L$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_R = \mathbf{X}_R / Z_R$ (the rays in normalized image space). Then:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L^\top \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{R} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_R) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the skew-symmetric matrix.** For any vector $\mathbf{t}$, the cross product $\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{v} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{v}$, where:

$$[\mathbf{t}]_\times = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -t_3 & t_2 \\ t_3 & 0 & -t_1 \\ -t_2 & t_1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

*Explanation:* This is a $3 \times 3$ antisymmetric matrix that performs the cross product via matrix multiplication.

**Step 6 — Define the Essential Matrix.**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}}$$

Then the constraint becomes:

$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L^\top \, \mathbf{E} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_R = 0}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Essential Matrix constraint** — a $3 \times 3$ matrix encoding all relative motion between the two calibrated cameras.

**Step 7 — Fundamental Matrix for uncalibrated cameras.** With $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_L^\top \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_R^{-1} \mathbf{x}_R = 0$$

Defining $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x}_L^\top \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_R = 0}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solving Common KTU Numerical Problems

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference Numerical Problem Solver**

| Given | Find | Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| $f, b, d$ | $Z$ | $Z = f b / d$ |
| $f, b, Z$ | $d$ | $d = f b / Z$ |
| $f, b, Z, \delta d$ | $\delta Z$ | $\delta Z = Z^2 \delta d / (f b)$ |
| $Z, \delta Z, f$ | Required $b$ for accuracy | $b = Z^2 \delta d / (f \cdot \delta Z)$ |
| $x_L, y_L, d, f, b$ | 3D point | $X = x_L b/d$, $Y = y_L b/d$, $Z = f b/d$ |
| $Z_{min}, Z_{max}, f$ | Range of $d$ values | $d_{min} = f b / Z_{max}$, $d_{max} = f b / Z_{min}$ |

### Worked Example (for pattern reference)

**Problem:** A stereo rig has $f = 800$ pixels, $b = 120$ mm. A pixel $(x_L, y_L) = (400, 300)$ matches a right-image pixel at $(370, 300)$.

**Step 1:** Compute disparity: $d = 400 - 370 = 30$ pixels.

**Step 2:** Compute depth: $Z = f \cdot b / d = 800 \times 120 / 30 = 3200$ mm $= 3.2$ m.

**Step 3:** Compute lateral coordinates: $X = x_L \cdot b / d = 400 \times 120 / 30 = 1600$ mm, $Y = y_L \cdot b / d = 300 \times 120 / 30 = 1200$ mm.

**Step 4:** Final 3D point: $\mathbf{X} = (1.6, 1.2, 3.2)$ m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Binocular Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene Point X] --> B[Left Camera]
    A --> C[Right Camera]
    B --> D[Left Image xL]
    C --> E[Right Image xR]
    D --> F[Camera Calibration K R t]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Epipolar Geometry: F or E]
    G --> H[Rectification]
    H --> I[Feature Detection and Matching]
    I --> J[Disparity Map d u v]
    J --> K[Triangulation Z = f b over d]
    K --> L[3D Point Cloud X Y Z]
    L --> M[Surface Reconstruction and Refinement]
    M --> N[Final 3D Model]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style N fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style J fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Canonical Rectified Stereo Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CL[Left Camera Center OL] -->|f| IL[Image Plane Left]
    CR[Right Camera Center OR] -->|f| IR[Image Plane Right]
    CL -->|baseline b| CR
    X[World Point X] -->|ray 1| IL
    X -->|ray 2| IR
    XL[Image point xL] -->|d xL minus xR| XR[Image point xR]
    style X fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style CL fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333
    style CR fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Epipolar Geometry — Constraint Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Left Image Point xL] --> Q1[Epipolar Line in Right Image]
    Q1 --> R1[Reduce 2D Search to 1D Line]
    P2[Right Image Point xR] --> Q2[Epipolar Line in Left Image]
    Q2 --> R1
    F[Fundamental Matrix F 3x3 rank 2] --> Q1
    F --> Q2
    style F fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style R1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Triangulation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Match xL in left image] --> B[Match xR in right image]
    B --> C[Compute disparity d = xL minus xR]
    C --> D{Is d equals 0}
    D -->|Yes| E[Point at infinity: Z tends to infinity]
    D -->|No| F[Z = f b divided by d]
    F --> G[X = xL b divided by d]
    F --> H[Y = yL b divided by d]
    G --> I[3D Point Cloud]
    H --> I
    E --> I
    style F fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#d0e0e3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Correspondence Search — Rectified vs Unrectified

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UNRECT[Unrectified Case]
    U1[Pixel in left image] --> U2[Search 2D region in right image]
    U2 --> U3[Computationally expensive]
    end
    subgraph RECT[Rectified Case]
    R1[Pixel in left image] --> R2[Search 1D horizontal scanline]
    R2 --> R3[Fast and tractable]
    end
    style UNRECT fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#333
    style RECT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#333
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define binocular reconstruction in computer vision. What is the role of disparity in recovering depth?**

**Model Answer:**

Binocular reconstruction is the process of recovering the 3D structure of a scene from two images captured by cameras at slightly different viewpoints, using the geometric principle of triangulation.

**Role of disparity:**
- Disparity $d = x_L - x_R$ is the horizontal pixel shift of a scene point between the left and right images.
- It is **inversely proportional** to depth: $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$.
- Greater disparity means the point is closer; smaller disparity means farther.
- Disparity is the **fundamental observable** that links 2D image measurements to 3D depth.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: What is the epipolar constraint? Write the mathematical form of the essential matrix constraint.**

**Model Answer:**

The epipolar constraint states that for any point in one image, its corresponding point in the other image must lie on a specific line called the **epipolar line**. This restricts the search for correspondences from a 2D region to a 1D line, drastically reducing computational cost.

**Mathematical form (Essential Matrix):**
For two calibrated cameras with normalized image points $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L$ and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_R$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L^\top \, \mathbf{E} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_R = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$$

The corresponding Fundamental Matrix form (uncalibrated):

$$\mathbf{x}_L^\top \, \mathbf{F} \, \mathbf{x}_R = 0$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the canonical rectified stereo imaging geometry. Derive the relationship between depth $Z$ and disparity $d$ for a stereo pair with focal length $f$ and baseline $b$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stereo camera system has a focal length of $f = 1000$ pixels and a baseline of $b = 200$ mm. For a scene point whose image coordinates are $x_L = 600$, $y_L = 400$ in the left image and $x_R = 580$ in the right image, calculate:
1. The disparity $d$.
2. The depth $Z$ of the point.
3. The 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$.
4. The depth error $\delta Z$ if the disparity measurement has an uncertainty of $\delta d = 1$ pixel.

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is epipolar geometry? Derive the essential matrix constraint for two calibrated cameras. Explain how the fundamental matrix differs from the essential matrix.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of rectification in stereo vision. Why is correspondence matching a 2D problem before rectification and a 1D problem after rectification? Mention two popular stereo correspondence algorithms.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions to Part B

### Solution to Question A (a)

**Canonical Rectified Stereo Geometry:**

In a canonical stereo setup:
- Two cameras with identical intrinsics (same $f$, same principal point).
- Optical axes are **parallel**.
- The two image planes are **coplanar** and aligned so that corresponding scanlines are horizontal.
- The right camera is translated by baseline $b$ along the $X$-axis of the left camera.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):** Two cameras separated by $b$, with parallel optical axes, observing the same 3D point $\mathbf{X}$ at depth $Z$.

**Derivation:**

Projecting $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^\top$ into both images:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_L &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} \\
x_R &= f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z}, \quad y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing disparity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
d = x_L - x_R &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z} \\
              &= f \cdot \frac{b}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $Z$:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}}$$

**Key observation:** Depth is **inversely proportional** to disparity. The product $f \cdot b$ is called the **stereo constant** of the system.

---

### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** $f = 1000$ pixels, $b = 200$ mm, $x_L = 600$, $y_L = 400$, $x_R = 580$, $\delta d = 1$ pixel.

**Part 1 — Disparity:**
$$d = x_L - x_R = 600 - 580 = 20 \text{ pixels}$$

**Part 2 — Depth:**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d} = \frac{1000 \times 200}{20} = 10000 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Part 3 — 3D Coordinates:**
$$X = \frac{x_L \cdot b}{d} = \frac{600 \times 200}{20} = 6000 \text{ mm} = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

$$Y = \frac{y_L \cdot b}{d} = \frac{400 \times 200}{20} = 4000 \text{ mm} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\mathbf{X} = (6.0,\ 4.0,\ 10.0) \text{ m}$$

**Part 4 — Depth Error:**
$$\delta Z = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \delta d}{f \cdot b} = \frac{(10000)^2 \times 1}{1000 \times 200} = \frac{10^8}{2 \times 10^5} = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

So the depth estimate is $Z = 10.0 \pm 0.5$ m, i.e., $\pm 5\%$ relative error.

---

### Solution to Question B (a)

**Epipolar Geometry:**
Epipolar geometry is the intrinsic projective geometry between two views. It describes the relationship between corresponding image points in terms of the relative camera motion, independent of the scene structure. The key elements are:
- **Epipoles** $e_L, e_R$ — the projection of one camera's center into the other image.
- **Epipolar lines** — the locus of possible matches for a given point in the other image.
- **Epipolar plane** — the plane containing the two camera centers and the 3D point.

**Derivation of Essential Matrix Constraint:**

For two cameras related by rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}_R &= \mathbf{R} \mathbf{X}_L + \mathbf{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

The coplanarity of $\mathbf{X}_L$, $\mathbf{X}_R$, and the baseline vector $\mathbf{t}$ gives:
$$(\mathbf{X}_L - \mathbf{X}_R) \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{X}_R) = 0$$

In normalized image coordinates ($\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{X} / Z$), and using $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$:
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L^\top \, [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_R = 0$$

Defining $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$:
$$\boxed{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_L^\top \, \mathbf{E} \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_R = 0}$$

**Essential vs Fundamental Matrix:**

| Feature | Essential Matrix $\mathbf{E}$ | Fundamental Matrix $\mathbf{F}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Camera calibration | **Required** | Not required |
| Coordinates used | **Normalized** image coords | Raw pixel coords |
| Relationship | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}_L^{-\top} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}_R^{-1}$ |
| Rank | 2 | 2 |
| DOF | 5 (3 for R, 2 for t) | 7 (5 from E + intrinsics) |
| Reconstruction scale | Metric | Up to projective |

---

### Solution to Question B (b)

**Role of Rectification:**

Rectification is the process of applying 2D projective transformations (homographies) to both images of a stereo pair so that:
1. Corresponding epipolar lines become **horizontal** and aligned.
2. Corresponding points lie on the **same row** (same $y$-coordinate).
3. The search for correspondences is restricted to a **single 1D scanline**.

**Why Correspondence Matching Becomes 1D After Rectification:**

**Before Rectification:** For a pixel at $(u_L, v_L)$ in the left image, its match in the right image lies somewhere on an **arbitrarily-oriented epipolar line**, which itself must be computed from the fundamental matrix. The search region is the entire image (2D search, $O(N^2)$ complexity).

**After Rectification:** Because the images are warped so that epipolar lines are horizontal and aligned to image rows, the search reduces to finding the matching pixel **on the same row** in the right image. This is a 1D search, reducing complexity to $O(N)$ per row.

**Mathematical Justification:** For rectified cameras, $y_L = y_R$ (the $v$-coordinates are equal), so the matching function is purely $d = u_L - u_R$.

**Two Popular Stereo Correspondence Algorithms:**

1. **Block Matching (Sum of Absolute Differences — SAD):** Compares a small window around each pixel in the left image with windows along the corresponding scanline in the right image; the location of minimum cost gives the disparity. Fast but sensitive to textureless regions.

2. **Semi-Global Matching (SGM):** Performs multiple 1D cost aggregations along several directions (typically 8–16) through the image, enforcing smoothness constraints. Provides dense, accurate disparity maps and is the industry standard (used in autonomous vehicles).

Other notable methods: **Dynamic Programming stereo**, **Graph Cuts**, **Belief Propagation**, and **deep learning-based networks** (e.g., PSMNet, RAFT-Stereo).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks — Examiner's Pitfall List:**

1. **Forgetting the Rectification Assumption** — The simple formula $Z = f b / d$ is **only valid for rectified cameras**. In a non-rectified pair, the same $y$-coordinate does not hold, and you must use full triangulation with $\mathbf{E}$ or $\mathbf{F}$. *(−2 to −3 marks)*

2. **Unit Inconsistency in $f$ and $b$** — If $f$ is in pixels and $b$ is in mm, the resulting $Z$ will be in mm (since $d$ is dimensionless pixels). Mixing $f$ in mm and $b$ in pixels gives nonsensical units. **Always state units.** *(−1 to −2 marks)*

3. **Sign Error in Disparity** — Some textbooks define $d = x_R - x_L$ (right minus left). The KTU convention is $d = x_L - x_R$ for left camera as reference. Mixing signs flips depth sign. *(−2 marks)*

4. **Skipping the Derivation Steps** — KTU examiners **demand explicit algebraic steps**, not just the final formula. Saying "$Z = f b / d$" without derivation gets only **partial credit**. Always show at least 4–5 intermediate steps. *(−3 to −5 marks)*

5. **Confusing Essential and Fundamental Matrix** — Using $\mathbf{F}$ without calibration context, or vice versa, signals incomplete understanding. Always specify which matrix applies. *(−2 marks)*
