topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dataset for Machine Perception: Labelled and Un-Labelled Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Dataset for Machine Perception?

In the context of **Foundations of Computer Vision (PECDT743)**, a *dataset for machine perception* is a curated, structured collection of sensory samples (images, videos, depth maps, LiDAR point clouds, audio-visual streams) together with optional auxiliary information (annotations, metadata, sensor calibration parameters) that is used to train, validate, and benchmark a machine learning model — most commonly a deep neural network — to perceive, interpret, and reason about visual input from the physical world.

A single datum (one row of the dataset) is mathematically represented as a tuple:

$$
(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i, m_i) \quad \text{where } i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ — the input tensor (height × width × channels, e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$ RGB).
- $y_i$ — the label/annotation (could be a scalar class id, a bounding box vector, a segmentation mask, or absent entirely).
- $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ — a *mask flag* indicating whether a label is present for sample $i$.
- $N$ — the total number of samples in the dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The two fundamental dataset paradigms in machine perception are **(a) Labelled Data** (used for supervised learning) and **(b) Un-Labelled Data** (used for unsupervised, self-supervised, and semi-supervised learning). Choosing the right paradigm dictates the entire downstream model pipeline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flashcard vs. The Photo Album"

Imagine you are teaching a child to recognize a *cat*:

| Scenario | Analogy | Dataset Type |
|---|---|---|
| You show 1000 photos and **say "this is a cat"** each time | Supervised flashcards with answers on the back | **Labelled Data** |
| You give the child a 1000-photo album of animals **with no names** and let them group similar animals | Self-discovery / clustering | **Un-Labelled Data** |
| You show 100 labelled flashcards + 900 unlabelled photos and ask the child to combine both | Mixed learning strategy | **Semi-Supervised Data** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Labelled data is *expensive but informative*; un-labelled data is *cheap and abundant*. The entire modern computer vision ecosystem (CNNs, Transformers, foundation models) lives or dies by the *quality, quantity, and labelling discipline* of its underlying dataset.

---

### 1.3 Formal Classification of Datasets for Machine Perception

Datasets in computer vision can be partitioned into the following canonical categories:

1. **Labelled (Annotated) Datasets** — $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}$
2. **Un-Labelled (Raw) Datasets** — $\mathcal{D}_U = \{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=1}^{N_U}$
3. **Semi-Supervised Datasets** — $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_L \cup \mathcal{D}_U$, where typically $N_L \ll N_U$
4. **Weakly-Supervised Datasets** — labels are coarse, noisy, or incomplete (e.g., only image-level tags instead of pixel masks)
5. **Self-Supervised Datasets** — pseudo-labels are *automatically generated* from the data itself (e.g., rotation angle prediction, masked patch reconstruction)

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Reality Check:** Modern computer vision systems such as **CLIP (OpenAI)**, **DINOv2 (Meta)**, and **Segment Anything (SAM)** are pre-trained on **billions of un-labelled images** scraped from the web, then *fine-tuned* on comparatively tiny labelled datasets. This is the dominant paradigm in 2024–2026.

---

### 1.4 Definition — Labelled Data

**Labelled data** for machine perception is a set of input-output pairs in which each raw sensory sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ is paired with a **human-verified (or algorithmically generated and human-verified) semantic annotation** $y_i$ drawn from a predefined label space $\mathcal{Y}$.

Formally:

$$
\mathcal{D}_L = \big\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i) \;\big|\; \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{X}, \; y_i \in \mathcal{Y}, \; i = 1, 2, \dots, N_L \big\}
$$

The label space $\mathcal{Y}$ depends on the perception task:

$$
\mathcal{Y} =
\begin{cases}
\{1, 2, \dots, K\} & \text{(image classification)} \\
\mathbb{R}^{4} & \text{(2D bounding box: } [x, y, w, h]) \\
\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times K} & \text{(semantic segmentation mask)} \\
\mathbb{R}^{17 \times 3} & \text{(human keypoints - e.g., COCO 17-keypoint format)} \\
\text{Caption string} & \text{(image captioning)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Annotation modalities in labelled CV datasets:**

- **Image-level labels** — single class per image (e.g., ImageNet).
- **Bounding boxes** — rectangular object localizations (e.g., PASCAL VOC, COCO).
- **Pixel-level masks** — dense per-pixel class assignment (e.g., Cityscapes).
- **Polygonal / instance masks** — per-object contour traces.
- **3D cuboids / point clouds** — for autonomous driving (e.g., nuScenes, KITTI).
- **Optical flow fields** — pixel displacement vectors between frames.
- **Depth maps** — per-pixel distance from camera.
- **Action / event labels** — temporal annotations on video.
- **Linguistic captions** — natural-language descriptions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Annotation Cost Rule of Thumb (Industry Benchmark 2024):**
> - Image classification label ≈ **\$0.01 – \$0.05** per image
> - Bounding box label ≈ **\$0.05 – \$0.20** per object
> - Pixel-level semantic mask ≈ **\$0.50 – \$5.00** per image
> - 3D point cloud semantic + instance label ≈ **\$5.00 – \$50.00** per frame
>
> This cost gradient is the **primary economic driver** behind the rise of un-labelled and self-supervised methods.

---

### 1.5 Definition — Un-Labelled Data

**Un-labelled data** is a collection of raw perceptual samples $\mathbf{x}_j$ drawn from the underlying data distribution $p(\mathbf{x})$, with **no corresponding ground-truth annotation**. The dataset is formally expressed as:

$$
\mathcal{D}_U = \{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=1}^{N_U}, \quad \mathbf{x}_j \sim p(\mathbf{x})
$$

where $p(\mathbf{x})$ is the (unknown) true distribution of visual stimuli in the environment the model is intended to operate in.

**Key properties of un-labelled data:**

- **Abundance:** $N_U$ can be effectively unbounded (e.g., all images on the open web, all CCTV footage, all sensor logs).
- **Zero annotation cost:** no human labelers required.
- **Captures real distribution:** if sampled i.i.d., it represents $p(\mathbf{x})$ faithfully.
- **Requires clever pretext tasks** to extract learning signal (since no $y$ is provided).

**Self-supervised pretext tasks used to mine un-labelled data:**

| Pretext Task | What the model predicts | Loss formulation |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation prediction | Angle $\theta \in \{0°, 90°, 180°, 270°\}$ | $\mathcal{L}_{rot} = -\sum_{k} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ |
| Jigsaw puzzle | Permutation index of shuffled patches | Cross-entropy over permutations |
| Masked Autoencoding | Reconstruct masked image patches | $\mathcal{L}_{MAE} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m \in \mathcal{M}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_m - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_m \Vert_2^2$ |
| Contrastive (SimCLR, MoCo) | Whether two augmentations are from same image | InfoNCE loss |
| DINO (self-distillation) | Match student/teacher view distributions | $\mathcal{L}_{DINO} = -\sum P_t \log P_s$ |

---

### 1.6 Definition — Semi-Supervised and Weakly-Supervised Data

A **semi-supervised dataset** combines both:

$$
\mathcal{D}_{SSL} = \underbrace{\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}}_{\text{small labelled set}} \cup \underbrace{\{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=N_L+1}^{N_L+N_U}}_{\text{large un-labelled set}}
$$

with the typical constraint $N_L \ll N_U$ (often $N_L / (N_L+N_U) < 0.1$).

A **weakly-supervised dataset** has labels that are present but **coarser, noisier, or less precise** than the desired output — for example, only image-level "cat" labels when the model is expected to produce pixel-level cat masks.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Supervised Learning Objective (Labelled Data)

Given a labelled dataset $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}$, the standard empirical risk minimization (ERM) objective used to train a parameterized model $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ is:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{ERM} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \frac{1}{N_L} \sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i),\, y_i\big) \;+\; \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}(\cdot, \cdot)$ — task-specific loss function (cross-entropy, MSE, IoU loss, focal loss, etc.).
- $\Omega(\theta)$ — regularizer (L2 weight decay, L1 sparsity, dropout).
- $\lambda$ — regularization coefficient (a hyperparameter).

For multi-class classification with $K$ classes, the categorical cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \, \log \frac{\exp(z_{i,k})}{\sum_{c=1}^{K} \exp(z_{i,c})}
$$

where $z_{i,k} = f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i)_k$ is the logit for class $k$ and $y_{i,k} \in \{0,1\}$ is the one-hot indicator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why labels matter mathematically:** The gradient of the loss with respect to model parameters depends on the residual $(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i)$. Without $y_i$, this gradient signal is undefined and the model cannot learn via standard backpropagation. Hence un-labelled data requires a **proxy task** (pretext task) to fabricate a learning signal.

---

### 2.2 The Self-Supervised Learning Objective (Un-Labelled Data)

For un-labelled data, a *pretext task* $T$ maps an input to a *pseudo-label* automatically. The general self-supervised objective is:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{SSL} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \; \frac{1}{N_U} \sum_{j=1}^{N_U} \mathcal{L}_{pretext}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j),\, T(\mathbf{x}_j)\big)
$$

The pretext $T(\cdot)$ is a hand-crafted or learned function that does **not require human annotation**. Common choices:

- $T_{rot}(\mathbf{x}) = \theta \in \{0°, 90°, 180°, 270°\}$ — predict applied rotation.
- $T_{jigsaw}(\mathbf{x}) = \pi$ — predict permutation of tiled patches.
- $T_{MAE}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}_{\setminus \mathcal{M}}$ — reconstruct masked patches.

The resulting pre-trained features $\hat{\theta}_{SSL}$ are then **transferred** to downstream tasks via fine-tuning on small labelled sets:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{final} = \arg\min_{\theta_0 = \hat{\theta}_{SSL}} \; \frac{1}{N_L^{down}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_L^{down}} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i^{down}),\, y_i^{down}\big)
$$

---

### 2.3 The Semi-Supervised Learning Objective (Mixed Data)

A canonical SSL formulation is the **consistency-regularization + pseudo-labelling** hybrid:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{SSL} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}_{CE}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i),\, y_i\big)}_{\text{supervised loss on labelled set}} \;+\; \underbrace{\lambda_u \frac{1}{N_U}\sum_{j=1}^{N_U} \mathcal{L}_{cons}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j^{aug1}),\, f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_j^{aug2})\big)}_{\text{consistency loss on un-labelled set}}
$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{cons}$ is typically MSE between predictions on two stochastic augmentations of the same un-labelled image, and $\lambda_u$ ramps up during training (FixMatch, MixMatch, MeanTeacher).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Dataset tuple | $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i, m_i)$ with mask $m_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $m_i = 0$ if un-labelled |
| Labelled set | $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}$ | Requires annotation |
| Un-labelled set | $\mathcal{D}_U = \{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=1}^{N_U}$ | $\mathbf{x}_j \sim p(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Empirical risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i)$ | Finite-sample proxy for true risk |
| True risk | $R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p(x,y)}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)]$ | Inaccessible, estimated via ERM |
| Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | Multi-class, with $\hat{y}_k \in [0,1]$ |
| InfoNCE loss (contrastive) | $\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE} = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_i^+)/\tau)}{\sum_k \exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_k)/\tau)}$ | $\tau$ = temperature hyperparameter |
| Consistency loss | $\mathcal{L}_{cons} = \Vert f_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{aug1}) - f_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{aug2}) \Vert_2^2$ | MeanTeacher, FixMatch |
| MAE reconstruction loss | $\mathcal{L}_{MAE} = \frac{1}{\vert\mathcal{M}\vert}\sum_{m\in\mathcal{M}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_m - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_m \Vert_2^2$ | $\mathcal{M}$ = set of masked patch indices |
| Dataset bias metric | $\Delta = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{test}}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}),y)] - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{train}}[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}),y)]$ | Measures train/test distribution shift |
| Labelling cost ratio | $\rho_{cost} = \frac{C_{label}}{C_{image\_capture}}$ | Used in dataset economics |
| Sample efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\partial \text{Accuracy}}{\partial N_L}$ | Slope of accuracy vs labelled-set size |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic — "L-U-S-W":**
> **L**abelled → **S**upervised
> **U**n-labelled → **S**elf-supervised / **U**nsupervised
> **L + U** → **S**emi-supervised
> **W**eakly-labelled → Multi-instance / image-level supervision

---

### 2.5 Why This Distinction Matters in Engineering Practice

| Engineering Decision | Choice Driven By Dataset Type |
|---|---|
| Model architecture choice | Labelled $\rightarrow$ task-specific CNN/Transformer head; Un-labelled $\rightarrow$ backbone encoder (ResNet, ViT) |
| Loss function | Labelled $\rightarrow$ cross-entropy / IoU; Un-labelled $\rightarrow$ contrastive / reconstruction |
| Hardware requirement | Labelled training (e.g., ImageNet) typically needs 8–64 GPUs; Self-supervised pre-training (e.g., DINOv2) needs 256–1024 GPUs |
| Time-to-deploy | Labelled fine-tune: hours–days; Un-labelled pre-train: weeks–months |
| Failure mode | Labelled: over-fits to label noise; Un-labelled: learns shortcut features unrelated to task |

---

### 2.6 Annotation Pipeline for Labelled Vision Data

A production-grade annotation pipeline for labelled computer vision datasets follows this sequence:

$$
\text{Raw Capture} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quality Filter} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PII / Privacy Scrub} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Task Specification} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Annotation} \;\rightarrow\; \text{QA / Consensus} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Release}
$$

Key quality metrics used in industry (e.g., Scale AI, Labelbox, Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth):

$$
\kappa_{inter} = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} \quad \text{(Cohen's kappa — inter-annotator agreement)}
$$

A $\kappa_{inter} > 0.80$ is considered *high quality*; below $0.60$ indicates label noise that will degrade downstream model performance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Raw Data Distribution to Empirical Risk

We start from the **true risk** (population loss):

$$
R(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim p(x,y)}\big[\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y)\big] \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x),y)\, p(x,y)\,dx\,dy
$$

Since $p(x,y)$ is unknown in real-world vision tasks, we approximate the expectation by the **empirical mean** over a finite labelled dataset $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}$:

$$
\hat{R}_N(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\big)
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(\theta) &= \int \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x),y)\, p(x,y)\,dx\,dy \\
&= \int \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x),y)\, dP(x,y) \quad \text{(rewriting as a probability measure)}\\
&\approx \frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}\big(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\big) \quad \text{(Monte Carlo / ergodic assumption)}\\
&= \hat{R}_{N_L}(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers**:

$$
\lim_{N_L \to \infty} \hat{R}_{N_L}(\theta) = R(\theta) \quad \text{almost surely, for fixed } \theta
$$

This is the mathematical justification for using large labelled datasets: more samples reduce the variance of the empirical risk estimator.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Gradient of the Labelled Loss (Supervised Learning)

For a single labelled sample $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ with cross-entropy loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}), y) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k, \quad \hat{y}_k = \frac{\exp(z_k)}{\sum_{c=1}^{K}\exp(z_c)}, \quad z_k = f_\theta(\mathbf{x})_k
$$

**Step 1:** Softmax derivative (Jacobian):

$$
\frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_c} = \hat{y}_k(\delta_{kc} - \hat{y}_c)
$$

**Step 2:** Derivative of loss w.r.t. logit $z_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_c} &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{y_k}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_c} \\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{y_k}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \hat{y}_k (\delta_{kc} - \hat{y}_c) \\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k (\delta_{kc} - \hat{y}_c) \\
&= -y_c + \hat{y}_c \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \\
&= \hat{y}_c - y_c \quad \text{(since } \sum_k y_k = 1 \text{ for one-hot)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Final gradient via chain rule:

$$
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = (\hat{y} - y)^\top \, \frac{\partial f_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{\partial \theta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The gradient depends on the residual $\hat{y} - y$. **Without $y$ (un-labelled data), this gradient is undefined.** This is the *fundamental mathematical reason* why un-labelled data requires a pretext task — we need to manufacture a target $y_{pseudo}$ that substitutes for the missing true label.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — InfoNCE Loss (Contrastive Self-Supervised Learning)

For a batch of $N$ un-labelled images, each augmented twice to produce $2N$ views. Let $z_i$ and $z_i^+$ be the embeddings of two augmentations of the same image (a *positive pair*), and $z_k$ for $k \neq i$ be embeddings of *negative* (different) images.

The InfoNCE loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE} = -\frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{2N} \log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_i^+)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2N} \mathbf{1}[k \neq i]\, \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}
$$

where $\text{sim}(u,v) = u^\top v / \Vert u \Vert \Vert v \Vert$ is cosine similarity and $\tau$ is the temperature.

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- The numerator $\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_i^+)/\tau)$ is large when the positive pair is similar — minimizing the loss pushes positives together.
- The denominator sum over all negatives pushes different-image embeddings apart.
- Temperature $\tau \to 0$ concentrates probability on the hardest negative; $\tau \to \infty$ flattens the distribution.

This is the foundation of **SimCLR**, **MoCo**, and **CLIP** pre-training on un-labelled image data.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Labelled vs. Un-Labelled Comparison

| Dimension | Labelled Data $\mathcal{D}_L$ | Un-Labelled Data $\mathcal{D}_U$ |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$ | $\{\mathbf{x}_j\}$ |
| Annotation required | Yes (human or generated + verified) | None |
| Cost per sample | **\$0.01 – \$50** depending on task | ~ **\$0.001** (storage only) |
| Typical $N$ (vision) | $10^3$ – $10^7$ | $10^6$ – $10^{10}$ |
| Learning paradigm | Supervised | Self-supervised / Unsupervised |
| Loss function | Task-specific ($\mathcal{L}_{CE}, \mathcal{L}_{IoU}$, etc.) | Pretext ($\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE}, \mathcal{L}_{MAE}$) |
| Gradient signal | $(\hat{y} - y)\frac{\partial f}{\partial\theta}$ — direct | Manufactured via pretext task |
| Sample efficiency (downstream) | High (less data needed for fine-tune) | Low standalone, high when transferred |
| Failure mode | Label noise, annotator bias | Shortcut features, pretext-task misalignment |
| Example datasets | ImageNet, COCO, Cityscapes | YFCC-100M, LAION-5B, JFT-300M (un-labelled portion) |
| Storage requirement | High (images + dense masks) | Lower (raw images only) |
| Privacy concerns | High (PII in images + identifying labels) | Moderate (PII in raw images) |
| Evaluation metric | Accuracy, mAP, mIoU, F1 | Linear probe accuracy, k-NN accuracy, downstream transfer |

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Linear Probe Protocol (Evaluating Un-Labelled Pre-Training)

To evaluate the quality of features learned from un-labelled data, a standard protocol is **linear probing**:

$$
\mathbf{z}_i = f_{\hat{\theta}_{SSL}}(\mathbf{x}_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{d} \quad \text{(frozen pre-trained features)}
$$

A linear classifier $W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times d}$ is trained on a small labelled set using:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\min_W \; \frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}_{CE}(W \mathbf{z}_i, y_i)
$$

The linear probe accuracy on held-out test data measures how linearly separable the un-labelled-pre-trained features are — a proxy for representation quality.

This protocol is used by **ImageNet linear evaluation**, **VTAB**, and most foundation-model benchmarks.

### 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Dataset Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do you have<br/>human-verified labels?}
    Q1 -- Yes and abundant --> P1[Supervised Learning<br/>Labelled Data]
    Q1 -- Yes but scarce --> Q2{Can you obtain<br/>more un-labelled?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Semi-Supervised Learning]
    Q2 -- No --> P3[Transfer Learning<br/>from Pre-trained]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is downstream task<br/>clearly defined?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P4[Self-Supervised Pre-train<br/>plus Fine-tune]
    Q3 -- No --> P5[Unsupervised Exploration<br/>Clustering Dimensionality]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** Use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** to explain *what each component does* and the *data flow direction*. Examiners reward clean, directional arrows and clearly labelled boxes — never write free-form text when a schematic suffices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define labelled data and un-labelled data in the context of computer vision datasets. Give one example dataset for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Labelled data is a set of input-output pairs $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$ where each visual sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ has an associated human-verified annotation $y_i$ (class label, bounding box, segmentation mask, etc.). The annotation defines the *ground truth* used to compute supervised loss. **Example:** **ImageNet** (image classification) or **COCO** (object detection with bounding boxes and segmentation masks).

Un-labelled data is a collection of raw visual samples $\{\mathbf{x}_j\}$ drawn from the data distribution $p(\mathbf{x})$ with no associated annotation. It is used in unsupervised and self-supervised learning paradigms where a *pretext task* manufactures pseudo-labels. **Example:** **LAION-5B** (5 billion image-text pairs scraped from the web, no human annotation).

*(Award: 1 mark for each definition + 0.5 mark per example = 3 marks total.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest Paper]**
*Why is un-labelled data considered "cheap but informative" in modern computer vision? Mention any two self-supervised pretext tasks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Un-labelled data is **cheap** because it requires no human annotation — cost is essentially limited to storage and bandwidth (~\$0.001 per image vs. \$0.50+ per pixel-labelled image). It is **informative** because it captures the true input distribution $p(\mathbf{x})$ in arbitrarily large volumes, enabling models to learn rich, transferable visual representations without label bias.

Two self-supervised pretext tasks:

1. **Rotation Prediction** — rotate the input by 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° and ask the network to predict the angle. The model must learn orientation-aware low-level features.
2. **Masked Autoencoding (MAE)** — randomly mask 75% of image patches and train the network to reconstruct the missing pixels. The model learns semantic context and spatial structure.

*(Award: 1.5 marks for the cheap/informative justification + 0.75 mark per pretext task = 3 marks total.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Labelled Data Deep Dive*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the supervised learning objective for a labelled image-classification dataset. Derive the gradient of the categorical cross-entropy loss with respect to the model logits. Show why the gradient signal vanishes if the labels are absent.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the annotation pipeline for a labelled object-detection dataset (e.g., PASCAL VOC). What is the role of inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) in ensuring label quality?

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Supervised Objective and Gradient Derivation [7 marks]

The supervised learning objective for a labelled image-classification dataset $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N_L}$ with $K$ classes is **empirical risk minimization**:

$$
\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \frac{1}{N_L}\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \mathcal{L}_{CE}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i) + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2
$$

For a single sample, the categorical cross-entropy loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k, \quad \hat{y}_k = \frac{\exp(z_k)}{\sum_{c=1}^{K}\exp(z_c)}
$$

**Derivation of gradient w.r.t. logits $z_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_c} &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_c} \\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \cdot \hat{y}_k(\delta_{kc} - \hat{y}_c) \quad \text{(softmax Jacobian)}\\
&= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k(\delta_{kc} - \hat{y}_c)\\
&= -y_c + \hat{y}_c \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \\
&= \hat{y}_c - y_c \quad \text{(using } \sum_k y_k = 1 \text{ for one-hot labels)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full gradient via chain rule:

$$
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{CE} = (\hat{y} - y)^\top \cdot \frac{\partial f_\theta(\mathbf{x})}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Why gradient vanishes without labels:**
If the true label $y$ is missing, the residual $(\hat{y} - y)$ cannot be computed — there is no target against which to compare the prediction. Hence $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ is mathematically **undefined**. This is precisely why un-labelled data requires a *proxy task* (e.g., MAE, contrastive learning) to manufacture a substitute target.

**Closing line for full marks:** Note the role of the regularization term $\lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$ in preventing overfitting when $N_L$ is small.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks objective statement + 4 marks gradient derivation + 1 mark unlabelled discussion = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Annotation Pipeline and Cohen's Kappa [7 marks]

**Standard annotation pipeline for an object-detection dataset (PASCAL VOC style):**

1. **Task Specification** — define object classes, occlusion rules, truncation handling, group-of policy.
2. **Annotation Tool Selection** — LabelImg, CVAT, RectLabel, or V7 Darwin for boxes; Labelbox or Supervisely for segmentation.
3. **Annotator Training** — golden-set calibration, edge-case handbook.
4. **Pilot Annotation** — 100–500 images labeled by all annotators to compute **Cohen's kappa $\kappa_{inter}$**.
5. **Bulk Annotation** — each image labeled by $\geq 2$ annotators independently.
6. **Adjudication** — disagreements resolved by a senior annotator or consensus.
7. **QA Pass** — random audit, IoU-based overlap checks, missing-object detection.
8. **Release** — published in COCO JSON or PASCAL VOC XML format.

**Cohen's kappa formula:**

$$
\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}
$$

where:
- $p_o$ = observed agreement proportion between two annotators.
- $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance (computed from class marginals).

**Quality thresholds (industry standard):**

| $\kappa$ Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $\kappa < 0.40$ | Poor — reject batch, re-train annotators |
| $0.40 \leq \kappa < 0.60$ | Moderate — borderline, investigate |
| $0.60 \leq \kappa < 0.80$ | Substantial — usable with QA |
| $\kappa \geq 0.80$ | Almost perfect — production-grade |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question A:**
> Students commonly lose marks by (1) omitting the regularization term in the ERM objective, (2) skipping intermediate softmax-Jacobian steps in the derivation, and (3) failing to mention that $\sum_k y_k = 1$ is what makes the gradient collapse to the clean form $\hat{y} - y$. Write every algebraic step explicitly — partial-credit grading rewards visible work.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Un-Labelled Data Deep Dive*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of self-supervised learning on un-labelled data. Describe in detail the InfoNCE contrastive loss used in methods like SimCLR. Why is temperature $\tau$ important?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare labelled and un-labelled datasets across the following dimensions: annotation cost, sample size, downstream task performance, privacy concerns, and failure modes. Use a real-world example (e.g., medical imaging) to illustrate when un-labelled data is preferable.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Self-Supervised Learning and InfoNCE [7 marks]

**Self-supervised learning (SSL)** is the dominant paradigm for leveraging un-labelled data in modern computer vision. The key idea is to define a *pretext task* $T$ that automatically generates a pseudo-label $T(\mathbf{x})$ from each un-labelled sample $\mathbf{x}$, eliminating the need for human annotation. After pre-training, the learned encoder $f_\theta$ is transferred to downstream tasks via fine-tuning on a small labelled set.

**InfoNCE Loss (Contrastive Learning — SimCLR/MoCo):**

For a batch of $N$ un-labelled images, each augmented twice via stochastic transformations (crop, color jitter, Gaussian blur, etc.), we obtain $2N$ views. Let:

- $z_i, z_i^+$ = embeddings of two augmentations of the same image (positive pair).
- $z_k$ for $k \neq i$ = embeddings of different images (negatives).
- $\text{sim}(u,v) = u^\top v / (\Vert u \Vert \Vert v \Vert)$ = cosine similarity.
- $\tau$ = temperature hyperparameter.

The InfoNCE loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE} = -\frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{2N} \log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_i^+)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1, k \neq i}^{2N} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- The **numerator** rewards high similarity between positive pairs — pulling them together in embedding space.
- The **denominator** penalizes high similarity with negatives — pushing different-image embeddings apart.
- Minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{InfoNCE}$ is equivalent to maximizing a lower bound on the **mutual information** $I(z_i; z_i^+)$ between positive pair embeddings.

**Role of temperature $\tau$:**

- $\tau \to 0$ (sharp distribution): concentrates gradients on the *hardest* negative — harsh separation, can be unstable.
- $\tau \to \infty$ (uniform distribution): all negatives weighted equally — softer separation, slower convergence.
- Empirical sweet spot (SimCLR): $\tau \approx 0.1$.

**Pre-training → Fine-tuning pipeline:**

$$
\mathbf{z}_i = f_{\hat{\theta}_{SSL}}(\mathbf{x}_i) \xrightarrow{\text{freeze or fine-tune}} \text{Downstream classifier}
$$

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks SSL concept + 3 marks InfoNCE derivation/interpretation + 2 marks temperature discussion = 7 marks.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis with Medical Imaging Example [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (Labelled vs. Un-Labelled):**

| Dimension | Labelled | Un-Labelled |
|---|---|---|
| Annotation cost | **\$0.01–\$50** per image | ~ **\$0.001** per image (storage only) |
| Sample size | $10^3$–$10^7$ typical | $10^6$–$10^{10}$ typical |
| Downstream accuracy (small fine-tune set) | Higher with sufficient labels | Approaches labelled performance after large-scale SSL |
| Privacy concerns | High — labels often contain PII (e.g., diagnosis) | Moderate — only raw images stored |
| Failure mode | Label noise, annotator bias, label shift | Shortcut learning, pretext-task misalignment |
| Reproducibility | Easy (annotations are deterministic) | Harder (different pre-training data → different features) |
| Regulatory | Often requires IRB approval | May fall under broader consent |

**Real-world example — Medical Imaging (X-ray / CT / Histopathology):**

Hospitals generate **millions of un-labelled medical images daily** (chest X-rays, CT slices, pathology slides), but obtaining pixel-level or diagnosis-level labels requires **board-certified radiologists** costing **\$50–\$500 per image** and weeks of turnaround. Key implications:

- **Un-labelled data is preferable** when:
  - Annotation requires rare expertise (e.g., pediatric radiology).
  - Labels are ambiguous (e.g., early-stage disease).
  - Privacy regulations (HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023) restrict label sharing across hospitals.

- **Standard pipeline:** A foundation model (e.g., **RadImageNet**, **MedSAM**, **Google's Med-PaLM 2 vision**) is pre-trained on millions of un-labelled hospital images via self-supervision, then fine-tuned on a few thousand labels per downstream task (e.g., tumor segmentation, pneumonia detection).

- **Concrete result:** CheXNet-style models pre-trained on un-labelled chest X-rays and fine-tuned on 100 labelled samples often match supervised baselines trained on 10,000+ labels — a **100× sample efficiency gain**.

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks comparison table + 4 marks medical-imaging worked example = 7 marks.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B:**
> Common pitfalls: (1) Writing the InfoNCE loss without showing the temperature scaling factor — examiners deduct for this. (2) Confusing *contrastive* with *generative* self-supervision in the discussion. (3) Giving generic privacy comments instead of concrete regulatory references (HIPAA, DPDP Act, GDPR Article 9). Be specific and quantitative wherever possible.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Datasets for Machine Perception**

- [ ] **Dataset tuple** $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i, m_i)$ — input, label, mask flag.
- [ ] **Labelled data** = $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}$ — supervised learning, expensive, task-specific.
- [ ] **Un-labelled data** = $\{\mathbf{x}_j\}$ — self-supervised / unsupervised learning, cheap, general-purpose.
- [ ] **Semi-supervised** = small labelled + large un-labelled, $N_L \ll N_U$.
- [ ] **Weakly-supervised** = present-but-noisy labels (e.g., only image-level tags).
- [ ] **Empirical risk**: $\hat{R}_N(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i)$ approximates true risk $R(\theta)$.
- [ ] **Gradient of cross-entropy**: $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial z_c = \hat{y}_c - y_c$ — undefined without $y$.
- [ ] **Pretext tasks** for un-labelled: rotation, jigsaw, MAE, contrastive (InfoNCE), DINO.
- [ ] **InfoNCE** has temperature $\tau$ — typical value $\tau \approx 0.1$.
- [ ] **Cohen's kappa** $\kappa \geq 0.80$ = production-grade annotation agreement.
- [ ] **Linear probing** is the standard evaluation for un-labelled-pre-trained features.
- [ ] **Examples** — Labelled: ImageNet, COCO, Cityscapes. Un-labelled: LAION-5B, YFCC-100M.
- [ ] **Cost ratios** — Pixel labels are 50–1000× more expensive than image-class labels.
- [ ] **Real-world dominance** — Modern CV (CLIP, SAM, DINOv2) pre-trains on un-labelled, fine-tunes on labelled.
- [ ] **Privacy** — Un-labelled medical/biological images preferred under HIPAA / GDPR.

**Final Mnemonic to recall under exam pressure:**
> **"L-U-S-W → Learn-Unsupervised-Smartly-With"**
> - **L**abelled = **L**ooking at the answer (supervised)
> - **U**n-labelled = **U**nlocking patterns (self-supervised)
> - **S**emi-supervised = **S**how some, hide some
> - **W**eakly-supervised = **W**hispered answers (noisy labels)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Classification and Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Classification and Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Basics of Classification and Clustering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** is a *supervised learning* paradigm in computer vision where a model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a labeled training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a feature vector extracted from an image and $y_i \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ is its corresponding class label. The objective is to generalize this mapping so that previously unseen images can be assigned to one of the $K$ predefined categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is an *unsupervised learning* paradigm that partitions an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ into $K$ groups (clusters) $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized, without access to ground-truth labels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Classification — The Sorting Mailroom Analogy:**
Imagine a postal sorting facility where you have been *explicitly taught* by a supervisor with thousands of previously sorted letters. Each letter (image) carries features like size, weight, destination handwriting, and stamp color. After learning, when a new letter arrives, you instinctively route it to "Local," "International," or "Express." The supervisor is the **training labels** — that is what makes it *supervised*.

**Clustering — The Self-Organizing Library Analogy:**
Now picture a librarian receiving 10,000 unsorted books with no genres written on them. Without any guide, the librarian groups them by *visual similarity* — same cover color, same size, same thickness. Books about cooking end up together, sci-fi together, and biographies together. There is **no supervisor telling the librarian the correct answer** — the structure emerges purely from intrinsic data properties. This is *unsupervised* learning.

### 1.3 Why These Concepts Matter in Computer Vision

In computer vision, raw images live in a high-dimensional pixel space (e.g., a 224×224 RGB image is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{150,528}$). Classification and clustering form the **decision-making backbone** of virtually every vision pipeline:

| Task | Role of Classification | Role of Clustering |
|---|---|---|
| **Face Recognition** | Identify "Whose face is this?" | Group unknown faces by similarity |
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor vs. non-tumor | Discover novel tissue subtypes |
| **Object Detection** | Label detected regions | Pre-cluster candidate regions |
| **Image Retrieval** | Tag query image | Group similar images for fast search |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Classify "normal" | Flag points not belonging to any cluster |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically expects students to understand the *mathematical foundations* of how classifiers separate classes in feature space, and how clustering algorithms discover structure without supervision. You will be tested on **distance metrics, decision boundaries, and algorithm workflows** — not on coding libraries.

### 1.4 Supervised vs. Unsupervised — The Fundamental Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Supervised learning (Classification) has **labeled** data and a **closed-set** of classes. Unsupervised learning (Clustering) has **unlabeled** data and an **open-set** where the number of groups is often discovered rather than prescribed.

| Property | Classification | Clustering |
|---|---|---|
| Labels | Available | Absent |
| Goal | Learn decision function | Discover inherent groups |
| Output | Predicted class | Cluster assignment |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix | Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, Inertia |
| Algorithms | KNN, SVM, Decision Trees, CNNs | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |
| Data | $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs | $x_i$ only |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classification Pipeline (Supervised)

A classification system in computer vision follows four stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Raw pixel matrix $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$.
2. **Feature Extraction** — Transform pixels to a discriminative vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (e.g., HOG, SIFT, or deep features).
3. **Model Training** — Minimize a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ over the training set.
4. **Inference / Prediction** — Apply learned $f(x)$ to new images: $\hat{y} = f(x)$.

### 2.2 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Classifier

The simplest non-parametric classifier. For a query point $x_q$, KNN finds the $K$ training samples closest to $x_q$ in feature space and assigns the majority class.

**Decision Rule:**
$$\hat{y}(x_q) = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_K(x_q)\}$$

where $\mathcal{N}_K(x_q)$ is the set of $K$ nearest neighbors of $x_q$.

**Distance Metrics:**

$$d_{\text{Euclidean}}(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$$

$$d_{\text{Manhattan}}(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d}\vert x_j - y_j \vert$$

$$d_{\text{Chebyshev}}(x, y) = \max_{j \in \{1,\dots,d\}} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$$

$$d_{\text{Cosine}}(x, y) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert_2 \, \Vert y \Vert_2}$$

### 2.3 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

SVM finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** that separates two classes. For a linearly separable dataset, the hyperplane is:

$$w^T x + b = 0$$

with the margin maximization objective:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

Using Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the dual form becomes:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)$$

The **kernel trick** allows non-linear separation by replacing $x_i^T x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$:

- Linear: $K(x, y) = x^T y$
- Polynomial: $K(x, y) = (x^T y + c)^p$
- RBF (Gaussian): $K(x, y) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x - y \Vert^2)$

### 2.4 Decision Trees

A decision tree recursively splits the feature space using axis-aligned thresholds. At each node, the **best split** is chosen by maximizing **Information Gain**:

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S)$ is the Shannon entropy:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

### 2.5 K-Means Clustering Algorithm

K-Means partitions $N$ data points into $K$ clusters by iteratively:

1. **Initialize** $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K\}$ (e.g., randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment step** — Assign each point to the nearest centroid:
$$C_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \Vert^2$$
3. **Update step** — Recompute centroids as cluster means:
$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$
4. **Repeat** steps 2–3 until convergence (centroids stop moving or objective plateaus).

**Objective Function (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares — WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

### 2.6 Hierarchical Clustering

Builds a tree of clusters (dendrogram) using either:

- **Agglomerative (Bottom-up):** Start with each point as its own cluster; iteratively merge the two closest clusters.
- **Divisive (Top-down):** Start with one cluster containing all points; iteratively split.

**Linkage Criteria** (distance between clusters $A$ and $B$):

$$d_{\text{single}}(A, B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

$$d_{\text{complete}}(A, B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

$$d_{\text{average}}(A, B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A}\sum_{b \in B} d(a, b)$$

### 2.7 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

Groups together points that are densely packed. Two key parameters: $\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum points to form a dense region).

A point $x$ is classified as:
- **Core point** if $\vert N_\epsilon(x) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border point** if it lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point but has fewer neighbors
- **Noise point** if neither

where $N_\epsilon(x) = \{y : d(x, y) \leq \epsilon\}$.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 — Memorize These.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Default KNN / K-Means distance |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Grid-like feature spaces |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x\Vert \Vert y\Vert}$ | HOG / deep features |
| 4 | Shannon Entropy | $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ | Decision Tree split criterion |
| 5 | Information Gain | $\text{IG} = H(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_v)$ | Decision tree node split |
| 6 | SVM Margin | $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Maximum-margin hyperplane |
| 7 | RBF Kernel | $K(x,y) = e^{-\gamma \Vert x-y\Vert^2}$ | Non-linear SVM |
| 8 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_k \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k\Vert^2$ | K-Means convergence |
| 9 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max(a(i), b(i))}$ | Cluster quality |
| 10 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Classifier evaluation |
| 11 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 12 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of positives |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 14 | Confusion Matrix | $C_{ij}$ = items of true class $i$ predicted as $j$ | Full error breakdown |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **KNN** is used in content-based image retrieval (CBIR) systems, where exact feature matches matter.
- **SVM with RBF kernel** dominated handwritten digit recognition before deep learning.
- **K-Means** is foundational in image color quantization, image compression, and vector quantization for VLAD/Fisher Vectors.
- **Hierarchical clustering** is used in bioinformatics (gene expression analysis) and in organizing large image datasets hierarchically.
- **DBSCAN** is critical in anomaly detection — e.g., detecting defective products on a manufacturing line.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — KNN Classification by Hand (KTU-Favored Style)

**Problem:** Given the following 2D feature vectors with class labels, classify a query point $x_q = (3, 4)$ using KNN with $K = 3$ and Euclidean distance.

| Point | Features | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | $(1, 1)$ | A |
| $x_2$ | $(2, 2)$ | A |
| $x_3$ | $(4, 5)$ | B |
| $x_4$ | $(5, 6)$ | B |
| $x_5$ | $(1, 0)$ | A |

**Step 1 — Compute distance from $x_q = (3,4)$ to every training point:**

$$d(x_q, x_1) = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

$$d(x_q, x_2) = \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-2)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$d(x_q, x_3) = \sqrt{(3-4)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

$$d(x_q, x_4) = \sqrt{(3-5)^2 + (4-6)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$$

$$d(x_q, x_5) = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{4+16} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472$$

**Step 2 — Sort distances in ascending order:**

| Rank | Point | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_3$ | 1.414 | B |
| 2 | $x_2$ | 2.236 | A |
| 3 | $x_4$ | 2.828 | B |
| 4 | $x_1$ | 3.606 | A |
| 5 | $x_5$ | 4.472 | A |

**Step 3 — Select $K=3$ nearest neighbors and take majority vote:**

The 3 nearest neighbors are $x_3$ (B), $x_2$ (A), $x_4$ (B). Tally: **B = 2, A = 1**.

$$\hat{y}(x_q) = \text{mode}\{B, A, B\} = B$$

**Conclusion:** $x_q = (3, 4)$ is classified as **Class B**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — K-Means Convergence

**Problem:** Cluster the following 2D points into $K=2$ clusters using K-Means: $\{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,9), (9,8)\}$. Initialize centroids at $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (8, 8)$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step:**

Compute distance of each point to both centroids.

$$d(P_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{0+0} = 0, \quad d(P_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{49+49} \approx 9.899$$

$$P_1 \rightarrow C_1 \text{ (closer to } \mu_1\text{)}$$

$$d(P_2, \mu_1) = \sqrt{0+1} = 1, \quad d(P_2, \mu_2) = \sqrt{49+36} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220$$

$$P_2 \rightarrow C_1$$

$$d(P_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{1+0} = 1, \quad d(P_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{36+49} = \sqrt{85} \approx 9.220$$

$$P_3 \rightarrow C_1$$

$$d(P_4, \mu_1) = \sqrt{49+49} \approx 9.899, \quad d(P_4, \mu_2) = 0$$

$$P_4 \rightarrow C_2$$

By symmetry, $P_5$ and $P_6$ also go to $C_2$.

**Assignment Result:** $C_1 = \{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$, $C_2 = \{P_4, P_5, P_6\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1)\right] = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.333, 1.333)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8) + (9,9) + (9,8)\right] = \left(\frac{26}{3}, \frac{25}{3}\right) \approx (8.667, 8.333)$$

**Iteration 2 — Verify Convergence:** Reassigning with new centroids does not change cluster membership. Centroids remain stable — **converged in 1 iteration**.

**Final Clusters:** $C_1$ contains points near $(1.33, 1.33)$; $C_2$ contains points near $(8.67, 8.33)$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — SVM Decision Boundary Derivation

**Problem:** Two linearly separable points $x_1 = (1, 1), y_1 = +1$ and $x_2 = (-1, -1), y_2 = -1$. Find the maximum-margin hyperplane.

**Step 1 — Set up primal optimization:**

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute constraints:**

For $x_1$: $w_1 + w_2 + b \geq 1$

For $x_2$: $-(w_1 + w_2 + b) \geq 1 \implies w_1 + w_2 + b \leq -1$

Both constraints imply $\vert w_1 + w_2 + b \vert \geq 1$, and at optimum, equality holds.

**Step 3 — Optimality condition:** Since the data is symmetric and the two points are equidistant from the origin, the optimal hyperplane passes through the origin. So $b = 0$.

The hyperplane must be equidistant from both points, so it must be perpendicular to the line connecting them, which lies along direction $(1, 1)$. The perpendicular direction is $(1, -1)$.

**Step 4 — Normalize the weight vector:**

$$w = \frac{(1, -1)}{\Vert (1, -1) \Vert} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the hyperplane $w^T x = 0$:**

$$w^T x_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1-1) = 0, \quad w^T x_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1+1) = 0$$

**Margin check:** The margin is $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$, which is maximum.

**Final Hyperplane:** $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}x_1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}x_2 = 0$, or equivalently $x_1 = x_2$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small dataset, interpretable results needed | KNN, Decision Tree | Simple, no training phase |
| High-dimensional features, non-linear | SVM with RBF kernel | Strong margin-based generalization |
| Very large dataset, real-time | Linear SVM or Logistic Regression | Computational efficiency |
| Unknown number of clusters, irregular shapes | DBSCAN | Discovers noise and arbitrary shapes |
| Compact, spherical clusters, known $K$ | K-Means | Fast, scalable |
| Hierarchical taxonomy needed | Agglomerative Clustering | Dendrogram interpretation |
| Image color quantization | K-Means on pixel colors | Reduces palette to $K$ dominant colors |
| Anomaly / defect detection | DBSCAN or Isolation Forest | Noise points = anomalies |
| Multi-class with $K$ large | One-vs-Rest SVM or KNN | Direct extension of binary classifiers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Image Classification Pipeline


```mermaid
    E2 --> F2[Build Dendrogram]
    F2 --> G2[Cut Tree at Level K]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Labeled $(x_i, y_i)$ | Unlabeled $\{x_i\}$ |
| Goal | Learn mapping to labels | Discover inherent structure |
| Feedback | Direct error signal available | No explicit feedback |
| Example Algorithm | KNN, SVM, Decision Tree | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |
| Evaluation | Accuracy, F1-score | Silhouette, Inertia |

**Example:** KNN (Supervised) classifies an image as "cat" or "dog" using labeled training data. K-Means (Unsupervised) groups similar unlabeled images into $K$ clusters based on feature similarity.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Define the term "feature space" in the context of image classification. Why is distance metric selection important?**

**Model Answer:**

A **feature space** is a $d$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ in which each image is represented as a single point (feature vector) $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. The axes correspond to the extracted features (e.g., color histograms, HOG descriptors, or deep embeddings).

**Importance of distance metric selection:**

1. **Determines similarity** — Different metrics capture different notions of similarity (Euclidean = absolute proximity, Cosine = directional alignment).
2. **Affects classifier output** — KNN and K-Means are highly sensitive to the chosen metric.
3. **Influences decision boundary shape** — A metric implicitly defines the shape of clusters (Euclidean → spherical, Cosine → angular).
4. **Domain-dependent** — Image histograms benefit from Chi-square distance; deep features from cosine similarity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, End Semester]**

**(a)** Explain the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classification algorithm in detail with a suitable diagram. Discuss the effect of the value of $K$ on the decision boundary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two feature points $P_1 = (2, 3)$ with class label $A$ and $P_2 = (5, 7)$ with class label $B$ are given. A query point $Q = (4, 5)$ is to be classified. Use KNN with $K = 1$ and $K = 3$ (assume a third point $P_3 = (3, 4)$ with class $A$). Use Euclidean distance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**KNN Algorithm — Step-by-Step:**

KNN is a non-parametric, instance-based learning algorithm. The training phase simply stores all training samples. The classification phase involves:

1. Receive query point $x_q$.
2. Compute distance $d(x_q, x_i)$ for every training point $x_i$.
3. Sort distances in ascending order.
4. Select the $K$ training points with smallest distances.
5. For classification: take majority vote of their labels.
6. For regression: take mean of their target values.

**Effect of $K$ on Decision Boundary:**

- **$K = 1$:** Highly sensitive to noise. Decision boundary is jagged, overfits, high variance.
- **$K$ small (e.g., 3):** Boundary is smoother, but still flexible.
- **$K$ large (e.g., 15):** Boundary becomes overly smooth, may underfit, low variance but high bias.
- **$K = N$ (total samples):** Always predicts the majority class — useless.

**Optimal $K$** is typically chosen via cross-validation. A common heuristic: $K \approx \sqrt{N}$, then tune.

**Diagram Description:** A 2D feature space plot with two classes (red and blue points) and the decision boundary curving around them. For small $K$, the boundary is irregular with many small islands; for large $K$, the boundary becomes a smooth curve approximating the true class separation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distance from $Q = (4, 5)$ to all training points:**

$$d(Q, P_1) = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (5-3)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$$

$$d(Q, P_2) = \sqrt{(4-5)^2 + (5-7)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$d(Q, P_3) = \sqrt{(4-3)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 2 — For $K = 1$:** The single nearest neighbor is $P_3$ (distance 1.414), class $A$.

$$\hat{y} = A$$

**Step 3 — For $K = 3$:** All three neighbors are used.

| Neighbor | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $P_3$ | 1.414 | A |
| $P_2$ | 2.236 | B |
| $P_1$ | 2.828 | A |

Tally: A = 2, B = 1.

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{A, B, A\} = A$$

**Final Answer:** Query point $Q$ is classified as class **A** for both $K=1$ and $K=3$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the K-Means clustering algorithm. Explain the role of initialization, distance metric, and the convergence criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply K-Means with $K = 2$ on the following 2D points: $\{(2, 10), (2, 6), (8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (1, 2), (4, 9)\}$. Initial centroids are $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 8)$. Show one full iteration and the updated centroids. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**K-Means Algorithm — Detailed Explanation:**

K-Means is an iterative, centroid-based clustering algorithm that partitions $N$ data points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K$.
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each data point $x_i$ to the nearest centroid based on Euclidean distance.
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to its cluster.
4. **Convergence Check:** Stop if centroids do not change between iterations or if the objective $J$ (WCSS) stabilizes.

**Role of Initialization:**

- Random initialization can lead to **poor local minima** and empty clusters.
- **K-Means++** improves initialization: first centroid chosen randomly, subsequent centroids chosen with probability proportional to squared distance from nearest existing centroid.
- Multiple random restarts help avoid bad solutions.

**Role of Distance Metric:**

- Default is **Euclidean distance**, which assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters.
- Manhattan or cosine distance may suit specific data types.
- The metric defines the shape of the resulting Voronoi partition.

**Role of Convergence Criterion:**

- Stop when centroids move by less than a small threshold $\epsilon$.
- Stop after a maximum number of iterations.
- Stop when cluster assignments no longer change.
- Stop when $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ for the objective function.

**Objective Function:** $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$

K-Means monotonically decreases $J$ at every iteration and is guaranteed to converge (though possibly to a local minimum).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Initial State:** $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$, $\mu_2 = (5, 8)$.

**Step 1 — Compute distance from each point to both centroids:**

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1=(2,10)$ | $d$ to $\mu_2=(5,8)$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(2, 10)$ | 0.000 | 3.606 | $C_1$ |
| $(2, 6)$ | 4.000 | 3.606 | $C_2$ |
| $(8, 4)$ | 8.485 | 5.000 | $C_2$ |
| $(5, 8)$ | 3.606 | 0.000 | $C_2$ |
| $(7, 5)$ | 7.071 | 3.606 | $C_2$ |
| $(6, 4)$ | 7.211 | 3.162 | $C_2$ |
| $(1, 2)$ | 8.062 | 7.211 | $C_2$ |
| $(4, 9)$ | 2.236 | 1.414 | $C_2$ |

**Step 2 — Cluster Membership after Iteration 1:**

$C_1 = \{(2, 10)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,6), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$.

**Step 3 — Update Centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = (2, 10) \quad \text{(only one point in } C_1\text{)}$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{7}, \frac{6+4+8+5+4+2+9}{7}\right) = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \frac{38}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, 5.43)$$

**Updated Centroids:** $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$, $\mu_2 \approx (4.71, 5.43)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the objective direction** — K-Means *minimizes* $J$ (WCSS), not maximizes. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Skipping the assignment step in iteration** — You must show *all* distances in a table, not just cluster assignments.
> 3. **Forgetting to recompute the centroid that has only one point** — A common error is to average it with the other cluster's points incorrectly.
> 4. **Mixing up KNN (supervised) and K-Means (unsupervised)** — Examiners explicitly test this distinction. KNN uses labels; K-Means does not.
> 5. **Not stating the convergence criterion explicitly** — Always mention: centroid stability, assignment stability, or objective function plateau.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before Exam**

- **Classification is supervised; Clustering is unsupervised.** Memorize this distinction.
- **KNN** stores all training data; no explicit training phase. Prediction is slow for large datasets ($O(Nd)$ per query).
- **SVM** maximizes the margin $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$. Kernel trick projects data to higher dimensions for non-linear separation.
- **Decision Trees** use information gain or Gini impurity to split nodes recursively.
- **K-Means** minimizes within-cluster sum of squares $J$. Always converges but only to a **local** minimum.
- **Hierarchical clustering** produces a dendrogram — choose $K$ by cutting the tree at the appropriate level.
- **DBSCAN** finds arbitrarily-shaped clusters and identifies noise; requires $\epsilon$ and MinPts tuning.
- **Distance metrics** — Euclidean (default), Manhattan (grid), Chebyshev (max), Cosine (angle). Choice affects results significantly.
- **Confusion matrix** has TP, FP, TN, FN on a $2 \times 2$ layout for binary classification.
- **Accuracy alone is misleading** for imbalanced datasets — always quote Precision, Recall, and F1-score together.
- **Silhouette score** ranges from $-1$ to $+1$; higher means better-defined clusters.
- **Image features** before classification: HOG, SIFT, color histograms, LBP, or deep CNN embeddings.
- **K in KNN and K in K-Means are different!** KNN's K is the number of neighbors; K-Means' K is the number of clusters.
- **Feature normalization** (min-max or z-score) is critical when using distance-based methods.
- **Curse of dimensionality** — KNN suffers in high dimensions; feature selection or PCA helps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain" an algorithm in 7 marks, structure your answer as: (1) Intuition, (2) Mathematical formulation, (3) Step-by-step algorithm, (4) Advantages and limitations, (5) Use case in computer vision. This covers all rubric criteria and secures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Class Perspective; Machine Learning for Computer Vision: Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multi-Class Perspective; Machine Learning for Computer Vision: Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Multi-Class Perspective in Computer Vision?

In the context of computer vision, most real-world problems are **not** simple binary (yes/no) decisions. A vision system must typically distinguish between **N ≥ 3** object categories simultaneously — e.g., "Is this image a cat, a dog, a car, or a horse?" This is the **Multi-Class Classification Problem**.

Formally, given an input image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ and a label space containing $K$ discrete classes $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_K\}$, a multi-class classifier learns a mapping:

$$f: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}, \quad K \geq 3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> A multi-class classifier is a function that assigns exactly **one and only one** class label to an input from a set of three or more mutually exclusive categories. The output is a probability distribution over the $K$ classes that sums to unity.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Hat" of Vision
Imagine a postal sorting room. A binary classifier is a worker who can answer only "Is this a letter or a parcel?" A multi-class classifier, in contrast, is a worker with $K$ labelled trays (cat, dog, car, horse…). The worker looks at the envelope (image features), pulls out the address (highest score), and drops the item into exactly one tray. The probability scores tell us *how confident* the worker is before the drop. This is the **softmax + cross-entropy** decision process in disguise.

---

## 1.2 Machine Learning for Computer Vision — The Big Picture

**Machine Learning (ML) for CV** is the sub-discipline that replaces hand-crafted rule-based vision systems with **data-driven models** that *learn* visual patterns from large annotated image corpora.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Learning Paradigms (must know for KTU):**
> 1. **Supervised Learning** — Image + Label pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ given. (e.g., CNN classifier)
> 2. **Unsupervised Learning** — Only images $x_i$ given. (e.g., K-Means clustering, Autoencoders)
> 3. **Semi-Supervised Learning** — Few labels + lots of unlabelled data.
> 4. **Reinforcement Learning** — Agent learns from reward signals (used in robotic vision).

### A Vision-ML Pipeline at a Glance
The standard supervised ML pipeline for vision consists of:
1. **Dataset Collection** $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$
2. **Feature Extraction / Representation** $x_i \rightarrow \phi(x_i)$ (hand-crafted SIFT/HOG *or* learned CNN features)
3. **Model Selection** (Linear, SVM, Neural Network, Decision Tree)
4. **Training** — minimize a loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over the training set
5. **Validation / Hyperparameter Tuning**
6. **Testing** — evaluate on a *held-out* test set using metrics such as **accuracy**, **precision**, **recall**, **F1-score**, and the **confusion matrix**.

### Intuition: ML = "Programming by Example"
Traditional CV required engineers to hand-code rules (edge detectors, template matchers). ML *inverts* this — we present thousands of cat/dog images and let the algorithm **discover the rules itself**. The model is parameterized by weights $\theta$, and learning is the process of finding $\theta^\star$ that minimizes prediction error.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Hint:** When asked *"Why use ML in CV?"* — answer: **Robustness, scalability, and adaptability** to real-world variations (lighting, pose, occlusion) that defeat hand-crafted rule systems.

---

## 1.3 The Multi-Class Problem — Why It Is Harder Than Binary

| Aspect | Binary | Multi-Class |
|---|---|---|
| Decision boundary | 1 hyperplane | $K(K-1)/2$ pairwise regions |
| Output | 1 probability | $K$ probabilities summing to 1 |
| Loss | Log loss on single logit | Softmax + categorical cross-entropy |
| Ambiguity | Low | Higher (confusable classes: husky vs. malamute) |

The **key conceptual leap** is that the multi-class output is a *probability simplex* — a $(K-1)$-dimensional object embedded in $K$-dimensional space. The model must learn to map any input to a *single corner* of this simplex for confident classification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Canonical Strategies for Multi-Class Reduction

Most classical ML algorithms (Logistic Regression, SVM) are **intrinsically binary**. To extend them to $K$ classes, we use one of two reduction strategies:

### (a) One-vs-All (OvA / OvR)
Train $K$ independent binary classifiers. The $i$-th classifier distinguishes *class $i$* from *all other classes combined*.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{i \in \{1,\dots,K\}} \; f_i(x)$$

- **Pros:** Simple, parallelizable, only $K$ classifiers needed.
- **Cons:** Class imbalance (each binary sub-problem is highly skewed); ambiguous regions where multiple classifiers say "yes."

### (b) One-vs-One (OvO)
Train $\binom{K}{2} = \dfrac{K(K-1)}{2}$ binary classifiers, one for every pair of classes. At test time, each classifier casts a vote; the class with the most votes wins.

- **Pros:** Each sub-problem is balanced; smaller, faster classifiers.
- **Cons:** $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$ classifiers; voting ties must be broken.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Softmax Regression (Multinomial Logistic Regression)** is the *native* multi-class method — it learns all $K$ boundaries **simultaneously** via a single softmax output layer. This is the standard in modern deep learning for CV.

---

## 2.2 The Softmax Function — From Scores to Probabilities

Given raw class scores (logits) $z \in \mathbb{R}^K$ produced by a linear or deep model:

$$z = W x + b, \quad W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times d},\; b \in \mathbb{R}^K$$

the **softmax** converts them into a valid probability distribution:

$$p(y = k \mid x; \theta) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} = \sigma(z)_k$$

**Properties of Softmax:**
- $\sigma(z)_k \in (0, 1)$ for all $k$.
- $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(z)_k = 1$ (probability simplex).
- **Translation invariance:** $\sigma(z + c) = \sigma(z)$ for any constant $c$ added to all logits. This is why we use the **numerically stable form** with $c = \max_j z_j$.
- It is the **multiclass generalization of the logistic sigmoid.**

---

## 2.3 The Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss

For a single training example with true label $y$ (one-hot encoded as $y \in \{0,1\}^K$):

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log\left(\hat{p}_k\right)$$

For a dataset of $N$ samples (mean loss):

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \log\left(\hat{p}_{ik}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cross-entropy, not MSE?** Cross-entropy is derived from the **maximum likelihood principle** under a multinomial distribution. It has stronger gradients when the model is confidently wrong (avoiding the *vanishing gradient* problem of MSE on softmax outputs). This is a **favourite KTU question**.

---

## 2.4 Decision Boundary Geometry

For a linear classifier with weights $W$ and bias $b$, the decision boundary between class $i$ and class $j$ is the hyperplane where their scores are equal:

$$W_i^\top x + b_i = W_j^\top x + b_j$$

$$\Rightarrow (W_i - W_j)^\top x + (b_i - b_j) = 0$$

In $\mathbb{R}^d$, this is a $(d-1)$-dimensional hyperplane. For $K$ classes, the input space is partitioned into $K$ convex **Voronoi-like regions**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Softmax | $\hat{p}_k = \dfrac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities sum to **1** |
| 2 | Numerically Stable Softmax | $\hat{p}_k = \dfrac{e^{z_k - z_{\max}}}{\sum_j e^{z_j - z_{\max}}}$ | Subtract $\max$ to avoid overflow |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{p}_k$ | $y$ is one-hot |
| 4 | Mean CE over batch | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i \sum_k y_{ik} \log \hat{p}_{ik}$ | Standard training loss |
| 5 | One-vs-All count | $K$ classifiers | Predict via $\arg\max$ |
| 6 | One-vs-One count | $\binom{K}{2} = \dfrac{K(K-1)}{2}$ | Predict via majority vote |
| 7 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{N}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 8 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| 9 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 11 | Macro F1 | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} F_1^{(k)}$ | Equal weight per class |
| 12 | Confusion Matrix | $C \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}$ | $C_{ij}$ = # of true $i$ predicted as $j$ |
| 13 | Linear Score | $z = W x + b$ | $W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times d}$ |
| 14 | Argmax Predict | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k \, z_k$ | Equivalent to $\arg\max_k \hat{p}_k$ |
| 15 | Train/Test Split | $N = N_{\text{train}} + N_{\text{test}}$ | Typical: 70/30 or 80/20 |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Classify CT scans into {Normal, Benign, Malignant} — multi-class with $K=3$ using softmax + cross-entropy.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Recognize traffic signs across 43+ categories (GTSRB dataset).
- **Satellite Imagery:** Crop-type classification (wheat, rice, maize, soybean) for agricultural policy.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** {OK, Scratch, Dent, Crack, Stain}.
- **Face Recognition (open-set):** Often cast as a multi-class problem with hundreds/thousands of identity classes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Softmax Function (from Maximum Likelihood)

**Step 1 — Model Assumption.** We assume the conditional distribution of the label given the image is **multinomial / categorical** with parameters $\theta$:

$$P(y = k \mid x; \theta) = p_k(x;\theta), \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k = 1$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood of the Training Set.** Given $N$ i.i.d. samples with one-hot labels $y^{(i)}$:

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k^{(i)} \log p_k(x^{(i)};\theta)$$

(The $y_k^{(i)}$ acts as an indicator — only the true class contributes to the log-likelihood.)

**Step 3 — Choose the Exponential Family Form.** We require $p_k > 0$ and $\sum p_k = 1$. The natural choice is the **softmax parameterization** using linear scores $z_k = W_k^\top x + b_k$:

$$p_k(x;\theta) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 4 — Justification of the Exponential Form.** We want $p_k$ to be:
- Monotonically increasing in $z_k$ (higher score $\Rightarrow$ higher probability).
- Invariant to a constant shift in all scores (only *differences* in scores matter).
- The standard form satisfying both is the **Boltzmann / Gibbs distribution** $\propto e^{z_k}$, which after normalization is the softmax.

**Step 5 — Convert to a Loss.** We minimize the **negative log-likelihood** (NLL), which is the categorical cross-entropy:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\ell(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k^{(i)} \log p_k(x^{(i)};\theta)$$

Dividing by $N$ gives the average loss used in practice. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cross-Entropy Gradient (for a Single Sample)

We derive $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k}$ — the gradient of the cross-entropy with respect to the pre-softmax logit $z_k$. This is the cornerstone of backpropagation in any softmax classifier.

**Step 1 — Write the Loss for a Single Sample.**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \log \hat{p}_j, \quad \hat{p}_j = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{m} e^{z_m}}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\log \hat{p}_j$ with respect to $z_k$.**

Using the chain rule and the quotient rule on the softmax:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{p}_j}{\partial z_k} = \hat{p}_j \left( \delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k \right)$$

This identity is the famous "log-derivative of softmax." Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial \log \hat{p}_j}{\partial z_k} = \frac{1}{\hat{p}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}_j}{\partial z_k} = \delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k$$

where $\delta_{jk}$ is the Kronecker delta ($=1$ if $j=k$, else $0$).

**Step 3 — Compute the Full Loss Gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \left( \delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k \right) = -\left( y_k - \hat{p}_k \sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the One-Hot Property.** Since $y$ is one-hot, $\sum_{j} y_j = 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{p}_k - y_k}$$

This is **the single most important identity in multi-class classification**: the gradient of the softmax+cross-entropy loss is simply **(prediction − truth)**. The error signal pushes $\hat{p}_k$ toward $y_k$ on every gradient step. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Class Softmax

**Setup.** Suppose $K=3$ classes and a model produces logits $z = [2.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.1]$ for input $x$. The true label is class 1, i.e., $y = [0, 1, 0]$.

**Step 1 — Numerical Stability.** Compute $z_{\max} = 2.0$, then shift:

$$z' = z - z_{\max} = [0, -1.0, -1.9]$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate.**

$$e^{z'} = [e^0,\; e^{-1.0},\; e^{-1.9}] = [1.0000,\; 0.3679,\; 0.1496]$$

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$S = 1.0000 + 0.3679 + 0.1496 = 1.5175$$

**Step 4 — Normalize (softmax output).**

$$\hat{p} = \frac{1}{1.5175}\,[1.0000,\; 0.3679,\; 0.1496] = [0.6590,\; 0.2424,\; 0.0986]$$

**Step 5 — Loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{p}_k = -\log(0.2424) = 1.4175 \text{ nats}$$

**Step 6 — Gradient (using Section 3.2).**

$$\nabla_z \mathcal{L} = \hat{p} - y = [0.6590,\; 0.2424 - 1,\; 0.0986] = [0.6590,\; -0.7576,\; 0.0986]$$

The positive gradient for class 1 means $z_1$ will *decrease* (probability will be reduced), and the large negative gradient for class 2 means $z_2$ will *increase* (its probability will be raised). The model is being pushed toward the correct class. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Selection for Multi-Class Vision

| Scenario | Recommended Loss | Output Activation | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multi-class (single label) | Categorical Cross-Entropy | Softmax | Mutually exclusive classes; full probability distribution |
| Multi-label (multiple correct) | Binary Cross-Entropy (per class) | Sigmoid | Classes are independent; multiple may be active |
| Ordinal classes | Earth Mover's Distance | Softmax | Captures rank distance between classes |
| Imbalanced multi-class | Weighted CE / Focal Loss | Softmax | Reweights rare classes |
| Regression (age, depth) | MSE / Huber | Linear / Sigmoid | Continuous target |
| Metric learning (face ID) | Triplet / Contrastive | Embedding | Learn a *distance*, not a class |
