# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End ML-for-CV Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Image Dataset X] --> B[Preprocessing: Resize, Normalize, Augment]
    B --> C[Train / Validation / Test Split]
    C --> D[Feature Extractor: HOG / SIFT / CNN Backbone]
    D --> E[Feature Vector phi x of size d]
    E --> F[Classifier: Linear / SVM / MLP]
    F --> G[Logits z in R to the K]
    G --> H[Softmax Activation]
    H --> I[Probability Distribution p hat]
    I --> J{Cross-Entropy Loss vs One-Hot Label}
    J --> K[Backpropagation: Gradient p hat minus y]
    K --> L[Update Weights via SGD / Adam]
    L --> M{Validation Accuracy Improved?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Save Best Model]
    M -- No --> O[Apply Regularization / Early Stop]
    N --> P[Evaluate on Test Set]
    O --> F
    P --> Q[Confusion Matrix and F1 Report]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Class Reduction Strategies (OvA vs OvO vs Native)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Image x] --> Q{K Classes to Predict?}

    Q --> R1[Strategy 1: One vs All OvA]
    Q --> R2[Strategy 2: One vs One OvO]
    Q --> R3[Strategy 3: Native Softmax Regression]

    R1 --> S1[Train K Binary Classifiers]
    S1 --> T1[Pick argmax of K scores]

    R2 --> S2["Train K choose 2 Binary Classifiers"]
    S2 --> T2[Majority Vote over all pairwise classifiers]

    R3 --> S3[Train 1 Multinomial Model with K outputs]
    S3 --> T3[Apply Softmax then argmax]

    T1 --> Out[Final Class Prediction y hat]
    T2 --> Out
    T3 --> Out
```

## 4.3 The Confusion Matrix — Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CM[Confusion Matrix C of size K by K]

    CM --> Diag["Diagonal C_ii = Correct Predictions TP per class"]
    CM --> RowSums["Row Sums = Actual Class Totals"]
    CM --> ColSums["Column Sums = Predicted Class Totals"]

    RowSums --> Recall["Recall_i = C_ii divided by RowSum_i"]
    ColSums --> Precision["Precision_i = C_ii divided by ColSum_i"]

    Diag --> Acc["Overall Accuracy = sum C_ii divided by N"]
    Recall --> F1["F1_i = 2 times P_i times R_i divided by P_i plus R_i"]
    Precision --> F1
```

## 4.4 Bias–Variance Tradeoff in Vision Models

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Underfitting: High Bias] --> B[Symptoms: Train Acc Low, Val Acc Low]
    C[Good Fit] --> D[Symptoms: Train Acc High, Val Acc High, Gap Small]
    E[Overfitting: High Variance] --> F[Symptoms: Train Acc Very High, Val Acc Much Lower]

    B --> G[Remedy: Bigger Model, More Features, Less Regularization]
    D --> H[Keep Current Model]
    F --> I[Remedy: More Data, Augmentation, Dropout, L2, Early Stop]
```

## 4.5 Softmax + Cross-Entropy Forward Pass (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Image x] --> W1["Linear Layer: z = W x + b"]
    W1 --> SM["Softmax: p_hat_k = exp z_k divided by sum exp z_j"]
    SM --> CE["Cross-Entropy: L = minus sum y_k log p_hat_k"]
    CE --> OPT["Optimizer: theta update equals theta minus eta times grad L"]
    OPT --> W1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (for KTU board answers):** When a question asks *"Draw the training pipeline of a multi-class image classifier"*, you can reproduce the structure in Diagram 4.1. Always label the **input → preprocessing → features → classifier → loss → optimizer** cycle. Examiners award marks for the *loop arrow* from optimizer back to model.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between One-vs-All and One-vs-One strategies for multi-class classification. State one advantage of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | One-vs-All (OvA) | One-vs-One (OvO) |
|---|---|---|
| # of binary classifiers | $K$ | $\dfrac{K(K-1)}{2}$ |
| Training | Each class vs. the rest | Each *pair* of classes |
| Prediction | Argmax of $K$ scores | Majority vote |
| Advantage | Simple, fast, only $K$ models | Balanced sub-problems, smaller per-classifier data |

**Example:** For $K=4$, OvA trains 4 classifiers; OvO trains 6 classifiers. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why is the softmax function preferred over a simple normalization $z_k / \sum z_j$ in multi-class classifiers?"**

**Model Answer:**
1. Softmax is **exponentially weighted** — it amplifies score differences and produces a sharper, more decisive distribution. A small logit gap becomes a large probability gap, which the cross-entropy loss can effectively optimize. *(1 mark)*
2. Softmax is **translation invariant** — adding a constant $c$ to all logits leaves the output unchanged, making it numerically stable. *(1 mark)*
3. Softmax arises naturally from the **maximum likelihood principle** for a multinomial distribution, giving it a sound probabilistic foundation. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In 14-mark questions on multi-class classification, marks are **frequently lost** at these three points:
> 1. **Forgetting to one-hot encode** the true label $y$ before applying cross-entropy.
> 2. **Confusing macro vs. micro F1** — macro averages per class equally, micro pools TP/FP/FN globally.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation of $\nabla_z \mathcal{L} = \hat{p} - y$** — KTU examiners specifically award 3–4 marks for this step.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain the **softmax function** and the **categorical cross-entropy loss** for a multi-class image classification problem. Show that the gradient of the cross-entropy loss with respect to the logits is $\hat{p}_k - y_k$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Consider a 3-class problem with classes {Cat, Dog, Car}. For a given test image, the model outputs logits $z = [3.2,\; 5.1,\; 1.4]$. The true label is "Dog". Compute the softmax probabilities, the cross-entropy loss, and the gradient vector with respect to the logits. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**1. Softmax Function.**
For $K$ classes with logits $z = [z_1, z_2, \dots, z_K]$:

$$\hat{p}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad k = 1, \dots, K$$

**Properties:** (i) $\hat{p}_k \in (0,1)$, (ii) $\sum_k \hat{p}_k = 1$, (iii) $\hat{p}_k$ increases monotonically with $z_k$. *(2 marks)*

**2. Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss.**
With one-hot label $y \in \{0,1\}^K$:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k$$

**3. Gradient Derivation.** *(3 marks — the key step)*

Step i: $\dfrac{\partial \hat{p}_j}{\partial z_k} = \hat{p}_j(\delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k)$.

Step ii: $\dfrac{\partial \log \hat{p}_j}{\partial z_k} = \delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k$.

Step iii: $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_j y_j(\delta_{jk} - \hat{p}_k) = -y_k + \hat{p}_k \sum_j y_j = \hat{p}_k - y_k$.

(Here we used $\sum_j y_j = 1$ from one-hot encoding.) **Conclude:** The gradient is simply the prediction error vector. *(2 marks for context/interpretation)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Numerically stable shift.** $z_{\max} = 5.1$, so $z' = [-1.9,\; 0,\; -3.7]$.

**Step 2 — Exponentiate.**
$e^{z'} = [e^{-1.9},\; e^{0},\; e^{-3.7}] = [0.1496,\; 1.0000,\; 0.0247]$.

**Step 3 — Sum.** $S = 0.1496 + 1.0000 + 0.0247 = 1.1743$.

**Step 4 — Softmax.** $\hat{p} = [0.1274,\; 0.8516,\; 0.0210]$.

**Step 5 — Loss.** True label "Dog" → $y = [0,1,0]$.

$$\mathcal{L} = -\log(0.8516) = 0.1605 \text{ nats}$$

**Step 6 — Gradient.**

$$\nabla_z \mathcal{L} = \hat{p} - y = [0.1274,\; -0.1484,\; 0.0210]$$

**Interpretation:** The model already predicts "Dog" with 85% confidence (correct). The small-magnitude negative gradient for class 2 means the optimizer will very gently *decrease* $z_2$ (and *increase* $z_1, z_3$ slightly) to refine the boundary. **No big weight updates are needed — the model is nearly converged on this sample.** *(1 mark for interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **multi-class classification problem** in computer vision. Compare and contrast the **One-vs-All**, **One-vs-One**, and **native Softmax Regression** approaches with a clear diagram of input-to-output mapping. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a 4-class image dataset with 1000 samples per class, a linear classifier is trained and evaluated. The resulting confusion matrix is:

|  | Pred: Cat | Pred: Dog | Pred: Car | Pred: Horse |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **True: Cat** | 820 | 90 | 50 | 40 |
| **True: Dog** | 70 | 840 | 40 | 50 |
| **True: Car** | 30 | 30 | 900 | 40 |
| **True: Horse** | 25 | 35 | 20 | 920 |

Compute the **per-class precision, recall, F1-score**, and the **macro-averaged F1**. Comment on the most frequently confused class pair. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**1. Multi-class classification definition.** A function $f: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ that maps an input image to exactly one of $K \geq 3$ mutually exclusive classes. *(1 mark)*

**2. Three strategies — comparison table.** *(3 marks)*

| Strategy | # Classifiers | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| One-vs-All | $K$ | Simple, modular | Imbalanced sub-problems |
| One-vs-One | $K(K-1)/2$ | Balanced sub-problems | Many classifiers; tie-breaking |
| Native Softmax | 1 (with $K$ outputs) | Single training loop; principled | Requires differentiable model |

**3. Diagram (verbal description for written exam).** Draw a flowchart: image → shared feature extractor → $K$ parallel output heads (OvA) OR $K(K-1)/2$ pair heads (OvO) OR single $K$-way softmax head (Native). The native approach's single head with $K$ outputs is the modern default. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Per-class Precision and Recall.**
For each class, Precision $= \dfrac{C_{ii}}{\sum_j C_{ji}}$ and Recall $= \dfrac{C_{ii}}{\sum_j C_{ij}}$.

- **Cat:** $P = \dfrac{820}{820+70+30+25} = \dfrac{820}{945} = 0.8677$.
  $R = \dfrac{820}{820+90+50+40} = \dfrac{820}{1000} = 0.8200$.
  $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.8677 \cdot 0.8200}{0.8677 + 0.8200} = 0.8432$.

- **Dog:** $P = \dfrac{840}{90+840+30+35} = \dfrac{840}{995} = 0.8442$.
  $R = \dfrac{840}{90+840+40+50} = \dfrac{840}{1020} = 0.8235$.
  $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.8442 \cdot 0.8235}{0.8442 + 0.8235} = 0.8337$.

- **Car:** $P = \dfrac{900}{50+40+900+20} = \dfrac{900}{1010} = 0.8911$.
  $R = \dfrac{900}{50+40+900+40} = \dfrac{900}{1030} = 0.8738$.
  $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.8911 \cdot 0.8738}{0.8911 + 0.8738} = 0.8824$.

- **Horse:** $P = \dfrac{920}{40+50+40+920} = \dfrac{920}{1050} = 0.8762$.
  $R = \dfrac{920}{40+50+40+920} = \dfrac{920}{1050} = 0.8762$.
  $F_1 = 0.8762$.

**Step 2 — Macro-Averaged F1.**

$$F_1^{\text{macro}} = \frac{0.8432 + 0.8337 + 0.8824 + 0.8762}{4} = \frac{3.4355}{4} = 0.8589$$

**Step 3 — Most Confused Pair.**
The off-diagonal cell with the largest value is $C_{\text{Cat, Dog}} = 90$, meaning **Cat is most often misclassified as Dog** (and vice-versa, $C_{\text{Dog, Cat}} = 70$). Total mutual confusion = 160. This is intuitive since cats and dogs share fur, four legs, and similar indoor contexts.

**Comment:** The classifier performs best on the **Car** class (high intra-class consistency, distinctive shape) and worst on the **Cat/Dog** pair (high inter-class visual similarity). *(1 mark for interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — 60 Second Scan:**

- ✅ Multi-class = $K \geq 3$ **mutually exclusive** classes. For independent labels, use **multi-label** with sigmoid + BCE instead.
- ✅ **Softmax** converts logits to probabilities: $\hat{p}_k = e^{z_k}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$. Properties: sums to 1, positive, translation-invariant.
- ✅ **Cross-entropy loss** is the negative log-likelihood under the multinomial assumption: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{p}_k$.
- ✅ The **gradient identity** $\nabla_z \mathcal{L} = \hat{p} - y$ is the most important derivation — memorize it.
- ✅ **OvA** = $K$ classifiers; **OvO** = $K(K-1)/2$ classifiers; **Native softmax** = single model with $K$ outputs.
- ✅ Confusion matrix $C \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}$: rows = actual, columns = predicted. Diagonal = correct.
- ✅ **Precision** = quality of positive predictions; **Recall** = coverage of actual positives; **F1** = harmonic mean.
- ✅ **Macro F1** averages per-class equally; **micro F1** pools globally. Use macro for imbalanced data.
- ✅ Cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for classification because it avoids vanishing gradients on softmax outputs.
- ✅ Linear classifier score: $z = Wx + b$, where $W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times d}$. Final prediction: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k z_k$.
- ✅ Always **one-hot encode** labels before computing cross-entropy — forgetting this is a top-1 mistake.
- ✅ ML pipeline order: **Data → Preprocess → Features → Model → Loss → Optimizer → Validate → Test**.
- ✅ Train/test split prevents data leakage; use stratified splits for imbalanced classes.
- ✅ For numerically stable softmax, always subtract $z_{\max}$ before exponentiating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board:** **"S-O-L-D-E-R"** —
> **S**oftmax, **O**ne-hot label, **L**og, **D**erivative (p̂−y), **E**valuate (precision/recall/F1), **R**egularize (to prevent overfitting).

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Learning Use Cases; Machine Learning Models for Vision: Pretrained Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Learning Use Cases in Computer Vision

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deep Learning Use Cases in Computer Vision** refer to the application domains in which deep neural network architectures—particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Transformers, and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)—are deployed to automatically extract hierarchical features from visual data (images and videos) and perform tasks such as classification, detection, segmentation, recognition, generation, and tracking with minimal human-engineered feature design.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, deep learning use cases in vision are categorized along three dimensions:
1. **Discriminative Tasks** (Classification, Detection, Segmentation)
2. **Generative Tasks** (Super-resolution, Style Transfer, Image Synthesis)
3. **Regressive / Metric Tasks** (Depth Estimation, Pose Estimation, Optical Flow)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a deep learning model as a child learning to recognize animals:**
> - A child first sees *edges* (whiskers, fur lines) — this is what the **early convolutional layers** learn.
> - Then the child sees *parts* (eyes, ears, tails) — this is what the **middle layers** learn.
> - Finally, the child sees the *whole object* (cat, dog, elephant) — this is what the **deeper layers** learn.
> - Once the child has learned animals in general, you can quickly teach them about *specific rare breeds* by showing a few examples. This is exactly **Transfer Learning with Pretrained Models**.

### Why Deep Learning Works for Vision

- **Spatial Hierarchy**: Visual data has a natural hierarchy (pixels → edges → textures → parts → objects).
- **Weight Sharing**: Convolutions exploit translation equivariance.
- **Automatic Feature Engineering**: No need for handcrafted HOG, SIFT, or LBP descriptors.
- **Scalability**: Performance improves with more data and compute.

### Key Deep Learning Use Cases — Categorical Overview

| Use Case | Task Type | Sample Application | Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Image Classification | Discriminative | Disease diagnosis from X-ray | Healthcare |
| Object Detection | Discriminative | Pedestrian detection | Autonomous Vehicles |
| Semantic Segmentation | Discriminative | Tumor boundary detection | Medical Imaging |
| Instance Segmentation | Discriminative | Counting cells in microscopy | Biology |
| Face Recognition | Metric | Phone unlock, surveillance | Security |
| Pose Estimation | Regressive | Sports analytics, AR/VR | Entertainment |
| Image Captioning | Generative | Accessibility for blind | Social Good |
| Style Transfer | Generative | Artistic filters | Creative Industry |
| Super-Resolution | Generative | Satellite image sharpening | Defense |
| Image Generation (GAN/Diffusion) | Generative | Synthetic training data | Research |
| Action Recognition | Discriminative | Violence detection in CCTV | Surveillance |
| Depth Estimation | Regressive | 3D scene reconstruction | Robotics |

### Standard Reference Datasets in Pretrained Vision Models

> [!NOTE]
> Most pretrained models in vision are originally trained on **ImageNet (ILSVRC-2012)** — a dataset containing **1.28 million** training images across **1000 classes**. This is the de facto pretraining benchmark for vision.

Other critical datasets:
- **COCO (Common Objects in Context)** — **330K images**, 80 object categories, used for detection/segmentation.
- **PASCAL VOC** — **20 classes**, 11.5K images.
- **Cityscapes** — Urban driving scenes for segmentation.
- **MNIST / CIFAR-10/100** — Low-resolution benchmarks.

---

## 1.2 Machine Learning Models for Vision: Pretrained Models

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **A Pretrained Model** in computer vision is a deep neural network whose weights have already been learned on a large-scale dataset (typically ImageNet) and can be reused as a starting point for a new, related task, either as a fixed feature extractor or as an initialization for fine-tuning.

The two primary paradigms for using pretrained models are:
1. **Feature Extraction** — Freeze convolutional base, train only the classifier head.
2. **Fine-Tuning** — Unfreeze some/all convolutional layers and continue training on the new dataset.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pretrained Models = Standing on the Shoulders of Giants.**
> - Imagine you need to build a house, but someone has already dug the foundation, poured the concrete, and erected the structural frame. You just need to add the walls, windows, and roof. That foundation is the **pretrained model**.
> - In academic terms: instead of initializing weights randomly (which requires enormous data and compute), you **inherit** useful, general-purpose visual features from a model that has already seen millions of images.

### Why Pretrained Models Are Essential (Engineering Motivation)

- **Data Efficiency**: Modern deep networks like ResNet-152 have **~60 million** parameters. Training from scratch requires millions of labeled images. A typical KTU student project may have only 500–5000 images.
- **Compute Efficiency**: Training ResNet from scratch takes **~10^18 FLOPs** and several days on multi-GPU clusters. Using pretrained weights reduces training to minutes on a single GPU.
- **Better Generalization**: Pretrained models have learned robust, low-bias features that improve performance on small datasets.
- **Faster Convergence**: Fine-tuning typically converges in **10–50 epochs**, whereas training from scratch may need **200+ epochs**.

### The Pretrained Model Ecosystem (Standards & Libraries)

| Library | Origin | Key Models Hosted | Common Framework |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torchvision.models** | PyTorch Team | ResNet, VGG, AlexNet, EfficientNet | PyTorch |
| **TensorFlow Hub** | Google | Inception, MobileNet, EfficientNet | TensorFlow |
| **Hugging Face Transformers** | HuggingFace | ViT, DETR, CLIP, SAM | PyTorch / TF |
| **Keras Applications** | Keras Team | Xception, NASNet, ResNet | TensorFlow |
| **ONNX Model Zoo** | Microsoft / Community | Cross-framework | Framework-agnostic |
| **timm (Ross Wightman)** | Community | 700+ models | PyTorch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transfer learning equation** that governs pretrained models is: `Knowledge from Source Domain (D_S) → Task (T_S) → New Target Domain (D_T) → New Task (T_T)`. The assumption is that `D_S ∩ D_T ≠ ∅` (low-level visual features transfer universally).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Pretrained Models Transfer

### The Transfer Learning Principle

The core hypothesis underlying pretrained models is the **Hierarchical Feature Reusability Hypothesis** (Yosinski et al., 2014):

> Lower convolutional layers learn **generic features** (edges, color blobs, textures) that are useful across almost all visual tasks, while deeper layers learn **task-specific features** (object parts, classes) that are more dataset-dependent.

This is the empirical justification for the "freeze-early, fine-tune-late" strategy in transfer learning.

### Operational Pipeline of Using a Pretrained Model

1. **Select a Source Model**: Choose a pretrained CNN (e.g., ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet).
2. **Remove the Classification Head**: Discard the final fully-connected layer (which has 1000 ImageNet outputs).
3. **Choose Adaptation Strategy**:
   - *Feature Extraction*: Freeze all conv layers, attach a new classifier, train only the new classifier.
   - *Fine-Tuning*: Unfreeze the last few conv blocks (or all) and train with a **low learning rate** (typically `1e-4` to `1e-5`).
4. **Configure Optimizer Differently**:
   - *Pre-trained layers*: Use a smaller learning rate.
   - *New classifier head*: Use the original (larger) learning rate.
5. **Train and Evaluate** on the target dataset.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Logic of a Pretrained CNN

A pretrained CNN can be formally expressed as a composition of functions:

$$
F(x) = f_L \circ f_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ f_2 \circ f_1(x)
$$

Where each layer $f_i$ performs:

$$
f_i(x) = \sigma(W_i * x + b_i)
$$

With:
- $W_i$ = convolution kernel weights at layer $i$
- $*$ = convolution operator
- $b_i$ = bias term
- $\sigma$ = non-linear activation (ReLU, GELU, SiLU)
- The final $f_L$ is typically a **softmax classifier** that maps feature vectors to class probabilities.

For **transfer learning**, we replace $f_L$ with $f_L^{new}$:

$$
F_{transfer}(x) = f_L^{new} \circ f_{L-1} \circ \dots \circ f_1(x)
$$

Where $f_L^{new}$ is randomly initialized and trained, while $W_1, \dots, W_{L-1}$ are inherited from pretraining.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 1: Core Formulas for Pretrained CNN Models

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Convolution Operation** | $y[i,j] = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} W[m,n] \cdot x[i+m, j+n]$ | Pixels (intensity values) | Core feature extraction |
| **Output Feature Map Size** | $O = \lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \rfloor + 1$ | Integer (pixels) | Layer dimensioning |
| **Receptive Field** | $r_l = r_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$ | Pixels | Context captured by layer |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Nats or bits | Classification objective |
| **Softmax Function** | $\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$ | Probability $\in [0,1]$ | Multi-class output |
| **Fine-Tune Learning Rate Scaling** | $\eta_{FT} = \eta_0 \cdot \beta^{k}$ | Scalar (multiplier) | Per-layer LR adaptation |
| **Transfer Learning Accuracy Gain** | $\Delta A = A_{pretrained} - A_{scratch}$ | Percentage points | Empirical justification |
| **Top-1 Accuracy (ImageNet)** | $A_{top1} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[\arg\max \hat{y}_i = y_i]$ | Percentage | Standard benchmark metric |
| **Top-5 Accuracy (ImageNet)** | $A_{top5} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i \in \text{Top-5}(\hat{y})]$ | Percentage | Standard benchmark metric |
| **Freezing Indicator** | $\theta_{frozen} \Rightarrow \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_{frozen}} = 0$ | Boolean | Disables gradient updates |
| **GFLOPs (Model Complexity)** | $G = 2 \cdot H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot K^2 \cdot 10^{-9}$ | Giga-FLOPs | Hardware planning |
| **Parameter Count (FC Layer)** | $P_{FC} = N_{in} \cdot N_{out} + N_{out}$ | Scalar | Memory footprint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $I$ = input spatial size
> - $K$ = kernel size
> - $P$ = padding
> - $S$ = stride
> - $H, W$ = feature map height, width
> - $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = input/output channels
> - $\beta$ = learning rate decay factor (typically 0.1 per decade)
> - $k$ = layer index from the output
> - $C$ = number of classes
> - $N$ = dataset size

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Major Pretrained Architectures

### A. VGG-16 / VGG-19 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014)

- **Depth**: 16 / 19 weight layers
- **Parameters**: **~138M** (VGG-16), **~143M** (VGG-19)
- **Input Size**: $224 \times 224 \times 3$
- **Kernel**: Uniform $3 \times 3$ convolutions
- **Strength**: Simple, uniform architecture; excellent feature extractor
- **Weakness**: Very large memory footprint (over **500MB** for weights)
- **Use Today**: Backbone for style transfer, perceptual loss

### B. ResNet-50 / ResNet-101 / ResNet-152 (He et al., 2015)

- **Depth**: 50 / 101 / 152 layers
- **Parameters**: **~25.6M / 44.5M / 60.2M**
- **Key Innovation**: **Residual connections** — identity shortcuts that solve vanishing gradients
- **Residual Block Formula**:

$$
y = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x
$$

Where $F(x, \{W_i\})$ is the residual mapping to be learned, and $x$ is the identity skip connection.

- **Top-1 ImageNet Accuracy**: **76.1% (ResNet-50)**, **78.6% (ResNet-152)**
- **Use Today**: Industry standard backbone; default for many detection/segmentation pipelines

### C. Inception-v3 (Szegedy et al., 2016)

- **Parameters**: **~23.8M**
- **Key Innovation**: Multi-scale parallel convolutions (1x1, 3x3, 5x5 in same layer)
- **Top-1 ImageNet Accuracy**: **77.9%**
- **Use Today**: Edge devices, transfer learning for medical imaging

### D. MobileNet-v2 / v3 (Howard et al., 2017/2019)

- **Parameters**: **~3.4M (v2)**, **~5.4M (v3-Large)**
- **Key Innovation**: **Depthwise Separable Convolutions** — factorize a standard convolution into depthwise + pointwise operations
- **FLOPs Reduction**: ~8–9× fewer FLOPs than standard convolution
- **Use Today**: Mobile apps, embedded systems, real-time inference

### E. EfficientNet-B0 to B7 (Tan & Le, 2019)

- **Compound Scaling**: Uniformly scales depth, width, and resolution using a compound coefficient $\phi$
- **Scaling Rule**:

$$
d = \alpha^{\phi}, \quad w = \beta^{\phi}, \quad r = \gamma^{\phi}
$$

Subject to: $\alpha \cdot \beta^2 \cdot \gamma^2 \approx 2$, with $\alpha \geq 1, \beta \geq 1, \gamma \geq 1$.

- **Top-1 Accuracy vs Parameters**: Outperforms predecessors at every scale
- **Use Today**: Google production pipelines, AutoML-derived models

### F. Vision Transformer (ViT) (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020)

- **Architecture**: Applies a pure Transformer (originally from NLP) to image patches
- **Input Processing**: Image is split into fixed-size patches (e.g., $16 \times 16$), linearly embedded, position-encoded
- **Key Innovation**: No convolutions; relies entirely on **self-attention**
- **Self-Attention Formula**:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

- **Use Today**: Foundation for CLIP, DINO, SAM, DETR

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Pretrained Model Used | Task | Business Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | CheXNet (DenseNet-121) | Pneumonia detection | Faster radiology |
| **Autonomous Driving** | YOLOv8, ResNet-101 | Pedestrian/vehicle detection | Safety critical |
| **Retail** | ResNet-50, ViT | Visual product search | E-commerce engagement |
| **Agriculture** | EfficientNet-B3 | Plant disease classification | Crop yield improvement |
| **Security** | FaceNet, ArcFace | Face recognition | Access control |
| **Manufacturing** | ResNet-50 | Defect detection | Quality assurance |
| **Satellite Imaging** | U-Net + ResNet backbone | Building segmentation | Urban planning |
| **Social Media** | CLIP, BLIP | Content moderation | Trust & safety |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Output Size of a Pretrained CNN

### Problem Statement

Given a pretrained ResNet-50 model receiving an input image of size $224 \times 224 \times 3$, calculate the spatial size of the output feature map after the **first convolutional block** (conv1: kernel size $7 \times 7$, stride $2$, padding $3$, 64 output channels).

### Step 1: Identify the Standard Output Size Formula

For a single convolution operation, the output spatial dimension $O$ is given by:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Known Values

We are given:
- Input size $I = 224$
- Kernel size $K = 7$
- Padding $P = 3$
- Stride $S = 2$

Plugging these into the formula:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{224 - 7 + 2(3)}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Numerator

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$
224 - 7 = 217
$$

$$
2(3) = 6
$$

$$
217 + 6 = 223
$$

### Step 4: Perform the Division

$$
\frac{223}{2} = 111.5
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Floor Function

$$
\left\lfloor 111.5 \right\rfloor = 111
$$

### Step 6: Add 1

$$
O = 111 + 1 = 112
$$

### Step 7: Final Output Specification

After the first conv block in ResNet-50, the feature map is:

$$
\text{Output Shape} = 112 \times 112 \times 64
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check**: ResNet-50 architecture documentation confirms the first block produces a $112 \times 112 \times 64$ feature map. ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Receptive Field Calculation

### Problem Statement

For a pretrained AlexNet architecture, calculate the **theoretical receptive field** of neurons in the 5th convolutional layer (conv5). Given:
- conv1: $K_1 = 11, S_1 = 4$
- conv2: $K_2 = 5, S_2 = 1$ (with pool: $S_{p1} = 2$)
- conv3: $K_3 = 3, S_3 = 1$ (with pool: $S_{p2} = 2$)
- conv4: $K_4 = 3, S_4 = 1$
- conv5: $K_5 = 3, S_5 = 1$ (with pool: $S_{p3} = 2$)

### Step 1: Receptive Field Recursion Formula

The recursive formula for receptive field at layer $l$:

$$
r_l = r_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i
$$

With base case: $r_0 = 1$.

### Step 2: Compute Cumulative Stride Product Up to Each Layer

Let $s_l = \prod_{i=1}^{l} S_i$ where we include pooling strides.

For conv1: $s_1 = 4$
For conv2 (after pool): $s_2 = 4 \times 2 = 8$
For conv3 (after pool): $s_3 = 8 \times 2 = 16$
For conv4: $s_4 = 16$
For conv5 (after pool): $s_5 = 16 \times 2 = 32$

### Step 3: Apply the Recursion Step by Step

**Layer 1** (conv1, $K_1 = 11$):

$$
r_1 = 1 + (11 - 1) \cdot 1 = 11
$$

**Layer 2** (conv2, $K_2 = 5$):

$$
r_2 = 11 + (5 - 1) \cdot 4 = 11 + 16 = 27
$$

**Layer 3** (conv3, $K_3 = 3$):

$$
r_3 = 27 + (3 - 1) \cdot 8 = 27 + 16 = 43
$$

**Layer 4** (conv4, $K_4 = 3$):

$$
r_4 = 43 + (3 - 1) \cdot 16 = 43 + 32 = 75
$$

**Layer 5** (conv5, $K_5 = 3$):

$$
r_5 = 75 + (3 - 1) \cdot 16 = 75 + 32 = 107
$$

### Step 4: Final Answer

The receptive field of conv5 neurons in AlexNet is:

$$
r_5 = 107 \text{ pixels}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means each neuron in the 5th convolutional layer of AlexNet "sees" a $107 \times 107$ region of the original $227 \times 227$ input image — which is nearly the entire image, justifying its use in ImageNet classification.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Cross-Entropy Loss for Transfer Learning

### Problem Statement

A pretrained ResNet-50 is being fine-tuned on a 5-class custom dataset. After a forward pass, the model's logits for one sample are $z = [2.0, 0.5, -1.0, 3.0, 0.2]$ and the true label is class index 3. Compute the **cross-entropy loss**.

### Step 1: Convert Logits to Probabilities Using Softmax

The softmax function:

$$
\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}
$$

Compute exponentials:

$$
e^{z_1} = e^{2.0} \approx 7.389
$$

$$
e^{z_2} = e^{0.5} \approx 1.649
$$

$$
e^{z_3} = e^{-1.0} \approx 0.368
$$

$$
e^{z_4} = e^{3.0} \approx 20.086
$$

$$
e^{z_5} = e^{0.2} \approx 1.221
$$

### Step 2: Sum the Exponentials

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} e^{z_j} = 7.389 + 1.649 + 0.368 + 20.086 + 1.221 = 30.713
$$

### Step 3: Compute Each Probability

$$
\hat{y}_1 = \frac{7.389}{30.713} \approx 0.2406
$$

$$
\hat{y}_2 = \frac{1.649}{30.713} \approx 0.0537
$$

$$
\hat{y}_3 = \frac{0.368}{30.713} \approx 0.0120
$$

$$
\hat{y}_4 = \frac{20.086}{30.713} \approx 0.6540
$$

$$
\hat{y}_5 = \frac{1.221}{30.713} \approx 0.0398
$$

### Step 4: Apply Cross-Entropy Formula

$$
L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)
$$

Since the true label is class 3 (0-indexed), $y_4 = 1$ and all other $y_c = 0$. So:

$$
L = -\log(\hat{y}_4) = -\log(0.6540)
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Logarithm

$$
\log(0.6540) \approx -0.4246
$$

$$
L = -(-0.4246) = 0.4246
$$

### Step 6: Final Answer

The cross-entropy loss is:

$$
L \approx 0.425 \text{ nats}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: Since the true class has high predicted probability (65.4%), the loss is small. During fine-tuning, the optimizer will adjust weights to push this probability closer to 1.0, reducing the loss toward 0.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pretrained Models

| Model | Year | Top-1 Acc (%) | Params (M) | GFLOPs | Pretrain Dataset | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AlexNet | 2012 | 63.3 | 60 | 0.7 | ImageNet | Baseline / Education |
| VGG-16 | 2014 | 71.5 | 138 | 15.5 | ImageNet | Feature extraction |
| ResNet-50 | 2015 | 76.1 | 25.6 | 4.1 | ImageNet | General purpose |
| ResNet-152 | 2015 | 78.6 | 60.2 | 11.6 | ImageNet | High accuracy |
| Inception-v3 | 2016 | 77.9 | 23.8 | 5.7 | ImageNet | Multi-scale features |
| MobileNet-v2 | 2018 | 72.0 | 3.4 | 0.3 | ImageNet | Mobile / Edge |
| EfficientNet-B0 | 2019 | 77.3 | 5.3 | 0.4 | ImageNet | Efficiency sweet spot |
| EfficientNet-B7 | 2019 | 84.4 | 66 | 37 | ImageNet | SOTA accuracy |
| ViT-B/16 | 2020 | 84.2 | 86 | 17.6 | ImageNet-21k | Transformer-based |
| ConvNeXt-Base | 2022 | 85.8 | 88.6 | 15.4 | ImageNet-22k | Modern CNN |
| Swin Transformer | 2021 | 86.3 | 88 | 15.4 | ImageNet-22k | Hierarchical attention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam**: The question "Compare ResNet and VGG in terms of accuracy and parameters" is a frequently tested concept. The expected answer must highlight **ResNet's residual connections** as the key innovation enabling deeper networks with **fewer parameters and higher accuracy**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Transfer Learning Pipeline with a Pretrained Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Pretrained CNN Backbone]
    B --> C[Conv Block 1: 112x112x64]
    C --> D[Conv Block 2: 56x56x256]
    D --> E[Conv Block 3: 28x28x512]
    E --> F[Conv Block 4: 14x14x1024]
    F --> G[Conv Block 5: 7x7x2048]
    G --> H{Fine-Tuning Strategy?}
    H -->|Feature Extraction| I[Freeze all conv layers]
    H -->|Fine-Tuning| J[Unfreeze last blocks]
    I --> K[Global Average Pooling]
    J --> K
    K --> L[New Classifier Head: Fully Connected]
    L --> M[Softmax Output: C classes]
    M --> N[Predicted Class Label]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Feature Extraction vs. Fine-Tuning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FE["Feature Extraction (Small Dataset)"]
        FE1[Input Image] --> FE2[Conv Layers - FROZEN]
        FE2 --> FE3[New Classifier - TRAINED]
        FE3 --> FE4[Output]
    end
    subgraph FT["Fine-Tuning (Moderate Dataset)"]
        FT1[Input Image] --> FT2[Early Conv Layers - FROZEN]
        FT2 --> FT3[Late Conv Layers - TRAINED with low LR]
        FT3 --> FT4[New Classifier - TRAINED with high LR]
        FT4 --> FT5[Output]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: ResNet Residual Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input x] --> B[Conv 1x1 - Reduce dims]
    B --> C[Batch Norm + ReLU]
    C --> D[Conv 3x3 - Main conv]
    D --> E[Batch Norm + ReLU]
    E --> F[Conv 1x1 - Expand dims]
    F --> G[Batch Norm]
    A --> H[Identity Shortcut]
    G --> I[Add: F(x) + x]
    H --> I
    I --> J[ReLU Activation]
    J --> K[Output y]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Vision Transformer (ViT) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Split into 16x16 Patches]
    B --> C[Linear Embedding of Patches]
    C --> D[Add Position Embeddings]
    D --> E[Add learnable class token]
    E --> F[Transformer Encoder Block x12]
    F --> G[MLP Head for Classification]
    G --> H[Class Prediction]
    subgraph ENC["Transformer Encoder Block"]
        ENC1[Layer Norm] --> ENC2[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
        ENC2 --> ENC3[Residual Connection]
        ENC3 --> ENC4[Layer Norm]
        ENC4 --> ENC5[MLP Feed-Forward]
        ENC5 --> ENC6[Residual Connection]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Deep Learning Use Case Categorization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deep Learning Use Cases in CV] --> B[Discriminative Tasks]
    A --> C[Generative Tasks]
    A --> D[Regressive Tasks]
    A --> E[Metric Learning Tasks]
    B --> B1[Image Classification]
    B --> B2[Object Detection]
    B --> B3[Semantic Segmentation]
    B --> B4[Action Recognition]
    C --> C1[Image Generation - GANs]
    C --> C2[Style Transfer]
    C --> C3[Super-Resolution]
    C --> C4[Image Inpainting]
    D --> D1[Depth Estimation]
    D --> D2[Pose Estimation]
    D --> D3[Optical Flow]
    D --> D4[3D Reconstruction]
    E --> E1[Face Recognition]
    E --> E2[Image Retrieval]
    E --> E3[Re-Identification]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Pretrained Model Loading Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Pretrained Model: ResNet50] --> B[Load Weights from Torchvision]
    B --> C{Replace Classifier Head?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Remove final FC layer]
    D --> E[Add new FC with C outputs]
    E --> F{Adaptation Strategy?}
    C -->|No| F
    F -->|Feature Extraction| G[Freeze all params]
    F -->|Fine-Tuning| H[Freeze early layers]
    H --> I[Unfreeze later layers with low LR]
    G --> J[Train on target dataset]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Evaluate on validation set]
    K --> L{Performance Acceptable?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Deploy Model]
    L -->|No| N[Adjust hyperparameters]
    N --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note**: All mermaid diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and clean uppercase labels without markdown formatting tags, ensuring successful rendering in GitHub, VSCode, and standard Mermaid renderers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pretrained Model in the Context of Computer Vision

**Model Answer:**

A pretrained model in computer vision is a deep neural network, typically a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), whose weights have already been learned by training on a large-scale benchmark dataset, most commonly **ImageNet (ILSVRC-2012)** containing 1.28 million images across 1000 classes. The pretrained weights capture **generic, low-to-mid level visual features** such as edges, textures, color gradients, and object parts, which are useful for a wide range of visual tasks.

The two main advantages of pretrained models are:
1. They reduce the requirement for large labeled datasets in the target task.
2. They significantly reduce training time and computational cost.

Pretrained models are used via two main strategies:
- **Feature Extraction**: The convolutional base is frozen and used as a fixed feature extractor. Only a new classifier head is trained.
- **Fine-Tuning**: The pretrained weights are used as initialization, and some or all layers are retrained on the new dataset with a low learning rate.

**Common pretrained models include VGG-16, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, MobileNet-v2, and EfficientNet-B0.**

---

### Question 2: List Any Five Deep Learning Use Cases in Computer Vision

**Model Answer:**

The following are five major deep learning use cases in computer vision:

1. **Image Classification**: Assigning a single class label to an entire image (e.g., ResNet classifying cats vs. dogs).

2. **Object Detection**: Locating and classifying multiple objects in an image with bounding boxes (e.g., YOLO, Faster R-CNN detecting pedestrians and cars).

3. **Semantic Segmentation**: Assigning a class label to every pixel in an image (e.g., U-Net segmenting medical scans into tumor/background regions).

4. **Face Recognition**: Identifying or verifying a person based on facial features (e.g., FaceNet, ArcFace in smartphone unlock).

5. **Pose Estimation**: Predicting the spatial configuration of human body joints (e.g., OpenPose, HRNet used in sports analytics and AR/VR).

**Other notable use cases**: Image generation (GANs, Diffusion models), style transfer, super-resolution, depth estimation, and visual question answering.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Pretrained Models and Transfer Learning

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**: 
Explain the concept of pretrained models in deep learning. Discuss the architecture of ResNet-50 with a neat block diagram. Why are residual connections important?

**Model Answer:**

A **pretrained model** is a deep neural network that has been previously trained on a large benchmark dataset and whose learned weights can be reused for a related task through transfer learning.

**Why ResNet-50?**
- It has 50 weight layers, ~25.6M parameters, and uses **residual connections** to enable training of very deep networks without suffering from vanishing gradients.

**ResNet-50 Architecture (Block Diagram)**:

The architecture consists of:
- **Initial Conv Layer**: $7 \times 7$ conv, 64 filters, stride 2
- **Max Pooling**: $3 \times 3$, stride 2
- **4 Stages of Residual Blocks**: Each stage contains multiple bottleneck blocks (1x1 → 3x3 → 1x1 convolutions)
- **Global Average Pooling**
- **Fully Connected Layer (1000 classes for ImageNet)**

**Residual Block Formula**:

$$
y = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x
$$

Where $F(x, \{W_i\})$ is the residual function to be learned, and $x$ is the identity shortcut.

**Importance of Residual Connections**:
1. They enable the training of networks that are **much deeper** (e.g., ResNet-152, ResNet-200) without performance degradation.
2. They mitigate the **vanishing gradient problem** by providing a direct path for gradient flow during backpropagation.
3. They allow the network to learn an **identity mapping** when additional layers are not useful, ensuring that deeper networks perform at least as well as shallower ones.
4. They reduce the **optimization difficulty** by reformulating the layers to learn residual mappings $F(x)$ rather than the full transformation $H(x)$.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**:
Compare feature extraction and fine-tuning approaches when using pretrained models. When is each approach preferred?

**Model Answer:**

**Feature Extraction**:
- The convolutional base of the pretrained model is **frozen** (no weight updates).
- Only a new classifier head (fully connected layers) is trained on the target dataset.
- Computational cost is low; training is fast.
- Suitable when the target dataset is **very small** (e.g., a few hundred images) and similar in domain to the source dataset (e.g., ImageNet).
- The pretrained features act as a **fixed descriptor** of the image.

**Fine-Tuning**:
- Some or all layers of the pretrained model are **unfrozen** and continue training on the new dataset.
- A **low learning rate** (typically $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $1 \times 10^{-5}$) is used to avoid drastic weight changes that would erase the pretrained knowledge — this is called **catastrophic forgetting**.
- Suitable when the target dataset is **moderate to large** (e.g., thousands to millions of images) or when the target domain **differs significantly** from the source (e.g., medical images vs. ImageNet natural images).
- Can yield higher accuracy than feature extraction but requires more compute and is more prone to overfitting on small datasets.

**Comparative Table**:

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Conv Layers** | Frozen | Partially or fully trainable |
| **Classifier Head** | Trainable | Trainable |
| **Learning Rate** | Standard (e.g., $1e^{-3}$) | Low for pre-trained layers (e.g., $1e^{-4}$) |
| **Training Time** | Fast | Slow |
| **Data Required** | Very small | Moderate to large |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low | Higher |
| **Performance** | Moderate | Higher (typically) |

**Decision Rule**:
- Dataset size < 1000 samples → **Feature Extraction**
- 1000 < Dataset size < 100,000 → **Fine-Tune last few layers**
- Dataset size > 100,000 → **Full Fine-Tuning or train from scratch**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Deep Learning Use Cases and Vision Architectures

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks**:
Discuss at least five deep learning use cases in computer vision. For each, mention a suitable architecture and a real-world application.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Image Classification**:
- **Architecture**: ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0
- **Application**: Skin cancer classification (HAM10000 dataset) — classifies dermatoscopic images as benign/malignant.
- **Output**: Single class label per image.

**2. Object Detection**:
- **Architecture**: YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, DETR
- **Application**: Autonomous vehicles detecting pedestrians, traffic signs, and other vehicles in real time.
- **Output**: Bounding boxes + class labels.

**3. Semantic Segmentation**:
- **Architecture**: U-Net, DeepLab-v3+, SegFormer
- **Application**: Medical image segmentation — delineating tumor boundaries in MRI/CT scans.
- **Output**: Per-pixel class label.

**4. Image Generation**:
- **Architecture**: Stable Diffusion, StyleGAN3, DALL-E
- **Application**: Creating synthetic training data, artistic content creation, virtual try-on for fashion e-commerce.
- **Output**: Synthesized images from text prompts or noise vectors.

**5. Face Recognition**:
- **Architecture**: FaceNet, ArcFace, DeepFace
- **Application**: Biometric authentication in smartphones, surveillance systems.
- **Output**: 128-D embedding vector used for verification/identification via cosine similarity.

**6. Pose Estimation** (bonus):
- **Architecture**: OpenPose, HRNet
- **Application**: Sports analytics, AR/VR body tracking, clinical gait analysis.

**7. Super-Resolution** (bonus):
- **Architecture**: ESRGAN, SRGAN
- **Application**: Enhancing satellite imagery, upscaling old photographs, medical image enhancement.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks**:
Explain the architecture of the Vision Transformer (ViT). How does it differ from CNN-based pretrained models, and what are its limitations?

**Model Answer:**

**Vision Transformer (ViT) — Architecture**:

The Vision Transformer, introduced by Dosovitskiy et al. (2020), applies a pure Transformer encoder (originally designed for NLP) directly to images. The pipeline is:

1. **Patch Embedding**: The input image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ is split into a grid of fixed-size patches (e.g., $16 \times 16$ pixels). Each patch is flattened and linearly projected into a $D$-dimensional embedding. For a $224 \times 224$ image with $16 \times 16$ patches, this yields $N = 196$ patch tokens.

2. **Position Embedding**: Learnable 1-D position embeddings are added to the patch embeddings to retain spatial information, since self-attention is permutation-invariant.

3. **Class Token**: A learnable `[CLS]` token is prepended to the sequence, analogous to BERT. Its final state is used for classification.

4. **Transformer Encoder**: The sequence is passed through $L$ stacked encoder blocks, each containing:
   - **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA)**
   - **Layer Normalization**
   - **MLP (Feed-Forward) Block**
   - **Residual Connections**

5. **Classification Head**: An MLP head applied to the final `[CLS]` token produces class probabilities via softmax.

**Self-Attention Formula**:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, ViT) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) ViT
$$

Where $Q$, $K$, and $ViT$ are the query, key, and value matrices, and $d_k$ is the dimension of the keys.

**Key Differences from CNN-based Models**:

| Aspect | CNN (e.g., ResNet) | ViT |
|---|---|---|
| **Inductive Bias** | Locality, translation equivariance | None (learns spatial relations) |
| **Receptive Field** | Local, grows with depth | Global from layer 1 |
| **Data Requirement** | Works with moderate data | Requires huge data (e.g., JFT-300M, ImageNet-21k) |
| **Inductive Prior** | Built-in via convolutions | Must be learned from data |
| **Compute (Training)** | Lower | Higher (quadratic in patch count) |
| **Interpretability** | Filter visualizations | Attention map visualizations |

**Limitations of ViT**:
1. **Data Hungry**: ViT underperforms CNNs when trained on small datasets (e.g., ImageNet-1k). It requires large-scale pretraining (JFT-300M or ImageNet-21k).
2. **Computationally Expensive**: Self-attention is $O(N^2)$ in the number of patches, making it expensive for high-resolution images.
3. **No Built-in Translation Invariance**: Unlike CNNs, ViT must learn spatial equivariance from data, which requires more samples.
4. **Quadratic Memory Cost**: The attention matrix scales quadratically with the number of tokens, limiting maximum input resolution.

**Hybrid Models** (e.g., Swin Transformer, ConvNeXt) have been proposed to combine the strengths of both CNNs (local inductive bias) and Transformers (global attention).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Pretrained Models with Random Initialization**: A common student error is to describe ResNet architecture without mentioning that it is *pretrained* on ImageNet. Always explicitly state the pretraining dataset.
> 2. **Avoid conflating "Feature Extraction" with "Fine-Tuning"**: Many students use these terms interchangeably in KTU answers, which costs significant marks. State clearly that feature extraction = frozen conv base; fine-tuning = trainable conv base with low LR.
> 3. **Missing Numerical Justification**: For ResNet questions, KTU examiners expect students to mention the vanishing gradient problem and the identity shortcut mathematically as $y = F(x) + x$. Do not skip the formula.
> 4. **ImageNet Numbers Must Be Accurate**: ImageNet has 1.28M training images, 1000 classes, and 50,000 validation images. Mixing up these numbers costs marks.
> 5. **In ViT Answers, Always Mention the Patch Tokenization Step**: Simply saying "ViT applies a Transformer to images" is too vague. The patch embedding step ($16 \times 16$ patches → linear projection → position embedding → CLS token) is mandatory.
> 6. **Forgetting to Specify Real-World Use Cases**: KTU's 2024 NEP scheme emphasizes outcome-based learning. Every use case should be tied to a real-world application (e.g., "ResNet-50 used in CheXNet for pneumonia detection from chest X-rays").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Pretrained Model** = A deep network (e.g., ResNet-50) already trained on a large dataset (typically **ImageNet, 1.28M images, 1000 classes**).
- [ ] **Transfer Learning** = Reusing pretrained weights for a new, related task.
- [ ] **Two Strategies**: (1) Feature Extraction (freeze conv base), (2) Fine-Tuning (unfreeze some layers with low LR).
- [ ] **Catastrophic Forgetting**: Risk during fine-tuning if learning rate is too high — pretrained knowledge gets overwritten.

### Architecture Highlights
- [ ] **ResNet-50**: 50 layers, 25.6M parameters, residual connections formula $y = F(x) + x$, ~76% Top-1 ImageNet accuracy.
- [ ] **VGG-16**: 16 layers, 138M parameters, uniform $3 \times 3$ filters, ~71.5% Top-1 accuracy.
- [ ] **EfficientNet**: Compound scaling of depth, width, and resolution via $\alpha^\phi, \beta^\phi, \gamma^\phi$.
- [ ] **MobileNet**: Depthwise separable convolutions for edge devices, ~3.4M parameters.
- [ ] **ViT**: Splits image into patches, applies Transformer encoder, requires large-scale pretraining.
- [ ] **Inception-v3**: Multi-scale parallel convolutions (1×1, 3×3, 5×5), ~23.8M parameters.

### Must-Know Formulas
- [ ] Output size: $O = \lfloor (I - K + 2P)/S \rfloor + 1$
- [ ] Cross-entropy loss: $L = -\sum y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$
- [ ] Softmax: $\hat{y}_i = e^{z_i} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$
- [ ] Residual block: $y = F(x, \{W_i\}) + x$
- [ ] Self-attention: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(Q K^T / \sqrt{d_k}) V$

### Deep Learning Use Cases (Minimum 5 to Memorize)
- [ ] Image Classification (ResNet)
- [ ] Object Detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN)
- [ ] Semantic Segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab)
- [ ] Face Recognition (FaceNet, ArcFace)
- [ ] Pose Estimation (OpenPose, HRNet)
- [ ] Image Generation (StyleGAN, Stable Diffusion)
- [ ] Super-Resolution (ESRGAN)

### High-Yield Exam Triggers
- [ ] "Explain transfer learning with pretrained models" → 7-mark question
- [ ] "Compare ResNet and VGG" → Always mention residual connections and parameter counts
- [ ] "Discuss deep learning applications in healthcare / autonomous vehicles" → Industry 4.0 focus
- [ ] "Differentiate between CNN and Vision Transformer" → 2024 trending topic

### Common Library References
- [ ] **torchvision.models** → ResNet, VGG, AlexNet, EfficientNet (PyTorch)
- [ ] **timm** → 700+ pretrained models (Ross Wightman, PyTorch)
- [ ] **Hugging Face** → ViT, DETR, CLIP, SAM (transformer-based)
- [ ] **TensorFlow Hub / Keras Applications** → Inception, MobileNet, Xception

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: Always tie theoretical concepts to **real-world applications** and **numerical benchmarks** (e.g., "ResNet-50 achieves 76.1% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet with 25.6M parameters, making it ideal for medical imaging transfer learning with small datasets"). This demonstrates outcome-based learning mastery as required by the NEP 2020 framework.

topics.title: Transfer Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transfer Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transfer Learning (TL)** is a deep learning paradigm in which a neural network model developed (pre-trained) for a *source domain* and *source task* is repurposed as the starting point for a second model trained on a *target domain* and *target task*. It is a cornerstone technique in modern Computer Vision where labeled data is scarce or expensive to obtain.

Mathematically, transfer learning involves a **source domain** $\mathcal{D}_S$ with learning task $\mathcal{T}_S$ and a **target domain** $\mathcal{D}_T$ with learning task $\mathcal{T}_T$. The goal is to leverage knowledge from $\langle \mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{T}_S \rangle$ to improve the learning of the predictive function $f_T(\cdot)$ in $\mathcal{D}_T$, where $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$ or $\mathcal{T}_S \neq \mathcal{T}_T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Polyglot Painter"**
> Imagine an artist who has spent 10 years mastering watercolor landscapes. If you now ask this artist to learn oil painting for portraits, they will not start from absolute zero. Their understanding of **color theory, composition, lighting, and brush control** transfers directly. They only need to learn the *new medium* (oil) and the *new subject* (portraits). The years of landscape experience accelerate their portrait learning dramatically. Transfer learning applies exactly this principle: a CNN trained on 1.4 million ImageNet images has already learned universal features (edges, textures, shapes) that are valuable for almost any new vision task.

### 1.3 Why Transfer Learning is Critical in Computer Vision

- **Data Scarcity Problem**: Real-world domain-specific image datasets (medical X-rays, satellite imagery, defect detection) often contain only hundreds to thousands of samples, which is *insufficient* to train deep networks from scratch (which typically need **millions** of images).
- **Computational Cost**: Training a state-of-the-art CNN like ResNet-152 from scratch on ImageNet requires approximately **~10^18 FLOPs** and weeks of GPU time — economically infeasible for most engineers.
- **Faster Convergence**: Pre-trained models converge in **10–100× fewer epochs** on downstream tasks.
- **Better Generalization**: Low-level visual features (Gabor-like filters, color blobs) are remarkably universal across natural images.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU 2024 specifically expects you to know the difference between **Feature Extraction** and **Fine-Tuning** — the two primary strategies of transfer learning. This is a guaranteed 14-mark question in ESE.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Source Domain** $\mathcal{D}_S$ | The original dataset on which the model was pre-trained (e.g., ImageNet) |
| **Target Domain** $\mathcal{D}_T$ | The new, typically smaller, dataset we want to solve |
| **Source Task** $\mathcal{T}_S$ | The original learning objective (e.g., 1000-class classification) |
| **Pre-trained Model** | A network whose weights $\theta_{pre}$ are already optimized on $\mathcal{D}_S$ |
| **Backbone** | The convolutional base of the pre-trained network |
| **Catastrophic Forgetting** | The phenomenon where fine-tuning erases useful pre-trained knowledge |
| **Freezing** | Disabling gradient updates for a layer (keeping its weights constant) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Transfer Learning

#### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Feature Extraction (Transfer as a Fixed Feature Vector)

In this strategy, the pre-trained convolutional base is treated as a **frozen feature extractor**. Only a new classifier head (fully connected layers) is trained on top of the extracted feature vectors.

- **Convolutional Base**: Frozen. Weights $\theta_{conv}$ remain unchanged: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{conv}} = 0$.
- **Classifier Head**: Trainable. Weights $\theta_{head}$ are updated via backpropagation.
- **Output**: For an input image $\mathbf{x}$, the feature vector is $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = f_{conv}(\mathbf{x}; \theta_{conv})$, which is fed into $f_{head}(\cdot; \theta_{head})$ to produce predictions.

**Why this works**: Early convolutional layers learn generic, task-agnostic features (edges, gradients, textures), while deeper layers learn task-specific features. By keeping the entire convolutional base frozen, we retain all the hierarchical visual knowledge.

#### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Fine-Tuning (Transfer with Selective Unfreezing)

Here, we **unfreeze** some of the top convolutional layers of the pre-trained model and continue training them with a *very small learning rate*. This allows the network to adapt its higher-level features to the new task without destroying the lower-level features.

- **Lower Conv Layers**: Frozen (universal features preserved).
- **Upper Conv Layers**: Trainable (task-specific adaptation).
- **Classifier Head**: Trainable (new output classes).
- **Learning Rate**: Typically $\eta = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ — *much smaller* than the $10^{-3}$ used for training from scratch.

**Why a small learning rate?** Large updates to pre-trained weights cause **catastrophic forgetting**, wiping out the carefully learned representations.

#### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Domain Adaptation

A specialized form where $\mathcal{D}_S$ and $\mathcal{D}_T$ are from related but different distributions (e.g., synthetic images vs. real images). Techniques include adversarial alignment and CORAL (Correlation Alignment).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-frequency exam topics for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transfer Learning Objective** | $\min_{\theta_T} \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_T) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta_T, \theta_S)$ | $\mathcal{L}_T$ = target loss, $\theta_S$ = source weights, $\theta_T$ = target weights, $\lambda$ = regularization weight |
| 2 | **Cross-Entropy Loss (Target Task)** | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | $C$ = number of classes, $y_i$ = ground truth, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted probability |
| 3 | **Feature Vector Extraction** | $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = f_{conv}(\mathbf{x}; \theta_{conv}^{*})$ | $\theta_{conv}^{*}$ = frozen pre-trained weights, $\mathbf{x}$ = input image |
| 4 | **Frozen Layer Gradient** | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_{frozen}} = 0$ | Indicates no weight update |
| 5 | **Fine-Tuning Weight Update** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_t)$ | $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$ for fine-tuning |
| 6 | **Learning Rate Scaling (Discriminative)** | $\eta_{\text{layer}_i} = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L-i}$ | $\gamma$ = decay factor (e.g., 0.95), $L$ = total layers |
| 7 | **Knowledge Distillation Loss** | $\mathcal{L}_{KD} = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{CE}(\hat{y}, y) + (1-\alpha) \mathcal{L}_{CE}(\hat{y}, \hat{y}_{teacher})$ | $\alpha$ = balancing weight, $\hat{y}_{teacher}$ = soft labels from source model |
| 8 | **Domain Discrepancy (MMD)** | $\text{MMD}^2 = \Vert \mathbb{E}[\phi(\mathbf{x}_S)] - \mathbb{E}[\phi(\mathbf{x}_T)] \Vert_{\mathcal{H}}^2$ | $\phi$ = feature mapping, $\mathcal{H}$ = Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space |
| 9 | **Freeze Ratio** | $r_{freeze} = \frac{N_{frozen}}{N_{total}}$ | Ratio of frozen to total parameters (typical: 0.7–0.9) |
| 10 | **Computational Savings** | $T_{TL} \approx \frac{T_{scratch}}{k}, \quad k \in [10, 100]$ | $T$ = training time, $k$ = acceleration factor |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Medical Imaging**: Pre-trained ResNet on ImageNet → fine-tuned for tumor detection in CT/MRI scans (e.g., CheXNet for pneumonia detection).
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Pre-trained models for road sign recognition in new geographic regions.
- **Industrial Defect Detection**: Surface inspection with only ~500 defect samples per category.
- **Satellite/Aerial Imagery**: Land-use classification with limited annotated overhead imagery.
- **Facial Recognition**: FaceNet and ArcFace use transfer learning to bootstrap from large general face datasets.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU**: If your target dataset has **< 1000 images per class**, use **feature extraction**. If it has **1000–100,000 images per class**, use **fine-tuning**. If it has **> 100,000 images per class**, training from scratch becomes competitive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mathematical Formulation of the Transfer Learning Optimization Problem

We start from the classical supervised learning objective and extend it to incorporate the source-task prior.

**Step 1 — Standard Supervised Learning (No Transfer)**:
The goal is to find optimal parameters $\theta^{*}$ that minimize the empirical risk on the target task:

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta), y_i)
$$

where $N_T$ is the number of target samples, $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta)$ is the model, and $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function.

**Step 2 — Add the Transfer Regularization Term**:
We modify the objective to penalize large deviations from the source-model parameters $\theta_S$. This anchors the fine-tuned model close to the pre-trained weights, preventing catastrophic forgetting:

$$
\theta_T^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta), y_i) + \lambda \cdot \Omega(\theta, \theta_S)
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the Regularization Form (L2 Distance)**:
The most common choice is the squared L2 norm, also known as **L2-SP (L2 Spatial Prior)**:

$$
\Omega(\theta, \theta_S) = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Vert \theta^{(l)} - \theta_S^{(l)} \Vert_2^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Objective**:

$$
\theta_T^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \underbrace{\frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta), y_i)}_{\text{Data fidelity term}} + \underbrace{\lambda \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Vert \theta^{(l)} - \theta_S^{(l)} \Vert_2^2}_{\text{Transfer regularization term}}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient of the Combined Objective**:
Taking the gradient with respect to $\theta^{(l)}$ (the $l$-th layer weights):

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \theta^{(l)}} = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta), y_i)}{\partial \theta^{(l)}} + 2\lambda (\theta^{(l)} - \theta_S^{(l)})
$$

**Step 6 — Final Parameter Update Rule (Fine-Tuning)**:

$$
\theta_{t+1}^{(l)} = \theta_t^{(l)} - \eta \left( \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \nabla_{\theta^{(l)}} \mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i; \theta_t), y_i) + 2\lambda (\theta_t^{(l)} - \theta_S^{(l)}) \right)
$$

This is the **canonical fine-tuning update rule** that you must know for KTU. The first term is the standard gradient, and the second term is a "spring force" pulling the weights back toward $\theta_S$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cross-Entropy Loss for a New Classification Head

When we replace the final 1000-class ImageNet classifier with a new $C$-class head, we derive the loss.

**Step 1 — Softmax Output of New Head**:
For a feature vector $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ and new weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times C}$, the logits are $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}^T \phi(\mathbf{x})$, and the softmax probabilities are:

$$
\hat{y}_c = \frac{\exp(z_c)}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} \exp(z_k)} = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_c^T \phi(\mathbf{x}))}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} \exp(\mathbf{w}_k^T \phi(\mathbf{x}))}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Entropy Loss for One Sample**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)
$$

**Step 3 — For a True Class $c^{*}$ (one-hot, so $y_{c^{*}} = 1$ and others are 0)**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\log(\hat{y}_{c^{*}}) = -\log \left( \frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_{c^{*}}^T \phi(\mathbf{x}))}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} \exp(\mathbf{w}_k^T \phi(\mathbf{x}))} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Log-Sum-Exp Expansion**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\mathbf{w}_{c^{*}}^T \phi(\mathbf{x}) + \log \left( \sum_{k=1}^{C} \exp(\mathbf{w}_k^T \phi(\mathbf{x})) \right)
$$

This form is the **numerically stable** cross-entropy loss used in practice.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Strategy Comparison Table

| Dimension | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning | Training from Scratch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frozen Layers** | All conv layers | Some upper conv layers | None |
| **Trainable Parameters** | ~1% (head only) | ~10–50% | 100% |
| **Target Dataset Size** | Very small (< 1k/class) | Medium (1k–100k/class) | Large (> 100k/class) |
| **Training Time** | Minutes to hours | Hours to days | Days to weeks |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low | Medium | High (if data is small) |
| **Risk of Catastrophic Forgetting** | None | Medium (mitigated by small $\eta$) | N/A |
| **Learning Rate** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Typical Accuracy Gain over Scratch** | +15–30% | +5–15% | Baseline (0%) |
| **When to Use (KTU Exam Answer)** | Tiny datasets, fast prototyping | Moderate datasets, best accuracy | Massive datasets, unique domain |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Discriminative Learning Rate Schedule

When fine-tuning, different layers should be trained at different learning rates. The intuition: early layers (universal features) need minimal updates, later layers (task-specific) need more.

**Step 1 — Define Layer-wise Learning Rate**:

$$
\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L-l}
$$

where $\eta_0$ is the base learning rate, $\gamma \in (0, 1)$ is the decay factor, $L$ is the total number of layers, and $l$ is the current layer index.

**Step 2 — Numerical Example**:
Suppose we have a 5-layer network ($L=5$), base rate $\eta_0 = 10^{-3}$, and $\gamma = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_5 &= 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5-5} = 10^{-3} \\
\eta_4 &= 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5-4} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_3 &= 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_2 &= 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5-2} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_1 &= 10^{-3} \cdot 0.5^{5-1} = 6.25 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the deeper (task-specific) layers get higher learning rates, while the shallower (universal) layers get tiny updates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Transfer Learning Workflow (End-to-End Pipeline)
