## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Transfer Learning. List any two pre-trained models commonly used in computer vision.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Transfer Learning is a deep learning technique in which a neural network model that has been trained on a large source dataset (e.g., ImageNet) is repurposed for a different but related target task, leveraging the previously learned feature representations to reduce data requirements, training time, and computational cost.

**Two Pre-trained Models (1 mark):**
1. **VGG-16 / VGG-19** — 16 and 19 layer networks from Visual Geometry Group, Oxford.
2. **ResNet-50** — 50-layer Residual Network with skip connections (Microsoft Research).

*(Acceptable alternatives: InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet, Xception, DenseNet121)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Feature Extraction and Fine-Tuning in the context of transfer learning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Frozen Layers** | All convolutional layers frozen | Some upper conv layers unfrozen |
| **Trainable Part** | Only new classifier head | Upper conv layers + classifier head |
| **Learning Rate** | Standard ($10^{-3}$) | Very small ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$) |
| **Use Case** | Very small target dataset | Medium-sized target dataset |

In **feature extraction**, the pre-trained CNN acts as a fixed feature extractor; in **fine-tuning**, the weights of selected deeper layers are updated to adapt to the new task.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the architecture and working of Transfer Learning with a neat block diagram. Discuss the various strategies used in transfer learning for image classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical formulation of the transfer learning objective function with L2-SP regularization. Explain how this formulation prevents catastrophic forgetting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Architecture and Working (7 marks):**

Transfer learning architecture consists of two main components:
1. **Pre-trained Backbone** (e.g., ResNet-50): A deep CNN trained on a large source domain like ImageNet (1.4M images, 1000 classes). It contains an input layer, multiple convolutional blocks (with batch normalization, ReLU, max-pooling), and a final pooling layer. The backbone has already learned a rich hierarchy of visual features:
   - **Early layers**: Edges, gradients, color blobs
   - **Middle layers**: Textures, patterns, parts
   - **Deep layers**: Object parts, semantic concepts

2. **New Classifier Head**: The original 1000-class softmax layer is removed and replaced with a new fully connected layer sized to the target task (e.g., 2 neurons for binary classification, 10 for CIFAR-10).

**Three Strategies (refer Section 4 Diagram 2):**

- **Strategy 1 — Feature Extraction**: Freeze entire backbone. Extract feature vectors $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = f_{conv}(\mathbf{x}; \theta_{conv}^{*})$. Train only the new FC head. Suitable for tiny datasets (< 1000 images per class). Fastest to train, lowest overfitting risk.

- **Strategy 2 — Fine-Tuning**: Freeze early conv layers, unfreeze upper conv layers + FC head. Use small learning rate ($\eta = 10^{-4}$) and discriminative learning rates. The model adapts its high-level features to the new domain. Yields 5–15% better accuracy than feature extraction on medium datasets.

- **Strategy 3 — Training from Scratch**: No transfer. Used only when target dataset is very large (> 100K images per class) and very different from ImageNet (e.g., medical microscopy, satellite SAR).

**Block Diagram** (Draw a simplified version of Section 4.1): Input Image → Frozen Convolutional Base → GAP → Dropout → New FC Layer → Softmax → Output.

**(b) Mathematical Derivation (7 marks):**

*(Reproduce the full derivation from Section 3.1 of these notes, including all 6 steps.)*

**Key points for full marks:**
- Start with standard empirical risk minimization (Step 1) — 1 mark
- Add transfer regularization term with $\lambda$ (Step 2) — 1 mark
- Choose L2-SP form $\Vert \theta - \theta_S \Vert_2^2$ (Step 3) — 1 mark
- Combine into final objective (Step 4) — 1 mark
- Compute gradient with the spring-force term (Step 5) — 1 mark
- Write final update rule and explain catastrophic forgetting prevention (Step 6) — 2 marks

**Explanation of Catastrophic Forgetting Prevention (2 marks):**
The term $2\lambda(\theta - \theta_S)$ acts as an "elastic spring" pulling each weight back toward its pre-trained value. When the data-fidelity gradient tries to push weights far from $\theta_S$ (erasing source knowledge), this regularization counter-force resists, ensuring that the fine-tuned weights stay in a neighborhood of the pre-trained values. The parameter $\lambda$ controls the trade-off: larger $\lambda$ = more preservation of source knowledge but less adaptation; smaller $\lambda$ = more adaptation but more forgetting risk.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how a pre-trained VGG-16 model can be adapted for a custom 5-class image classification task. Specify which layers are frozen and which are trainable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning. Propose and mathematically justify two techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) VGG-16 Adaptation for 5-Class Classification (7 marks):**

**VGG-16 Architecture Recap (2 marks):**
VGG-16 has 13 convolutional layers (organized in 5 blocks) and 3 fully connected layers. Total parameters: ~138 million. Pre-trained on ImageNet.

**Adaptation Steps (5 marks):**
1. **Load pre-trained VGG-16** with weights trained on ImageNet (excluding the top classification layer).
2. **Freeze all convolutional blocks** (Block 1 through Block 5) by setting `trainable = False`. This preserves the learned low-level and mid-level visual features.
3. **Remove the original FC layers** (which output 1000 classes).
4. **Add a new classifier head**:
   - `Flatten()` layer
   - `Dense(256, activation='relu')` with `Dropout(0.5)`
   - `Dense(5, activation='softmax')` for the 5 target classes
5. **Compile** with `optimizer = Adam(learning_rate = 1e-4)`, `loss = 'categorical_crossentropy'`.
6. **Train** on the custom 5-class dataset. Optionally, after initial convergence, unfreeze the last 2–3 conv layers of Block 5 and continue training with an even smaller learning rate (1e-5) for fine-tuning.

**Frozen layers**: All 13 conv layers of VGG-16 (Blocks 1–5).
**Trainable layers**: New Flatten + Dense(256) + Dense(5) classifier head.

**(b) Catastrophic Forgetting & Mitigation (7 marks):**

**Phenomenon Explanation (2 marks):**
Catastrophic forgetting occurs when, during fine-tuning on a target task, the network's weights drift far from their pre-trained values, causing the model to *forget* the universal visual features it originally learned. The accuracy on the source task (or the model's general feature quality) degrades drastically.

**Technique 1: Small Learning Rate + Early Layer Freezing (2.5 marks)**
*Mathematical Justification*: From the fine-tuning update rule $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$, the weight displacement per step is $\Delta \theta = -\eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$. By setting $\eta = 10^{-4}$ instead of $10^{-2}$, we reduce $\Delta \theta$ by a factor of 100, ensuring each step perturbs the pre-trained weights only slightly. Freezing early layers (setting $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \theta_{early} = 0$) guarantees that the most universal, most-valuable features are never modified.

**Technique 2: L2-SP Regularization (2.5 marks)**
*Mathematical Justification*: As derived in Section 3.1, adding $\lambda \Vert \theta - \theta_S \Vert_2^2$ to the loss creates a quadratic penalty for weight deviation. The gradient contribution $2\lambda(\theta - \theta_S)$ is proportional to the distance from pre-trained weights, so as weights drift, a restoring force grows linearly. This bounds the total deviation: $\Vert \theta_t - \theta_S \Vert \leq \frac{\eta}{\lambda} \Vert \nabla \mathcal{L} \Vert_{\max}$, giving a theoretical guarantee of bounded forgetting.

*(Alternative Technique 3 accepted: Knowledge Distillation with teacher model, or Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) using Fisher information.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "Transfer Learning" with "Multi-task Learning"**: TL uses *sequential* learning (source → target); MTL uses *simultaneous* joint training. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the learning rate**: When writing about fine-tuning, you *must* state $\eta \approx 10^{-4}$ or justify why a small rate is used. Marks are specifically allocated for this.
> 3. **Not drawing the block diagram**: Part B (a) questions on transfer learning almost always require a block diagram. Not drawing one leads to a **2-mark penalty**.
> 4. **Confusing "fine-tuning" with "training from scratch"**: Fine-tuning *starts from* pre-trained weights with a small learning rate. Scratch training *initializes* weights randomly with a normal learning rate.
> 5. **Missing the cross-entropy formula**: If a question mentions "loss function for transfer learning," failing to write $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not specifying the freeze ratio**: Always state approximately *how many* layers are frozen (e.g., "all 13 conv layers of VGG-16 frozen, only FC head trained").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- [x] **Definition**: TL = reusing a model trained on source $\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{T}_S$ for a target $\mathcal{D}_T, \mathcal{T}_T$ where $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$.
- [x] **Two main strategies**: (1) Feature Extraction (freeze all conv), (2) Fine-Tuning (unfreeze top conv + small $\eta$).
- [x] **Key pre-trained models**: VGG-16, ResNet-50, InceptionV3, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0.
- [x] **Fine-tuning learning rate**: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ (100× smaller than scratch training).
- [x] **Cross-entropy loss**: $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$.
- [x] **Transfer objective with L2-SP**: $\mathcal{J} = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum \mathcal{L}_T + \lambda \Vert \theta - \theta_S \Vert_2^2$.
- [x] **Update rule**: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \left( \nabla \mathcal{L} + 2\lambda(\theta_t - \theta_S) \right)$.
- [x] **Catastrophic forgetting**: Erosion of pre-trained knowledge during aggressive fine-tuning.
- [x] **Discriminative LR**: $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L-l}$ (deeper layers get higher rates).
- [x] **Dataset size rule**: < 1k → Feature Extraction; 1k–100k → Fine-Tuning; > 100k → Scratch.
- [x] **Universal features**: Early conv layers learn edges, textures — task-agnostic.
- [x] **Domain Adaptation**: Specialized TL for related but distributionally different domains (e.g., synthetic → real).

**Memory Mnemonic — "FT-CF-LR"**:
- **F**eature extraction for small data
- **T**iny learning rate for fine-tuning
- **C**atastrophic forgetting is the enemy
- **L**2-SP regularization saves the day

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fine-Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fine-Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of deep learning and computer vision, **Fine-Tuning** is a transfer learning technique that involves taking a pre-trained neural network (typically a Convolutional Neural Network trained on a large dataset such as ImageNet) and continuing the training process on a smaller, domain-specific dataset. During fine-tuning, the weights of the pre-trained model are updated (either partially or fully) using backpropagation, allowing the network to adapt its learned feature representations to the new task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT743 Module 3):**
> *Fine-tuning is the process of adjusting the parameters of a pre-trained deep learning model by training it further on a new, often smaller, dataset to specialize the model for a specific computer vision task while retaining the general feature extraction capabilities learned from the original large-scale training.*

**Key Terminology in Fine-Tuning:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Pre-trained Model** | A neural network already trained on a large benchmark dataset (e.g., ResNet on ImageNet with **1.2M images** across **1000 classes**). |
| **Source Domain** | The original large dataset on which the model was initially trained. |
| **Target Domain** | The new, typically smaller, dataset for the specific downstream task. |
| **Frozen Layers** | Network layers whose weights are kept fixed (no gradient updates) during fine-tuning. |
| **Unfrozen Layers** | Layers whose weights are updated via backpropagation during fine-tuning. |
| **Learning Rate (η)** | The step size used for weight updates. During fine-tuning, a much smaller η is used compared to training from scratch. |
| **Feature Extraction** | The process of using pre-trained convolutional layers to extract generic features. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Experienced Chef" Analogy:**
> Imagine you hire a **master chef** (the pre-trained model) who has spent **20 years** learning to cook cuisines from all around the world. They already understand fundamental cooking principles — heat control, seasoning, knife skills, and flavor balancing (the low-level features learned in early network layers).
>
> Now, you want them to specialize in making authentic **Kerala Sadya** (your target domain with a small dataset). You don't send them back to culinary school to re-learn how to boil water. Instead, you **fine-tune** their skills by:
> - Letting them observe and practice a few **specific Kerala dishes** (your smaller dataset).
> - Making **small, careful adjustments** to their existing techniques (a small learning rate).
> - Keeping their **fundamental knife and cooking skills intact** (frozen early layers) while adjusting their **recipe-specific knowledge** (unfrozen later layers).
>
> This is exactly what fine-tuning does to a neural network — it leverages the "experience" of a well-trained model and makes **targeted, gentle adjustments** to specialize it for a new task.

### 1.3 Why Fine-Tuning Matters in Computer Vision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Problem Fine-Tuning Solves:**
> Training a deep CNN from scratch requires **massive labeled datasets** (often millions of images) and **enormous computational resources** (GPUs/TPUs running for days or weeks). For most practical computer vision applications — medical imaging, satellite imagery, industrial defect detection — such large datasets are **unavailable**. Fine-tuning solves this by:
> 1. **Reducing data requirements** by **10x to 1000x**.
> 2. **Cutting training time** from days to hours.
> 3. **Improving generalization** on small datasets by leveraging learned priors.
> 4. **Enabling rapid prototyping** for new vision tasks.

### 1.4 Types of Fine-Tuning Strategies

There are three primary fine-tuning paradigms used in modern computer vision:

1. **Full Fine-Tuning:** All layers of the pre-trained model are unfrozen and updated. Best when the target dataset is moderately large (e.g., **>10,000 images**).
2. **Feature Extraction + Partial Fine-Tuning:** Early convolutional layers (which learn generic features like edges, textures) are frozen, while later layers and the classifier head are unfrozen and trained. Best for small datasets.
3. **Discriminative Fine-Tuning (or Differential Learning Rates):** Different layers are fine-tuned with **different learning rates** — earlier layers get a smaller η, later layers get a larger η, reflecting that earlier features are more general and need less adjustment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Fine-Tuning

Fine-tuning is fundamentally an optimization problem. Given a pre-trained model with weights $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$ (obtained from training on the source domain), we wish to find updated weights $\mathbf{W}^*$ that minimize a loss function $\mathcal{L}$ on the target domain dataset.

**The General Fine-Tuning Objective:**

$$\mathbf{W}^{*} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}; \mathcal{D}_{\text{target}})$$

where:
- $\mathbf{W}$ represents the trainable parameters of the model.
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{target}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is the target dataset with $N$ samples.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}$ is the task-specific loss function (e.g., **Cross-Entropy Loss** for classification, **Mean Squared Error** for regression).

**Initial Condition:** The optimization is **initialized** at $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$ (the pre-trained weights), not randomly:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(0)}_{\text{init}} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{pretrained}}$$

This is what distinguishes fine-tuning from training from scratch.

### 2.2 The Cross-Entropy Loss (Most Common in Vision Fine-Tuning)

For a $K$-class image classification problem, the **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \cdot \log(\hat{p}_{ik})$$

where:
- $N$ = number of training samples.
- $K$ = number of classes.
- $y_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ = ground truth label (one-hot encoded).
- $\hat{p}_{ik}$ = predicted probability of sample $i$ belonging to class $k$, computed via the **Softmax function**:

$$\hat{p}_{ik} = \frac{e^{z_{ik}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{ij}}}$$

Here, $z_{ik}$ is the logit (raw output) of the network for sample $i$ and class $k$.

### 2.3 Weight Update Rule (Gradient Descent)

At each iteration $t$ of fine-tuning, the weights are updated as:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(t)})$$

where:
- $\eta$ = learning rate (typically **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$** for fine-tuning, compared to **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$** for training from scratch).
- $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}$ = gradient of the loss with respect to the weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Fine-Tuning:** The learning rate $\eta$ must be **significantly smaller** than the rate used for training from scratch. This prevents **catastrophic forgetting** — the phenomenon where the model abruptly loses the knowledge learned during pre-training.

### 2.4 Freezing and Unfreezing Mechanism

In a CNN with $L$ layers, we partition the layers into two sets:
- $\mathcal{F}$ = set of **frozen** layers (no gradient updates).
- $\mathcal{U}$ = set of **unfrozen** layers (weights updated).

For frozen layers, the gradient update is **suppressed**:

$$\mathbf{W}_l^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{W}_l^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \mathbb{1}[l \in \mathcal{U}] \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}_l} \mathcal{L}$$

where $\mathbb{1}[l \in \mathcal{U}]$ is the **indicator function** that equals **1** if layer $l$ is unfrozen and **0** if frozen.

In practice, this is implemented by setting `requires_grad = False` for frozen layer parameters in PyTorch.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Purpose / Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Fine-Tuning Objective | $\mathbf{W}^{*} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}; \mathcal{D}_{\text{target}})$ | Formal definition of the optimization problem |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik}\log(\hat{p}_{ik})$ | Classification loss for vision tasks |
| Softmax Function | $\hat{p}_{ik} = \frac{e^{z_{ik}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_{ij}}}$ | Converts logits to class probabilities |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\mathbf{W}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}}\mathcal{L}$ | Core weight update rule |
| Frozen Layer Mask | $\mathbb{1}[l \in \mathcal{U}]$ | Controls which layers receive gradient updates |
| Discriminative LR | $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L-l)}$ | Layer-wise learning rate (where $\alpha < 1$ is a decay factor) |
| Catastrophic Forgetting Bound | $\vert \mathbf{W}^{(t)} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)} \vert^2 \leq \epsilon$ | Constraint to prevent loss of pre-trained knowledge |
| Typical Fine-Tune LR | $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$ | Much smaller than scratch training LR ($\approx 10^{-2}$) |

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

Fine-tuning is the **backbone of modern applied computer vision** and is used extensively in:

- **Medical Imaging:** Fine-tuning ImageNet-pretrained ResNets for tumor detection in MRI/CT scans with only a few hundred labeled examples.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Adapting pre-trained object detection models (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) to specific road environments.
- **Satellite Image Analysis:** Fine-tuning models for land-use classification, deforestation monitoring, and disaster assessment.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Detecting manufacturing defects with very limited defect samples.
- **Facial Recognition & Biometrics:** Adapting face recognition models to specific demographic populations.
- **Agriculture:** Plant disease detection from leaf images using fine-tuned MobileNet or EfficientNet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The choice of which layers to freeze/unfreeze is governed by the **size of the target dataset** and the **similarity between source and target domains**. This is a frequently tested concept.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why a Small Learning Rate is Critical in Fine-Tuning

We will derive the theoretical justification for using a small learning rate during fine-tuning to avoid catastrophic forgetting.

**Step 1 — Define the Pre-trained Weight Vector:**
Let $\mathbf{W}^{(0)} = (w_1^{(0)}, w_2^{(0)}, \ldots, w_M^{(0)})$ represent the vector of $M$ pre-trained weights, which have been optimized to a (local) minimum of the source loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{source}}$.

This means:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)}) \approx \mathbf{0}$$

The gradient of the source loss at the pre-trained point is approximately zero because the model has converged on the source task.

**Step 2 — Express the Target Loss via Taylor Expansion:**
Using the first-order Taylor expansion of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}$ around the pre-trained point $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}) &\approx \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)}) + \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)})^T \cdot (\mathbf{W} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)}) + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \mathbf{W} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)} \Vert^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a small perturbation $\Delta\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)}$, this simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)} + \Delta\mathbf{W}) &\approx \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)}) + \mathbf{g}^T \Delta\mathbf{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{g} = \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)})$ is the gradient of the target loss at the pre-trained point.

**Step 3 — Compute the Gradient Descent Update:**
A single gradient descent step with learning rate $\eta$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\mathbf{W} &= -\eta \cdot \mathbf{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

The change in the source loss due to this update is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} &\approx \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)})^T \cdot \Delta\mathbf{W} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} \Delta\mathbf{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)}) \approx \mathbf{0}$ (from Step 1), the first-order term vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} &\approx \frac{1}{2} \Delta\mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} \Delta\mathbf{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} = \nabla^2_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}}$ is the **Hessian matrix** of the source loss.

**Step 4 — Substitute the Update Direction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} &\approx \frac{1}{2} (-\eta \mathbf{g})^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} (-\eta \mathbf{g}) = \frac{\eta^2}{2} \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} \mathbf{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result:**
The increase in source loss (i.e., the degree of "forgetting") is **quadratic in** $\eta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} &= \frac{\eta^2}{2} \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} \mathbf{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that the damage to pre-trained knowledge grows as $\eta^2$. Therefore, reducing $\eta$ by a factor of 10 reduces catastrophic forgetting by a factor of 100. This is the rigorous mathematical justification for using a **small learning rate** in fine-tuning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A small $\eta$ ensures the model makes **gentle, controlled adjustments** to its weights, preserving the general-purpose features learned during pre-training while adapting to the new task.

### 3.2 Derivation: Discriminative Learning Rate Schedule

In discriminative fine-tuning, we assign a different learning rate to each layer. Let layer $l$ have learning rate $\eta_l$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L - l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\eta_0$ = base learning rate (for the final/classification layer).
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ = decay factor (commonly **0.95 or 0.9**).
- $L$ = total number of layers.
- $l$ = current layer index (counting from input, so $l = 0$ is the first layer, $l = L$ is the last).

**Example Calculation:**
For a ResNet-50 (with 50 layers, using the convention where $l$ ranges from 1 to 50):

- Final classification layer ($l = 50$): $\eta_{50} = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^0 = \eta_0 = 10^{-3}$
- Middle layer ($l = 25$): $\eta_{25} = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{25} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.95^{25}$

Let's compute $0.95^{25}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.95^{25} &= e^{25 \ln(0.95)} = e^{25 \times (-0.05129)} = e^{-1.2823} \approx 0.2774
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\eta_{25} \approx 10^{-3} \times 0.2774 = 2.774 \times 10^{-4}$

- First convolutional layer ($l = 1$): $\eta_1 = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{49} = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.95^{49}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.95^{49} &= e^{49 \ln(0.95)} = e^{49 \times (-0.05129)} = e^{-2.5132} \approx 0.0810
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\eta_1 \approx 10^{-3} \times 0.0810 = 8.10 \times 10^{-5}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Early layers (low-level feature extractors like edge detectors) receive the **smallest** learning rate ($\approx 8 \times 10^{-5}$), while the final classification layer receives the **largest** ($\approx 10^{-3}$). This matches the intuition that early features are universal and should change little, while task-specific layers need more aggressive adjustment.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Training Strategies

| **Parameter** | **Training from Scratch** | **Feature Extraction** | **Full Fine-Tuning** | **Discriminative Fine-Tuning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Weights** | Random (e.g., He/Xavier init) | Pre-trained | Pre-trained | Pre-trained |
| **Layers Frozen** | None (all trainable) | All conv layers frozen | None (all trainable) | Early layers frozen or low LR |
| **Learning Rate** | High ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$) | Moderate ($10^{-3}$) | Low ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$) | Varies per layer |
| **Dataset Size Required** | Very Large ($>10^6$ images) | Small to Moderate | Moderate ($>10^3$ images) | Small to Large |
| **Training Time** | Very Long (days–weeks) | Short (minutes–hours) | Moderate (hours) | Moderate (hours) |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | High (on small data) | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Risk of Catastrophic Forgetting** | N/A | Very Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Unlimited data & compute | Very small datasets | Moderately-sized datasets | Most practical scenarios |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fine-Tuning Pipeline Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Domain: Large Dataset (e.g., ImageNet: 1.2M images, 1000 classes)"] --> B["Pre-Trained Model Training (e.g., ResNet-50, VGG-16, EfficientNet)"]
    B --> C["Pre-Trained Weights: W(0) saved as .pth or .h5 file"]
    C --> D{"Select Fine-Tuning Strategy"}
    D --> E["Strategy 1: Full Fine-Tuning (all layers unfrozen)"]
    D --> F["Strategy 2: Partial Fine-Tuning (freeze early conv layers)"]
    D --> G["Strategy 3: Discriminative LR (layer-wise learning rates)"]
    E --> H["Replace Classifier Head with new K classes"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Target Domain: Small Dataset (e.g., 500 medical images, 3 classes)"]
    I --> J["Forward Pass: Compute predictions y_hat"]
    J --> K["Compute Loss: Cross-Entropy L = -sum y*log(y_hat)"]
    K --> L{"Loss Converged?"}
    L --> M["Yes: Save Fine-Tuned Model W*"]
    L --> N["No: Backpropagation - Compute Gradients dL/dW"]
    N --> O["Update Weights: W = W - eta * gradient (small eta = 1e-4)"]
    O --> J
    M --> P["Deploy for Target Computer Vision Task"]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Layer Freezing Strategy in a CNN

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Image: 224 x 224 x 3"] --> B["Conv Block 1: Edge and Color Detection (FROZEN - requires_grad = False)"]
    B --> C["Conv Block 2: Texture and Pattern Detection (FROZEN)"]
    C --> D["Conv Block 3: Part Detection Wheels, Eyes, Leaves (FROZEN)"]
    D --> E["Conv Block 4: Object-Level Features (UNFROZEN - Trainable)"]
    E --> F["Conv Block 5: Task-Specific High-Level Features (UNFROZEN - Trainable)"]
    F --> G["Global Average Pooling Layer"]
    G --> H["New Classifier Head: Fully Connected Layer with K output neurons (REPLACED and TRAINED)"]
    H --> I["Softmax Output: Class Probabilities P1, P2, ..., PK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The earlier convolutional blocks (Blocks 1–3) learn **generic, low-level features** (edges, textures, simple patterns) that are **universal** across virtually all vision tasks. These are frozen. The deeper blocks (Blocks 4–5) learn **task-specific high-level features** and are unfrozen. The final classification layer is **replaced** to match the number of classes $K$ in the target task.

### 4.3 Catastrophic Forgetting Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pre-Trained Model - High Accuracy on Source Task: 95 percent"] --> B["Fine-Tuning with LARGE Learning Rate eta = 1e-2"]
    B --> C["Catastrophic Forgetting: Source Accuracy drops to 30 percent"]
    C --> D["Target Accuracy: 85 percent (marginal improvement)"]
    E["Pre-Trained Model - High Accuracy on Source Task: 95 percent"] --> F["Fine-Tuning with SMALL Learning Rate eta = 1e-4"]
    F --> G["Gentle Weight Updates: Source Accuracy remains 90 percent"]
    G --> H["Target Accuracy: 88 percent (strong improvement)"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The right panel demonstrates the **ideal fine-tuning behavior** — a small learning rate preserves source knowledge (90% retained) while achieving high target accuracy (88%). The left panel shows **catastrophic forgetting** caused by an excessively large learning rate.

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End Fine-Tuning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PhaseA["PHASE 1: PRE-TRAINING (Done Once, Offline)"]
        A1["Large-Scale Dataset: ImageNet, COCO, or JFT-300M"] --> A2["Deep CNN Backbone: ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B3"]
        A2 --> A3["Pre-Trained Weights: W_pretrained stored on disk"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseB["PHASE 2: FINE-TUNING (Done Per Target Task)"]
        B1["Load W_pretrained into Model Architecture"] --> B2["Freeze Early Conv Layers requires_grad = False"]
        B2 --> B3["Replace Final FC Layer: Old 1000 classes to New K classes"]
        B3 --> B4["Set Small Learning Rate: eta = 1e-4"]
        B4 --> B5["Train on Target Dataset D_target with Backpropagation"]
        B5 --> B6{"Validation Loss Plateaued?"}
        B6 --> B7["Yes: Save Fine-Tuned Weights W_star"]
        B6 --> B5
    end
    subgraph PhaseC["PHASE 3: INFERENCE (Deployment)"]
        C1["New Unseen Image: x_test"] --> C2["Forward Pass through Fine-Tuned Model"]
        C2 --> C3["Output: Predicted Class Label and Confidence Score"]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B7 --> C2
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define fine-tuning in the context of deep learning for computer vision. List any two advantages of fine-tuning over training a model from scratch.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Fine-tuning is a transfer learning technique in which a neural network that has been pre-trained on a large dataset (source domain) is further trained on a smaller, task-specific dataset (target domain) by updating its weights using backpropagation with a **small learning rate**.

**Advantages:**
1. **Reduced Data Requirement:** Fine-tuning requires significantly less labeled data (even a few hundred images can suffice) compared to training from scratch, which often needs millions of labeled examples.
2. **Faster Convergence and Lower Computational Cost:** Since the model starts from already-optimized weights rather than random initialization, it converges in far fewer epochs, saving GPU time and energy. For example, fine-tuning ResNet-50 on a custom dataset may take **1–2 hours** on a single GPU, whereas training from scratch could take **days**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is catastrophic forgetting? Explain how the choice of learning rate helps to mitigate it during fine-tuning.*

**Model Answer:**

**Catastrophic Forgetting** is the phenomenon in which a neural network, upon being trained on a new task, **abruptly loses** the knowledge and feature representations it had previously learned from the source task. This happens because large gradient updates drastically shift the weights away from their well-optimized pre-trained values.

**Role of Learning Rate:** The learning rate $\eta$ controls the step size of weight updates. During fine-tuning, a **small learning rate** (typically $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$) is used because:
- The change in source loss due to a gradient step is proportional to $\eta^2$ (as shown in the derivation in Section 3.1).
- A small $\eta$ ensures that weight updates are **gentle and controlled**, preserving the low-level and mid-level features learned during pre-training.
- For example, reducing $\eta$ from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ (a 100x reduction) reduces catastrophic forgetting by approximately **10,000x** (since the damage scales as $\eta^2$).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on fine-tuning questions by:
> 1. **Confusing fine-tuning with feature extraction** — remember, fine-tuning involves *updating* weights, while feature extraction keeps them frozen.
> 2. **Not justifying the learning rate choice** — always state *why* a small $\eta$ is used (to prevent catastrophic forgetting).
> 3. **Ignoring the domain similarity factor** — the fine-tuning strategy depends on how similar the source and target domains are.
> 4. **Forgetting to replace the classifier head** — the final fully-connected layer must be replaced to match the new number of classes $K$.
> 5. **Not mentioning data augmentation or regularization** — for small datasets, these are essential companions to fine-tuning.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of fine-tuning in pre-trained deep convolutional neural networks. Discuss the different strategies of fine-tuning with appropriate diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A research team is fine-tuning a VGG-16 model pre-trained on ImageNet (1000 classes) for a custom plant disease classification task with 5 classes and only 800 labeled images. Design a suitable fine-tuning strategy. Justify your choices of learning rate, frozen layers, and classifier modification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept and Strategies of Fine-Tuning:**

Fine-tuning is the process of taking a CNN pre-trained on a large dataset and further training it on a new, typically smaller, dataset. The key insight is that early convolutional layers learn **generic, transferable features** (edges, textures, color gradients), while deeper layers learn **task-specific features** (object parts, semantic concepts).

**The three main strategies are:**

**Strategy 1 — Full Fine-Tuning:**
All layers of the pre-trained network are unfrozen, and the entire model is trained on the new dataset with a small learning rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}_l^{(t+1)} &= \mathbf{W}_l^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}_l} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}} \quad \forall l \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is suitable when the target dataset is **large enough** (typically $N > 10,000$) and the target domain is significantly different from the source domain.

**Strategy 2 — Feature Extraction (Partial Fine-Tuning):**
The convolutional backbone is frozen, and only the newly added classifier head is trained.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}_l^{(t+1)} &= \mathbf{W}_l^{(t)} \quad \text{for} \quad l \in \mathcal{F} \quad \text{(frozen)} \\
\mathbf{W}_{\text{head}}^{(t+1)} &= \mathbf{W}_{\text{head}}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}_{\text{head}}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}} \quad \text{(trainable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is suitable when the target dataset is **very small** ($N < 1,000$) and similar to the source domain.

**Strategy 3 — Discriminative Fine-Tuning:**
A different learning rate is assigned to each layer group:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L - l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This provides a balanced approach suitable for most practical scenarios.

**(b) Fine-Tuning Strategy for Plant Disease Classification:**

**Given:** VGG-16 pre-trained on ImageNet (1000 classes, 1.2M images), target task: 5-class plant disease classification, 800 images.

**Recommended Strategy: Feature Extraction + Partial Fine-Tuning with Discriminative Learning Rates**

**Justification:**

**1. Classifier Head Replacement:** The original VGG-16 has a final fully-connected layer outputting 1000 neurons. This must be **replaced** with a new FC layer outputting **5 neurons** (one per disease class). This is initialized with **random weights** and trained fully.

**2. Frozen Layers (Blocks 1–3 of VGG-16):** VGG-16 has 5 convolutional blocks. Since the target domain (plant leaves) is **visually similar** to ImageNet in terms of low-level features (edges, textures, color distributions), we **freeze Blocks 1–3**. These layers already extract highly relevant generic features (leaf veins, color patches, texture patterns). Freezing them prevents overfitting on the small 800-image dataset.

**3. Unfrozen Layers (Blocks 4–5 + New Classifier):** The deeper blocks learn more abstract, task-specific features. With only 800 images, we fine-tune only Blocks 4–5 along with the new classifier head, and use a small learning rate.

**4. Learning Rate Choice:** We set $\eta_0 = 10^{-3}$ for the classifier head and use discriminative learning rates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Block 5}} &= 10^{-4} \\
\eta_{\text{Block 4}} &= 10^{-5} \\
\eta_{\text{Blocks 1-3}} &= 0 \quad \text{(frozen)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**5. Additional Regularization:** Given only 800 images, we apply **data augmentation** (rotation, flipping, color jittering) and **dropout** ($p = 0.5$) in the classifier head to prevent overfitting.

**6. Expected Outcome:** This strategy should achieve **90–95% test accuracy** despite the limited data, compared to **60–70%** if training from scratch, demonstrating the power of transfer learning through fine-tuning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the transfer learning pipeline using fine-tuning. Describe the mathematical formulation of the fine-tuning optimization problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast: (i) Fine-tuning vs. Feature Extraction, (ii) Fine-tuning vs. Training from Scratch. Use a suitable table or comparison matrix. Discuss the role of the learning rate in preventing catastrophic forgetting with a mathematical proof outline. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transfer Learning Pipeline and Mathematical Formulation:**

**Pipeline (as shown in the diagrams in Section 4):**

1. **Pre-training Phase:** A deep CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) is trained on a large source dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{source}}$ (e.g., ImageNet) to obtain pre-trained weights $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$.
2. **Transfer Phase:** The pre-trained weights are loaded into a new model architecture. The final classification layer is replaced with a new head matching the target task's number of classes $K$.
3. **Fine-Tuning Phase:** The model is trained on the target dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{target}}$ with a small learning rate, updating weights via backpropagation.
4. **Deployment Phase:** The fine-tuned model is used for inference on new, unseen images.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

The fine-tuning optimization problem is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}^{*} &= \arg\min_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}; \mathcal{D}_{\text{target}}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad \Vert \mathbf{W} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)} \Vert^2 &\leq \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint $\Vert \mathbf{W} - \mathbf{W}^{(0)} \Vert^2 \leq \epsilon$ ensures that the fine-tuned weights do not deviate excessively from the pre-trained weights, thereby limiting catastrophic forgetting.

The loss function for a multi-class classification task is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} &= -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{ik} \log(\hat{p}_{ik}) \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{p}_{ik} = \frac{e^{z_{ik}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{ij}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The weights are updated iteratively via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}^{(t+1)} &= \mathbf{W}^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\mathbf{W}^{(t)})
\end{aligned}
$$

with the **initialization** $\mathbf{W}^{(0)} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{pretrained}}$ and a **small learning rate** $\eta \approx 10^{-4}$.

**(b) Comparative Analysis and Catastrophic Forgetting:**

**(i) Fine-Tuning vs. Feature Extraction:**

| **Aspect** | **Fine-Tuning** | **Feature Extraction** |
|---|---|---|
| Weight Updates | Backpropagation updates weights of some/all layers | Weights remain frozen; only classifier is trained |
| Computational Cost | Moderate | Very Low |
| Flexibility | High — model adapts to new data | Low — model relies entirely on pre-trained features |
| Overfitting Risk | Moderate | Very Low |
| Best Dataset Size | Moderate (1K–100K images) | Very Small (<1K images) |

**(ii) Fine-Tuning vs. Training from Scratch:**

| **Aspect** | **Fine-Tuning** | **Training from Scratch** |
|---|---|---|
| Initialization | Pre-trained weights $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$ | Random initialization $\mathbf{W}^{(0)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ |
| Data Required | Small to Moderate | Very Large (millions of images) |
| Training Time | Hours | Days to Weeks |
| GPU Memory | Moderate | Very High |
| Generalization on Small Data | Excellent (leverages prior knowledge) | Poor (high overfitting risk) |
| Use Case | Domain-specific applications | Novel architectures, unlimited data |

**Mathematical Proof Outline — Role of Learning Rate in Preventing Catastrophic Forgetting:**

As derived in Section 3.1, the increase in source-domain loss after one fine-tuning step is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} &\approx \frac{\eta^2}{2} \cdot \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{H}_{\text{source}} \mathbf{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{g} = \nabla_{\mathbf{W}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{target}}(\mathbf{W}^{(0)})$ and $\mathbf{H}_{\text{source}}$ is the Hessian of the source loss at $\mathbf{W}^{(0)}$.

This shows that catastrophic forgetting scales as **$\eta^2$**. Therefore:
- If $\eta = 10^{-2}$: $\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} \propto (10^{-2})^2 = 10^{-4}$ (significant forgetting)
- If $\eta = 10^{-4}$: $\Delta \mathcal{L}_{\text{source}} \propto (10^{-4})^2 = 10^{-8}$ (negligible forgetting)

This **quadratic relationship** provides the rigorous mathematical justification for using a very small learning rate during fine-tuning. It also motivates **discriminative learning rates**, where $\eta$ is adapted per layer to balance adaptation (for later layers) with preservation (for earlier layers).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- **Core Definition:** Fine-tuning = continued training of a pre-trained model on a new, smaller dataset with a **small learning rate**.

- **Why Fine-Tune:** Solves the **data scarcity problem** in deep learning — enables strong performance with **100x to 1000x less data** than training from scratch.

- **Three Strategies:** Full Fine-Tuning | Feature Extraction (freeze backbone) | Discriminative Fine-Tuning (layer-wise learning rates).

- **Learning Rate Rule:** Use $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$ during fine-tuning — **always smaller** than the scratch-training rate ($\eta \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$).

- **Catastrophic Forgetting:** The loss of pre-trained knowledge due to large weight updates. Scales as $\eta^2$.

- **Classifier Head Replacement:** Always **replace** the final fully-connected layer to match $K$ target classes (even if $K$ equals the original number of classes, random re-initialization of the head is standard practice).

- **Freeze Early Layers:** Early conv layers learn **generic features** (edges, textures) — freeze them to reduce parameters and prevent overfitting on small datasets.

- **Unfreeze Later Layers:** Deeper layers learn **task-specific features** — unfreeze them for task adaptation.

- **Discriminative LR Formula:** $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \alpha^{(L-l)}$ where $\alpha \approx 0.9$ to $0.95$.

- **Regularization Companions:** Always pair fine-tuning (on small data) with **data augmentation**, **dropout**, and **early stopping** to prevent overfitting.

- **Domain Similarity Matters:** If source and target domains are **similar** (e.g., natural images → natural images), freeze more layers. If **dissimilar** (e.g., natural images → medical X-rays), unfreeze more layers or use a higher learning rate.

- **Common Pre-trained Models:** ResNet (50/101/152), VGG-16/19, InceptionV3, EfficientNet (B0–B7), MobileNet (V2/V3) — all pre-trained on ImageNet with **1000 classes** and **1.2M training images**.

- **KTU Favorite Questions:** Expect questions on (1) defining fine-tuning, (2) explaining catastrophic forgetting, (3) comparing fine-tuning strategies, and (4) designing a fine-tuning pipeline for a given scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN / ConvNet)** is a specialised class of deep, feed-forward artificial neural networks designed to process data that has a known, grid-like topology. In the context of Computer Vision, a CNN systematically applies learnable filters (kernels) across an input image to extract hierarchical, translation-invariant feature representations. It is mathematically grounded in the discrete convolution operation combined with non-linear activations, spatial pooling, and fully connected classification heads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Within the Foundations of Computer Vision course, *Convolutional Networks* form the computational backbone of nearly every modern visual recognition pipeline — including image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, and image generation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a detective looking for clues (edges, corners, textures, shapes) inside a large photograph.

- **The Magnifying Glass Analogy:** A CNN slides a small "magnifying glass" (the **kernel / filter**) across the entire image. Wherever the pattern inside the glass matches the image region beneath it, a high response is generated. Different magnifying glasses look for different clues — one might look for vertical edges, another for circular blobs.
- **Layered Inspection:** The first layer of detectives only sees crude features (edges). They pass their notes to the next layer, which combines them to recognise textures (corners, T-junctions). The third layer combines textures to recognise object parts (eyes, wheels). The final layer combines parts to recognise whole objects (cat, car).
- **The Voting Analogy:** Pooling is like a manager summarising the votes of a group of detectives into one decision: *"Did we find the pattern here? What was the strongest signal?"*

This hierarchical, local-to-global reasoning is exactly what a CNN does — automatically and with **learnable filters**.

---

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of a CNN

A standard CNN architecture is composed of three repeating structural blocks, typically followed by a classification head:

1. **Convolutional Layer (`CONV`)** — Performs the discrete convolution of learnable kernels with the input feature map.
2. **Activation Layer (typically `ReLU`)** — Introduces non-linearity so the network can model complex, non-linear mappings.
3. **Pooling Layer (`POOL`)** — Down-samples the feature map spatially to gain translation invariance and reduce computation.
4. **Fully Connected Layer (`FC`)** — Flattens the high-level features and acts as a classifier.
5. **Softmax Layer** — Converts logits into class probabilities for multi-class classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Property:** A CNN exploits the *spatial locality* and *translation invariance* of natural images. Locality means neighbouring pixels are strongly correlated. Translation invariance means a cat shifted 10 pixels to the right is still a cat. Weight sharing across spatial positions enforces both properties while drastically reducing parameters.

---

## 1.4 Why Fully Connected Networks Fail on Images

Consider a small RGB image of size $224 \times 224 \times 3$. Flattening this gives an input vector of length $150{,}528$. A single fully connected layer to 1,000 hidden neurons would require:

$$W_{FC} = 150{,}528 \times 1{,}000 = 150{,}528{,}000 \text{ parameters}$$

A CNN with the same receptive field uses **two orders of magnitude fewer parameters**, is **statistically more efficient**, and **generalises better** to unseen translations of objects.

---

## 1.5 Key Hyperparameters of a Convolutional Layer

| Hyperparameter | Notation | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Kernel Size | $K$ (or $K_h \times K_w$) | Spatial extent of the filter |
| Stride | $S$ | Step size when sliding the kernel |
| Padding | $P$ | Zero-padding around the input border |
| Number of Filters | $F$ | Output depth / number of feature maps |
| Dilation | $D$ | Spacing between kernel elements |

These four parameters $(K, S, P, F)$ completely describe the geometry of a convolution operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Discrete Convolution Operation (Forward Pass)

For an input feature map $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H_{in} \times W_{in} \times C_{in}}$ and a learnable kernel $W \in \mathbb{R}^{K_h \times K_w \times C_{in}}$ with bias $b \in \mathbb{R}$, a single output activation $Y[i, j]$ at spatial coordinate $(i, j)$ is computed as:

$$Y[i, j] = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{m=0}^{K_h-1} \sum_{n=0}^{K_w-1} \sum_{c=0}^{C_{in}-1} W[m, n, c] \cdot X[i + m, j + n, c] + b\right)$$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is a non-linear activation function (usually ReLU).

The output passes through every spatial location with the **same kernel** — this is the *weight sharing* property that makes CNNs parameter-efficient.

---

## 2.2 Why Convolution Works: The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Local Receptive Field:** Early visual cortex (V1) neurons respond only to small image patches. The kernel mimics this biological receptive field.
- **Weight Sharing:** The same edge-detector kernel is useful everywhere in the image. Sharing weights reduces parameters from $\mathcal{O}(H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out})$ to $\mathcal{O}(K^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out})$.
- **Translation Equivariance:** If the input shifts by $\Delta$, the feature map shifts by the same $\Delta$. This is built into the mathematical structure of convolution.
- **Hierarchical Composition:** Stacking layers increases the *effective receptive field* — a neuron in layer $L$ effectively "sees" a region of size $(L \cdot (K-1) + 1)$ in the original input.

---

## 2.3 Pooling Operations

Pooling provides **spatial down-sampling** and **local translation invariance**.

- **Max Pooling:** Retains the strongest activation within a window.

$$Y[i, j] = \max_{(m, n) \in \text{window}} X[i+m, j+n]$$

- **Average Pooling:** Averages activations within a window.

$$Y[i, j] = \frac{1}{\vert \text{window} \vert} \sum_{(m, n) \in \text{window}} X[i+m, j+n]$$

- **Global Average Pooling (GAP):** Replaces the FC head by averaging each feature map into a single scalar, drastically reducing parameters.

---

## 2.4 Activation Functions Used Inside CNNs

| Function | Definition | Range | Gradient Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| ReLU | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $1$ for $x>0$, $0$ for $x<0$ (kills gradient when dead) |
| Leaky ReLU | $f(x) = \max(\alpha x, x), \; \alpha = 0.01$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Non-zero for $x<0$, prevents dead neurons |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Saturates → vanishing gradient |
| Tanh | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Saturates but zero-centred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always explicitly mention **ReLU** as the default activation for modern CNNs because it avoids vanishing gradients and is computationally cheap ($O(1)$ — just a comparison with zero).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Convolution Output Size (Spatial) | $O = \frac{W_{in} - K + 2P}{S} + 1$ | $O$ is integer; round **down** |
| Conv Output Volume | $W_{out} \times H_{out} \times F$ | $F$ = number of filters |
| Receptive Field Growth | $r_L = r_{L-1} + (K-1) \cdot \prod_{l=1}^{L-1} S_l$ | $L$ indexes layers from input |
| Parameters in CONV Layer | $(K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot F$ | The "+1" is for the bias term |
| Parameters in FC Layer | $N_{in} \cdot N_{out} + N_{out}$ | Each neuron has one bias |
| FLOPS per CONV Layer | $2 \cdot K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot F \cdot H_{out} \cdot W_{out}$ | Multiply–accumulate = 2 ops |
| ReLU | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Element-wise |
| Max Pooling | $Y[i,j] = \max X[i+m, j+n]$ | No learnable parameters |
| Softmax | $p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Used at classification head |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Used with softmax |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNNs are the de-facto engine behind:

- **Medical Imaging:** Tumour segmentation in MRI / CT scans (U-Net, ResNet backbones).
- **Autonomous Driving:** Real-time object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN).
- **Satellite Remote Sensing:** Land-cover classification, crop monitoring.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** Surface crack identification on manufactured parts.
- **Biometric Security:** Face recognition on smartphones.
- **Document Analysis:** OCR pipelines used in banking and legal tech.

> [!NOTE]
> The economic and engineering impact of CNNs is immense — every modern smartphone, hospital imaging system, and self-driving car relies on convolutional feature extractors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Spatial Dimension of a Convolution

**Given:** Input feature map with width $W_{in}$, kernel size $K$, stride $S$, padding $P$.

**Step 1 — Effect of padding:** Adding $P$ zeros on each side increases the effective input width:

$$\tilde{W}_{in} = W_{in} + 2P$$

**Step 2 — Number of valid positions to place the kernel:** When sliding a window of size $K$ across $\tilde{W}_{in}$ positions with step size $S$, the number of non-overlapping integer positions is:

$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{\tilde{W}_{in} - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**

$$\boxed{\,O = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2P - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1\,}$$

The same formula applies independently to the height dimension.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Receptive Field of a Stack of Layers

The **receptive field** of a neuron in layer $L$ is the region of the input image that can influence its activation.

**Step 1 — Receptive field of a single conv layer** with kernel size $K$ and stride $S$ on top of an input of size $r_{L-1}$:

$$r_L = (r_{L-1} - 1) \cdot S + K$$

**Step 2 — Expand recursively.** Let $r_0 = 1$ (each input pixel has a receptive field of 1). After two layers with $(K_1, S_1)$ and $(K_2, S_2)$:

$$r_1 = (1 - 1) \cdot S_1 + K_1 = K_1$$

$$r_2 = (K_1 - 1) \cdot S_1 + K_2$$

$$r_3 = \big((K_1 - 1) \cdot S_1 + K_2 - 1\big) \cdot S_2 + K_3$$

**Step 3 — Generalised form:**

$$\boxed{\,r_L = r_{L-1} + (K_L - 1) \cdot \prod_{l=1}^{L-1} S_l\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The receptive field must be large enough to cover the entire object of interest. If your object occupies a $64 \times 64$ patch but the deepest neuron only sees a $32 \times 32$ patch, classification will fail.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parameter Count in a CONV vs FC Layer

**Step 1 — CONV Layer.** A single filter has $K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in}$ weights and 1 bias. With $F$ filters:

$$\text{Params}_{CONV} = F \cdot \big(K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} + 1\big)$$

**Step 2 — FC Layer.** Each of $N_{out}$ output neurons connects to all $N_{in}$ inputs:

$$\text{Params}_{FC} = N_{in} \cdot N_{out} + N_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Comparison.**

Suppose we have $C_{in} = 64$, $F = 128$, $K = 3 \times 3$, and the feature map before this layer is $32 \times 32 \times 64$.

- **CONV parameters:** $128 \times (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 64 + 1) = 128 \times 577 = \mathbf{73{,}856}$.
- **FC parameters** (flattening the input to $32 \times 32 \times 64 = 65{,}536$, connecting to 128 outputs): $65{,}536 \times 128 + 128 = \mathbf{8{,}388{,}736}$.

The CONV layer uses **113× fewer parameters** while producing the same dimensionality of output feature maps.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Forward Pass Through a Single Convolution (Numeric Trace)

Let the input be a $5 \times 5$ matrix $X$ and the kernel be a $3 \times 3$ matrix $K$ with stride $S=1$ and no padding.

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\
3 & 0 & 1 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
2 & 1 & 3 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
K = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -1 \\
1 & 0 & -1 \\
1 & 0 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Output dimension:** $O = (5 - 3)/1 + 1 = 3$, so output is $3 \times 3$.

**Step 2 — Top-left patch convolution:**

$$
Y[0,0] = (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (0)(-1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (2)(-1) + (3)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(-1)
$$

Compute each term:

$$
Y[0,0] = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 - 2 + 3 + 0 - 1 = \mathbf{1}
$$

**Step 3 — Top-middle patch** (shift by 1 column):

$$
Y[0,1] = (2)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(-1) + (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (1)(-1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (2)(-1)
$$

$$
Y[0,1] = 2 + 0 - 1 + 1 + 0 - 1 + 0 + 0 - 2 = \mathbf{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply ReLU and complete the full output:**

$$
Y = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & -1 & 2 \\
-2 & 1 & -1 \\
1 & 2 & -1
\end{bmatrix} \;\xrightarrow{\text{ReLU}}\;
\sigma(Y) = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 2 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice how the vertical edge-detector kernel produced large positive values where the original image had vertical intensity transitions. This is exactly the intuition the network learns from data.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Backpropagation Through Convolution (Conceptual)

**Step 1 — Loss gradient w.r.t. kernel weights** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W[m, n, c]}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W[m, n, c]} = \sum_{i, j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Y[i, j]} \cdot X[i + m, j + n, c]
$$

This is itself a **convolution** between the upstream gradient and the input — hence the elegance of CNN backprop.

**Step 2 — Loss gradient w.r.t. input** $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X[i, j, c]}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X[i, j, c]} = \sum_{m, n} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Y[i-m, j-n, c]} \cdot W[m, n, c]
$$

This is a **transposed convolution** (sometimes called *deconvolution*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** You are **not** required to derive the full backprop equations during the exam — only state the structure and explain that weight updates follow the standard gradient descent rule $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CNN Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE HxWx3] --> B[CONV LAYER K=3 S=1 P=1 F=16]
    B --> C[RELU ACTIVATION]
    C --> D[MAX POOL 2x2 S=2]
    D --> E[CONV LAYER K=3 S=1 P=1 F=32]
    E --> F[RELU ACTIVATION]
    F --> G[MAX POOL 2x2 S=2]
    G --> H[CONV LAYER K=3 S=1 P=1 F=64]
    H --> I[RELU ACTIVATION]
    I --> J[FLATTEN LAYER]
    J --> K[FULLY CONNECTED 128 NEURONS]
    K --> L[RELU]
    L --> M[FULLY CONNECTED NUM CLASSES]
    M --> N[SOFTMAX]
    N --> O[CLASS PROBABILITIES]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style O fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

---

## 4.2 Convolution Operation — Sliding Window Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT FEATURE MAP 5x5]
        I1[1 2 0 1 3]
        I2[0 1 2 1 0]
        I3[3 0 1 2 1]
        I4[1 2 0 1 0]
        I5[2 1 3 0 1]
    end

    subgraph K[KERNEL 3x3 EDGE DETECTOR]
        K1[1 0 -1]
        K2[1 0 -1]
        K3[1 0 -1]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT FEATURE MAP 3x3]
        O1[Z11 Z12 Z13]
        O2[Z21 Z22 Z23]
        O3[Z31 Z32 Z33]
    end

    K -->|stride 1| Z11
    K -->|shift right| Z12
    K -->|shift right| Z13
    K -->|next row| Z21
    K -->|continue| Z22
    K -->|continue| Z23
    K -->|continue| Z31
    K -->|continue| Z32
    K -->|continue| Z33

    style IN fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style K fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style OUT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of a CNN Forward Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PIXEL VALUES] --> F1[FEATURE EXTRACTION BLOCK]
    F1 --> F2[LOW LEVEL EDGES AND GRADIENTS]
    F2 --> F3[MID LEVEL TEXTURES AND CORNERS]
    F3 --> F4[HIGH LEVEL OBJECT PARTS]
    F4 --> F5[GLOBAL OBJECT REPRESENTATION]
    F5 --> C1[CLASSIFICATION HEAD]
    C1 --> C2[DECISION SOFTMAX]
    C2 --> R[FINAL CLASS LABEL]

    subgraph BACKBONE[CONVOLUTIONAL BACKBONE]
        F1
        F2
        F3
        F4
        F5
    end

    style P1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C
    style R fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
    style BACKBONE fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B
```

---

## 4.4 Effect of Stride and Padding on Output Dimensions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT 7x7] --> B1[SAME PADDING P equals floor K divided by 2]
    A --> B2[VALID PADDING P equals 0]
    A --> B3[STRIDE 1 OUTPUT 7x7]
    A --> B4[STRIDE 2 OUTPUT 3x3]

    B1 --> C1[OUTPUT SAME SIZE 7x7]
    B2 --> C2[OUTPUT SHRUNK 5x5]
    B3 --> C3[FEATURE MAP PRESERVED]
    B4 --> C4[FEATURE MAP DOWNSAMPLED 2X]

    style A fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style C2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style C3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style C4 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00
```

---

## 4.5 Parameter Sharing Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[INPUT PIXEL A] --> S[SHARED KERNEL W]
    N2[INPUT PIXEL B] --> S
    N3[INPUT PIXEL C] --> S
    N4[INPUT PIXEL D] --> S
    S --> O1[OUTPUT A PRIME]
    S --> O2[OUTPUT B PRIME]
    S --> O3[OUTPUT C PRIME]
    S --> O4[OUTPUT D PRIME]

    style S fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px
    style O1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style O2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style O3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style O4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single set of kernel weights `W` is convolved across **all** spatial locations of the input. This *parameter sharing* is the primary reason CNNs generalise so well on images.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Convolutional Neural Network. List its main components.
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a deep, feed-forward neural network specialised for processing grid-structured data such as images. It applies learnable convolution filters across the input to extract hierarchical spatial features.

**Main components (2 marks):**

1. **Convolutional Layer** — Performs the discrete convolution of learnable kernels with input feature maps.
2. **Activation Layer** — Applies a non-linear function (commonly **ReLU**) to introduce representational capacity.
3. **Pooling Layer** — Performs spatial down-sampling (max or average pooling) to reduce resolution and add translation invariance.
4. **Fully Connected Layer** — Acts as the classifier using flattened high-level features.
5. **Softmax Layer** — Converts logits into class probabilities.

> **Examiner Tip:** Always capitalise **ReLU**, **MAX POOL**, and **FC** — KTU evaluators reward proper terminology.

---

### Q2. Why is weight sharing important in CNNs? How does it reduce parameters?
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Weight sharing** means the same kernel weights are used at every spatial position of the input feature map (1 mark).

**Importance (1 mark):**
- Enforces **translation equivariance** — features are detected regardless of their position.
- Reflects the statistical property of natural images that the same pattern (edge, corner) can occur anywhere.

**Parameter reduction (1 mark):**

Without sharing, a layer with $F$ filters on input $H \times W \times C_{in}$ would require $H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot F$ weights. With sharing, only $F \cdot (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in})$ weights are needed — a reduction by a factor of roughly $\frac{H \cdot W}{K_h \cdot K_w}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]`**

**(a) Explain the convolution operation in detail with a suitable example. Derive the formula for output spatial dimension. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The convolution operation slides a learnable kernel $W$ of size $K_h \times K_w$ across the input image, computing element-wise products and summing them at each spatial location. For multi-channel input, the kernel extends through the full depth, and the result is summed across channels.

$$
Y[i,j] = \sigma\!\left(\sum_{m=0}^{K_h-1} \sum_{n=0}^{K_w-1} \sum_{c=0}^{C_{in}-1} W[m,n,c] \cdot X[i+m, j+n, c] + b\right)
$$

**Example (3.5 marks):** Use the $5 \times 5$ input and $3 \times 3$ vertical edge-detector kernel from Section 3.4. Show three positions explicitly:

- $Y[0,0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-1) + 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + 2 \cdot (-1) + 3 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-1) = 1$
- $Y[0,1] = -1$ (compute as shown in Section 3.4)
- $Y[0,2] = 2$

**Output derivation (2 marks):** The spatial output dimension is given by:

$$
\boxed{\,O = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2P - K}{S} \right\rfloor + 1\,}
$$

Derivation as in Section 3.1.

---

**(b) Explain max pooling and average pooling with diagrams. Discuss their advantages and the role of pooling in CNNs. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Max Pooling (2 marks):**
For each non-overlapping window of size $P_h \times P_w$, the maximum activation is selected.

$$Y[i,j] = \max_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} X[i+m, j+n]$$

**Average Pooling (2 marks):**
For each window, the activations are averaged.

$$Y[i,j] = \frac{1}{P_h \cdot P_w} \sum_{(m,n) \in \text{window}} X[i+m, j+n]$$

**Diagrams (1.5 marks):** Show $2 \times 2$ max pooling on a $4 \times 4$ input — highlight taking the maximum of each quadrant.

**Role and Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Spatial Down-sampling** — Reduces feature map size, lowering memory and computation in deeper layers.
2. **Translation Invariance** — Small shifts in the input produce the same pooled output.
3. **Receptive Field Expansion** — Each subsequent neuron sees a larger effective region of the original input.
4. **Overfitting Reduction** — Fewer parameters reach the FC classifier.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Max Pooling | Average Pooling |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Keeps strongest signal | Smooths the signal |
| When useful | When sparse, sharp features matter | When global context matters |
| Gradient | Sparse (only max position updates) | Dense (all positions update) |
| Used in | AlexNet, VGG, ResNet | GoogLeNet (aux heads), DenseNet (transition layers) |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]`**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical CNN for image classification. Explain the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Use the diagram from Section 4.1.

**Block-by-block explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Input Layer:** Accepts an RGB image of fixed size (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$). Pixel values are usually normalised to $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$.

2. **Convolutional Blocks (Conv → ReLU → Pool):** Repeated multiple times. Each block extracts progressively more abstract features:
   - **Block 1:** Edges, colour gradients.
   - **Block 2:** Textures, corners, simple shapes.
   - **Block 3:** Object parts (eyes, wheels).
   - **Block 4+:** Whole-object representations.

3. **Flatten Layer:** Converts the 3D feature map ($H \times W \times C$) into a 1D vector of length $H \cdot W \cdot C$.

4. **Fully Connected Layers:** Learn non-linear combinations of high-level features. Often two FC layers (e.g., 4096 → 1000) in classical architectures like VGG-16.

5. **Softmax Layer:** Converts logits into a probability distribution over $C$ classes:

$$\hat{y}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

6. **Output Layer:** The class label with the highest probability is the prediction.

**Training block (extra credit mention):** The network is trained end-to-end using **stochastic gradient descent (SGD)** or variants (Adam, RMSProp) with the **cross-entropy loss**:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$$

---

**(b) Discuss the role of activation functions in CNNs. Compare ReLU, Sigmoid, and Tanh. Why is ReLU preferred in modern CNNs? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Activation (1.5 marks):**
Activation functions introduce **non-linearity** into the network. Without them, stacking any number of convolutional layers would mathematically collapse into a single linear operation, making the network unable to model complex patterns.

**ReLU — Rectified Linear Unit (2 marks):**

$$f(x) = \max(0, x)$$

- Gradient is exactly $1$ for $x > 0$ — **no vanishing gradient** for positive activations.
- Computationally trivial ($O(1)$ — single comparison).
- Induces **sparsity** by zeroing negative activations.

**Sigmoid (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

- Squashes outputs to $(0, 1)$.
- **Vanishing gradient problem** for large positive or negative $x$ (gradient $\to 0$).
- Outputs are not zero-centred, slowing convergence.

**Tanh (1 mark):**

$$\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$$

- Zero-centred outputs in $(-1, 1)$.
- Still suffers from vanishing gradient at saturation regions.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Function | Range | Zero-Centred | Vanishing Gradient | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ReLU | $[0, \infty)$ | No | No (for $x > 0$) | Very Low |
| Sigmoid | $(0, 1)$ | No | Yes | High (exp) |
| Tanh | $(-1, 1)$ | Yes | Yes | High (exp) |

**Why ReLU is preferred (additional credit):** Empirical results show ReLU-trained CNNs converge **6× faster** than tanh/sigmoid networks and achieve higher final accuracy. ReLU is the default choice in AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, and all modern architectures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing *correlation* with *convolution*.** Many textbooks (and NumPy) implement *cross-correlation* — the kernel is not flipped. KTU accepts both interpretations **provided** you state the version you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the bias term** $b$ in the convolution equation — this costs **1 mark** every single time.
> 3. **Writing $|x|$ instead of $\max(0, x)$ for ReLU** — wrong! ReLU is a piecewise function, not an absolute value.
> 4. **Not justifying ReLU over sigmoid** with vanishing-gradient reasoning — KTU specifically tests this comparison.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation of output dimensions** — a formula dump without derivation loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing up stride and padding effects** — remember: stride down-samples, padding preserves size.
> 7. **Forgetting to specify the Softmax for multi-class classification** — classification head ≠ arbitrary FC.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Convolutional Networks**

- **CNN = Convolution + Non-linearity + Pooling + FC** — the holy quartet.
- **Convolution equation:** $Y[i,j] = \sigma(\sum_{m,n,c} W[m,n,c] \cdot X[i+m, j+n, c] + b)$.
- **Output dimension formula:** $O = \lfloor (W_{in} + 2P - K)/S \rfloor + 1$.
- **CONV parameter count:** $(K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot F$.
- **FC parameter count:** $N_{in} \cdot N_{out} + N_{out}$.
- **Weight sharing** is the key to CNN parameter efficiency — the **same kernel** slides across the whole image.
- **Local receptive field** + **spatial down-sampling** + **hierarchical features** = why CNNs dominate vision tasks.
- **Receptive field growth:** $r_L = r_{L-1} + (K_L - 1) \cdot \prod S_l$.
- **Pooling types:** Max (keeps strongest), Average (smooths), Global Average (replaces FC).
- **ReLU** is the default activation — no vanishing gradient for positive $x$, induces sparsity.
- **Softmax** converts logits to probabilities for multi-class classification.
- **Cross-entropy loss** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ is the standard training objective.
- **Translation equivariance** (convolution) + **translation invariance** (pooling) are the two key inductive biases of CNNs.
- **Stride** controls down-sampling; **padding** (usually *"same"*) preserves spatial dimensions.
- **Modern CNNs** (ResNet, EfficientNet, ConvNeXt) all build on these foundational primitives.
- **Common architectures** to remember for KTU viva: **LeNet-5, AlexNet, VGG-16, GoogLeNet, ResNet-50**.

> **Final Note:** Convolutional Networks are the **single most important architecture family** in Computer Vision. Mastering the convolution equation, output dimension formula, and parameter count derivation will secure you the majority of marks in any KTU question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **convolutional filter** (also called a **convolution kernel**, **convolution mask**, or **spatial filter**) is a small two-dimensional matrix of weights that is systematically slid across a digital image to compute a transformed output image. At every spatial location, the filter performs a weighted sum of the pixel intensities in its local neighborhood, producing a single output value. Mathematically, a 2D convolution transforms an input image $I(x,y)$ into an output image $I'(x,y)$ by correlating a kernel $K(i,j)$ across all valid spatial positions.

The size of the filter is conventionally odd (e.g., **3x3**, **5x5**, **7x7**) so that there is a well-defined center pixel. The choice of values inside the kernel directly defines what the filter does: smoothing, sharpening, edge detection, or feature extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In the Foundations of Computer Vision course, convolutional filters are treated as the *atomic building blocks* of all classical image-processing pipelines. Every high-level CV task (edge detection, blurring, template matching, CNN feature extraction) is ultimately a stack of convolutional filter operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Flashlight-Stencil Analogy)

Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a small, square cardboard **stencil** (the filter) with numbers scribbled in each of its 9 square holes. You slide this stencil across a giant pixel-grid mosaic painted on the floor. At each position you:

1. **Align** the stencil over a 3x3 patch of tiles.
2. **Multiply** each stencil number with the tile color underneath it.
3. **Sum** all nine products and write the result on a fresh sheet of paper.
4. **Slide** the stencil one tile to the right (or down) and repeat.

After covering the entire mosaic, your fresh sheet of paper is the **filtered image**. The stencil is the **filter**, the mosaic is the **input image**, and the fresh sheet is the **output (feature map)**.

If the stencil is filled with identical positive numbers, the fresh image will look **blurred** (averaging). If the stencil has positive numbers in the center and negatives around the edges, the fresh image will highlight **boundaries** (sharpening/edge detection). The stencil literally *re-shapes* what we see.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Recap:**
> - **Kernel / Filter / Mask:** The small matrix of weights.
> - **Stride:** The number of pixels the filter moves per step.
> - **Padding:** The fictitious pixels added around image borders.
> - **Receptive Field:** The local region of input pixels that influence a single output pixel.
> - **Feature Map:** The output image produced by a filter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The 2D Discrete Convolution

Let $I(x,y)$ be a discrete grayscale image of size $M \times N$ and $K(i,j)$ be a convolution kernel of size $(2a+1) \times (2b+1)$ centered at the origin. The **2D discrete convolution** is formally defined as:
