This justifies why the FC layers can perform global classification from these features. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Input Size:** Students using $224 \times 224$ (a common textbook figure derived from the *caffe* implementation note) get slightly different numbers ($55 \times 55$ in Conv1 only works with $227$). Use **$227 \times 227$** to match the original paper, or explicitly state the convention used. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Internal Choice Alternative] **(a)** Explain the architecture of **VGG19** with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the pooling strategy adopted and compare it with AlexNet. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given an input image of $224 \times 224 \times 3$, compute the output dimensions of the feature map after every block of convolutions in VGG19. Also compute the **total number of trainable parameters** in VGG19 (considering the FC layers). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **VGG19 Architecture (19 weight layers):** VGG19 consists of **5 convolutional blocks**, each ending with a max pooling layer, followed by 3 fully connected layers. **Block-wise structure:** - **Block 1:** 2 × Conv(3×3, 64) → MaxPool(2×2, s=2). Output: 112×112×64. - **Block 2:** 2 × Conv(3×3, 128) → MaxPool(2×2, s=2). Output: 56×56×128. - **Block 3:** 4 × Conv(3×3, 256) → MaxPool(2×2, s=2). Output: 28×28×256. - **Block 4:** 4 × Conv(3×3, 512) → MaxPool(2×2, s=2). Output: 14×14×512. - **Block 5:** 4 × Conv(3×3, 512) → MaxPool(2×2, s=2). Output: 7×7×512. - **FC6:** 4096 units + Dropout 0.5. - **FC7:** 4096 units + Dropout 0.5. - **FC8:** 1000-way Softmax. **Pooling Strategy in VGG19:** - **Type:** Max Pooling. - **Configuration:** $f=2, s=2$ — **non-overlapping** pooling. - **Placement:** Once at the end of each of the 5 convolutional blocks, totaling **5 pooling layers**. - **Effect:** Each pooling reduces the spatial resolution by exactly half, taking $224 \to 112 \to 56 \to 28 \to 14 \to 7$. **Comparison with AlexNet Pooling:** | **Aspect** | **AlexNet** | **VGG19** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Pooling type | Max Pooling | Max Pooling | | Configuration | $f=3, s=2$ (overlapping) in early layers | $f=2, s=2$ (non-overlapping) | | Frequency | After Conv1, Conv2, Conv5 (3 times) | After every block (5 times) | | Spatial reduction | $227 \to 27 \to 13 \to 6$ (irregular) | $224 \to 112 \to 56 \to 28 \to 14 \to 7$ (uniform halving) | | Design philosophy | Aggressive early reduction | Gradual, uniform reduction | **Key Distinction:** VGG19's *uniform* halving allows the network to maintain a smoothly growing receptive field, while AlexNet's irregular reduction was a design choice driven by GPU memory constraints in 2012. #### Model Solution to (b): **Output dimension calculation per block:** | **Block** | **Input H × W × C** | **Conv (preserves size)** | **MaxPool halves HW** | **Output H × W × C** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $224 \times 224 \times 3$ | $224 \times 224 \times 64$ | $\lfloor(224-2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 112$ | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ | | 2 | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ | $112 \times 112 \times 128$ | $\lfloor(112-2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 56$ | $56 \times 56 \times 128$ | | 3 | $56 \times 56 \times 128$ | $56 \times 56 \times 256$ | $\lfloor(56-2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 28$ | $28 \times 28 \times 256$ | | 4 | $28 \times 28 \times 256$ | $28 \times 28 \times 512$ | $\lfloor(28-2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 14$ | $14 \times 14 \times 512$ | | 5 | $14 \times 14 \times 512$ | $14 \times 14 \times 512$ | $\lfloor(14-2)/2\rfloor + 1 = 7$ | $7 \times 7 \times 512$ | | Flatten | $7 \times 7 \times 512 = 25088$ | — | — | 25088 | **Parameter count:** **Convolutional parameters (Block-wise):** | **Block** | **Conv Layers** | **Parameters per layer** | **Block Total** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | 2 (3→64) | $(9\cdot 3 + 1)\cdot 64 = 1792$ | $3{,}584$ | | 2 | 2 (64→128) | $(9\cdot 64 + 1)\cdot 128 = 73{,}856$ | $147{,}712$ | | 3 | 4 (128→256, 256→256 ×3) | $295{,}168 + 3\cdot(9\cdot 256 + 1)\cdot 256$ | $2{,}359{,}808$ | | 4 | 4 (256→512, 512→512 ×3) | $1{,}180{,}160 + 3\cdot(9\cdot 512 + 1)\cdot 512$ | $9{,}440{,}256$ | | 5 | 4 (512→512 ×4) | $4 \cdot (9\cdot 512 + 1)\cdot 512 = 9{,}440{,}256$ | $9{,}440{,}256$ | | **Conv Total** | 16 | — | $\mathbf{\sim 20.4 \text{ M}}$ | **Fully Connected parameters:** - **FC6:** $(25088 + 1) \cdot 4096 = 102{,}764{,}544$. - **FC7:** $(4096 + 1) \cdot 4096 = 16{,}781{,}312$. - **FC8:** $(4096 + 1) \cdot 1000 = 4{,}097{,}000$. - **FC Total:** $\mathbf{\sim 123.6 \text{ M}}$. **Grand Total:** $\sim 20.4\text{M} + 123.6\text{M} \approx \mathbf{138 \text{ million trainable parameters}}$. **Key Observation:** FC layers account for $\sim 89.5\%$ of VGG19's parameters — a major reason for the design trend (GoogLeNet, MobileNet) toward replacing FC with **Global Average Pooling**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — FC parameter calculation:** A common error is forgetting the **bias term** ($+1$) in the formula. Always include it unless explicitly told "without bias." Also, VGG19 is sometimes listed as having $\sim 144$M parameters depending on whether one includes the input preprocessing layer; the **138M figure is the canonical count** for the 19-layer model. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Pooling is parameter-free** ($P_{pool} = 0$) — never write that it has weights. - **Max Pooling** is the default in AlexNet and VGG19; it preserves the strongest activations. - **Average Pooling** smooths features; **Global Average Pooling** replaces FC layers in modern architectures. - **AlexNet uses overlapping max pooling** with $f=3, s=2$ in early layers — reduces top-1 error by ~0.4%. - **VGG19 uses non-overlapping max pooling** with $f=2, s=2$ after each of 5 conv blocks. - **Output dimension formula:** $n_{out} = \lfloor (n_{in} + 2p - f)/s \rfloor + 1$. - **Receptive field grows with depth**, amplified by pooling. In VGG19, the final pool neuron has RF = 94 × 94 out of a 224 × 224 input. - **AlexNet = 8 layers, ~60M parameters**, won ILSVRC 2012 (top-5 error 15.3%). - **VGG19 = 19 weight layers, ~138M parameters**, runner-up at ILSVRC 2014. - **VGG19's FC layers hold ~89% of total parameters** — this is why GAP-based architectures emerged. - **Pooling happens per-channel**, with no cross-channel mixing. - **Overlapping pooling is now rarely used** — VGG-style $2 \times 2$ stride-2 pooling is the modern default, although modern architectures like ResNet and ConvNeXt often use **strided convolutions** to replace pooling entirely. - **Translation invariance from pooling** is *local*; modern architectures prefer explicit mechanisms (e.g., data augmentation, batch normalization) for robustness. - **For KTU exams, always state the input size convention explicitly** — AlexNet's $227 \times 227$ (paper) vs. $224 \times 224$ (caffe) discrepancy is a common trap. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Tip (PECDT743):** When asked "compare pooling in AlexNet and VGG19," always present a *side-by-side table* comparing pooling type, configuration ($f, s$), frequency, and the resulting feature map dimensions. Examiners award full marks only when both architectures are *explicitly contrasted* with numerical support, not merely described separately.

topics.title: Segmentation Using Clustering Methods: Human Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmentation Using Clustering Methods: Human Vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (sets of pixels) based on intensity, color, texture, or spatial continuity. **Segmentation using clustering methods** refers to the unsupervised classification of pixels (or feature vectors) into groups (clusters) such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized, without relying on training labels.

**Human Vision** in the context of computer vision is modeled as a perceptual system that segments scenes based on gestalt principles (similarity, proximity, continuity, closure), luminance/contrast gradients, and pre-attentive features, providing the biological inspiration for computational segmentation algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically examines how clustering algorithms emulate the *pre-attentive grouping* capability of the human visual cortex (V1–V4 areas), bridging biological perception with computational feature grouping.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a crowded auditorium. Instantly—without thinking—your brain groups people into "rows," "the speaker's podium," and "empty seats." This automatic grouping is **Human Visual Segmentation**. Now, suppose a robot enters the same room with only a camera. It "sees" 10 million individual pixels. To mimic your brain, the robot uses **clustering methods** to group nearby, similarly-colored pixels into coherent "regions" (faces, walls, floor). The clustering algorithm acts as the robot's *artificial cortex*, applying the same gestalt rules your eyes apply every waking second.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The foundational metrics used universally in KTU board examinations for clustering-based segmentation are:

- **Euclidean Distance:** The standard L2 norm metric, $d = \sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}$
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average squared deviation of pixels from cluster centroid, measured in intensity units squared
- **Silhouette Coefficient:** Validity index ranging in $[-1, +1]$ measuring cluster cohesion vs. separation
- **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia):** Compactness metric, $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Clustering-Based Segmentation

**Step 1 — Feature Space Construction**
Each pixel $p_i = (x_i, y_i)$ is mapped to a multi-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{f}_i = [R, G, B, x, y]^T$ (color + spatial coordinates). The human eye does this in the LGN (Lateral Geniculate Nucleus) for color, and V1 for spatial position.

**Step 2 — Initialization of Centroids**
Choose $k$ initial cluster centers $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_k$. Methods include *Forgy* (random samples), *Random Partition*, or *K-Means++* (probabilistic seeding favoring distant centroids).

**Step 3 — Assignment (E-Step / Expectation)**
Every pixel vector $\mathbf{f}_i$ is assigned to its nearest centroid using a distance metric (usually Euclidean). Mathematically:
$$C_j^{(t)} = \{ \mathbf{f}_i : \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \le \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_l^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \;\; \forall \; l = 1, 2, \dots, k \}$$

**Step 4 — Update (M-Step / Maximization)**
Centroids are recomputed as the mean of all vectors assigned to that cluster:
$$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j^{(t)}} \mathbf{f}_i$$

**Step 5 — Convergence Check**
Iterate Steps 3–4 until centroids stabilize ($\vert\mu_j^{(t+1)} - \mu_j^{(t)}\vert < \epsilon$) or maximum iterations reached.

**Step 6 — Label Mapping**
Each pixel receives the integer label of its assigned cluster, producing the segmented image.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why feature space?** Humans do not perceive pixels in isolation; they aggregate over color, motion, and edge gradients — a feature vector mimics this multi-channel integration.
- **Why iterative EM?** Human perception is also iterative: you refine your "grouping" decision (e.g., reassigning an ambiguous shadow to a new object) as you scan the scene.
- **Why Euclidean distance?** It corresponds to the psychophysical *just-noticeable-difference (JND)* curve of human luminance perception, which is approximately log-linear in the perceptual domain.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $d(\mathbf{f}_i, \mu_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(f_{id} - \mu_{jd})^2}$ | Euclidean distance between pixel vector and centroid | pixels in feature space |
| 2 | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$ | WCSS / Inertia (objective minimized by K-Means) | squared distance units |
| 3 | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{N_j}\sum_{i \in C_j}\mathbf{f}_i$ | Centroid update rule | mean of pixel features |
| 4 | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Silhouette coefficient for sample $i$ | dimensionless, $[-1, 1]$ |
| 5 | $I(x,y) = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Luminance (perceptual brightness) | $0$ to $255$ |
| 6 | $E_{kmeans} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\min_{j}\vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$ | Total K-Means quantization error | squared distance units |
| 7 | $P(\mu_j) = \frac{D(x)^2}{\sum_l D(x_l)^2}$ | K-Means++ seeding probability | probability, $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | $\nabla I(x,y) = \left[\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\right]$ | Image gradient (edge map) | intensity / pixel |
| 9 | $k_{opt} = \arg\max_k \; s(k)$ | Optimal cluster count by silhouette | integer count |
| 10 | $LAB = f(RGB)$ | CIE-L*a*b* perceptual color space transform | perceptual units |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power **medical image analysis** (tumor segmentation in MRI), **autonomous vehicles** (road/lane/pedestrian grouping), and **satellite remote sensing** (land-cover classification). Companies like Tesla, Siemens Healthineers, and ISRO use variants of these formulas daily.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Objective Function (Why we Minimize WCSS)

The objective of K-Means is to find the partition $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares. We derive the optimal centroid given a fixed partition first.

**Given a fixed partition** $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k$, the WCSS is:
$$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$$

**Step 1:** We minimize $J$ with respect to $\mu_j$ (the centroid of cluster $j$). Taking the partial derivative and setting it to zero:
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} (\mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

**Step 2:** This is the standard mean-update equation. Solving:
$$\sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{f}_i - \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \mu_j = 0$$

**Step 3:** Since $\mu_j$ is constant within cluster $j$, $\sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \mu_j = N_j \mu_j$. Therefore:
$$N_j \mu_j = \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{f}_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_j = \frac{1}{N_j} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \mathbf{f}_i$$

**Conversion Logic:** This proves the *centroid must equal the arithmetic mean* of all points assigned to that cluster for the WCSS to be locally minimized.

**Step 4 (Assignment Derivation):** For a fixed $\mu$, we want to assign each $\mathbf{f}_i$ to the cluster minimizing its squared distance:
$$C_j^* = \arg\min_{j} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$$

**Step 5 (Convergence Proof):** Each iteration of (Step 3) followed by (Step 4) is guaranteed to *monotonically decrease* $J$ because:
- Updating $\mu_j$ to the cluster mean minimizes $J$ for the current partition.
- Reassigning points to nearest centroid can only decrease or maintain $J$ for the current $\mu$.

Since $J \ge 0$ is bounded below, the algorithm **must converge** in finite steps.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A $4 \times 4$ grayscale image patch (16 pixels) with intensity values $\{10, 12, 11, 13, \; 110, 115, 108, 112, \; 50, 52, 48, 51, \; 105, 108, 110, 107\}$ is to be segmented using K-Means with $k=2$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = 10, \; \mu_2 = 50$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment:**

| Pixel $f_i$ | $\vert f_i - 10 \vert^2$ | $\vert f_i - 50 \vert^2$ | Cluster Assigned |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 10 | 0 | 1600 | 1 |
| 12 | 4 | 1444 | 1 |
| 11 | 1 | 1521 | 1 |
| 13 | 9 | 1369 | 1 |
| 110 | 10000 | 3600 | 2 |
| 115 | 11025 | 4225 | 2 |
| 108 | 9604 | 3364 | 2 |
| 112 | 10404 | 3844 | 2 |
| 50 | 1600 | 0 | 2 |
| 52 | 1764 | 4 | 2 |
| 48 | 1444 | 4 | 2 |
| 51 | 1681 | 1 | 2 |
| 105 | 9025 | 3025 | 2 |
| 108 | 9604 | 3364 | 2 |
| 110 | 10000 | 3600 | 2 |
| 107 | 9409 | 3249 | 2 |

**Result:** $C_1 = \{10, 12, 11, 13\}$ (4 pixels), $C_2 = \{110, 115, 108, 112, 50, 52, 48, 51, 105, 108, 110, 107\}$ (12 pixels).

**Iteration 1 — Centroid Update:**
$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{10+12+11+13}{4} = 11.5$$
$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{110+115+108+112+50+52+48+51+105+108+110+107}{12} = \frac{1076}{12} = 89.67$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-assignment:** All pixels originally in $C_1$ stay (since $89.67$ is far). Pixels with intensity $50, 52, 48, 51$ now compute:
- $|50 - 11.5|^2 = 1482.25$ vs $|50 - 89.67|^2 = 1574.69$ → stays in $C_2$ (barely).

The algorithm converges, and the final segmentation divides the patch into a *dark background region* (10–13) and a *bright foreground region* (48–115).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** K-Means in pure intensity space may mislabel mid-gray pixels (around 50) if the centroids are far apart. This is *exactly* why modern pipelines use **LAB color space** and **spatial coordinates** jointly — mimicking the human visual cortex's combination of color and location cues.

### 3.3 Silhouette Coefficient Derivation

For each sample $\mathbf{f}_i$ in cluster $C_j$:

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{N_j - 1} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_l \in C_j, l \ne i} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{f}_l \vert\vert$$

$$b(i) = \min_{m \ne j} \frac{1}{N_m} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_l \in C_m} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{f}_l \vert\vert$$

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $s(i) \approx 1$: Well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$: On boundary.
- $s(i) < 0$: Possibly misclassified.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: K-Means Segmentation Pipeline (Human-Vision-Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Feature Vector Construction f = R G B x y]
    B --> C[Initialize k Centroids mu 1 to mu k]
    C --> D{Assignment Step E Step}
    D --> E[Compute Euclidean Distance d fi muj]
    E --> F[Assign Pixel to Nearest Cluster]
    F --> G[Update Centroids M Step]
    G --> H{Convergence Check epsilon}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I[Generate Segmentation Map]
    I --> J[Post processing Morphological Ops]
    J --> K[Final Segmented Output]
    
    L[Human Visual Cortex V1 V4] -.-> B
    L -.-> E
    L -.-> F
```

**Caption:** The diagram shows the iterative Expectation–Maximization loop of K-Means, with dotted arrows indicating how the human visual cortex (V1: edge detection, V4: color/feature integration) provides biological inspiration at each computational step.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Human Vision → Clustering Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bio["Human Visual System"]
        direction TB
        B1[Retina: Photoreceptor Sampling] --> B2[LGN: Luminance/Color Channel]
        B2 --> B3[V1: Edge and Gradient Detection]
        B3 --> B4[V2: Texture Grouping]
        B4 --> B5[V4: Color and Form Integration]
        B5 --> B6[IT Cortex: Object Recognition]
    end
    
    subgraph Comp["Computational Clustering System"]
        direction TB
        C1[Image Sensor: Pixel Sampling] --> C2[Color Space Convert: RGB to LAB]
        C2 --> C3[Gradient Filter: Sobel / Canny]
        C3 --> C4[Texture Descriptor: GLCM / LBP]
        C4 --> C5[Feature Stack: 5D Vector]
        C5 --> C6[K-Means / Mean Shift]
    end
    
    B1 -.-> C1
    B2 -.-> C2
    B3 -.-> C3
    B4 -.-> C4
    B5 -.-> C5
    B6 -.-> C6
```

**Caption:** A side-by-side mapping of biological visual hierarchy (left) to the computational clustering pipeline (right). Each cortical layer corresponds to a specific algorithmic stage, illustrating the isomorphism between neuroscience and computer vision segmentation.

### 4.3 Flowchart: Choosing the Optimal Cluster Count $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Image Loaded] --> P1[Compute WCSS for k = 2 to 10]
    P1 --> P2[Plot Elbow Curve]
    P2 --> P3{Elbow Point Detected?}
    P3 -->|Yes| P4[Select k at Elbow]
    P3 -->|No| P5[Compute Silhouette Scores]
    P5 --> P6[Select k with Max s]
    P4 --> P7[Final k Selected]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> E[Run K-Means with k]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Explain how the gestalt principles of human perception influence clustering-based image segmentation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Gestalt principles—similarity, proximity, continuity, and closure—describe how the human visual system groups visual elements into coherent objects. In clustering-based segmentation, these are implemented as:
- **Similarity →** Euclidean/color distance metric in feature space (pixels of similar color are grouped).
- **Proximity →** Spatial $(x, y)$ terms in the feature vector (nearby pixels favored).
- **Continuity →** Connectivity constraints in connected-component labeling post-processing.
- **Closure →** Morphological closing operations to fill gaps.

This biological inspiration is why K-Means and Mean-Shift algorithms in LAB color space often produce perceptually pleasing segmentations, mimicking pre-attentive vision. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is the LAB color space preferred over RGB for clustering-based image segmentation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LAB color space is **perceptually uniform**, meaning the Euclidean distance between two LAB colors corresponds closely to the *just-noticeable-difference (JND)* perceived by the human eye. In contrast, RGB distances do not match human perception (e.g., the green channel dominates perceived brightness). Additionally, LAB separates luminance ($L$) from chrominance ($a, b$), allowing clustering to operate independently on intensity and color, which reduces illumination artifacts. This makes LAB clusters correspond more naturally to human-segmented regions, improving segmentation quality. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Describe the K-Means clustering algorithm for image segmentation. Explain the role of the WCSS objective function with its mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $256 \times 256$ RGB image is to be segmented using K-Means with $k = 3$ in 5-dimensional feature space $[R, G, B, x, y]^T$. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of including spatial coordinates, and propose an improved method that overcomes the limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) K-Means Algorithm (7 marks):**

The K-Means algorithm partitions $N$ pixel feature vectors $\{\mathbf{f}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ into $k$ disjoint clusters $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k$ by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS):

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j} \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$$

where $\mu_j$ is the centroid of cluster $j$. The algorithm proceeds iteratively:

1. **Initialization:** Select $k$ initial centroids (Forgy, Random Partition, or K-Means++).
2. **Assignment (E-step):** Assign each $\mathbf{f}_i$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
$$C_j^{(t)} = \{ \mathbf{f}_i : \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \le \vert\vert \mathbf{f}_i - \mu_l^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \;\; \forall l\}$$
3. **Update (M-step):** Recompute centroids as cluster means:
$$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{|C_j^{(t)}|} \sum_{\mathbf{f}_i \in C_j^{(t)}} \mathbf{f}_i$$
4. **Convergence:** Repeat 2–3 until $\vert\mu_j^{(t+1)} - \mu_j^{(t)}\vert < \epsilon$ for all $j$.

The WCSS objective function serves as the **mathematical criterion** ensuring that clusters are compact; minimization of $J$ guarantees intra-cluster similarity is maximized, satisfying the gestalt "similarity" principle of human vision. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Spatial Coordinates in Feature Vector (7 marks):**

**Advantages:**
- Enforces **proximity** (gestalt principle): spatially adjacent pixels are more likely to belong to the same cluster.
- Produces **spatially coherent** regions rather than scattered "salt-and-pepper" misclassifications.
- Reduces sensitivity to noise because isolated outlier pixels in a homogeneous region are pulled toward the dominant cluster.

**Disadvantages:**
- Clusters tend to be **spatially compact blobs**, often failing to capture elongated or irregularly shaped objects (e.g., a thin river, a road).
- Equal weighting of color and spatial dimensions can distort the segmentation; choosing weights $w_c, w_s$ becomes an arbitrary hyperparameter.
- Computational cost increases by 2 dimensions ($O(Nkd)$ per iteration).

**Improved Method — SLIC (Simple Linear Iterative Clustering):**
SLIC adapts K-Means by:
1. Limiting the search space to a $2S \times 2S$ region around each centroid (where $S = \sqrt{N/k}$), reducing complexity to $O(N)$.
2. Using a weighted 5D distance:
$$D = \sqrt{\left(\frac{d_c}{m}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d_s}{S}\right)^2}$$
   where $d_c$ is LAB color distance, $d_s$ is spatial distance, and $m$ is the compactness parameter.
3. Producing **superpixels** — perceptually meaningful atomic regions that respect object boundaries.

This addresses the limitations of naïve spatial-coordinate inclusion by adaptively balancing color and spatial fidelity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the biological hierarchy of the human visual system and map each layer to its corresponding computational step in clustering-based segmentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare K-Means and Mean-Shift clustering algorithms for image segmentation. Discuss when each is preferred, citing computational complexity and parameter sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Human Visual System Mapping (7 marks):**

| Biological Layer | Function | Computational Equivalent |
|------------------|----------|--------------------------|
| Retina | Photoreceptor sampling; A/D conversion of light | Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) sampling |
| LGN | Luminance/chrominance channel separation | RGB → LAB color space conversion |
| V1 (Primary) | Edge detection, orientation tuning | Sobel/Canny gradient filters |
| V2 | Texture, contour integration | GLCM/LBP texture descriptors |
| V4 | Color and form integration | Feature vector assembly $\mathbf{f} = [L, a, b, x, y]$ |
| IT Cortex | Object recognition, segmentation | K-Means / Mean-Shift / Watershed |

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Retina → LGN → V1 → V2 → V4 → IT Cortex
   ↓        ↓    ↓    ↓    ↓        ↓
 Sensor   RGB→LAB  Edge  Texture  Feature  Cluster
                            Vector
```

**Explanation:** The retina samples the image (analogous to digital pixel acquisition). The LGN segregates parvocellular (color) and magnocellular (luminance/motion) channels — computationally realized by switching to LAB space. V1 detects oriented edges via Gabor-like filters, equivalent to gradient operators. V2 and V4 integrate these into texture and form features, which are concatenated into the clustering input vector. Finally, the IT cortex groups features into objects, which is precisely the role of clustering algorithms. The **isomorphism** between these layers is the foundation of bio-inspired computer vision. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) K-Means vs. Mean-Shift Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | K-Means | Mean-Shift |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| **Cluster count $k$** | Required as input | Automatically determined |
| **Cluster shape** | Spherical / isotropic | Arbitrary (mode-seeking) |
| **Robustness to outliers** | Low (sensitive) | High (kernel-based) |
| **Computational complexity** | $O(NkdT)$ per iteration | $O(N^2)$ naive; $O(N\log N)$ with KD-trees |
| **Convergence** | Guaranteed to local minimum | Guaranteed (mode of density) |
| **Parameter sensitivity** | Initialization-sensitive | Bandwidth $h$ is critical |
| **Best for** | Compact, well-separated regions | Irregular, density-based regions |

**When to prefer K-Means:**
- When the user has prior knowledge of $k$ (e.g., segmenting into 3 tissue types in MRI).
- When clusters are expected to be roughly spherical and equally sized.
- When computational speed is critical (real-time video segmentation).

**When to prefer Mean-Shift:**
- When $k$ is unknown or varies across the image.
- For object tracking and image retrieval applications.
- When the underlying cluster structure is non-convex (e.g., handwriting, medical lesions).

**Conclusion:** For KTU 2024 applications, K-Means remains the **default choice** in introductory implementations, while Mean-Shift is preferred for advanced, non-parametric segmentation. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write "K-Means converges to the global minimum." **Incorrect** — it converges only to a *local minimum* depending on initialization. Use K-Means++ or multi-restart to mitigate. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing "WCSS" with "BCSS" (Between-Cluster Sum of Squares). The K-Means objective is WCSS only.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to **normalize features** (especially spatial $(x, y)$) before clustering. Image coordinates range in $[0, 255]$ but intensity also ranges in $[0, 255]$ — failing to normalize biases clustering toward spatial coordinates. **(−2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying the **color space** (RGB vs. LAB vs. HSV) when describing a segmentation pipeline. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires color-space awareness. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In the K-Means derivation, students often forget to show the partial derivative step. The full derivation $\partial J / \partial \mu_j = -2\sum(\mathbf{f}_i - \mu_j) = 0$ must be written. **(−2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Clustering = unsupervised grouping**; no labels are required, unlike classification.
- [x] **K-Means** minimizes WCSS, converges to a *local* minimum, and is sensitive to initialization.
- [x] **K-Means++** is the gold-standard seeding method (probability $\propto D(x)^2$).
- [x] **Mean-Shift** automatically determines $k$ via density-mode seeking, ideal when cluster count is unknown.
- [x] **Feature space** typically includes color (LAB preferred) and spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ — never use RGB alone for perceptually accurate segmentation.
- [x] **Gestalt principles** (similarity, proximity, continuity, closure) are the biological inspiration for algorithmic design.
- [x] **WCSS** (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) is the objective function of K-Means.
- [x] **Silhouette coefficient** $s \in [-1, +1]$ is the standard internal validity index.
- [x] **Elbow method** is used to heuristically choose $k$.
- [x] **SLIC superpixels** adapt K-Means to produce perceptually meaningful, boundary-respecting atomic regions.
- [x] **Human visual cortex hierarchy:** Retina → LGN → V1 (edges) → V2 (texture) → V4 (color/form) → IT (objects).
- [x] **Convergence proof:** Each K-Means iteration monotonically decreases $J \ge 0$, so the algorithm terminates.
- [x] **LAB > RGB > HSV** ordering of color-space preference for clustering (perceptual uniformity).
- [x] **Always normalize** features before clustering when dimensions have different units/scales.
- [x] **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4 (Apply clustering techniques for image segmentation)** at Bloom's Level 3 (Apply) and Level 4 (Analyze).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grouping and Gestalt; Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels: Simple Clustering Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Grouping and Gestalt; Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels: Simple Clustering Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Grouping in Computer Vision

**Grouping** is the cognitive and computational process of organizing image elements (pixels, edges, regions) into meaningful higher-level structures that correspond to objects, surfaces, or parts. In computer vision, grouping attempts to bridge the gap between low-level image primitives and high-level object recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Grouping is the process of organizing low-level image features (pixels, edges, or regions) into perceptually meaningful entities based on properties like similarity, proximity, and continuity, thereby reducing the representational complexity of an image from individual pixels to coherent structures.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a crowded classroom. Instead of seeing 50 individual students as 50 separate entities, your brain instantly groups them into rows, columns, or maybe "my friends vs strangers" clusters. You are not consciously counting heads; you are **grouping**. This automatic, hard-wired capability of human perception is exactly what computer vision tries to replicate algorithmically. The **Gestalt school of psychology** (early 20th century, German researchers like Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka) was the first to formally study and document this behavior.

## 1.2 The Gestalt Principles

The **Gestalt principles** (German for "form" or "shape") are a set of laws describing how humans naturally perceive visual elements as organized patterns. They form the theoretical foundation of image grouping in computer vision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU exams, the following Gestalt principles are considered *core* and frequently appear as short-answer questions worth 3 marks.

| Principle | Core Idea | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **Proximity** | Elements close to each other are grouped | Spacing |
| **Similarity** | Elements with similar features group | Color, shape, size |
| **Continuity** | Elements aligned along a smooth path group | Curves, lines |
| **Closure** | Incomplete shapes are perceived as whole | Gaps in boundaries |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric regions are perceived as unified figures | Mirror symmetry |
| **Common Fate** | Elements moving in the same direction group | Motion, velocity |
| **Figure-Ground** | Distinguishing objects (figure) from background (ground) | Contrast, occlusion |

## 1.3 Image Segmentation by Clustering Pixels

**Image segmentation** is the partitioning of an image into non-overlapping, semantically meaningful regions. When performed by **clustering pixels**, we treat pixels (or features extracted from pixels) as points in a feature space and apply unsupervised grouping algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Pixel-based clustering segmentation partitions the set of image pixels $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N\}$ into $K$ disjoint clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ such that pixels within the same cluster are more similar to each other than to pixels in other clusters, based on a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ computed from pixel $p_i$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a satellite image of a city. Some pixels represent buildings (gray, textured), some represent roads (dark, elongated), and others represent parks (green, smooth). If you plot every pixel as a point in 3D space using the axes **(Red, Green, Blue)**, the three region-types naturally form three visible **clouds** in space. A clustering algorithm is like drawing boundaries around these clouds to recover the city's semantic map.

## 1.4 Simple Clustering Methods — Overview

Simple clustering methods for image segmentation use straightforward distance metrics and agglomerative procedures. They are computationally efficient and serve as baseline algorithms for the segmentation task.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Simple Clustering (Bolded Constants):**
> - **Euclidean Distance:** $L_2$ norm, $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$
> - **Manhattan Distance:** $L_1$ norm, $d = \sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$
> - **Chebyshev Distance:** $L_\infty$ norm, $d = \max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Gestalt Principles — Theoretical Foundation

The Gestaltists proposed the famous maxim: **"The whole is other than the sum of its parts."** This implies that perception of a structured scene is not merely the sum of individual pixels but emerges from the way elements are organized.

### 2.1.1 Principle of Proximity
Elements that are spatially close tend to be perceived as a group. In image processing, this translates to the assumption that pixels belonging to the same object are spatially adjacent.

**Why it matters:** Most segmentation algorithms (region growing, watershed) implicitly rely on proximity. A pixel's neighbors are more likely to share its label than faraway pixels.

### 2.1.2 Principle of Similarity
Pixels with similar color, intensity, or texture are grouped, regardless of distance.

**How it works:** This is the explicit assumption behind **feature-space clustering** — pixels with similar feature vectors belong to the same cluster.

### 2.1.3 Principle of Continuity
The visual system prefers continuous, smooth contours over abrupt changes. Edges are perceived as following the smoothest possible path.

**Engineering utility:** Used in **edge-linking** algorithms and in the formulation of **active contours (snakes)**.

### 2.1.4 Principle of Closure
The mind completes incomplete figures. This is the basis for **boundary completion** and **figure-ground separation** in algorithms like normalized cuts.

### 2.1.5 Principle of Symmetry and Common Fate
Symmetric regions are perceptually salient, and elements sharing motion vectors are grouped. Common fate is fundamental to **motion segmentation** in videos.

## 2.2 The Role of Grouping in Computer Vision Pipeline

In a typical KTU-prescribed vision pipeline, grouping is the **bridge** between:

$$\text{Image Acquisition} \rightarrow \text{Low-level Processing} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Grouping/Segmentation}} \rightarrow \text{Recognition/Description}$$

Grouping reduces the dimensionality of the visual data. Instead of processing $\sim 10^6$ pixels in a 1MP image, grouping might yield $\sim 10^3$ coherent regions, making higher-level recognition tractable.

## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation of Pixel Clustering

Given a set of $N$ pixels with feature vectors $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, the goal is to find cluster assignments $\{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$ where $z_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ such that:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i : z_i = k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$$

is minimized, where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the centroid (mean) of cluster $k$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Standard similarity measure in feature space |
| Manhattan Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\vert x_i - y_i\vert$ | Faster, robust to outliers |
| Within-Cluster Scatter (Inertia) | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{i \in C_k}\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k\vert\vert^2$ | K-means objective |
| Cluster Centroid Update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k}\mathbf{x}_i$ | Mean of all points in cluster |
| Agglomerative Linkage (Single) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{i \in C_a, j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Connects closest pair across clusters |
| Agglomerative Linkage (Complete) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{i \in C_a, j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Uses farthest pair; tighter clusters |
| Agglomerative Linkage (Average) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a\vert \cdot \vert C_b\vert}\sum_{i \in C_a, j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Measures clustering quality |
| Davies-Bouldin Index | $DB = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\max_{k' \neq k}\left(\frac{\sigma_k + \sigma_{k'}}{d(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k'})}\right)$ | Lower is better |
| K-means Convergence | $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)}\vert < \epsilon$ | Stopping criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention:** $\vert C_k \vert$ denotes the cardinality (number of elements) of cluster $C_k$. Never confuse with absolute value — always read in context.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor segmentation in MRI/CT scans using K-means on intensity feature vectors.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Road and obstacle segmentation for scene understanding.
- **Remote Sensing:** Land-use classification from satellite imagery.
- **Industrial Inspection:** Defect detection by clustering pixel colors/textures.
- **Biometrics:** Skin segmentation in face detection pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 K-Means Clustering — Full Derivation and Algorithm

**K-Means** is the canonical example of a *simple clustering method* and the most frequently asked algorithm in KTU examinations.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Given $N$ data points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and an integer $K$, find cluster assignments and centroids minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (WCSS):

$$J(\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k\}, \{z_i\}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i : z_i = k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Centroid Update Rule

To find the optimal centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ for a fixed assignment $\{z_i\}$, take the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = -2 \sum_{i : z_i = k} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = 0$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:

$$\sum_{i : z_i = k} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i : z_i = k} \mathbf{x}_i - \sum_{i : z_i = k} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = 0$$

$$\sum_{i : z_i = k} \mathbf{x}_i - \vert C_k \vert \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = 0$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i}$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal centroid is simply the **arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to the cluster.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Assignment Update Rule

For a fixed centroid set $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k\}$, the optimal assignment of point $\mathbf{x}_i$ is to the cluster whose centroid is **closest** in Euclidean distance. This follows by observing that $J$ is a sum of independent non-negative terms, and minimizing the term $\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$ for each $i$ independently:

$$z_i = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$$

### 3.1.4 Lloyd's Algorithm — Complete Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 (Initialize):** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K^{(0)}$ (randomly, or via K-means++ seeding).

**Step 2 (Assignment Step):** For each data point $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign it to the nearest centroid:

$$z_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2$$

**Step 3 (Update Step):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of assigned points:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{i : z_i^{(t)} = k} \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 4 (Convergence Check):** If $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)}$ for all $k$, or if $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)}\vert < \epsilon$, stop. Otherwise, return to Step 2.

### 3.1.5 Convergence Proof Sketch

K-Means is guaranteed to converge in finite steps because:
1. The assignment step cannot increase $J$ (each point goes to its closest centroid).
2. The update step minimizes $J$ with respect to centroids (by the derivation above).
3. There are finitely many possible assignments ($K^N$), so the algorithm must terminate.

## 3.2 Worked Example — K-Means on 1D Intensity Data

**Given:** Five pixels with intensity values $\mathbf{x} = [10, 12, 25, 28, 30]$; $K = 2$.

**Initial centroids:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = 10$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = 25$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step:**

For $x_1 = 10$: $d(10, 10) = 0$, $d(10, 25) = 15$ → assign to cluster 1.
For $x_2 = 12$: $d(12, 10) = 2$, $d(12, 25) = 13$ → assign to cluster 1.
For $x_3 = 25$: $d(25, 10) = 15$, $d(25, 25) = 0$ → assign to cluster 2.
For $x_4 = 28$: $d(28, 10) = 18$, $d(28, 25) = 3$ → assign to cluster 2.
For $x_5 = 30$: $d(30, 10) = 20$, $d(30, 25) = 5$ → assign to cluster 2.

Result: $C_1 = \{10, 12\}$, $C_2 = \{25, 28, 30\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \frac{10 + 12}{2} = 11$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \frac{25 + 28 + 30}{3} = 27.67$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step:**

For $x_1 = 10$: $d(10, 11) = 1$, $d(10, 27.67) = 17.67$ → cluster 1.
For $x_2 = 12$: $d(12, 11) = 1$, $d(12, 27.67) = 15.67$ → cluster 1.
For $x_3 = 25$: $d(25, 11) = 14$, $d(25, 27.67) = 2.67$ → cluster 2.
For $x_4 = 28$: $d(28, 11) = 17$, $d(28, 27.67) = 0.33$ → cluster 2.
For $x_5 = 30$: $d(30, 11) = 19$, $d(30, 27.67) = 2.33$ → cluster 2.

**Iteration 2 — Update Step:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(2)} = \frac{10 + 12}{2} = 11 \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(2)} = \frac{25 + 28 + 30}{3} = 27.67 \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

**Convergence reached.** Final clusters: $C_1 = \{10, 12\}$, $C_2 = \{25, 28, 30\}$.

## 3.3 Agglomerative Clustering — Full Derivation

**Agglomerative clustering** is a hierarchical, bottom-up method. It starts with $N$ singleton clusters and iteratively merges the two closest clusters until only $K$ remain.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm

**Step 1:** Initialize $\mathcal{C} = \{\{p_1\}, \{p_2\}, \ldots, \{p_N\}\}$ (each pixel is its own cluster).

**Step 2:** Compute the pairwise distance matrix $D$ where $D[i, j] = d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

**Step 3:** Find the pair $(C_a, C_b)$ minimizing the chosen linkage distance.

**Step 4:** Merge $C_a$ and $C_b$ into $C_{ab} = C_a \cup C_b$.

**Step 5:** Update the distance matrix $D$ using the linkage formula.

**Step 6:** Repeat Steps 3-5 until $K$ clusters remain.

### 3.3.2 Linkage Distance Derivation

**Single Linkage:** $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{i \in C_a, j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$

**Complete Linkage:** $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{i \in C_a, j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$

**Average Linkage:** $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{i \in C_a} \sum_{j \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$

### 3.3.3 Lance-Williams Update Formula

When clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$ are merged into $C_{a \cup b}$, the distance to any other cluster $C_c$ is updated as:

$$d(C_{a \cup b}, C_c) = \alpha_a \cdot d(C_a, C_c) + \alpha_b \cdot d(C_b, C_c) + \beta \cdot d(C_a, C_b) + \gamma \cdot \vert d(C_a, C_c) - d(C_b, C_c) \vert$$

The coefficients $(\alpha_a, \alpha_b, \beta, \gamma)$ define the linkage type. For example, **average linkage** uses $\alpha_a = \frac{\vert C_a \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert}$, $\alpha_b = \frac{\vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert}$, $\beta = \gamma = 0$.

## 3.4 Divisive Clustering — Analytical Overview

Divisive clustering is the **top-down** counterpart of agglomerative clustering. It starts with all points in one cluster and recursively splits clusters.

**Steps:**
1. Begin with $\mathcal{C} = \{\mathcal{P}\}$ (all pixels in one cluster).
2. At each step, select a cluster to split (often the one with largest internal variance or diameter).
3. Apply a partitioning algorithm (e.g., 2-means) to divide the chosen cluster.
4. Repeat until $K$ clusters are obtained or a stopping criterion is met.

**Computational complexity:** Generally more expensive than agglomerative for large $N$ because the top-level decision is harder to reverse.

## 3.5 Mean-Shift Clustering — Theoretical Foundation

**Mean-shift** is a non-parametric, density-based clustering method particularly useful for image segmentation.

### 3.5.1 Kernel Density Estimate

Given $N$ data points, the kernel density estimate at location $\mathbf{y}$ is:

$$\hat{f}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{N h^d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} K\left(\frac{\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

where $K(\cdot)$ is a kernel function (commonly the **Gaussian kernel**: $K(\mathbf{u}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert\vert \mathbf{u} \vert\vert^2}{2}\right)$) and $h$ is the **bandwidth** parameter.

### 3.5.2 Mean-Shift Vector

Taking the gradient of $\hat{f}(\mathbf{y})$ and setting it to zero yields the **mean-shift vector**:

$$\mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i \cdot g\left(\frac{\vert\vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_i \vert\vert^2}{h^2}\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} g\left(\frac{\vert\vert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_i \vert\vert^2}{h^2}\right)} - \mathbf{y}$$

where $g(\cdot)$ is the derivative of the kernel profile. The mean-shift procedure iteratively moves the window center $\mathbf{y} \leftarrow \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{m}(\mathbf{y})$ until convergence.

### 3.5.3 Application to Image Segmentation

For an RGB image, treat each pixel as a 3D point in color space (or 5D if spatial coordinates are included: $(x, y, R, G, B)$). Run mean-shift from a grid of starting points; pixels that converge to the same mode belong to the same segment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Clustering-Based Segmentation

The following diagram depicts the complete functional flow of clustering-based image segmentation, mapping all interactions between the various algorithmic stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Feature Extraction]
    B --> C[Pixel Feature Vectors]
    C --> D{Clustering Algorithm}
    D --> E[K-Means]
    D --> F[Agglomerative]
    D --> G[Mean Shift]
    D --> H[Divisive]
    E --> I[Cluster Assignment Map]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Region Labeling]
    J --> K[Post-Processing]
    K --> L[Segmented Output]
    
    M[User Parameters] --> D
    M --> B
    N[Distance Metric] --> D
    O[Bandwidth h] --> G
    P[Number of Clusters K] --> E
    P --> F
    P --> H
```

## 4.2 Gestalt Principle Application Map

The following diagram shows how each Gestalt principle maps to a corresponding computer vision technique.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Principle: Proximity] --> T1[Region Growing, Watershed]
    P2[Principle: Similarity] --> T2[K-Means, Mean Shift]
    P3[Principle: Continuity] --> T3[Edge Linking, Snakes]
    P4[Principle: Closure] --> T4[Boundary Completion, Hough Transform]
    P5[Principle: Symmetry] --> T5[Symmetry Detection]
    P6[Principle: Common Fate] --> T6[Motion Segmentation, Optical Flow]
    P7[Principle: Figure-Ground] --> T7[Normalized Cuts, GrabCut]
    
    T1 --> OUT[Image Segmentation Output]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T5 --> OUT
    T6 --> OUT
    T7 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 K-Means Iterative Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start] --> S1[Initialize K Centroids]
    S1 --> S2[For Each Pixel x_i]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Distance to All Centroids]
    S3 --> S4[Assign to Nearest Centroid]
    S4 --> S5[All Pixels Assigned?]
    S5 --> S6[Recompute Centroids as Means]
    S6 --> S7[Convergence Check]
    S7 --> S8[Centroids Unchanged?]
    S8 --> S9[Yes: Output Clusters]
    S8 --> S10[No: Return to Assignment Step]
    S10 --> S2
    S9 --> S11[End]
```

## 4.4 Agglomerative Clustering Dendrogram Concept

A dendrogram is a tree-like structure that records the merge sequence in agglomerative clustering. The following schematic illustrates a binary merging hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Level 0: All Singletons] --> L1[Level 1: First Merge]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2: Second Merge]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Final K Clusters]
    
    N1[Pixel A] --> M1[Cluster AB]
    N2[Pixel B] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Cluster ABC]
    N3[Pixel C] --> M2
    N4[Pixel D] --> M3[Cluster DE]
    N5[Pixel E] --> M3
    M2 --> M4[Cluster ABCDE]
    M3 --> M4
```

## 4.5 Mean-Shift Iteration Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Window Center y0] --> B[Compute Mean-Shift Vector m y0]
    B --> C[Update y1 = y0 + m y0]
    C --> D[Converged?]
    D --> E[Yes: Mode Found]
    D --> F[No: Continue Iteration]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Assign All Pixels Converging Here to Same Cluster]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List and explain any four Gestalt principles used in image grouping."**

**Model Answer:**

The Gestalt principles describe how humans perceptually group visual elements. Four key principles are:

1. **Principle of Proximity:** Pixels or objects that are spatially close to one another are perceived as belonging to the same group. The visual system uses spatial distance as a primary cue for organization.

2. **Principle of Similarity:** Elements that share similar visual features (color, intensity, shape, texture) are grouped together regardless of their spatial distance. This is the foundation of feature-space clustering algorithms like K-means.

3. **Principle of Continuity:** The visual system prefers smooth, continuous contours over abrupt changes. Elements aligned along a smooth path are perceived as forming a single coherent entity.

4. **Principle of Closure:** Incomplete or broken figures are perceived as complete wholes. The mind "fills in" missing information, which is exploited in algorithms for boundary completion and contour detection.

(Alternative: **Symmetry, Common Fate, Figure-Ground** may also be mentioned for full credit.)

**[Marks Distribution: 1 mark for listing, 2 marks for explanation (0.5 each)]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is meant by image segmentation by clustering pixels? Mention any two simple clustering methods."**

**Model Answer:**

**Image segmentation by clustering pixels** is the process of partitioning the pixels of an image into multiple groups (clusters) such that pixels within the same cluster are more similar to each other (in terms of intensity, color, texture, or spatial features) than to pixels in other clusters. The segmentation is performed by treating each pixel as a point in a multidimensional feature space and applying an unsupervised clustering algorithm.

**Two simple clustering methods:**

1. **K-Means Clustering:** An iterative, centroid-based algorithm that partitions pixels into K clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances. It alternates between assigning pixels to the nearest centroid and updating centroids as cluster means.

2. **Agglomerative Clustering:** A hierarchical, bottom-up method that starts with each pixel as a separate cluster and iteratively merges the two closest clusters based on a linkage criterion (single, complete, or average linkage) until K clusters remain.

(Alternative: **Mean-Shift, Divisive Clustering** may also be cited.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**"Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm for image segmentation with its mathematical formulation. Apply K-Means on the following 1D intensity data to obtain 2 clusters: $\{8, 10, 15, 18, 22, 25\}$. Assume initial centroids are $\mu_1 = 8$ and $\mu_2 = 22$."**

#### Part (a): Mathematical Formulation of K-Means (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** K-Means clustering aims to partition a set of $N$ data points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$ into $K$ disjoint clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (WCSS).

**Objective Function:**

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Select $K$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K^{(0)}\}$ randomly or via K-Means++.

2. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:

$$z_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2$$

3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$$

4. **Convergence:** Iterate Steps 2 and 3 until centroids no longer change or the change in $J$ falls below a threshold $\epsilon$.

**Convergence Guarantee:** K-Means always converges to a local minimum in finite steps because the number of possible cluster assignments is bounded by $K^N$.

**Advantages:** Simple, fast, scales well to large datasets.
**Disadvantages:** Sensitive to initialization, requires specifying $K$ in advance, assumes spherical clusters.

---

#### Part (b): Worked Example on 1D Data (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Data points: $\{8, 10, 15, 18, 22, 25\}$; $K = 2$; initial centroids $\mu_1 = 8$, $\mu_2 = 22$.

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step:**

For each point, compute $\vert x - \mu_1 \vert$ and $\vert x - \mu_2 \vert$:

- $x = 8$: $\vert 8 - 8 \vert = 0$, $\vert 8 - 22 \vert = 14$ → Cluster 1
- $x = 10$: $\vert 10 - 8 \vert = 2$, $\vert 10 - 22 \vert = 12$ → Cluster 1
- $x = 15$: $\vert 15 - 8 \vert = 7$, $\vert 15 - 22 \vert = 7$ → Tie (assign to Cluster 1 by convention)
- $x = 18$: $\vert 18 - 8 \vert = 10$, $\vert 18 - 22 \vert = 4$ → Cluster 2
- $x = 22$: $\vert 22 - 8 \vert = 14$, $\vert 22 - 22 \vert = 0$ → Cluster 2
- $x = 25$: $\vert 25 - 8 \vert = 17$, $\vert 25 - 22 \vert = 3$ → Cluster 2

Result: $C_1^{(1)} = \{8, 10, 15\}$, $C_2^{(1)} = \{18, 22, 25\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{8 + 10 + 15}{3} = 11$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{18 + 22 + 25}{3} = 21.67$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step:**

- $x = 8$: $\vert 8 - 11 \vert = 3$, $\vert 8 - 21.67 \vert = 13.67$ → Cluster 1
- $x = 10$: $\vert 10 - 11 \vert = 1$, $\vert 10 - 21.67 \vert = 11.67$ → Cluster 1
- $x = 15$: $\vert 15 - 11 \vert = 4$, $\vert 15 - 21.67 \vert = 6.67$ → Cluster 1
- $x = 18$: $\vert 18 - 11 \vert = 7$, $\vert 18 - 21.67 \vert = 3.67$ → Cluster 2
- $x = 22$: $\vert 22 - 11 \vert = 11$, $\vert 22 - 21.67 \vert = 0.33$ → Cluster 2
- $x = 25$: $\vert 25 - 11 \vert = 14$, $\vert 25 - 21.67 \vert = 3.33$ → Cluster 2

Result: $C_1^{(2)} = \{8, 10, 15\}$, $C_2^{(2)} = \{18, 22, 25\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Update Step:**

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \frac{8 + 10 + 15}{3} = 11 \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \frac{18 + 22 + 25}{3} = 21.67 \quad (\text{unchanged})$$

**Convergence achieved.**

**Final clusters:** $C_1 = \{8, 10, 15\}$, $C_2 = \{18, 22, 25\}$ with centroids $\mu_1 = 11$ and $\mu_2 = 21.67$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Discuss the role of Gestalt principles in image segmentation. With neat diagrams, explain agglomerative and divisive clustering methods. Compare their computational complexity and segmentation quality."**

#### Part (a): Gestalt Principles in Image Segmentation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Gestalt principles, formulated by the Gestalt school of psychology in the early 20th century, describe how humans perceptually group visual elements into coherent structures. These principles serve as the theoretical foundation for many image segmentation algorithms.

**Key Gestalt Principles and Their Algorithmic Counterparts:**

1. **Proximity:** Pixels close in space form a region. Implemented in **region growing, watershed algorithms**, where local neighborhoods are aggregated.

2. **Similarity:** Pixels with similar features (color, intensity, texture) belong to the same object. The basis of **K-Means, mean-shift, and graph-cut** segmentation.

3. **Continuity:** Smooth, continuous contours are preferred. Used in **edge-linking, active contour models (snakes)** for boundary detection.

4. **Closure:** The mind completes broken figures. Exploited in **Hough transform** for detecting partially occluded shapes.

5. **Figure-Ground:** Distinguishing objects from background. Implemented in **normalized cuts, GrabCut, and saliency-based methods**.

6. **Common Fate:** Pixels moving together are grouped. Central to **motion segmentation using optical flow**.

**Role in Segmentation:** Gestalt principles guide algorithm design by encoding human perceptual priors. They help transform a low-level pixel grid into perceptually meaningful regions that align with human visual judgment, which is critical for tasks like object recognition and scene understanding.

---

#### Part (b): Agglomerative vs. Divisive Clustering (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Agglomerative Clustering (Bottom-Up):**
- Starts with $N$ singleton clusters (each pixel is its own cluster).
- Iteratively merges the two closest clusters based on a linkage criterion (single, complete, average).
- Produces a hierarchy represented as a **dendrogram**.
- A cut at level $K$ in the dendrogram yields $K$ final clusters.
- **Complexity:** $O(N^3)$ naive, $O(N^2 \log N)$ with priority queue, $O(N^2)$ space.

**Divisive Clustering (Top-Down):**
- Starts with all $N$ points in a single cluster.
- Recursively splits the most heterogeneous cluster using a partitioning algorithm (e.g., 2-Means, or the cluster with largest diameter).
- Also produces a dendrogram, but built top-down.
- **Complexity:** $O(2^N)$ in worst case (deciding the optimal split is NP-hard), but heuristics reduce this to $O(N^2 \log N)$ or better.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| **Starting State** | $N$ clusters | 1 cluster |
| **Decision Reversibility** | Cannot undo merges | Cannot undo splits |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N^2 \log N)$ typical | $O(2^N)$ worst case |
| **Cluster Shape** | Better for elongated (single link) | Tends to produce balanced splits |
| **Scalability** | Limited by $N$ | More scalable for large $N$ |
| **Segmentation Quality** | Sensitive to noise and outliers | Can miss fine-grained substructure |

**Diagram of Dendrogram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Pixels in One Cluster] --> B[Split into Two]
    B --> C[Split Sub-cluster A]
    B --> D[Split Sub-cluster B]
    C --> E[Smaller Sub-cluster A1]
    C --> F[Smaller Sub-cluster A2]
    D --> G[Smaller Sub-cluster B1]
    D --> H[Smaller Sub-cluster B2]
```

**Conclusion:** Agglomerative methods are more commonly used in practice due to their lower complexity and ease of implementation. Divisive methods are preferred when the goal is to discover global structure in data.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the centroid update formula:** Many students write $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$ instead of dividing by $\vert C_k \vert$. **Always divide by the number of points in the cluster.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to iterate K-Means until convergence:** A single assignment-update cycle is **not** K-Means. You must repeat until centroids stabilize.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Gestalt principles with image processing filters:** Gestalt principles are **perceptual organization rules**, not algorithmic filters. Mentioning "blur" or "edge detection" as Gestalt principles is incorrect.
> 
> 4. **Not specifying the linkage type in agglomerative clustering:** Single, complete, and average linkages produce different results. Always state which one is used.
> 
> 5. **Computational complexity errors:** $O(N^2)$ is not the same as $O(N^2 \log N)$. Be precise.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the tie-breaking rule in worked examples:** When distances are equal, state your convention explicitly to avoid ambiguity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Grouping** bridges low-level pixel processing and high-level object recognition.
- The **Gestalt maxim** is: *"The whole is other than the sum of its parts."*
- The **seven core Gestalt principles** are: Proximity, Similarity, Continuity, Closure, Symmetry, Common Fate, and Figure-Ground.
- **Image segmentation by clustering** treats pixels as points in a feature space and groups them using unsupervised learning.
- **K-Means** minimizes the **WCSS** objective: $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$.
- The K-Means centroid update rule is the **arithmetic mean** of cluster members: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$.
- K-Means **converges to a local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum.
- **Agglomerative clustering** is **bottom-up**; **divisive clustering** is **top-down**.
- Linkage types for agglomerative clustering: **single, complete, average** (and Ward's method).
- **Mean-shift** is a non-parametric, density-based method that finds modes of the data distribution.
- The **bandwidth parameter $h$** critically controls mean-shift results.
- For KTU exams: **always state assumptions, formulas explicitly, and converge the algorithm fully in worked examples.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering and Segmentation by K-Means; Object Detection: YOLO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clustering and Segmentation by K-Means; Object Detection: YOLO

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image Segmentation & Clustering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (sets of pixels), commonly called *segments* or *super-pixels*, such that pixels within a region share homogeneous low-level features (intensity, colour, texture) while being dissimilar from pixels in neighbouring regions.

**K-Means clustering**, in the context of computer vision, is an **unsupervised vector-quantisation algorithm** that partitions $N$ feature vectors (pixels) into $K$ disjoint clusters by iteratively minimising the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called **Inertia**. Each pixel is represented as a point in a multi-dimensional feature space (typically RGB = 3-D, or Lab = 3-D, or RGB+XY = 5-D for spatial coherence).

---

## 1.2 Object Detection — YOLO Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **YOLO (You Only Look Once)** is a **unified, single-stage, real-time object detection framework** that reframes detection as a *single regression problem* from image pixels to spatially separated bounding boxes and associated class probabilities. The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid, and a single Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) predicts all bounding boxes, confidence scores, and class probabilities in **one forward pass**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **K-Means Analogy — "Sorting Laundry by Colour":**
> Imagine you dump a pile of mixed T-shirts on a table. You pick *K* shirts at random as your "centroid representatives" (e.g., a red, a blue, and a green one). Now, every other shirt walks over and stands next to the closest-coloured representative. After all shirts have grouped, you pick a new representative — the **average colour** of each group. Repeat until the representatives stop moving. The groups you get are the *clusters*, and each region of similar-coloured pixels becomes one *segment* of the image.

> [!TIP]
> **YOLO Analogy — "A Photographer Scanning a Stadium Crowd":**
> Instead of cropping the photo into 1000 small windows and asking "Is there a face here? Here? Here?" (the slow, *two-stage* R-CNN approach), a smart photographer **glances at the entire image once**, mentally overlays a 7×7 grid, and in each grid cell, instantly reports *"the centre of a person is HERE, the person is THIS big, and they are 90% likely to be a cricketer."* That single glance — that single forward pass — is exactly what YOLO does.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU High-Yield Highlights)

| Symbol / Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **K** | Number of clusters / segments (user-defined hyper-parameter) |
| **μ_k** | Centroid of cluster $k$ |
| **S × S** | YOLO grid resolution (e.g., 7×7 in YOLOv1) |
| **B** | Number of bounding boxes predicted per grid cell |
| **C** | Number of object classes |
| **λ_coord = 5** | YOLO loss weighting for coordinate error |
| **λ_noobj = 0.5** | YOLO loss weighting for "no object" confidence error |
| **IoU** | Intersection over Union — overlap ratio of two boxes |
| **WCSS / J** | Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (objective to minimise) |
| **NMS** | Non-Maximum Suppression — post-processing step |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means — Operational Logic Breakdown

The K-Means algorithm proceeds in **four rigorous steps** that alternate between an *assignment phase* and an *update phase* until convergence.

**Step 1 — Centroid Initialisation**
- Place $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_K$ in feature space.
- *Naïve method*: random selection of $K$ data points.
- *Improved method (K-Means++)*: pick the first centroid uniformly at random; each subsequent centroid is chosen with probability proportional to $\vert\vert x_i - \mu_{nearest} \vert\vert^2$, ensuring better spread.

**Step 2 — Assignment Step (Expectation-like E-step)**
- For every pixel $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, compute Euclidean distance to each centroid.
- Assign $x_i$ to the cluster whose centroid is nearest.
- This is the "Why": it ensures each point belongs to its most similar cluster, locally minimising within-cluster variance.

**Step 3 — Update Step (Maximisation-like M-step)**
- Recompute each centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to that cluster.
- This is the "How": by setting $\partial J / \partial \mu_k = 0$, the mean is mathematically proven to be the *unique minimiser* of the sum of squared distances within a cluster.

**Step 4 — Convergence Check**
- Terminate if one of the following holds:
  - Centroid positions no longer change (Euclidean shift < tolerance, typically $10^{-4}$).
  - Cluster assignments remain unchanged between iterations.
  - A maximum iteration cap is reached.
- **Guarantee:** The objective function $J$ decreases monotonically, ensuring convergence to a *local minimum* (not necessarily global).

---

## 2.2 YOLO — Operational Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — Grid Discretisation**
- Divide the input image of size $H \times W$ into an $S \times S$ regular grid.
- Each grid cell is responsible for detecting an object whose **centre falls inside that cell**.

**Step 2 — Bounding Box Prediction**
- Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, each parameterised by **5 values**:
  - $(x, y)$ — box centre (offsets relative to grid cell bounds, normalised to $[0,1]$).
  - $(w, h)$ — box width and height (normalised to image dims, predicted as square root for stability).
  - $c$ — **confidence score** = $\Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$.

**Step 3 — Class Probability Prediction**
- Each cell predicts $C$ conditional class probabilities $\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object})$.
- Total output tensor shape: $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$.

**Step 4 — Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**
- Discard overlapping redundant boxes: keep only the box with the highest confidence in each neighbourhood.
- Threshold typically at IoU > 0.5.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are essential for KTU ESE 2024 calculations. **Strictly no `|` pipe characters inside table cells** — LaTeX $\vert$ is used for absolute value.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J \;=\; \sum\limits_{i=1}^{N}\sum\limits_{k=1}^{K} w_{ik}\, \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2$ | K-Means objective (WCSS / Inertia) | Scalar $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | $\mu_k \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum\limits_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Centroid update rule | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 3 | $d(x_i,\mu_k) \;=\; \sqrt{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij}-\mu_{kj})^2}$ | Euclidean distance | Scalar $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | $w_{ik} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k=\arg\min_j \vert\vert x_i-\mu_j \vert\vert \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Indicator / membership matrix | $\{0,1\}$ |
| 5 | $\text{IoU}(B_1,B_2) \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Area}(B_1 \cap B_2)}{\text{Area}(B_1 \cup B_2)}$ | Intersection over Union | $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | $\text{Area}(B_1 \cup B_2) \;=\; A_1 + A_2 - \text{Area}(B_1 \cap B_2)$ | Union area via inclusion-exclusion | pixels² |
| 7 | $C \;=\; \Pr(\text{Obj}) \cdot \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$ | YOLO confidence score | $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{YOLO}} \;=\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{conf}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}$ | Decomposed loss (see §3.4) | Scalar |
| 9 | $v_i \;=\; \dfrac{x_i - x_{\text{cell}}}{W_{\text{cell}}}$ | Grid-relative offset normalisation | $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | $w_i,h_i \;=\; \sigma(\cdot)$ bounded by image size | Width/height in YOLO (often sqrt-stabilised) | pixels |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **K-Means segmentation** powers medical imaging (tumour isolation in MRI), satellite imagery (land-cover classification), and video compression (colour quantisation).
- **YOLO** is deployed in autonomous driving (Tesla-style real-time obstacle detection), surveillance, robotic vision, and industrial defect detection — wherever sub-50 ms inference is mandatory.
- The convergence guarantee of K-Means (monotonic $J$ decrease) makes it predictable in production pipelines; YOLO's single-pass design trades some accuracy for the 30–150 FPS throughput critical in real-time systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: K-Means Centroid is the Cluster Mean

**Goal:** Prove that the centroid $\mu_k$ that minimises $J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2$ is the arithmetic mean of its cluster members.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_k(\mu_k) &= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2 \\
&= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left[ (x_i - \mu_k)^T (x_i - \mu_k) \right] \\
&= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left[ x_i^T x_i - 2\mu_k^T x_i + \mu_k^T \mu_k \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* We expand the squared Euclidean norm as a dot product of the difference with itself, yielding three scalar terms: a constant ($x_i^T x_i$), a linear term ($-2\mu_k^T x_i$), and a quadratic term ($\mu_k^T \mu_k$).

Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_k$ and set it to zero for the optimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mu_k} J_k &= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left[ -2 x_i + 2\mu_k \right] = 0 \\
\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i &= \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_k \\
\therefore \quad \mu_k &= \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Setting the gradient to zero gives the *first-order optimality condition*. Solving for $\mu_k$ shows it equals the mean. The second derivative $2\vert C_k \vert I \succ 0$ confirms this is a minimum, completing the proof.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on 2-D Points

**Data:** $X = \{(1,1),\,(1,2),\,(2,1),\,(8,8),\,(9,8),\,(9,9)\}$, $K = 2$.
**Initial centroids:** $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$, $\mu_2^{(0)} = (9,9)$.

### Iteration 1 — Assignment Step

For each point, compute $d(x, \mu_1)$ and $d(x, \mu_2)$ and assign to the nearer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\!\big((1,1),\mu_1\big) &= 0, & d\!\big((1,1),\mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{128} \approx 11.31 &\Rightarrow C_1 \\
d\!\big((1,2),\mu_1\big) &= 1, & d\!\big((1,2),\mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{113} \approx 10.63 &\Rightarrow C_1 \\
d\!\big((2,1),\mu_1\big) &= 1, & d\!\big((2,1),\mu_2\big) &= \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 &\Rightarrow C_2 \\
d\!\big((8,8),\mu_1\big) &= 9.90, & d\!\big((8,8),\mu_2\big) &= 1 &\Rightarrow C_2 \\
d\!\big((9,8),\mu_1\big) &= 10.63, & d\!\big((9,8),\mu_2\big) &= 1 &\Rightarrow C_2 \\
d\!\big((9,9),\mu_1\big) &= 11.31, & d\!\big((9,9),\mu_2\big) &= 0 &\Rightarrow C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Two natural clusters emerge almost immediately because the Euclidean gap between the two groups is far larger than the intra-group spread.

### Iteration 1 — Update Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{1}\big[(1,1)+(1,2)+(2,1)\big] = \left(\tfrac{4}{3},\tfrac{4}{3}\right) \\
\mu_2^{(1)} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\big[(2,1)+(8,8)+(9,8)+(9,9)\big] = \left(\tfrac{28}{4},\tfrac{26}{4}\right) = (7, 6.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* Three points stayed in $C_1$, four in $C_2$. Apply the mean formula derived in §3.1.

### Iteration 2 — Re-assignment

Re-check assignments with new centroids — all six points retain their clusters, and centroid positions no longer change. **Algorithm converges in 2 iterations.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: YOLO Bounding-Box Coordinate Encoding

**Goal:** Convert raw CNN output into interpretable bounding-box coordinates relative to the image.

The CNN predicts offsets $t_x, t_y$ (centre) and $t_w, t_h$ (size). The grid cell $(c_x, c_y)$ has top-left corner at integer coordinates; the image has width $W$, height $H$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_x &= \sigma(t_x) + c_x \\
b_y &= \sigma(t_y) + c_y \\
b_w &= p_w \cdot e^{t_w} \\
b_h &= p_h \cdot e^{t_h}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* In **YOLOv1**, $b_x, b_y$ are offsets inside the cell, normalised by sigmoid $\sigma$ to $[0,1]$, then shifted by the cell origin $(c_x, c_y)$. In **YOLOv2/v3**, the box dimensions use **exponential scaling** of *anchor priors* $(p_w, p_h)$ — this is "How": the network predicts a *multiplicative correction* to a learned anchor, ensuring positive dimensions regardless of sign of $t_w$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Full YOLO Loss Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \right] \\
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right] \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 \\
