&\quad + \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning (line-by-line):*
- **Line 1** — *Localisation loss for box centres*. Weighted by $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ to emphasise coordinate accuracy. Indicator $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}}$ fires only when object exists in cell $i$, box $j$.
- **Line 2** — *Localisation loss for width & height*. The square-root trick $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ *equalises the relative error* — a 10-pixel error on a small box matters more than on a large box, and the square root transforms absolute differences into a relative measure.
- **Line 3** — *Confidence loss for cells containing an object*.
- **Line 4** — *Confidence loss for empty cells*, *down-weighted* by $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ to prevent the network from being overwhelmed by background cells (most of the grid is empty!).
- **Line 5** — *Classification loss* — squared error on class probabilities, evaluated only for cells containing an object.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Intersection over Union (IoU)

**Given two ground-truth / predicted boxes (axis-aligned):**
- Box $A = (x_{1A}, y_{1A}, x_{2A}, y_{2A}) = (10, 10, 50, 50)$
- Box $B = (x_{1B}, y_{1B}, x_{2B}, y_{2B}) = (30, 30, 70, 70)$

**Step 1 — Area of each box:**
$$
A_A = (50-10)\times(50-10) = 40\times 40 = 1600 \text{ pixels}^2
$$
$$
A_B = (70-30)\times(70-30) = 40\times 40 = 1600 \text{ pixels}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Intersection rectangle (element-wise max for top-left, min for bottom-right):**
$$
x_{1I} = \max(10,30) = 30,\quad y_{1I} = \max(10,30) = 30
$$
$$
x_{2I} = \min(50,70) = 50,\quad y_{2I} = \min(50,70) = 50
$$

**Step 3 — Intersection area:**
$$
A_I = (50-30)\times(50-30) = 20\times 20 = 400 \text{ pixels}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Union area (inclusion–exclusion):**
$$
A_U = A_A + A_B - A_I = 1600 + 1600 - 400 = 2800 \text{ pixels}^2
$$

**Step 5 — IoU:**
$$
\text{IoU}(A,B) = \frac{A_I}{A_U} = \frac{400}{2800} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429
$$

*Interpretation:* IoU of ~14 % indicates weak overlap; typical YOLO detection threshold requires IoU > 0.5 to count as a true positive. The threshold is the **Why** behind why NMS is necessary — many low-IoU redundant boxes get pruned.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithmic Comparison

| Aspect | K-Means (Segmentation) | YOLO (Detection) |
|---|---|---|
| **Learning Paradigm** | Unsupervised, iterative | Supervised, end-to-end CNN |
| **Input** | Feature vectors (pixels) | Raw RGB image |
| **Output** | $K$ cluster labels per pixel | $(x,y,w,h,C,c_1,\dots,c_C)$ per box |
| **Objective** | Minimise WCSS | Minimise multi-task loss $\mathcal{L}$ |
| **Complexity per iter** | $O(NKd)$ | One CNN forward: $O(\text{layers})$ |
| **Convergence** | Local minimum, monotone $J$ | SGD-based, no closed form |
| **Real-time?** | Fast but no semantic meaning | Yes (30–150 FPS for v3–v5) |
| **Limitation** | Sensitive to $K$, initialisation, outliers | Struggles with tiny/overlapping objects |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Clustering Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Image Pixels] --> B[Extract Feature Vectors RGB or Lab]
    B --> C[Initialise K Centroids Random or KMeans++]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Compute Euclidean Distance to Each Centroid]
    D --> E[Assign Each Pixel to Nearest Centroid Cluster]
    E --> F[Update Step: Recompute Each Centroid as Cluster Mean]
    F --> G{Centroids Changed More Than Tolerance}
    G -->|Yes| D
    G -->|No| H[Convergence Achieved]
    H --> I[Map Cluster Labels Back to Image Plane]
    I --> J[Output Segmented Image with K Regions]
```

**Reading guide for KTU:**
1. The loop between $D$ and $F$ is the **iterative heart** of K-Means.
2. The decision node $G$ uses a tolerance check (e.g., $\vert\vert\mu^{(t+1)} - \mu^{(t)}\vert\vert < 10^{-4}$).
3. The final step $I$ is the "image-domain projection" — converting abstract cluster IDs into a colour-coded segmentation map.

---

## 4.2 YOLO End-to-End Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image HxWx3] --> B[Resize to Fixed Size 448x448 or 416x416]
    B --> C[Convolutional Backbone 24 conv layers then 2 FC layers]
    C --> D[Feature Tensor S x S x Bx5 + C]
    D --> E[Decode Boxes: Apply Sigmoid and Anchor Scaling]
    E --> F[Filter Boxes: Confidence Threshold at 0.5]
    F --> G[Apply Non Maximum Suppression IoU Threshold at 0.5]
    G --> H[Output Final Bounding Boxes with Class Labels]
```

**Reading guide for KTU:**
1. The CNN backbone in YOLOv1 uses a GoogLeNet-inspired architecture; YOLOv3+ uses **Darknet-53**.
2. The output tensor $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ is the *unified prediction head* — this is the "single look" that gives YOLO its name.
3. NMS is essential: without it, the same object would receive many overlapping high-confidence boxes.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual K-Means Segmentation — Pixel Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pixel A: Red Region]:::red -->|low distance| C1[Centroid 1 Red]
    P2[Pixel B: Red Region]:::red -->|low distance| C1
    P3[Pixel C: Blue Region]:::blue -->|low distance| C2[Centroid 2 Blue]
    P4[Pixel D: Blue Region]:::blue -->|low distance| C2
    C1 -->|update mean| M1[Mean of Red Cluster]
    C2 -->|update mean| M2[Mean of Blue Cluster]
    classDef red fill:#ff7f7f,stroke:#900,color:#000
    classDef blue fill:#7fbfff,stroke:#006,color:#000
```

**Reading guide for KTU:**
- This depicts the **vector-space view** of K-Means: pixels are points, centroids are attractors, and colour represents cluster identity.
- The dotted edges labelled "low distance" represent the assignment step; the arrows to $M_1, M_2$ represent the update step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define K-Means clustering. Briefly explain how it is applied to image segmentation. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** K-Means is an unsupervised clustering algorithm that partitions $N$ feature vectors into $K$ disjoint clusters by iteratively minimising the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS).
>
> **Application to image segmentation:**
> 1. Each pixel is converted to a feature vector $(R, G, B)$ or $(L, a, b)$.
> 2. $K$ initial centroids are placed in the colour space.
> 3. **Assignment:** each pixel is assigned to its nearest centroid using Euclidean distance.
> 4. **Update:** each centroid is recomputed as the mean of its assigned pixels.
> 5. Steps 3–4 repeat until centroids stabilise, producing $K$ colour-homogeneous segments.
>
> Each pixel's final cluster label is mapped back to its spatial location, yielding a segmented image with $K$ regions.

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

---

### Q2. What is YOLO? Mention any two key advantages of YOLO over traditional object detection methods. `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a single-stage, real-time object detection framework that frames detection as a regression problem from image pixels to bounding boxes and class probabilities, performed in a single CNN forward pass.
>
> **Key Advantages:**
> 1. **Speed:** YOLO processes images at 45–150 FPS (depending on version), making it suitable for real-time applications like autonomous driving — far faster than region-proposal methods like R-CNN.
> 2. **Global context awareness:** Because the entire image is processed in one pass, YOLO implicitly encodes contextual information about object classes (e.g., it rarely confuses a *television* with a *monitor* inside a bedroom scene), unlike sliding-window approaches that see only local patches.
> 3. *(Optional third)* **Unified architecture:** a single CNN replaces the multi-stage pipeline (region proposal + classification), simplifying training and deployment.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *In every module, the student answers either OR choice. Both Question A and Question B below carry 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Attempt ANY ONE full question.*

---

### ❖ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with its objective function. Apply K-Means to cluster the following 6 two-dimensional points into K = 2 clusters: $\{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,8), (9,9)\}$. Take initial centroids as $(1,1)$ and $(9,9)$. Show two iterations. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Objective Function (2 marks):**

$$
J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_{ik}\, \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2
$$

where $w_{ik} = 1$ if $x_i \in C_k$, else $0$.

**Step 2 — Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Initialise $K$ centroids.
2. Repeat until convergence:
   - **Assignment:** $w_{ik} = 1$ iff $k = \arg\min_j \vert\vert x_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$.
   - **Update:** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 1 (2 marks):**

| Point | $d(x, \mu_1=(1,1))$ | $d(x, \mu_2=(9,9))$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | 0 | $\sqrt{128}=11.31$ | $C_1$ |
| $(1,2)$ | 1 | $\sqrt{113}=10.63$ | $C_1$ |
| $(2,1)$ | 1 | $\sqrt{98}=9.90$ | $C_2$ |
| $(8,8)$ | 9.90 | 1 | $C_2$ |
| $(9,8)$ | 10.63 | 1 | $C_2$ |
| $(9,9)$ | 11.31 | 0 | $C_2$ |

*Cluster memberships:* $C_1 = \{(1,1), (1,2)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,1), (8,8), (9,8), (9,9)\}$.

**New centroids:**
$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\tfrac{1+1}{2}, \tfrac{1+2}{2}\right) = (1, 1.5)
$$
$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\tfrac{2+8+9+9}{4}, \tfrac{1+8+8+9}{4}\right) = (7, 6.5)
$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 2 (1 mark):**

Re-compute distances with $\mu_1 = (1, 1.5)$ and $\mu_2 = (7, 6.5)$. All points retain the same cluster assignments — centroids do not change further → **Convergence achieved in 2 iterations.**

---

#### Q.A (b) Explain the architecture of YOLO with a neat block diagram. Describe how the grid-based detection mechanism works. `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Input Image → Resize (448×448) → Conv Layers (24 layers) → FC Layers (2 layers) → Output Tensor (S×S×(B·5+C)) → NMS → Final Boxes
```

**Detailed explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Input layer:** Image resized to $448 \times 448 \times 3$.
2. **Convolutional backbone:** 24 convolutional layers extract hierarchical features (edges → textures → parts → objects). Inspired by GoogLeNet, with $1 \times 1$ reduction layers interleaved.
3. **Fully connected layers:** 2 FC layers flatten the feature map to a $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$ tensor.
4. **Output tensor:** $S = 7$ in YOLOv1; $B = 2$ boxes/cell; $C = 20$ (PASCAL VOC). Final tensor size: $7 \times 7 \times 30$.
5. **Grid-based detection:** the image is divided into a $7 \times 7$ grid. Each cell:
   - Predicts 2 bounding boxes with $(x,y,w,h,\text{confidence})$.
   - Predicts 20 class probabilities $\Pr(\text{Class}_i \mid \text{Object})$.
   - Is responsible for any object whose centre lies within that cell.

**Why this is fast:** Only **one forward pass** is needed per image, as opposed to thousands in sliding-window methods.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B — (14 Marks)

#### Q.B (a) Derive and explain the YOLO loss function. Why are different weighting factors $\lambda_{\text{coord}}$ and $\lambda_{\text{noobj}}$ used? `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Full Loss Function (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i}-\sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i}-\sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right] \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2}\sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 \\
&\quad + \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Coordinate loss (line 1):** Penalises errors in bounding-box centre $(x,y)$ and size $(w,h)$. Square root is applied to $(w,h)$ to make the metric *scale-invariant*.
2. **Confidence loss for "object" cells (line 2, first term):** Penalises confidence error in cells containing an object.
3. **Confidence loss for "no object" cells (line 2, second term):** Penalises background confidence errors, *down-weighted* by $\lambda_{\text{noobj}}$.
4. **Classification loss (line 3):** Penalises class-probability errors, evaluated only in cells that contain an object.

**Why the weighting factors? (1 mark):**

- $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ **upscales** the localisation error to dominate training, because accurate box coordinates are critical for detection.
- $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ **downscales** the background error, because the vast majority of grid cells ($7 \times 7 = 49$, but only a few contain objects) are empty; without this down-weighting, the network would trivially learn to predict "no object everywhere" and the gradient signal from rare positive cells would be drowned out.

---

#### Q.B (b) Explain Intersection over Union (IoU) and Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS). Compute the IoU for Box A = (10,10,50,50) and Box B = (30,30,70,70). `[7 marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**IoU Definition (2 marks):**

$$
\text{IoU}(B_1, B_2) = \frac{\text{Area}(B_1 \cap B_2)}{\text{Area}(B_1 \cup B_2)}
$$

It measures overlap between two bounding boxes, ranging from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

**NMS Explanation (2 marks):**
1. Sort all predicted boxes by confidence score in descending order.
2. Pick the box with highest confidence, add it to the final list.
3. Remove all boxes that overlap with it above an IoU threshold (typically 0.5).
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until no boxes remain.
5. **Purpose:** Eliminates duplicate detections of the same object, retaining only the most confident one.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

- Area of $A$ = $40 \times 40 = 1600$ px²
- Area of $B$ = $40 \times 40 = 1600$ px²
- Intersection rectangle: $(30,30)$ to $(50,50)$ → Area = $20 \times 20 = 400$ px²
- Union area: $1600 + 1600 - 400 = 2800$ px²
- **IoU = 400 / 2800 ≈ 0.143**

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU 2024 evaluations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root** in the YOLO size-loss term $(\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2$ — examiners specifically check this; omitting it loses 1 full mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $\lambda_{\text{coord}}$ and $\lambda_{\text{noobj}}$ values** — students frequently swap the constants 5 and 0.5. Memorise: *coordinate = high (5), no-object = low (0.5)*.
> 3. **Not stating K-Means converges to a LOCAL minimum, not global** — this is a favourite conceptual trap.
> 4. **Wrong IoU sign or omitting union formula** — always write $A_1 + A_2 - A_I$, never just $A_1 + A_2$.
> 5. **In K-Means numericals, not showing the update step's explicit arithmetic** — partial marks are lost when students jump straight from distances to "final centroids" without intermediate calculation.
> 6. **Confusing *coordinate offsets* $b_x, b_y$ (cell-relative) with absolute image coordinates** — they are *normalised within the cell*, not the full image.
> 7. **Failing to mention Non-Maximum Suppression** in any YOLO question — even when asked only for architecture, a 1-line NMS mention is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — KTU Module 4 High-Yield Points:**

### K-Means Clustering & Segmentation
- K-Means is **unsupervised** and partitions $N$ points into $K$ clusters.
- Objective: minimise $J = \sum w_{ik} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2$.
- Two-step iteration: **Assignment** + **Update**.
- Centroid update = **arithmetic mean** of cluster members (proved by $\nabla_{\mu_k} J = 0$).
- **Convergence guarantee:** monotonic decrease of $J$; terminates at a local minimum.
- For segmentation: each pixel is a 3-D (or 5-D) feature vector in colour space.
- Sensitivity to initialisation → use **K-Means++** for better spread.

### YOLO Object Detection
- YOLO = **single-stage, one-shot** detection (real-time, 45–150 FPS).
- Image → $S \times S$ grid → each cell predicts $B$ boxes + $C$ class probabilities.
- Output tensor: $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$.
- Confidence $C = \Pr(\text{Object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$.
- Loss combines localisation + confidence + classification, weighted asymmetrically ($\lambda_{\text{coord}}=5$, $\lambda_{\text{noobj}}=0.5$).
- Square-root trick on $(w,h)$ for **scale-invariance** in size error.
- **NMS** is mandatory post-processing to prune redundant overlapping boxes.
- **IoU** measures overlap: $\text{IoU} = A_{\cap}/A_{\cup}$, range $[0,1]$.
- Advantages over R-CNN family: speed, global context, unified architecture.
- Limitation: struggles with very small / heavily occluded objects (later versions v3–v8 mitigate with multi-scale FPN).

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"K-Means minimises WCSS by iterative mean-update; YOLO maximises detection throughput by single-pass regression — together they represent the **unsupervised-segmentation → supervised-detection** pipeline of classical-to-modern computer vision."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Segmentation: Mask R-CNN and Instance Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Segmentation: Mask R-CNN and Instance Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Instance Segmentation** is a high-level computer vision task that combines the goals of **Object Detection** (localize and classify objects with bounding boxes) and **Semantic Segmentation** (assign a class label to every pixel). The objective is to produce a **per-pixel mask** for *each individual object instance* in the image, while simultaneously predicting a class label and a bounding box for that instance.

Formally, given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, the model outputs a set:
$$
\mathcal{O} = \{ (c_i, b_i, M_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, N \}
$$
where $c_i$ is the class label, $b_i = (x, y, w, h)$ is the bounding box, and $M_i \in \{0,1\}^{h \times w}$ is the binary segmentation mask for the $i^{th}$ detected object instance.

**Mask R-CNN** (Region-based Convolutional Neural Network with Mask Branch), introduced by He et al. (2017), is a state-of-the-art deep learning architecture that extends **Faster R-CNN** by adding a parallel **mask prediction branch**. It is the canonical solution for instance segmentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mask R-CNN is treated as the foundational instance segmentation model. Students must know the architecture, the role of **RoIAlign**, the three loss heads (classification, box regression, mask), and the difference between *instance* and *semantic* segmentation.

## 1.2 The Three Families of Segmentation

| Task | Output Type | Distinguishes Instances? |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Per-pixel class label | ❌ No (all "cars" are the same color) |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Per-pixel mask + per-instance ID | ✅ Yes (each "car" gets its own mask) |
| **Panoptic Segmentation** | Both unified | ✅ Yes + Stuff classes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon Animal Workshop" 🎈

Imagine you are a kindergarten teacher tasked with making a poster of a farm scene containing **three identical-looking white sheep**:

- **Image Classification** says: "There is a sheep in this picture." (One label, no location)
- **Object Detection** says: "There are three sheep, here are their bounding boxes." (Rectangles around each one)
- **Semantic Segmentation** says: "Every pixel belonging to 'sheep' is colored white." (But you cannot tell Sheep #1 from Sheep #2)
- **Instance Segmentation** says: "Sheep #1 is colored blue, Sheep #2 is colored green, Sheep #3 is colored red, **pixel-by-pixel**." (Every object gets its own colored sticker)

> **Mask R-CNN is the machine that produces the "colored stickers" for each individual sheep.**

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics

- **mAP @ IoU=0.5** : Mean Average Precision at Intersection-over-Union threshold of **0.5** — the standard instance segmentation metric on COCO.
- **mAP @ [0.5:0.95]** : Averaged mAP over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05 (the primary COCO metric).
- **AP$^S$, AP$^M$, AP$^L$** : AP for small, medium, large objects.
- **Mask mAP** : Computed using mask IoU instead of box IoU.

> [!NOTE]
> In Mask R-CNN literature, "AP" almost always refers to **Mask AP** unless explicitly stated otherwise, and is reported on the **COCO** benchmark by default.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of Mask R-CNN

Mask R-CNN is composed of **three logical stages** that operate in sequence:

### Tier 1 — Backbone (Feature Extractor)
- A deep CNN (typically **ResNet-50** or **ResNet-101** with **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)**).
- Converts the input image into a hierarchy of feature maps at multiple scales.
- **Why?** Multi-scale features are critical because objects in real images come in vastly different sizes.

### Tier 2 — Region Proposal Network (RPN)
- A lightweight CNN that slides over the feature map and proposes candidate object regions (**anchors**).
- Outputs:
  - **Objectness score** (foreground / background) for each anchor.
  - **Bounding box deltas** to refine anchor coordinates.
- **Why?** It reduces the search space from ~200k locations to ~2000 high-quality proposals.

### Tier 3 — Detection + Mask Heads (Per-Region Processing)
For each Region of Interest (RoI) extracted from the feature map:
1. **RoIAlign** layer extracts a **fixed-size feature map** (typically **$7 \times 7$**).
2. The features are fed into **three parallel branches**:
   - **Classification head** $\rightarrow$ predicts class $c$ (softmax over $K+1$ classes).
   - **Box regression head** $\rightarrow$ refines bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$.
   - **Mask head** $\rightarrow$ predicts a binary mask of size $m \times m$ (typically **$28 \times 28$**) for each of the $K$ classes.

## 2.2 RoIAlign — The Critical Innovation

Faster R-CNN used **RoIPool**, which introduced a quantization error by snapping RoI boundaries to discrete grid cells. For pixel-accurate mask prediction, this misalignment was catastrophic.

**RoIAlign** solves this by:
- Sampling the RoI into a grid of $h \times w$ bins.
- In each bin, computing the value at **exactly 4 regularly sampled locations** using **bilinear interpolation**.
- Aggregating (typically by max-pooling) the 4 samples per bin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RoIAlign yields a **pixel-to-pixel alignment** between the input and the extracted features — this is the *single most important architectural contribution* that made Mask R-CNN's masks so accurate.

## 2.3 The Multi-Task Loss Function

Mask R-CNN is trained by minimizing a **sum of three losses** computed per RoI:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls} + \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{mask}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{L}_{cls}$ is the **cross-entropy** loss for classification over $K+1$ classes.
- $\mathcal{L}_{box}$ is the **smooth L1** loss for bounding box regression.
- $\mathcal{L}_{mask}$ is the **per-pixel sigmoid + binary cross-entropy** loss, applied independently for each of the $K$ classes, producing a $K \times m^2$ output.

> **Key design choice:** $\mathcal{L}_{mask}$ is computed **only for the ground-truth class** of the RoI. This decouples mask prediction from class prediction and is a major improvement over FCN-style multi-class softmax masks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{cls}$ | Classification loss | $-\sum_{i} p_i \log \hat{p}_i$ | dimensionless |
| $L_{box}$ | Box regression loss (smooth L1) | $\sum_{i \in \{x,y,w,h\}} \text{smooth}_{L_1}(t_i - \hat{t}_i)$ | pixels |
| $L_{mask}$ | Mask loss (per-pixel BCE) | $-\frac{1}{m^2}\sum_{1 \le i,j \le m} [y_{ij} \log \hat{y}_{ij} + (1-y_{ij})\log(1-\hat{y}_{ij})]$ | dimensionless |
| IoU | Intersection over Union | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | unitless ratio |
| AP | Average Precision | $\sum_n (R_n - R_{n-1}) P_n$ | unitless ratio |
| mAP | Mean of AP across classes | $\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} AP_k$ | unitless ratio |
| RoIAlign grid | Feature size | $7 \times 7$ (det heads), $14 \times 14$ (mask head) | pixels |
| Mask output | Resolution | $m \times m$ where $m=28$ | pixels |
| Anchor scales | FPN levels | $\{32^2, 64^2, 128^2, 256^2, 512^2\}$ | pixels |
| Aspect ratios | Anchor shapes | $\{1:1, 1:2, 2:1\}$ | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> When writing the IoU formula in any KTU exam answer, prefer $\dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ — the examiner will check for correctness of numerator/denominator placement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RoIAlign Coordinate Sampling

**Given:** An RoI defined by continuous (non-quantized) coordinates $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ on a feature map, to be pooled into a $h \times w$ output (e.g., $7 \times 7$).

**Step 1 — Compute the bin size** of the RoI:
$$
\text{bin}_w = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{w}, \qquad \text{bin}_h = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{h}
$$
This is the *continuous* size of one output bin, **not rounded to integers** (this is the key difference from RoIPool).

**Step 2 — For each bin $(i, j)$ where $0 \le i < h$ and $0 \le j < w$**, compute the 4 sampling locations in the original feature map:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{a} &= x_1 + j \cdot \text{bin}_w + 0.25 \cdot \text{bin}_w \\
x_{b} &= x_1 + j \cdot \text{bin}_w + 0.75 \cdot \text{bin}_w \\
y_{c} &= y_1 + i \cdot \text{bin}_h + 0.25 \cdot \text{bin}_h \\
y_{d} &= y_1 + i \cdot \text{bin}_h + 0.75 \cdot \text{bin}_h
\end{aligned}
$$
The offsets 0.25 and 0.75 are chosen so that the 4 sample points are **evenly spread** within the bin.

**Step 3 — Apply bilinear interpolation** at each of the 4 points $(x_a, y_c), (x_b, y_c), (x_a, y_d), (x_b, y_d)$ using the surrounding feature map pixels.

**Step 4 — Aggregate** the 4 interpolated values (typically by **max** or **average**) to produce a single value for bin $(i, j)$.

> **Significance:** Unlike RoIPool, *no* rounding to integer pixel indices is performed. The fractional coordinates are preserved, so a sub-pixel shift in the input produces a corresponding sub-pixel shift in the output — eliminating the misalignment that plagued mask predictions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Task Loss Calculation

**Given:** A training RoI with ground-truth class $c^*$, ground-truth box $b^*$, and ground-truth mask $M^* \in \{0,1\}^{m \times m}$.

**Step 1 — Classification loss** (cross-entropy over $K+1$ classes):
$$
\mathcal{L}_{cls} = -\log \hat{p}_{c^*}
$$
where $\hat{p}_{c^*}$ is the predicted softmax probability for the true class.

**Step 2 — Box regression loss** (smooth L1, only for foreground RoIs):
$$
\mathcal{L}_{box} = \sum_{i \in \{x,y,w,h\}} \text{smooth}_{L_1}\!\left(t_i - \hat{t}_i\right)
$$
where
$$
\text{smooth}_{L_1}(x) = \begin{cases} 0.5x^2 & \text{if } \vert x \vert < 1 \\ \vert x \vert - 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$
and $t_i, \hat{t}_i$ are the true and predicted box parameterizations.

**Step 3 — Mask loss** (applied only on the ground-truth class slice):
$$
\mathcal{L}_{mask} = -\frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{1 \le i,j \le m} \Big[ M^*_{ij} \log \hat{M}^{c^*}_{ij} + (1 - M^*_{ij}) \log (1 - \hat{M}^{c^*}_{ij}) \Big]
$$
The mask branch outputs $K$ slices $\hat{M}^1, \hat{M}^2, \dots, \hat{M}^K$, each of size $m \times m$, after a **per-pixel sigmoid** (not softmax). The loss is computed **only on the slice corresponding to $c^*$**.

**Step 4 — Combine** with the same weighting as in the original paper:
$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls} + \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{mask}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mask branch does **not** compete across classes. Predicting the correct mask for class $c^*$ is sufficient — the other $K-1$ slices are ignored. This decouples segmentation from classification and is the key reason Mask R-CNN outperforms its FCN-based predecessors.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mask R-CNN vs. Other Segmentation Models

| Feature | FCN | U-Net | Mask R-CNN | YOLACT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Encoder-Decoder | Encoder-Decoder (skip) | Two-stage detection + mask | One-stage prototype |
| **Instance aware?** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Speed (FPS)** | ~5 | ~10 | ~5–10 | ~30–45 |
| **Mask resolution** | Full | Full | $28 \times 28$ upsampled | Full |
| **Backbone** | VGG/ResNet | Custom | ResNet-FPN | ResNet |
| **Output type** | Pixel class map | Pixel class map | Boxes + Class + Mask | Boxes + Class + Linear combo of prototypes |
| **Training data (COCO mAP)** | ~21 | ~25 | ~37 (R-50) | ~29 |
| **Best suited for** | Semantic seg | Biomedical | High-accuracy instance seg | Real-time instance seg |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — IoU Calculation

**Given:** A predicted bounding box $B_p = (0, 0, 10, 10)$ and a ground-truth box $B_g = (2, 2, 8, 8)$ on a 2D image plane. Compute the IoU.

**Step 1 — Intersection rectangle:**
$$
x_1 = \max(0, 2) = 2, \quad y_1 = \max(0, 2) = 2
$$
$$
x_2 = \min(10, 8) = 8, \quad y_2 = \min(10, 8) = 8
$$
$$
\text{Area}_{int} = (8 - 2) \times (8 - 2) = 36
$$

**Step 2 — Area of each box:**
$$
\text{Area}_p = 10 \times 10 = 100, \qquad \text{Area}_g = 6 \times 6 = 36
$$

**Step 3 — Union area:**
$$
\text{Area}_{union} = \text{Area}_p + \text{Area}_g - \text{Area}_{int} = 100 + 36 - 36 = 100
$$

**Step 4 — IoU:**
$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{36}{100} = 0.36
$$

Since $0.36 < 0.5$, the prediction would be considered a **False Positive** at the standard IoU=0.5 threshold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mask R-CNN End-to-End Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data flow of Mask R-CNN, from raw image input to the final per-instance mask output. All node IDs are alphanumeric and use safe labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[ResNet Backbone]
    B --> C[Feature Pyramid Network FPN]
    C --> D[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    D --> E[Anchor Boxes Generated]
    E --> F[Non-Maximum Suppression NMS]
    F --> G[Filtered Region Proposals]
    G --> H[RoIAlign Layer]
    H --> I1[Classification Head]
    H --> I2[Box Regression Head]
    H --> I3[Mask Prediction Head]
    I1 --> J1[Class Labels per RoI]
    I2 --> J2[Refined Bounding Boxes]
    I3 --> J3[28x28 Binary Masks]
    J1 --> K[Final Output Assembly]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Per-Instance Mask Upsampled to Full Resolution]
    L --> M[Visualization on Original Image]
```

## 4.2 RoIAlign vs RoIPool — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Feature Map] --> B1[RoI Continuous Coordinates]
    B1 --> C1[RoIPool Quantize to Integer]
    C1 --> D1[Bin Features]
    B1 --> C2[RoIAlign Keep Continuous]
    C2 --> D2[Compute 4 Sample Points per Bin]
    D2 --> E2[Bilinear Interpolation]
    E2 --> F2[Aggregate by Max or Average]
    D1 --> F1[Aggregate by Max]
    F1 --> G1[Output Misaligned]
    F2 --> G2[Output Pixel Aligned]
```

## 4.3 Three-Head Branch Architecture Detail

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[RoIAlign Feature 7x7x256] --> S1[Conv 3x3 + ReLU]
    R --> S2[Conv 3x3 + ReLU]
    R --> S3[4 Convs 3x3 + ReLU then 2x Deconv]
    S1 --> T1[FC Layer 1024]
    S1 --> T2[FC Layer 1024]
    S2 --> T3[FC 81 Classes Softmax]
    S2 --> T4[FC 4 Coordinates]
    S3 --> T5[Conv 1x1 Output K x 28 x 28]
    T3 --> U1[Class Prediction]
    T4 --> U2[Box Refinement]
    T5 --> U3[Mask Prediction]
```

## 4.4 Annotation Guide for the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for slow learners:**
> - **Backbone (ResNet-FPN)** = the "eyes" of the network that see at multiple scales.
> - **RPN** = the "spotter" that shouts "Hey, I think there's an object here!"
> - **RoIAlign** = the "ruler" that crops each spotted region precisely.
> - **Three heads** = three specialists: one classifies ("What is it?"), one draws the tight box ("Where exactly?"), and one cuts the silhouette ("Trace the outline pixel-by-pixel").
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**Differentiate between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Semantic Segmentation | Instance Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Classify every pixel into a category | Classify every pixel **and** separate each object instance |
| **Instance aware?** | ❌ No — two overlapping "cars" get the same label | ✅ Yes — each "car" gets a unique ID |
| **Output** | $H \times W$ class map | A set of (class, box, binary mask) tuples |
| **Example** | In a parking lot image, all cars are colored "red" | Each car gets a distinct mask (Car 1, Car 2, Car 3) |

*Example:* In a picture of a flock of identical white sheep — semantic segmentation colors all sheep white, while instance segmentation produces a *separate* binary mask for *each* sheep.

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition of each, 1 mark for example/contrast)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**What is RoIAlign? Why is it preferred over RoIPool in Mask R-CNN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RoIAlign** is a feature pooling layer in Mask R-CNN that extracts a **fixed-size feature map** (e.g., $7 \times 7$) from an RoI of *continuous* (non-quantized) coordinates by sampling **4 regularly spaced points per bin** and using **bilinear interpolation** to compute their values. **(2 marks)**

It is preferred over **RoIPool** because RoIPool performs *quantization* (rounding) of RoI boundaries to integer pixel indices, which introduces a **misalignment of ~10–20%** between the input image and the pooled features. For pixel-level mask prediction, this misalignment is unacceptable. RoIAlign preserves sub-pixel accuracy, yielding a **pixel-to-pixel alignment** between the original image and the output features. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Mask R-CNN with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each of its three parallel heads.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive and explain the multi-task loss function used to train Mask R-CNN. Highlight the special property of the mask loss.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

Mask R-CNN extends **Faster R-CNN** with an additional **mask prediction branch**. The architecture has **three sequential stages**:

**1. Backbone — ResNet + FPN (1 mark):**
- A ResNet-50 or ResNet-101 extracts hierarchical features.
- A **Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)** creates multi-scale feature maps $\{{P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5}\}$ corresponding to strides $\{4, 8, 16, 32\}$.

**2. Region Proposal Network (RPN) (1 mark):**
- Slides a $3 \times 3$ convolutional window over the FPN feature maps.
- At each spatial location, evaluates **$k$ anchor boxes** (default $k=3$ aspect ratios).
- Outputs:
  - **2k scores** — objectness (foreground vs background).
  - **4k box deltas** — anchor refinement.
- After NMS, the top ~2000 proposals are forwarded to the next stage.

**3. RoIAlign + Three Parallel Heads (3 marks):**
For each of the ~2000 RoIs, the **RoIAlign** layer extracts a fixed $7 \times 7$ feature map (or $14 \times 14$ for the mask head). This is fed into **three sibling branches**:
- **Classification head** — two FC layers (1024 units each) → softmax over $K+1$ classes. Output: class probability vector $\hat{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{K+1}$.
- **Box regression head** — two FC layers → 4 box coordinate refinements $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$.
- **Mask head** — a small fully convolutional network (4 conv layers + 1 deconv layer) that produces a $K \times m \times m$ output (default $K \times 28 \times 28$). After applying a per-pixel sigmoid, the slice corresponding to the predicted class is the binary mask for that instance.

**4. Inference (1 mark):**
- Boxes are decoded from $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ predictions.
- NMS is applied independently per class.
- Surviving masks are resized to the box dimensions and thresholded at 0.5 to produce binary output masks.

**Block Diagram Summary:**

```
Input Image
   ↓
ResNet-FPN Backbone
   ↓
RPN → Proposals
   ↓
RoIAlign (7x7 features)
   ↓
   ├── Classification head → Class
   ├── Box regression head → Bbox
   └── Mask head (28x28) → Mask
   ↓
Per-Instance Final Output
```

**(Mark distribution: 1 backbone, 1 RPN, 3 three heads, 1 inference, 1 diagram)**

---

#### Part (b) — Loss Function Derivation (7 marks)

The total loss is the sum of three terms:
$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls} + \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{mask}
$$

**1. Classification loss $\mathcal{L}_{cls}$ (1 mark):**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{cls} = -\sum_{i=0}^{K} p_i \log \hat{p}_i
$$
This is the standard multi-class cross-entropy over the $K+1$ classes (including background).

**2. Box regression loss $\mathcal{L}_{box}$ (1 mark):**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{box} = \sum_{i \in \{x, y, w, h\}} \text{smooth}_{L_1}(t_i - \hat{t}_i)
$$
where
$$
\text{smooth}_{L_1}(z) = \begin{cases} 0.5 z^2 & \text{if } \vert z \vert < 1 \\ \vert z \vert - 0.5 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**3. Mask loss $\mathcal{L}_{mask}$ — the special design (4 marks):**
The mask head outputs a tensor $\hat{M} \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times m \times m}$. A **per-pixel sigmoid** is applied, so each pixel has a probability of being foreground **independently for each of the $K$ classes** (no inter-class competition).

For a given RoI with ground-truth class $c^*$, the loss is computed **only on the $c^*$-th slice**:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{mask} = -\frac{1}{m^2} \sum_{1 \le u, v \le m} \Big[ M^*_{uv} \log \hat{M}^{c^*}_{uv} + (1 - M^*_{uv}) \log(1 - \hat{M}^{c^*}_{uv}) \Big]
$$

**Special property:** Unlike FCN-style segmentation (which uses a single softmax over all classes per pixel and thus couples class prediction with mask prediction), Mask R-CNN's per-class sigmoid + class-disjoint loss **decouples** mask prediction from classification. This means a class can be correctly masked even when classification is uncertain, and **competing classes do not suppress** the correct mask. This is the key reason for Mask R-CNN's superior mask quality.

**(Mark distribution: 1 each for L_cls and L_box, 4 for L_mask with the special property emphasized)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is instance segmentation? Explain the Mask R-CNN pipeline. Why is RoIAlign critical for mask prediction?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A predicted bounding box $B_p = (10, 10, 50, 50)$ and a ground-truth box $B_g = (20, 15, 50, 50)$ are given. Compute:
- (i) The Intersection over Union (IoU). (2 marks)
- (ii) The True Positive / False Positive status at IoU threshold 0.5. (1 mark)
- (iii) The Dice coefficient. (2 marks)
- (iv) Compare the suitability of IoU vs. Dice for evaluating mask quality. (2 marks)

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Instance Segmentation & Mask R-CNN Pipeline (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Instance segmentation is a vision task that requires **detecting each individual object instance**, classifying it, and producing a **per-pixel binary mask** that delineates its exact spatial extent.

**Pipeline (5 marks):**

1. **Input image preprocessing** — image is resized/normalized and passed through the backbone.
2. **Backbone feature extraction** — ResNet-50/101 + FPN produces multi-scale feature maps.
3. **RPN** — generates ~2000 region proposals with objectness scores.
4. **RoIAlign** — extracts a fixed-size $7 \times 7$ (or $14 \times 14$) feature crop from each proposal *without quantization*.
5. **Three parallel heads** — predict class, refine box, and generate a $28 \times 28$ mask.
6. **Post-processing** — NMS per class, mask thresholding at 0.5, mask upsampling to the original image size.

**Why RoIAlign is critical (1 mark):** Mask prediction requires **pixel-to-pixel alignment** between the input image and the output mask. RoIPool's quantization (rounding RoI boundaries to integer indices) introduces a misalignment of up to 1 pixel per bin, which — when stacked over many bins — accumulates into a significant error. For coarse tasks like classification this is harmless, but for fine-grained mask boundaries it produces **blurry, misaligned masks**. RoIAlign eliminates quantization by using **bilinear interpolation at sub-pixel locations**, preserving the geometric fidelity essential for high-quality masks.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $B_p = (x_1^p, y_1^p, x_2^p, y_2^p) = (10, 10, 50, 50)$ and $B_g = (x_1^g, y_1^g, x_2^g, y_2^g) = (20, 15, 50, 50)$.

**(i) IoU Calculation (2 marks):**

Intersection coordinates:
$$
x_1^{int} = \max(10, 20) = 20, \quad y_1^{int} = \max(10, 15) = 15
$$
$$
x_2^{int} = \min(50, 50) = 50, \quad y_2^{int} = \min(50, 50) = 50
$$
$$
\text{Area}_{int} = (50 - 20) \times (50 - 15) = 30 \times 35 = 1050
$$

Areas:
$$
\text{Area}_p = (50-10)\times(50-10) = 40 \times 40 = 1600
$$
$$
\text{Area}_g = (50-20)\times(50-15) = 30 \times 35 = 1050
$$

Union:
$$
\text{Area}_{union} = 1600 + 1050 - 1050 = 1600
$$

IoU:
$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{1050}{1600} = 0.65625
$$

**(ii) Status at threshold 0.5 (1 mark):**
Since $0.65625 \ge 0.5$, the prediction is a **True Positive (TP)** at this threshold.

**(iii) Dice coefficient (2 marks):**
The Dice coefficient is:
$$
\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \times 1050}{1600 + 1050} = \frac{2100}{2650} \approx 0.7925
$$

**(iv) IoU vs. Dice for mask evaluation (2 marks):**
- **IoU** penalizes the *union* (denominator), so it is more **sensitive to large false-positive regions** — even small extra false regions can drag IoU down sharply.
- **Dice** uses $2 \times \text{intersection}$ in the numerator, so it gives **more weight to the overlap** and is **more forgiving** of false positives but harsher on false negatives in small-mask scenarios.
- For **instance segmentation** of small objects, **Dice tends to be more informative** (less sensitive to trivial FP pixels in large backgrounds), while **IoU** is the standard for COCO-style evaluation because of its strict geometric interpretation and threshold-based ranking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Mask R-CNN Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing RoIAlign with RoIPool.** Many students write "RoIAlign quantizes to integer bins" — this is the *opposite* of the truth. RoIAlign deliberately avoids quantization. **Worth 1–2 marks loss.**
> 2. **Forgetting that the mask loss uses sigmoid (per-class), not softmax (across classes).** This is the **defining feature** of Mask R-CNN's mask branch. A wrong answer here loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Calling the mask branch an FCN with one output** — it actually outputs $K$ slices, and the loss is computed *only* on the ground-truth class slice.
> 4. **Confusing the mask output size.** It is $K \times m \times m$ (where $K$ = number of classes, $m=28$), not just $m \times m$.
> 5. **Drawing Faster R-CNN and calling it Mask R-CNN.** You must show the third (mask) branch in the block diagram.
> 6. **Mixing up Semantic vs. Instance segmentation definitions.** Always state the *instance-aware* property explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

- **Instance Segmentation** = Object Detection + Semantic Segmentation (per-instance pixel masks).
- **Mask R-CNN** = Faster R-CNN + a parallel **mask branch** + **RoIAlign**.
- **Three heads:** Classification (softmax $K+1$), Box Regression (smooth L1), Mask (per-pixel sigmoid, $K \times 28 \times 28$).
- **Total loss:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls} + \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{mask}$.
- **RoIAlign** uses **bilinear interpolation at 4 sample points per bin** — no quantization.
- **Mask loss** is computed **only on the ground-truth class slice** (decoupled from classification).
- **Backbone:** ResNet-50/101 + **FPN** for multi-scale features.
- **RPN** generates ~2000 proposals; NMS reduces to a few hundred.
- **Standard metrics:** Mask mAP @ IoU=0.5 and Mask mAP @ [0.5:0.95] on COCO.
- **Output format:** $\{(c_i, b_i, M_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ — class, box, mask per instance.
- **IoU formula:** $\dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$; threshold 0.5 is the standard for TP/FP.
- **Dice formula:** $\dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$.
- **Common variants:** Mask R-CNN with ResNet-50-FPN (fastest), ResNet-101-FPN (best accuracy), ResNeXt-101-FPN (highest accuracy).
- **Related architectures to know:** YOLACT (real-time), PANet (better feature aggregation), Cascade Mask R-CNN (multi-stage refinement).
- **COCO benchmark reference mAP (Mask R-CNN, R-50-FPN):** ~37.3 mask mAP.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: U-Net and Semantic Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** U-Net and Semantic Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semantic Segmentation — Formal Definition

**Semantic Segmentation** is a dense pixel-wise classification task in computer vision where every pixel in an input image is assigned a class label from a predefined set of categories. Unlike image classification (which produces one label per image) or object detection (which produces bounding boxes), semantic segmentation produces an output mask of the same spatial resolution as the input, where each pixel is classified independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT743 — Module 4):**
> Semantic segmentation is a *core dense prediction* problem. The output is a **segmentation map** (also called a *label map*) of dimensions $H \times W \times C$, where $C$ is the number of classes.

Mathematically, given an input image $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, the segmentation network $f_\theta$ produces:

$$\hat{Y} = f_\theta(X), \quad \hat{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$$

where $\hat{Y}_{i,j,k}$ is the predicted probability that pixel $(i,j)$ belongs to class $k$.

## 1.2 U-Net — Formal Definition

**U-Net** is a symmetric encoder–decoder convolutional neural network architecture introduced by Olaf Ronneberger et al. in **2015** for biomedical image segmentation. Its defining feature is the **U-shaped topology** comprising:
1. A **contracting path** (encoder) that captures *context*.
2. A **symmetric expanding path** (decoder) that enables *precise localization*.
3. **Skip connections** that concatenate feature maps from the encoder to the decoder at corresponding resolution levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "U-Net"?** Because a side-ways schematic diagram of the network literally traces the letter **"U"** — the spatial resolution shrinks on the left (encoder) and is restored on the right (decoder).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy for Semantic Segmentation: "The Coloring Book"
Imagine you are given a **black-and-white line drawing** (the input image) and a **set of colored pencils**, each representing a class (e.g., red = person, green = road, blue = sky, yellow = car). Your task is to color **every single pixel** such that the color correctly represents *what object* that pixel belongs to. That is semantic segmentation.

### Analogy for U-Net: "The Sculptor with a Memory"
Think of U-Net as a **sculptor carving a statue**:
- The **encoder** is the sculptor chiseling away details to understand the overall *shape and context* (what is this object?).
- The **decoder** is the sculptor rebuilding the statue at full resolution (where exactly is each part?).
- The **skip connections** are *photographs the sculptor keeps referring to* — the original fine-grained details from before chiseling, ensuring that small features are not lost during the aggressive downsampling.

> [!TIP]
> The encoder alone throws away spatial information to gain semantic meaning. The decoder alone cannot recover fine detail. **Skip connections are the bridge** that gives the decoder "access to the original photograph."

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Key Terms)

The following metrics are universally used to evaluate semantic segmentation models and are **board-exam favorites**:

- **IoU (Intersection over Union / Jaccard Index)**: measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth masks.
- **Dice Coefficient (F1-score for segmentation)**: similar to IoU but weighted toward the overlapping region.
- **Pixel Accuracy**: fraction of correctly classified pixels.
- **Mean IoU (mIoU)**: average IoU across all classes.
- **Boundary F1 (BF1)**: precision/recall on segmentation boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics range in $[0, 1]$, where **1 = perfect segmentation** and **0 = no overlap**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The U-Net Architecture — Structural Breakdown

U-Net has four repeating blocks per side:

### A. Contracting Path (Encoder) — Left Side
- Repeated application of two **$3 \times 3$ unpadded convolutions**, each followed by a **ReLU** activation.
- A **$2 \times 2$ max-pooling** operation with stride **2** for downsampling.
- **Number of feature channels doubles** at each downsampling step: $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$.

### B. Bottleneck (Bridge) — Bottom of the U
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU.
- Captures the most abstract, semantically rich but spatially compressed representation.

### C. Expansive Path (Decoder) — Right Side
- **$2 \times 2$ transposed convolution** (up-convolution) that halves the number of feature channels while doubling spatial resolution.
- **Skip connection**: feature map from the corresponding encoder level is **cropped and concatenated** along the channel axis.
- Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU follow each concatenation.

### D. Final Layer
- A $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of classes $C$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

| Component | Purpose | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| Convolution $3 \times 3$ | Learn local features | Detects edges, textures, patterns |
| ReLU | Non-linearity | Lets the network learn complex patterns |
| Max-pool $2 \times 2$ | Spatial downsampling | "Step back to see the bigger picture" |
| Transposed conv | Spatial upsampling | "Zoom in but lose some sharpness" |
| Skip connection | Preserve spatial detail | "Refer back to the original photograph" |
| $1 \times 1$ conv | Class projection | "Vote per pixel for each class" |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and set-membership symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to keep markdown tables valid.

### Segmentation Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pixel Accuracy** | $\text{PA} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Quick sanity check; biased toward large classes |
| **Precision** | $\text{P} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Measures false-positive sensitivity |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{R} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Measures false-negative sensitivity |
| **IoU (Jaccard)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Standard for benchmarks (Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC) |
| **Dice Coefficient** | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Preferred in medical imaging |
| **Mean IoU (mIoU)** | $\text{mIoU} = \dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{IoU}_c$ | $[0, 1]$ | Primary ranking metric in leaderboards |

### Loss Functions

| Loss | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy** | $L_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Multi-class segmentation baseline |
| **Weighted CE** | $L_{WCE} = -\sum_{c} w_c \, y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Class imbalance |
| **Focal Loss** | $L_{FL} = -\alpha (1-\hat{y})^\gamma \log(\hat{y})$ | Extreme class imbalance |
| **Dice Loss** | $L_{Dice} = 1 - \text{Dice}$ | Direct optimization of overlap |
| **Combined CE + Dice** | $L = \lambda L_{CE} + (1-\lambda) L_{Dice}$ | State-of-the-art training stability |

### Convolution / Transposed Convolution Output Size

| Operation | Output Size Formula |
|---|---|
| Valid Convolution | $n_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{in}} - k + 1}{s}$ (must be integer) |
| Same Convolution (with padding $p$) | $n_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{in}} + 2p - k}{s} + 1$ |
| Max Pool $2 \times 2$, stride 2 | $n_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{in}}}{2}$ |
| Transposed Conv (stride 2) | $n_{\text{out}} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{in}}$ |

Where $n_{\text{in}}$ = input spatial size, $k$ = kernel size, $s$ = stride, $p$ = padding.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging**: Tumor boundary segmentation in MRI/CT scans (U-Net's original use case).
- **Autonomous Driving**: Road, lane, pedestrian, vehicle segmentation (Cityscapes benchmark).
- **Satellite Imaging**: Land-cover classification, deforestation mapping.
- **Agriculture**: Crop vs. weed segmentation for precision spraying.
- **Industrial Inspection**: Surface defect pixel-level localization.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of IoU (Jaccard Index) from Set Theory

We define two sets:
- $A$ = the set of pixels predicted as the positive class.
- $B$ = the set of pixels actually belonging to the positive class (ground truth).

The **Jaccard Index** is defined as the ratio of the intersection to the union:

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

We now expand both numerator and denominator using the **inclusion–exclusion principle**:

$$\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$$

Therefore:

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$$

Now we translate each set-cardinality term into confusion-matrix quantities. Recall:
- $TP = \vert A \cap B \vert$ (true positives — correctly predicted).
- $FP = \vert A \setminus B \vert = \vert A \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$.
- $FN = \vert B \setminus A \vert = \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$.

Substituting:

$$\vert A \vert = TP + FP$$

$$\vert B \vert = TP + FN$$

Plugging into the numerator and denominator:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{TP}{(TP + FP) + (TP + FN) - TP} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

This is the standard KTU-examinable form of the **IoU formula**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dice Coefficient from IoU

The Dice coefficient is also called the **Sørensen–Dice index**. It is defined as:

$$D(A, B) = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

We want to show its algebraic relation to IoU. Starting from the IoU derivation above:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$$

Let $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$ and $y = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert$. Then:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{x}{y - x}$$

And:

$$D = \frac{2x}{y}$$

To relate them, multiply the Dice numerator and denominator by an appropriate factor. We observe:

$$\frac{2x}{y} = \frac{x}{(y/2)} = \frac{x}{(y - x)/2 + x/2}$$

A more direct approach is to compute $D$ in terms of IoU. Let $J = \text{IoU} = \frac{x}{y - x}$. Solving for $y$:

$$y = \frac{x}{J} + x = x \left(\frac{1 + J}{J}\right)$$

Substituting into Dice:

$$D = \frac{2x}{y} = \frac{2x}{x \left(\frac{1 + J}{J}\right)} = \frac{2J}{1 + J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **closed-form conversion** between Dice and IoU:
> $$D = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$$

Conversely, inverting:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{D}{2 - D}$$

This relationship is **commonly tested** in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss for a Single Pixel

For a single pixel with ground-truth one-hot label $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_C)$ and predicted softmax probability $\hat{y} = (\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \ldots, \hat{y}_C)$, the **categorical cross-entropy** is:

$$L = - \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$$

Since $y$ is one-hot, exactly one term is nonzero. Suppose the true class is $c^*$, then $y_{c^*} = 1$ and all other $y_c = 0$:

$$L = - \log(\hat{y}_{c^*})$$

For an image with $N$ pixels:

$$L_{\text{total}} = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log\left(\hat{y}_{i, c^*_i}\right) = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

This is the per-image cross-entropy loss used in U-Net training.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — U-Net Layer-by-Layer Dimensions

Let the input image be **$572 \times 572 \times 1$** (the original U-Net paper's input). The network has **5 levels** including the bottleneck.

| Level | Block | Operation | Spatial Dim | Channels |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Input | — | $572 \times 572$ | 1 |
| 1 | Encoder 1 | 2× Conv 3×3 + ReLU | $568 \times 568$ | 64 |
| 1 | Pool 1 | MaxPool 2×2 / 2 | $284 \times 284$ | 64 |
| 2 | Encoder 2 | 2× Conv 3×3 + ReLU | $280 \times 280$ | 128 |
| 2 | Pool 2 | MaxPool 2×2 / 2 | $140 \times 140$ | 128 |
| 3 | Encoder 3 | 2× Conv 3×3 + ReLU | $136 \times 136$ | 256 |
| 3 | Pool 3 | MaxPool 2×2 / 2 | $68 \times 68$ | 256 |
| 4 | Encoder 4 | 2× Conv 3×3 + ReLU | $64 \times 64$ | 512 |
| 4 | Pool 4 | MaxPool 2×2 / 2 | $32 \times 32$ | 512 |
| 5 | Bottleneck | 2× Conv 3×3 + ReLU | $28 \times 28$ | 1024 |
| 6 | Up-conv 1 | TransposedConv 2×2 | $56 \times 56$ | 512 |
| 6 | Concat + Conv | Concat(Enc4) → 2× Conv 3×3 | $52 \times 52$ | 1024 → 512 |
| 7 | Up-conv 2 | TransposedConv 2×2 | $104 \times 104$ | 256 |
| 7 | Concat + Conv | Concat(Enc3) → 2× Conv 3×3 | $100 \times 100$ | 512 → 256 |
| 8 | Up-conv 3 | TransposedConv 2×2 | $200 \times 200$ | 128 |
| 8 | Concat + Conv | Concat(Enc2) → 2× Conv 3×3 | $196 \times 196$ | 256 → 128 |
| 9 | Up-conv 4 | TransposedConv 2×2 | $392 \times 392$ | 64 |
| 9 | Concat + Conv | Concat(Enc1) → 2× Conv 3×3 | $388 \times 388$ | 128 → 64 |
| 10 | Final Conv | 1×1 Conv | $388 \times 388$ | $C$ (num classes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the output is **smaller** than the input due to the **unpadded convolutions**. This is the original U-Net design — a border-pixel loss was acceptable for the microscopy images used. Modern implementations use **same-padding convolutions** to preserve spatial dimensions end-to-end.

## 3.5 Skip Connection Math — Concatenation

At each decoder level $\ell$, the up-sampled feature map $U_\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{H_\ell \times W_\ell \times F_\ell}$ is concatenated with the cropped encoder feature map $E_\ell \in \mathbb{R}^{H_\ell \times W_\ell \times F_\ell}$ along the channel axis:

$$D_\ell^{\text{raw}} = \text{Concat}(U_\ell, E_\ell) \in \mathbb{R}^{H_\ell \times W_\ell \times 2F_\ell}$$

Followed by two $3 \times 3$ convolutions that reduce channels back to $F_\ell$:

$$D_\ell = \text{Conv}_{3\times 3}\left(\text{ReLU}\left(\text{Conv}_{3\times 3}\left(\text{ReLU}(D_\ell^{\text{raw}})\right)\right)\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{H_\ell \times W_\ell \times F_\ell}$$

This operation preserves both **high-level semantics** (from $U_\ell$) and **low-level spatial detail** (from $E_\ell$).
