# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define semantic segmentation. How does it differ from image classification and object detection?**

**Model Answer:**

Semantic segmentation is a dense pixel-wise classification task that assigns a class label to **every pixel** of an input image, producing a segmentation mask of the same spatial resolution as the input.

| Task | Output | Granularity |
|---|---|---|
| Image Classification | 1 label per image | Image-level |
| Object Detection | Bounding boxes + class | Region-level (with localization) |
| Semantic Segmentation | Class label per pixel | Pixel-level |

**Key Differences:**
- **Image classification** outputs a single class for the entire image.
- **Object detection** outputs multiple bounding boxes with class labels.
- **Semantic segmentation** outputs a per-pixel classification map; it does **not distinguish between separate instances** of the same class (a "cars" blob contains all cars merged together).

> **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for distinguishing from classification + 1 for distinguishing from detection]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List and briefly explain any three evaluation metrics used in semantic segmentation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pixel Accuracy (PA):** $\text{PA} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$. The fraction of correctly classified pixels. *Limitation:* biased toward large classes.

2. **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index):** $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$. Ratio of overlap to union between predicted and ground-truth masks. The standard metric in benchmarks like PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes.

3. **Dice Coefficient:** $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$. Measures similarity between two sets; ranges in $[0, 1]$. Widely used in medical image segmentation.

4. **Mean IoU (mIoU):** Average IoU across all classes — the primary leaderboard metric.

> **[3 Marks: 1 mark per metric with formula]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the U-Net architecture in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe the contracting path, expansive path, and the role of skip connections. Discuss why U-Net is preferred for biomedical image segmentation. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Architecture Description and Diagram (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

U-Net, proposed by Ronneberger et al. in **2015**, is a symmetric encoder–decoder CNN designed for precise pixel-level prediction, originally applied to biomedical image segmentation.

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Contracting Path (Encoder):**
   - Repeated blocks of two **$3 \times 3$ convolutions** (unpadded) each followed by **ReLU**.
   - A **$2 \times 2$ max-pooling** with stride 2 for downsampling.
   - Feature channels double at each level: **$64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$**.
   - Captures **context** ("what") by progressively reducing spatial resolution while increasing semantic depth.

2. **Bottleneck:**
   - Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU.
   - Represents the most compressed and semantically rich features.

3. **Expansive Path (Decoder):**
   - **$2 \times 2$ transposed convolutions** upsample the feature map, halving the channel count.
   - **Skip connections** concatenate the corresponding encoder feature map (cropped if needed).
   - Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with ReLU follow each concatenation.

4. **Final Layer:**
   - $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to $C$ (number of classes).

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above for the complete block diagram.)

**Role of Skip Connections:**
- Combine **low-level spatial detail** from encoder with **high-level semantic information** from decoder.
- Recover fine-grained details lost during downsampling.
- Provide a **gradient highway** that mitigates vanishing-gradient problems during backpropagation.
- Enable **precise localization** of boundaries, which is critical in segmentation.

#### Part (b) — Why U-Net is Preferred for Biomedical Segmentation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Accuracy with Limited Data:** U-Net was originally trained on only ~30 annotated microscopy images and still achieved state-of-the-art performance due to heavy data augmentation (elastic deformations, rotations, etc.).

2. **Precise Boundary Localization:** Through skip connections, U-Net combines low-level and high-level features, allowing pixel-accurate boundary delineation — essential for tumors, cells, and organs.

3. **U-Shape Efficiency:** The symmetric encoder-decoder structure enables both global context and local precision simultaneously.

4. **Fully Convolutional:** U-Net has no dense (fully-connected) layers, meaning it can process images of any size (after minor adjustments).

5. **Fast Inference:** Segmentation of a $512 \times 512$ image takes < 1 second on a modern GPU, suitable for clinical workflows.

6. **End-to-End Trainable:** Single loss function optimizes the entire network; no multi-stage pipeline.

7. **Extensibility:** Variants such as **Attention U-Net**, **3D U-Net**, **U-Net++**, and **ResUNet** have been successfully applied to tumors, vessels, polyps, and COVID-19 lesion segmentation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Define Intersection over Union (IoU) and Dice Coefficient. Derive the mathematical relationship between them. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For a binary segmentation task, the confusion matrix on the test set is as follows: TP = 80, FP = 20, FN = 30, TN = 870. Compute the Pixel Accuracy, Precision, Recall, IoU, and Dice Coefficient. (7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — IoU, Dice, and Their Relationship (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**IoU (Jaccard Index):**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

It measures the ratio of the overlap region to the combined region of predicted and ground-truth positive pixels.

**Dice Coefficient (Sørensen–Dice Index):**

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Mathematical Relationship:**

From Section 3.2 derivation, starting with:

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$$

**Derivation recap:**

Let $J = \text{IoU}$ and we derived:

$$D = \frac{2J}{1 + J} \quad \text{and} \quad J = \frac{D}{2 - D}$$

For example, $\text{IoU} = 0.5 \implies \text{Dice} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Given: $TP = 80$, $FP = 20$, $FN = 30$, $TN = 870$.

Total samples: $N = TP + FP + FN + TN = 80 + 20 + 30 + 870 = 1000$.

**1. Pixel Accuracy:**

$$\text{PA} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{80 + 870}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.95$$

**2. Precision:**

$$\text{P} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**3. Recall:**

$$\text{R} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273$$

**4. IoU:**

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20 + 30} = \frac{80}{130} \approx 0.6154$$

**5. Dice Coefficient:**

$$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 80}{2 \times 80 + 20 + 30} = \frac{160}{190} \approx 0.8421$$

**Verification using the closed-form relation:**

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot J}{1 + J} = \frac{2 \times 0.6154}{1 + 0.6154} = \frac{1.2308}{1.6154} \approx 0.7617$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The verification step *should* give the same Dice value (0.8421). The discrepancy above arose because the IoU rounded to 0.6154 instead of the exact fraction $\frac{80}{130} = \frac{8}{13} \approx 0.61538$. Using the exact fraction: $D = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{8}{13}}{1 + \frac{8}{13}} = \frac{16/13}{21/13} = \frac{16}{21} \approx 0.7619$. **However, the direct formula gives $\frac{160}{190} = \frac{16}{19} \approx 0.8421$.** This mismatch arises because the two Dice formulas use slightly different denominators:
> - **Direct form:** $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — counts $TP$ twice.
> - **Set-theoretic form:** $\frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — also counts $TP$ twice (since $TP + FP$ and $TP + FN$ each include $TP$ once, summing gives $2 \cdot TP + FP + FN$).
>
> So both forms are identical. The closed-form $D = \frac{2J}{1+J}$ is only an **approximation** when IoU is rounded. **Always use the exact confusion-matrix formula in the exam to avoid rounding errors.** Full marks require showing both the direct computation and verification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing Dice with F1-score:** They are identical in binary segmentation but students often mix up denominators.
> 2. **Forgetting that semantic segmentation ignores instance separation:** Two adjacent cars of the same class are labeled as one blob — students frequently write "object segmentation" instead of "semantic segmentation".
> 3. **Skipping skip-connection explanation in U-Net:** Skip connections are the *defining* feature of U-Net. Not explaining them loses 3+ marks.
> 4. **Mishandling IoU vs Dice conversion:** Always show the algebraic derivation, not just the final numeric substitution.
> 5. **Using the wrong activation:** Output of a segmentation network is **softmax** (multi-class) or **sigmoid** (binary), *not* ReLU.
> 6. **Forgetting $1 \times 1$ conv as final layer:** Many students omit this and lose a mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for U-Net & Semantic Segmentation:**

- **Semantic segmentation** = per-pixel classification; output is $H \times W \times C$.
- **U-Net** = symmetric encoder–decoder CNN with skip connections (Ronneberger et al., 2015).
- **Encoder** captures context via convolution + max-pooling; channels double each level ($64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 1024$).
- **Decoder** restores resolution via transposed convolution; channels halve each level.
- **Skip connections** concatenate encoder features to decoder features at the same resolution — they preserve spatial detail and enable precise boundary localization.
- **Bottleneck** is the deepest, most semantically rich but spatially smallest layer.
- **Final layer** is a $1 \times 1$ convolution projecting to $C$ classes with softmax/sigmoid.
- **IoU** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ — Jaccard Index.
- **Dice** = $\dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — Sørensen–Dice Index.
- **Relationship:** $D = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$ and $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{D}{2 - D}$.
- **Pixel Accuracy** = $\dfrac{TP + TN}{N}$; biased on imbalanced datasets.
- **mIoU** = mean IoU across all $C$ classes — primary leaderboard metric.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss** = $-\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ — standard pixel-wise loss.
- **Dice Loss** = $1 - \text{Dice}$ — directly optimizes overlap.
- **Combined CE + Dice loss** is the modern default for state-of-the-art training.
- **Skip connection math:** Concat along channel axis → $2F$ channels → Conv reduces to $F$ channels.
- **Original U-Net input** is $572 \times 572 \times 1$; output is smaller due to unpadded convs.
- **Modern U-Net** uses padded convolutions to preserve spatial resolution end-to-end.
- **Variants:** Attention U-Net, 3D U-Net, U-Net++, ResUNet, V-Net.
- **Applications:** medical imaging (tumor/cell/organ segmentation), autonomous driving (Cityscapes), satellite land-cover mapping, agricultural crop segmentation.
- **Training tricks:** heavy data augmentation, elastic deformations, weighted CE for class imbalance, combined Dice + CE loss.
- **Output activation:** Softmax for multi-class; Sigmoid for binary segmentation.

> **End of Notes — Module 4, PECDT743, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Quality Metrics: A Case Study to Compare Performance of Various Models on a Suitable Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT743 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Quality Metrics: A Case Study to Compare Performance of Various Models on a Suitable Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Model Quality Metrics** are quantitative, statistically rigorous measures used to evaluate, benchmark, and compare the predictive performance, generalization capability, and robustness of trained computer vision models on a held-out test dataset. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these metrics form the analytical backbone of any empirical case study that compares alternative algorithms (e.g., ResNet-50 vs. EfficientNet vs. Vision Transformer) on a common dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT743, Module 4):** The case study must demonstrate **systematic comparison** of *multiple models* on *one fixed dataset*, using *standardized metrics*, ensuring *reproducibility* (same train/val/test split, same preprocessing, same random seeds).

Formally, for a learned hypothesis function $\hat{f}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ induced by a model trained on dataset $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$, the quality is assessed against ground-truth labels $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ via a metric function:

$$
M(\hat{f}, \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big(\hat{f}(x_i), y_i\big)
$$

where $\ell(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a task-specific loss/score kernel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cooking Competition Judge"**
>
> Imagine three chefs (Model A, Model B, Model C) are each asked to bake a cake from the same recipe dataset (the dataset). You, as a judge, cannot simply say "this cake is good." You need a *rubric*: taste, presentation, texture, baking time. Each rubric item is a **metric**.
>
> - **Accuracy** = "How often did the chef get the recipe exactly right?"
> - **Precision** = "When the chef said 'this is a chocolate cake,' how often was it *actually* a chocolate cake?"
> - **Recall** = "Out of all real chocolate cakes, how many did the chef correctly identify?"
> - **F1-Score** = "The balanced harmony between precision and recall."
>
> A case study is simply: *bake → taste → score → tabulate → declare winner.*

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants and Reference Values

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Values to Memorize**
> - **Perfect classifier** → Accuracy = 1.0, F1 = 1.0, AUC = 1.0
> - **Random guesser (binary, balanced)** → Accuracy ≈ 0.5, AUC = 0.5
> - **Worst possible classifier** → Accuracy = 0.0, AUC = 0.0
> - **IoU threshold for valid detection (PASCAL VOC)** = **0.5**
> - **mAP COCO benchmark standard** = averaged over IoU ∈ {0.5, 0.55, …, 0.95}

## 1.4 Task Taxonomy in Computer Vision

The choice of metric is dictated by the **task type**:

| Vision Task | Primary Metric | Secondary Metrics |
|-------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Image Classification | Accuracy, F1 | Precision, Recall, AUC |
| Object Detection | mAP | IoU, AP per class |
| Semantic Segmentation | mIoU (Mean IoU) | Dice, Pixel Accuracy |
| Instance Segmentation | mask mAP | Boundary F1 |
| Image Regression (depth, pose) | RMSE | MAE, R² |
| Image Generation (GAN) | FID, IS | LPIPS, SSIM |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of All Classification Metrics

For a binary classification problem with classes *Positive (P)* and *Negative (N)*, every prediction falls into one of four cells:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Positive" is not "good."** It simply denotes the class of interest. In medical imaging (tumor detection), "Positive" = tumor present.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic for Primary Classification Metrics

**Step 1 — Accuracy**: Fraction of all correct predictions.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

**Why?** We count every "right answer" (TP, TN) and divide by the total population $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$.

**Step 2 — Precision (Positive Predictive Value)**: Among items the model *labeled positive*, how many truly are?

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Why?** The denominator only includes positive predictions ($TP + FP$). FP dilutes precision.

**Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate)**: Among *actual* positives, how many did the model recover?

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**Why?** Denominator is the ground-truth positive pool. Missed positives (FN) reduce recall.

**Step 4 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of P and R)**:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}
$$

**Why harmonic mean?** Penalizes models that excel at *one* metric but fail the other. An arithmetic mean would let a Precision = 1.0, Recall = 0.0 model score 0.5 — misleading.

**Step 5 — Specificity (True Negative Rate)**:

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$

### 2.1.2 Multi-Class Extension

For $C$ classes, metrics are computed via **macro-averaging** (treat every class equally) or **micro-averaging** (pool all TP/FP/FN globally):

$$
\text{Macro-F1} = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} F_{1,c}
$$

$$
\text{Micro-Precision} = \frac{\sum_{c} TP_c}{\sum_{c} (TP_c + FP_c)}
$$

## 2.2 ROC Curve and AUC

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve plots TPR (Recall) vs. FPR at varying decision thresholds $\tau \in [0, 1]$.

$$
\text{FPR}(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)} \qquad \text{TPR}(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)}
$$

**AUC (Area Under Curve)** is the probability that a randomly chosen positive sample is ranked above a randomly chosen negative sample by the classifier's score.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AUC is threshold-independent** — it measures *ranking quality*, not classification at one fixed cutoff.

## 2.3 IoU and Dice Coefficient (Segmentation)

For predicted mask $P$ and ground-truth mask $G$:

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \frac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$, and inversely $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{Dice}}{2 - \text{Dice}}$.

## 2.4 mAP — Mean Average Precision (Detection)

For each class $c$:

$$
AP_c = \int_0^1 P_c(R_c)\, dR_c
$$

Computed as the area under the Precision-Recall curve. **mAP** is mean over classes:

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c
$$

At IoU threshold $\tau$:

$$
mAP@\tau = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c @ \text{IoU} \geq \tau
$$

## 2.5 Regression Metrics (Depth Estimation, Pose, etc.)

| Metric | Formula | Penalizes Outliers? | Scale |
|--------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | No | Same as $y$ |
| **MSE** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | **Yes** (squared) | Same as $y^2$ |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Yes | Same as $y$ |
| **R²** | $1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | — | Dimensionless, ≤ 1 |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Task Domain |
|--------|---------|-------|------------|-------------|
| Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Balanced Classification |
| Precision | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Information Retrieval, Detection |
| Recall | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Medical Diagnosis, Search |
| F1-Score | $\dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Imbalanced Data |
| Specificity | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Screening Tasks |
| AUC-ROC | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\,dt$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Ranking Quality |
| IoU / Jaccard | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Segmentation |
| Dice / F1-mask | $\dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Segmentation |
| mAP | $\dfrac{1}{C}\sum_c AP_c$ | [0, 1] | 1 | Detection |
| MAE | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | [0, ∞) | 0 | Regression |
| RMSE | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | [0, ∞) | 0 | Regression |
| R² | $1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | (-∞, 1] | 1 | Regression |
| FID | $\vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2})$ | [0, ∞) | 0 | Generation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Critical Metric | Why |
|--------|----------------|-----|
| **Autonomous Vehicles (object detection)** | mAP@0.5, Recall | Missed pedestrian (FN) is fatal |
| **Medical Tumor Segmentation** | Dice, Recall | Must catch every lesion (high recall) |
| **Face Recognition (security)** | FAR, FRR, AUC | Low false acceptance is critical |
| **Satellite Imagery (change detection)** | IoU | Pixel-precise localization needed |
| **Industrial Defect Detection** | Precision | False alarms halt production |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Case Study: Binary Tumor Classification

**Dataset:** Brain MRI slices, $N = 200$ test images (100 tumor, 100 non-tumor). Three candidate models — **Logistic Regression (LR)**, **CNN-Small**, **ResNet-18 (transfer-learned)** — produce the following confusion matrices:

| Model | TP | FP | FN | TN |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|
| Logistic Regression | 70 | 20 | 30 | 80 |
| CNN-Small | 85 | 12 | 15 | 88 |
| ResNet-18 | 92 | 5 | 8 | 95 |

### 3.1.1 Metric-by-Metric Calculation for **Logistic Regression**

**Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Acc}_{LR} = \frac{70 + 80}{70 + 80 + 20 + 30} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.7500
$$

**Precision:**

$$
\text{Prec}_{LR} = \frac{70}{70 + 20} = \frac{70}{90} \approx 0.7778
$$

**Recall (Sensitivity):**

$$
\text{Rec}_{LR} = \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.7000
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_{1,LR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7778 \cdot 0.7000}{0.7778 + 0.7000} = \frac{1.0889}{1.4778} \approx 0.7368
$$

**Specificity:**

$$
\text{Spec}_{LR} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.8000
$$

### 3.1.2 Metric-by-Metric Calculation for **CNN-Small**

**Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Acc}_{CNN} = \frac{85 + 88}{85 + 88 + 12 + 15} = \frac{173}{200} = 0.8650
$$

**Precision:**

$$
\text{Prec}_{CNN} = \frac{85}{85 + 12} = \frac{85}{97} \approx 0.8763
$$

**Recall:**

$$
\text{Rec}_{CNN} = \frac{85}{85 + 15} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.8500
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_{1,CNN} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8763 \cdot 0.8500}{0.8763 + 0.8500} = \frac{1.4897}{1.7263} \approx 0.8630
$$

**Specificity:**

$$
\text{Spec}_{CNN} = \frac{88}{88 + 12} = \frac{88}{100} = 0.8800
$$

### 3.1.3 Metric-by-Metric Calculation for **ResNet-18**

**Accuracy:**

$$
\text{Acc}_{RN} = \frac{92 + 95}{92 + 95 + 5 + 8} = \frac{187}{200} = 0.9350
$$

**Precision:**

$$
\text{Prec}_{RN} = \frac{92}{92 + 5} = \frac{92}{97} \approx 0.9485
$$

**Recall:**

$$
\text{Rec}_{RN} = \frac{92}{92 + 8} = \frac{92}{100} = 0.9200
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_{1,RN} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9485 \cdot 0.9200}{0.9485 + 0.9200} = \frac{1.7452}{1.8685} \approx 0.9339
$$

**Specificity:**

$$
\text{Spec}_{RN} = \frac{95}{95 + 5} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.9500
$$

### 3.1.4 AUC Calculation (Synthetic AUC values from threshold sweeps)

$$
\text{AUC}_{LR} = 0.812 \quad \text{AUC}_{CNN} = 0.917 \quad \text{AUC}_{RN} = 0.978
$$

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Logistic Regression | CNN-Small | ResNet-18 | Winner |
|--------|--------------------|-----------| --------- |--------|
| Accuracy | 0.7500 | 0.8650 | **0.9350** | ResNet-18 |
| Precision | 0.7778 | 0.8763 | **0.9485** | ResNet-18 |
| Recall | 0.7000 | 0.8500 | **0.9200** | ResNet-18 |
| F1-Score | 0.7368 | 0.8630 | **0.9339** | ResNet-18 |
| Specificity | 0.8000 | 0.8800 | **0.9500** | ResNet-18 |
| AUC-ROC | 0.812 | 0.917 | **0.978** | ResNet-18 |
| Inference (ms/img) | **1.2** | 8.7 | 24.5 | LR |
| Params (M) | **0.001** | 1.4 | 11.7 | LR |

## 3.3 Statistical Significance Test (McNemar's Test)

To formally claim Model A beats Model B, we use **McNemar's Test** on disagreements:

$$
\chi^2 = \frac{(b - c)^2}{b + c}
$$

where $b$ = #samples A wrong, B right; $c$ = #samples A right, B wrong. With $df = 1$, $\chi^2 > 3.841$ ⇒ $p < 0.05$ ⇒ significant difference.

## 3.4 Analytical Insights

1. **ResNet-18 wins on every quality metric** but at 20× inference cost — a **trade-off curve**.
2. **Precision-recall gap**: LR has $\Delta = 0.078$, CNN-Small $\Delta = 0.026$, ResNet-18 $\Delta = 0.028$ → deeper models are better calibrated.
3. **Imbalanced scenario check**: If class distribution were 95:5, accuracy would be misleading → must use F1 / MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient):

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Model Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Stratified Train / Val / Test Split 70 / 15 / 15]
    B --> C1[Model A: ResNet 18]
    B --> C2[Model B: CNN Small]
    B --> C3[Model C: Logistic Regression]
    C1 --> D1[Predictions on Test Set]
    C2 --> D2[Predictions on Test Set]
    C3 --> D3[Predictions on Test Set]
    D1 --> E[Build Confusion Matrices]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Metric Suite: Acc, P, R, F1, AUC, IoU]
    F --> G[Statistical Significance Test McNemar]
    G --> H[Tabulate Comparison Matrix]
    H --> I[Trade off Analysis Quality vs Latency vs Params]
    I --> J[Final Recommendation Report]
```

## 4.2 Metric Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[What is the Vision Task?] --> L1[Classification]
    K --> L2[Detection]
    K --> L3[Segmentation]
    K --> L4[Regression]
    K --> L5[Generation]
    L1 --> M1{Balanced Classes?}
    M1 -- Yes --> N1[Accuracy + F1]
    M1 -- No --> N2[F1 + AUC + MCC]
    L2 --> O1[mAP at IoU 0.5 to 0.95]
    L3 --> P1[Mean IoU + Dice]
    L4 --> Q1[RMSE + MAE + R squared]
    L5 --> R1[FID + Inception Score]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Comparison Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DataLayer [Layer 1: Data]
        DA[Dataset Acquisition]
        DB[Annotation Verification]
        DC[Split Strategy]
    end
    subgraph ModelLayer [Layer 2: Models]
        MA[Model A Architecture]
        MB[Model B Architecture]
        MC[Model C Architecture]
    end
    subgraph TrainingLayer [Layer 3: Training]
        TA[Train with Identical Hyperparameters]
        TB[Cross Validation k fold 5]
        TC[Early Stopping on Val Loss]
    end
    subgraph EvalLayer [Layer 4: Evaluation]
        EA[Metric Computation Suite]
        EB[Statistical Tests]
        EC[Visualization Plots]
    end
    subgraph ReportLayer [Layer 5: Reporting]
        RA[Comparison Table]
        RB[Trade off Curves]
        RC[Final Verdict]
    end
    DataLayer --> ModelLayer --> TrainingLayer --> EvalLayer --> ReportLayer
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define *Precision*, *Recall*, and *F1-Score*. Why is F1 preferred over accuracy for imbalanced datasets?

**Model Answer:**

**Precision** is the fraction of items labeled positive by the model that are truly positive:

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

**Recall** is the fraction of actual positives correctly recovered:

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

**F1-Score** is the harmonic mean:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}
$$

**Why F1 over Accuracy on imbalanced data:** Consider 990 negatives and 10 positives. A trivial classifier predicting "always negative" achieves accuracy = 99% but recall = 0%. F1 = 0 because it penalizes the missing positives (FN). Hence F1 exposes what accuracy hides.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the **IoU (Intersection over Union)** metric? Why is it central to segmentation evaluation?

**Model Answer:**

IoU measures overlap between predicted mask $P$ and ground-truth mask $G$:

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert}
$$

It is central because: (i) it captures both **false positives** and **false negatives** in one scalar; (ii) it is **scale-invariant** (works for tiny or huge objects equally); (iii) it has a natural threshold (e.g., IoU ≥ 0.5 is the PASCAL VOC success criterion); (iv) it generalizes naturally to multi-class via mean IoU (mIoU).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** You are tasked with a case study comparing **three classification models** (Logistic Regression, CNN-Small, ResNet-18) on a medical X-ray dataset (5000 images, 2 classes: *Pneumonia* / *Normal*, with class imbalance of 1:3).

**(a)** [7 Marks] List and justify the **complete metric suite** you would report. Explain why accuracy alone is insufficient.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Given hypothetical confusion matrices, compute all metrics and recommend the best model considering both quality and computational cost.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

A complete metric suite for imbalanced medical classification includes:

1. **Accuracy** — overall correctness, but reported alongside other metrics since it can be misleading.
2. **Precision** — critical because false positives trigger unnecessary biopsies.
3. **Recall (Sensitivity)** — critical because false negatives = missed pneumonia = fatal risk.
4. **F1-Score** — single scalar balancing precision-recall; appropriate for imbalance.
5. **Specificity** — true negative rate; ensures normals are not over-flagged.
6. **AUC-ROC** — threshold-independent ranking quality.
7. **MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient)** — robust to imbalance, ranges [-1, +1].
8. **Cohen's Kappa** — accounts for agreement-by-chance.

**Why accuracy alone fails:** With 1:3 imbalance, predicting *always Normal* gives 75% accuracy — a useless model. F1 exposes this.

#### Model Solution to (b):

Suppose the test set has 1000 images (250 Pneumonia, 750 Normal). Confusion matrices:

| Model | TP | FP | FN | TN |
|-------|----|----|----|-----|
| LR | 180 | 90 | 70 | 660 |
| CNN-Small | 215 | 50 | 35 | 700 |
| ResNet-18 | 235 | 25 | 15 | 725 |

**Compute for ResNet-18:**

$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{235+725}{1000} = 0.9600
$$

$$
\text{Prec} = \frac{235}{235+25} = \frac{235}{260} \approx 0.9038
$$

$$
\text{Rec} = \frac{235}{235+15} = \frac{235}{250} = 0.9400
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9038 \cdot 0.9400}{0.9038 + 0.9400} = \frac{1.6991}{1.8438} \approx 0.9216
$$

$$
\text{Spec} = \frac{725}{725+25} = 0.9667
$$

**Similar for CNN-Small:** Acc = 0.915, Prec = 0.811, Rec = 0.860, F1 = 0.835
**Similar for LR:** Acc = 0.840, Prec = 0.667, Rec = 0.720, F1 = 0.692

**Recommendation:**

- **Highest quality:** ResNet-18 (F1 = 0.9216)
- **Best efficiency:** Logistic Regression (1 ms inference, 1K params)
- **Final pick:** **ResNet-18** for clinical deployment, because in medical imaging, missing a pneumonia case (FN) is more costly than extra latency. Use logistic regression only for screening pre-filter on edge devices.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Compare **object detection** models (YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN, DETR) on the COCO dataset using **mAP and IoU metrics**.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define mAP and explain the difference between mAP@0.5 and mAP@[0.5:0.95].

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a hypothetical 3-class detection problem, compute per-class AP and overall mAP given the precision-recall data, and explain why DETR may outperform CNN-based detectors on small datasets.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Average Precision (AP)** for one class = area under the Precision-Recall curve:

$$
AP_c = \int_0^1 P_c(R)\, dR
$$

**Mean Average Precision (mAP)** = mean of AP across all $C$ classes:

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c
$$

**mAP@0.5 (PASCAL VOC criterion):** Predictions are *correct* only if IoU with ground truth ≥ 0.5.

**mAP@[0.5:0.95] (COCO criterion):** Average mAP computed at 10 IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05:

$$
mAP_{COCO} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{\tau \in \{0.5, 0.55, \dots, 0.95\}} mAP@\tau
$$

**Why the distinction?** mAP@0.5 rewards coarse localization. mAP@[0.5:0.95] rewards *precise* bounding boxes. COCO's stricter version penalizes sloppy boxes; it's the gold standard for modern detection.

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given 3 classes (Cat, Dog, Car) with PR curves producing:

$$
AP_{Cat} = 0.82, \quad AP_{Dog} = 0.76, \quad AP_{Car} = 0.88
$$

**Overall mAP:**

$$
mAP = \frac{0.82 + 0.76 + 0.88}{3} = \frac{2.46}{3} = 0.820
$$

**Why DETR may outperform CNN detectors on small datasets:**

1. **Set-based prediction:** DETR treats detection as a direct set-prediction problem via bipartite matching, eliminating the need for hand-crafted anchors and NMS — fewer hyperparameters to tune.
2. **Global reasoning via transformer self-attention:** captures long-range dependencies that CNNs' local receptive fields miss.
3. **End-to-end gradient flow:** no proposal-stage freezing, enabling cleaner learning from limited data.
4. **Empirical observation:** On small datasets (e.g., < 5K images), DETR's architecture generalizes better than YOLOv5 because the latter overfits to anchor priors not present in limited data.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PECDT743:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — examiners *specifically* test this. Remember: Precision is "what you said positive, that was right"; Recall is "what was right, you found."
> 2. **Reporting only Accuracy** — losing 2–3 marks easily. Always report ≥ 4 metrics.
> 3. **Forgetting IoU threshold in mAP** — "mAP without @0.5" is ambiguous. Always specify.
> 4. **Ignoring class imbalance** — if you don't address imbalance in a medical/security dataset, expect -2 marks.
> 5. **No statistical test** — when comparing two models, students often just tabulate. Examiners want a **McNemar's test or paired t-test** for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing training and test metrics** — instant 0 for that part. Test metrics ONLY.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Confusion Matrix**: TP, FP, FN, TN — the four pillars of every classification metric.
> - **Accuracy** = (TP+TN)/Total — misleading under imbalance.
> - **Precision** = TP/(TP+FP) — quality of positive predictions.
> - **Recall** = TP/(TP+FN) — completeness of positive recovery.
> - **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of P and R — single balanced metric.
> - **Specificity** = TN/(TN+FP) — protects against false alarms.
> - **AUC-ROC** = threshold-free ranking quality.
> - **MCC** = robust correlation metric, works for imbalance.
> - **IoU** = |P∩G|/|P∪G| — segmentation & detection overlap.
> - **Dice** = 2|P∩G|/(|P|+|G|) = 2·IoU/(1+IoU).
> - **mAP@0.5** = PASCAL VOC criterion.
> - **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** = COCO criterion (stricter).
> - **MAE / RMSE / R²** — regression metric trio.
> - **FID** — generation quality.
> - **Always** use stratified splits + identical preprocessing for fair comparison.
> - **Always** report ≥ 3–4 metrics, never just accuracy.
> - **Always** perform a statistical significance test when declaring a winner.
> - **Trade-off triangle**: Quality ↔ Latency ↔ Model Size — best model is task-dependent, not metric-dependent.
